title: Asphyxiants Safety Program
summary: A comprehensive Asphyxiants Safety Program covering hazard identification, atmospheric monitoring, ventilation, gas storage and handling, leak response, alarm procedures, training, contractor coordination, emergency response, and program auditing for simple and chemical asphyxiants.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, evaluating, communicating, and controlling hazards associated with simple asphyxiants and chemical asphyxiants in the workplace at [Company Name]. The purpose is to prevent injury, illness, serious injury, fatality, property damage, and operational disruption resulting from oxygen-deficient atmospheres, oxygen displacement, impaired oxygen transport, impaired cellular oxygen use, and related hazardous conditions.

Asphyxiant hazards can cause rapid incapacitation and death, often without warning signs detectable by normal human senses. This program requires systematic hazard identification, engineering controls, atmospheric monitoring, ventilation, safe work practices, training, and emergency preparedness to protect all personnel from asphyxiant-related incidents.

This procedure supports compliance with applicable OSHA standards, industry codes, and company policies. It applies to both simple asphyxiants (gases that displace oxygen, such as nitrogen, argon, and helium) and chemical asphyxiants (substances that interfere with oxygen transport or cellular oxygen use).
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, laboratories, production areas, test areas, maintenance activities, field locations, storage areas, utility systems, and non-routine activities where employees or contractors may be exposed to asphyxiants. It applies to employees, supervisors, managers, EHS personnel, engineers, technicians, operators, maintenance personnel, contractors, and emergency response personnel as applicable.

This procedure applies to hazards associated with, but not limited to:
• Nitrogen
• Argon
• Helium
• Carbon dioxide
• Cryogenic gases and liquefied gas systems
• Refrigerants
• Process gases
• Inerting and purging operations
• Pressurized gas systems
• Enclosed or poorly ventilated spaces
• Bulk gas storage and distribution systems
• Gas cabinets, vessels, pits, vaults, tanks, test cells, and confined spaces
• Combustion-related atmospheres or other oxygen-deficient environments

This SOP applies to both:
• Simple asphyxiants — gases that displace oxygen in the breathing zone without other significant toxic effects
• Chemical asphyxiants — substances that interfere with oxygen transport in the blood or oxygen use at the cellular level

definitions.definition: A substance capable of causing oxygen deficiency in the breathing zone or impairing the body's ability to transport or use oxygen, resulting in the risk of injury, illness, or death.
A gas that displaces oxygen in the surrounding atmosphere without producing significant toxic effects at concentrations that cause oxygen deficiency. Examples include nitrogen, argon, and helium.

A substance that interferes with the body's ability to transport oxygen (e.g., carbon monoxide binding to hemoglobin) or to use oxygen at the cellular level (e.g., hydrogen cyanide inhibiting cytochrome oxidase).
An atmosphere containing less than 19.5 percent oxygen by volume.
An atmosphere containing greater than 23.5 percent oxygen by volume, which increases fire and combustion risk.
The use of calibrated instruments to measure oxygen concentration and the presence of hazardous gases or vapors in the breathing zone or work area.
A liquefied gas maintained at extremely low temperatures, typically below minus 150 degrees Celsius, which can rapidly expand into large volumes of gas and displace oxygen upon release.

Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health — any condition that poses an immediate or delayed threat to life, would cause irreversible adverse health effects, or would interfere with an individual's ability to escape unaided from a dangerous atmosphere.
An area with limited openings for entry and exit and limited or restricted natural ventilation, where hazardous gas accumulation or oxygen deficiency may occur.
An unplanned or uncontrolled discharge of gas into the work environment, whether from equipment failure, leak, venting, operational upset, or human error.
The introduction of fresh air into or removal of contaminated or oxygen-deficient air from a work area by natural or mechanical means.
A calibrated instrument designed to continuously or periodically measure and display oxygen concentration in the surrounding atmosphere, typically with audible and visual alarms at preset thresholds.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Develop and maintain this program. Conduct hazard assessments for asphyxiant-related operations and areas. Establish monitoring requirements, alarm setpoints, and area classifications. Review and approve controls for higher-risk work. Verify training, calibration, and inspection programs. Support incident investigation and corrective action. Conduct periodic program audits and reviews. Provide technical guidance on gas hazards, ventilation, monitoring, and emergency response.

Ensure personnel under their direction understand and comply with this procedure. Verify that hazard assessments, monitoring, ventilation, and controls are in place before work begins. Stop work when conditions are unsafe or not in compliance. Ensure only trained and authorized personnel work in designated asphyxiant hazard areas. Report incidents, alarms, leaks, and near misses. Ensure equipment is maintained and inspected. Support training, drills, and program improvements.

Design, install, and maintain gas systems, ventilation systems, and monitoring systems in compliance with applicable codes and this procedure. Evaluate and address asphyxiant hazards during system design, modification, and change management. Ensure leak prevention through proper materials, fittings, connections, pressure relief, and inspection programs. Support hazard assessments and incident investigations as needed.

Follow all requirements of this procedure. Attend required training and maintain awareness of asphyxiant hazards in their work areas. Use monitoring equipment and PPE as required. Report leaks, alarms, equipment malfunctions, abnormal conditions, and near misses immediately. Evacuate when directed or when alarm conditions exist. Do not enter areas suspected of being oxygen-deficient without authorization and proper controls. Do not attempt unauthorized rescue.

Comply with this procedure and all applicable site safety requirements. Inform the host employer of gases and systems they will bring onsite or use. Use monitoring, ventilation, and controls as required by the scope of work and this program. Report all incidents, leaks, alarms, and near misses to the host employer. Participate in coordination meetings and pre-job briefings. Follow all evacuation and alarm response requirements.

Maintain readiness and training for asphyxiant-related emergencies, including oxygen-deficiency rescue. Use appropriate respiratory protection, monitoring, and rescue equipment. Coordinate with supervision, EHS, and outside agencies as required. Do not enter hazardous atmospheres without proper equipment, training, and team support. Participate in drills and exercises.

training: Recognition of asphyxiant hazards — simple asphyxiants and chemical asphyxiants
Common gases and applications onsite including nitrogen, argon, helium, carbon dioxide, and cryogenic systems
Oxygen-deficiency hazards and physiological effects — rapid onset, no warning odor or color
Use of oxygen monitors, multi-gas detectors, and fixed detection systems
Alarm systems — alarm meanings, setpoints, and required responses
Ventilation requirements — when required, how to verify, limitations
Work planning expectations — hazard assessment, permits, JSAs, and pre-task reviews
Entry restrictions — areas requiring authorization, monitoring, or permits
Leak and alarm response — stop work, evacuate, warn others, notify supervision and EHS
Emergency actions — evacuation, found-down response, prohibition on unauthorized rescue
Cryogenic safety where applicable — expansion ratios, cold-contact hazards, pressure buildup
Connections to confined space entry, hazard communication, and respiratory protection programs
Reporting requirements for incidents, alarms, near misses, and equipment failures
CPR and first aid awareness for oxygen-deficiency emergencies
ppe: Portable oxygen monitor or calibrated multi-gas detector as required by area classification or task
Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA) or supplied air respirator for oxygen-deficient or IDLH atmospheres — managed under the Respiratory Protection Program
Safety glasses or chemical splash goggles based on the hazard profile
Face shield where chemical splash or cryogenic spray is possible
Hard hat in designated areas
Chemical-resistant or cryogenic gloves as applicable to the gas or liquid handled
Steel-toe safety footwear
Hearing protection in high-noise areas associated with gas systems or ventilation equipment
Protective clothing or cryogenic apron where contact with cryogenic fluids is possible
High-visibility vest where required by site traffic management

NOTE: Air-purifying respirators do not supply oxygen and shall not be used for oxygen-deficient atmospheres. Any respiratory protection for oxygen-deficient or IDLH conditions must be managed under the Respiratory Protection Program.
hazards: Oxygen displacement leading to rapid unconsciousness, brain damage, or death — often without warning
Simple asphyxiant gases (nitrogen, argon, helium) that are colorless, odorless, and undetectable by human senses
Chemical asphyxiants (carbon monoxide, hydrogen cyanide) that impair oxygen transport or cellular oxygen use
Rapid expansion of cryogenic fluids displacing large volumes of breathable air
Accumulation of heavier-than-air gases in pits, trenches, vaults, and low-lying areas
Lighter-than-air gas accumulation in ceiling pockets, overhead spaces, and poorly ventilated upper areas
Cryogenic burns and cold-contact injuries from liquefied gases
Pressure hazards from compressed gas cylinders, bulk systems, and pressure relief discharges
Material embrittlement and equipment failure from cryogenic temperatures
Fire and explosion risk from oxygen-enriched atmospheres
Ventilation failure allowing undetected gas accumulation
Fog or vapor clouds obscuring visibility during cryogenic releases
Cascading hazards when gas releases affect adjacent occupied areas
procedure:
**7.1 HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND ASSESSMENT**

All systems, areas, and tasks involving asphyxiants shall be evaluated to identify potential hazards and determine the controls required to protect personnel.

Hazard assessments shall consider:
• Type and properties of gas (density, expansion ratio, toxicity, physical state)
• Quantity of gas available for release (cylinder, bulk, process, cryogenic)
• Ventilation conditions — natural airflow, mechanical ventilation, dead zones
• Room or area geometry — size, ceiling height, below-grade features, enclosures
• Credible leak or release scenarios — connection failures, valve operations, pressure relief, maintenance activities
• Occupancy — number of workers, duration, proximity to release points
• Alarm coverage — fixed detectors, portable monitors, alarm audibility
• Adjacent operations — shared spaces, occupied areas that could be affected
• Purge, vent, or inerting tasks that intentionally introduce asphyxiant gases

Examples of higher-risk areas requiring assessment:
• Inert gas rooms and gas distribution manifolds
• Pits, vaults, trenches, and below-grade spaces
• Cryogenic transfer and fill areas
• Gas cabinets and ventilated enclosures
• Bulk gas storage and distribution systems
• Test cells, pressure vessels, and enclosed process areas
• Laboratories using inert or cryogenic gases
• Areas adjacent to pressure relief or vent discharge points

**7.2 CLASSIFICATION OF ASPHYXIANT HAZARD AREAS**

[Company Name] shall classify areas where oxygen deficiency or asphyxiant exposure could reasonably occur. Classifications shall be documented and communicated to affected personnel.

Classification categories include:
• Low potential areas — adequate ventilation, small gas quantities, low likelihood of significant release
• Credible release areas — sufficient gas volume, ventilation limitations, or conditions where oxygen deficiency could develop during normal operations, maintenance, or credible upset
• Areas requiring continuous monitoring or restricted access — where fixed detection, alarms, and access controls are warranted
• Areas tied to confined space controls — where the area also meets the definition of a confined space
• Authorized personnel only areas — where access is limited to trained, authorized individuals with specific controls

Signage shall be posted at entrances to classified areas as appropriate:
• WARNING — ASPHYXIANT HAZARD — OXYGEN DEFICIENCY MAY OCCUR — AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL ONLY
• DANGER — OXYGEN DEFICIENT ATMOSPHERE POSSIBLE — DO NOT ENTER WITHOUT AUTHORIZATION

Area classifications shall be reviewed when systems, ventilation, gas quantities, or operations change.

**7.3 WORK PLANNING AND AUTHORIZATION**

Work involving asphyxiant hazards shall be planned before work begins. The level of planning shall be appropriate to the risk.

Planning shall address:
• Gas involved — type, quantity, physical state, properties
• Release points — connections, valves, vents, relief devices, maintenance break points
• Monitoring requirements — portable, fixed, continuous, pre-entry
• Ventilation — natural, mechanical, adequacy, verification
• Isolation — LOTO, valve closures, blanking, disconnects
• PPE — respiratory protection, cryogenic PPE, monitoring equipment
• Communication — between workers, with adjacent areas, with supervision
• Emergency actions — evacuation routes, alarm response, rescue provisions
• Adjacent personnel — notification, barricading, exclusion zones
• Permits — confined space, hot work, line break, permit to work as applicable
• Barricades — physical barriers, signage, access restrictions
• Non-routine activities — venting, purging, sampling, draining, disconnecting gas systems

Possible controls and authorizations include:
• Task risk assessment or Job Safety Analysis (JSA)
• Permit to work or task-specific work permit
• Confined space entry permit where applicable
• Line break permit for opening gas systems
• Lockout/Tagout for energy isolation
• EHS review and approval for higher-risk work
• Engineering review for system modifications
• Operations approval for process-related tasks

**7.4 ATMOSPHERIC MONITORING**

Atmospheric monitoring shall be performed where oxygen deficiency or other asphyxiant hazards may be present or could develop during work activities.

Monitoring methods include:
• Fixed oxygen detectors and alarm systems in classified areas
• Portable oxygen monitors or multi-gas detectors for task-specific monitoring
• Continuous monitoring during work in higher-risk areas or during operations that could release asphyxiant gases
• Pre-entry atmospheric checks before entering enclosed or below-grade areas
• Post-upset or post-alarm checks before reoccupying evacuated areas

Acceptable atmospheric conditions:
• Oxygen concentration between 19.5% and 23.5% by volume
• Oxygen less than 19.5% — oxygen-deficient atmosphere — hazardous
• Oxygen greater than 23.5% — oxygen-enriched atmosphere — hazardous (fire risk)

All monitoring equipment shall be properly calibrated, bump-tested before use, and maintained according to manufacturer requirements and company procedures.

Monitoring strategy shall consider:
• Gas behavior — density relative to air, stratification potential
• Worker breathing zone location
• Possible accumulation in low-lying areas, ceiling pockets, or dead zones
• Multiple sampling points where applicable
• Continuous versus periodic monitoring based on hazard severity and work duration

**7.5 VENTILATION REQUIREMENTS**

Ventilation shall be provided where needed to prevent oxygen deficiency or hazardous gas accumulation in occupied areas.

Ventilation design and operation shall consider:
• Space size, geometry, and configuration
• Gas properties — density, expansion ratio, release rate
• Occupancy — number of workers, duration of exposure
• Air paths — supply and exhaust locations, potential dead zones
• Adjacent areas that could be affected by displaced air

For mechanical ventilation:
• Air supply shall be from a clean, uncontaminated source
• Airflow shall be directed to the worker breathing zone and work area
• Ventilation effectiveness shall be verified by atmospheric monitoring
• Ventilation equipment shall be inspected and maintained regularly

Ventilation shall not be assumed effective without atmospheric verification.

Recirculation of potentially contaminated or oxygen-deficient air is prohibited unless specifically engineered, tested, and approved for the application.

**7.6 GAS STORAGE, HANDLING, AND USE**

Compressed gases, cryogenic fluids, and asphyxiant systems shall be stored, handled, and used in accordance with applicable codes, manufacturer requirements, and this procedure.

Requirements include:
• Cylinders shall be secured upright in approved racks, carts, or restraints to prevent falling or damage
• Cylinders shall be protected from heat, direct sunlight, physical impact, and corrosive conditions
• Only compatible regulators, fittings, and connections shall be used — no adapters or modifications without engineering approval
• Leak checks shall be performed on new connections and after maintenance using approved methods
• All containers shall be properly labeled with contents, hazards, and handling information
• Valves shall be opened slowly and controlled — rapid valve operations on high-pressure systems are prohibited
• Gas shall not be vented into occupied areas unless ventilation and monitoring confirm safe conditions

Special precautions for cryogenic systems:
• Cryogenic fluids can expand rapidly into very large volumes of gas — one liter of liquid nitrogen produces approximately 694 liters of gas at ambient conditions
• Oxygen displacement can occur extremely quickly in enclosed or poorly ventilated areas
• Fog or vapor clouds may obscure visibility and indicate cold gas at or near ground level
• Pressure buildup in sealed or poorly vented cryogenic vessels can cause rupture
• Cold-contact hazards can cause severe burns, tissue damage, and frostbite
• Material embrittlement may cause equipment failure at cryogenic temperatures

**7.7 LEAK PREVENTION AND LEAK RESPONSE**

**Leak Prevention:**
• Regular inspection and maintenance of gas systems, fittings, valves, regulators, and piping
• Use of approved components, materials, and connections rated for the service
• Review of pressure relief device settings and discharge locations
• Leak testing of new installations, modifications, and post-maintenance connections
• Maintenance and testing of fixed detection and alarm systems
• Management of change review for system modifications

**Leak Response — Step by Step:**
1. Stop work if conditions are or may become unsafe
2. Warn affected personnel in the immediate area
3. Evacuate if oxygen deficiency or hazardous exposure is possible — do not delay
4. Do not re-enter until the atmosphere has been evaluated and declared safe by authorized personnel
5. Notify supervision and EHS immediately
6. Isolate the release remotely if safe, authorized, and practical to do so
7. Initiate emergency response as required by the severity of the release

Employees shall not attempt to stop a leak in a hazardous atmosphere unless trained, authorized, properly equipped, and conditions allow safe action. Unauthorized intervention in an oxygen-deficient or unknown atmosphere is prohibited.

**7.8 ENTRY INTO SUSPECT OR OXYGEN-DEFICIENT ATMOSPHERES**

No employee shall enter an area suspected of being oxygen-deficient or otherwise hazardous from asphyxiant exposure unless:
• The area has been evaluated by atmospheric monitoring
• Necessary controls are in place (ventilation, isolation, PPE, communication, rescue provisions)
• Entry is authorized under applicable procedures

Where the area also meets the definition of a confined space, the [Company Name] Confined Space Entry Program applies and takes precedence for entry controls.

Entry into IDLH or oxygen-deficient atmospheres is prohibited except under approved emergency or specialty response procedures with:
• Proper respiratory protection (SCBA or supplied air)
• Trained and equipped personnel
• Rescue provisions and backup team
• Command oversight and communication

Unauthorized rescue attempts are strictly prohibited. Personnel discovering a downed or unresponsive person in a potentially oxygen-deficient area shall not enter but shall immediately activate the emergency response system and prevent others from entering.

**7.9 ALARM RESPONSE AND EVACUATION**

All personnel shall respond immediately to oxygen deficiency or related atmospheric alarms.

Required actions:
• Stop work immediately
• Evacuate the affected area using established routes
• Warn others in the area
• Notify supervision and EHS
• Prevent unauthorized re-entry into the affected area
• Do not re-enter until the atmosphere has been evaluated and the area has been declared safe by authorized personnel

Repeated alarms shall be investigated. Treating recurring alarms as nuisance events without evaluation, root cause review, and corrective action is prohibited.

Alarm systems shall be tested and maintained on a regular schedule. Alarm setpoints shall be established by EHS based on applicable standards, gas properties, and site conditions.

**7.10 CONFINED, ENCLOSED, AND LOW-LYING AREAS**

Pits, vaults, trenches, tunnels, utility spaces, tanks, vessels, test enclosures, and poorly ventilated rooms present elevated asphyxiant risk because gases can accumulate undetected.

Additional controls for these areas include:
• Pre-entry atmospheric monitoring before any person enters
• Continuous monitoring during occupancy
• Mechanical ventilation to maintain safe oxygen levels
• Restricted access — authorized personnel only
• Permit controls where required by area classification
• Confined space review and entry procedures where applicable
• Attendant or standby personnel for higher-risk entries
• Rescue planning and equipment staging

Oxygen-deficient atmospheres often have no warning odor, color, or visible sign. Personnel cannot rely on their senses to detect oxygen deficiency. Instrumented monitoring is the only reliable method to confirm a safe atmosphere.

**7.11 TRAINING AND HAZARD COMMUNICATION**

All personnel who work in or near asphyxiant hazard areas shall receive training before initial assignment and when hazards, procedures, equipment, or responsibilities change.

Training topics shall include:
• Simple asphyxiants versus chemical asphyxiants — differences and hazards
• Common gases and applications at [Site Name]
• Oxygen deficiency — causes, physiological effects, rapid onset
• Limitations of relying on symptoms — oxygen deficiency impairs judgment before victims recognize danger
• Alarm systems — types, meanings, setpoints, required responses
• Monitoring equipment — portable monitors, fixed detectors, proper use and limitations
• Ventilation — requirements, verification, limitations
• Safe work practices — planning, permits, JSAs, pre-task reviews
• Emergency response — evacuation, alarm response, found-down procedures, unauthorized rescue prohibition
• Reporting — incidents, alarms, leaks, near misses, equipment failures
• Connections to related programs — Confined Space Entry, Hazard Communication, Respiratory Protection

Hazard communication shall include:
• Labels on gas cylinders, cryogenic vessels, and bulk systems
• Safety Data Sheets (SDSs) accessible for all asphyxiant gases
• Warning signage at classified areas and entry points
• Line and pipe markings where applicable
• Postings and bulletins as needed for awareness and updates

**7.12 PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT**

PPE shall be selected based on the full hazard profile of the task, area, and materials involved.

Applicable PPE may include:
• Eye protection — safety glasses or chemical splash goggles
• Face shields — where cryogenic splash or chemical spray is possible
• Gloves — chemical-resistant or cryogenic gloves appropriate to the materials handled
• Footwear — steel-toe safety boots
• Cryogenic PPE — apron, face shield, and cryogenic gloves where contact with cryogenic fluids is possible
• Hearing protection — in high-noise areas associated with gas systems, ventilation equipment, or alarms
• Protective clothing — as required by the hazard assessment
• Respiratory protection — SCBA or supplied air for oxygen-deficient or IDLH atmospheres

**CRITICAL:** Air-purifying respirators (APRs) do not supply oxygen and shall not be used for oxygen-deficient atmospheres. Any respiratory protection required for oxygen-deficient or IDLH conditions shall be managed under the [Company Name] Respiratory Protection Program.

PPE shall be inspected before each use, maintained in serviceable condition, and replaced when damaged or expired.

**7.13 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION**

Before contractor work begins in or near asphyxiant hazard areas, the host employer and contractor shall exchange hazard information and coordinate controls.

[Company Name] shall inform contractors of:
• Known asphyxiant hazard areas, classifications, and signage
• Fixed alarm systems — locations, alarm meanings, and required response actions
• Access restrictions, monitoring requirements, and ventilation provisions
• Emergency actions, evacuation routes, and assembly points
• Reporting requirements for incidents, leaks, alarms, and near misses

Contractors shall inform [Company Name] of:
• Gases and gas systems they will bring onsite, use, or install
• Hazards introduced by their work — including venting, purging, or pressurizing activities
• Controls they will implement, including monitoring, ventilation, and PPE
• Any incidents, leaks, alarms, or near misses associated with their work

Where multiple employers share the same area, coordination shall ensure that one employer's activities do not create uncontrolled asphyxiant hazards for another employer's personnel.

**7.14 EMERGENCY RESPONSE**

Emergency response planning for asphyxiant-related incidents shall address:
• Alarm notification and activation
• Evacuation procedures and assembly points
• Access control — preventing unauthorized entry into hazardous areas
• Atmospheric evaluation before re-entry or rescue
• Leak isolation — remote shutoff where available and safe
• Medical response for oxygen-deficiency exposure
• Confined space rescue implications where applicable
• Rescue restrictions — trained and equipped personnel only
• Coordination with site emergency response team and outside agencies as needed

**Found-Down Response:**
If a person is found down, unresponsive, or in distress in an area where asphyxiant hazards may exist:
• Do NOT rush in without evaluating the atmosphere — this is the leading cause of multiple-fatality asphyxiant incidents
• Activate the emergency response system immediately
• Prevent additional personnel from entering the area
• Provide responders with relevant gas type, area, and system information
• Use only trained and properly equipped rescue personnel if entry is required
• Ensure rescue personnel have appropriate respiratory protection (SCBA or supplied air)

Drills and exercises shall be conducted periodically to verify readiness and response effectiveness.

**7.15 INCIDENT REPORTING AND INVESTIGATION**

The following events shall be reported and investigated per [Company Name] incident reporting procedures:
• Oxygen alarms or atmospheric alarm activations
• Gas leaks, unplanned releases, or uncontrolled venting
• Abnormal releases from pressure relief devices or process upsets
• Near misses involving asphyxiant exposure or oxygen deficiency
• Detector or monitoring equipment failures
• Ventilation system failures affecting classified areas
• Unauthorized entry into restricted asphyxiant hazard areas
• Personnel symptoms potentially related to oxygen deficiency or gas exposure

Investigations shall evaluate:
• Gas involved and source of release
• Ventilation conditions at the time of the event
• Detector and alarm performance
• Alarm response and evacuation effectiveness
• Procedure adequacy and compliance
• Training adequacy
• Design, maintenance, or equipment issues
• Human factors
• Corrective and preventive actions

Findings and corrective actions shall be documented, tracked, and communicated to affected personnel.

**7.16 PROGRAM REVIEW AND AUDITING**

EHS shall conduct periodic reviews of this program to verify effectiveness and identify opportunities for improvement.

Reviews shall include:
• Hazard area inventory and classification accuracy
• Fixed detection and alarm system coverage and condition
• Calibration and maintenance records for monitoring equipment
• Training completion and currency
• Gas storage and handling practices
• Incident trends, alarm frequency, and near-miss patterns
• Contractor coordination effectiveness
• Ventilation system adequacy and maintenance
• Facility or operational changes that may affect asphyxiant hazards
• Corrective action completion and effectiveness

Deficiencies shall be corrected in a timely manner and tracked to closure. Program review findings shall be communicated to management and used to update procedures, training, and controls as needed.
emergencyResponse: If an oxygen alarm activates or asphyxiant release is suspected — stop work, evacuate immediately, and warn others in the area.
Do NOT enter an oxygen-deficient area to rescue a downed worker without SCBA, training, and team support. Unauthorized rescue is the leading cause of multiple fatalities in asphyxiant incidents.
Activate the site emergency response system and call emergency services. Provide location, number of affected personnel, gas type, and area description.
Prevent additional personnel from entering the affected area. Establish access control and direct people away from the hazard zone.
If safe to do so, isolate the gas source remotely — close supply valves, shut down systems, or activate emergency shutoff from outside the affected area.
Administer supplemental oxygen to affected personnel if trained and equipped. Move affected persons to fresh air if they can be reached safely.
Initiate CPR if a worker is unresponsive and not breathing, provided rescuer safety is confirmed.
Complete incident report and initiate investigation per [Company Name] incident reporting procedures. Preserve the scene and document conditions.
documentation: Hazard assessments and area classification records
Gas inventories and system documentation where required
Training records for all personnel covered by this program
Oxygen monitor and multi-gas detector calibration and bump test records
Fixed detection system inspection, testing, and maintenance records

Atmospheric monitoring logs for task-specific and area-specific monitoring
Incident, alarm, leak, and near-miss reports and investigation records
Corrective and preventive action records
Contractor coordination records and pre-job briefing documentation
Audit findings and program review records
Permits or task risk assessments for higher-risk asphyxiant work
Rescue drill and exercise records

title: Confined Space Entry Program
summary: A comprehensive confined space entry program covering space identification and classification, permit-required entry, atmospheric testing, hazard isolation, ventilation, attendant duties, rescue planning, hot work controls, contractor coordination, alternate entry, reclassification, and program auditing.

sections.purpose: This procedure establishes minimum requirements for identifying, evaluating, classifying, and controlling entry into confined spaces at [Company Name] facilities to prevent injury, illness, fatalities, property damage, and operational disruption. This program addresses atmospheric hazards (oxygen deficiency/enrichment, flammable gases and vapors, toxic contaminants), engulfment hazards, hazardous energy (mechanical, electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, chemical, thermal, gravitational), configuration hazards (converging walls, sloping floors, restricted egress), thermal hazards, and emergency response concerns. It establishes the requirements for entry permits, hazard isolation, atmospheric testing, ventilation, communication, personal protective equipment, rescue planning, and coordination with contractors performing confined space work.
sections.scope: This SOP applies to all [Company Name] operations, facilities, departments, field locations, test areas, production areas, maintenance activities, and contractor work involving confined spaces at [Site Name]. It applies to all employees, authorized entrants, attendants, entry supervisors, rescue personnel, EHS staff, supervisors, managers, contractors, subcontractors, and visitors who may enter, work in or near, or be affected by confined space entry activities. Confined spaces include but are not limited to tanks, vessels, silos, bins, hoppers, pits, vaults, manholes, tunnels, pipelines, boilers, heat exchangers, trenches deeper than four feet, and any other space meeting the confined space criteria defined in this procedure. [Applicable State / Local Requirements] may impose additional requirements beyond OSHA standards and shall be incorporated where applicable.

definitions.definition: An employee who has been trained and authorized to enter a permit-required confined space under the conditions specified on the entry permit.

A trained individual stationed outside a permit-required confined space who monitors entrants, maintains accurate headcount, maintains communication, controls access, and summons rescue when necessary. The attendant shall not enter the space.

The absolute closure of a pipe, line, or duct by fastening a solid plate (blind flange or spectacle blind) that completely covers the bore and is capable of withstanding the maximum upstream pressure.

A space that is large enough and configured so that an employee can bodily enter and perform work, has limited or restricted means for entry or exit (e.g., tanks, vessels, silos, storage bins, hoppers, vaults, pits, manholes, tunnels, pipelines), and is not designed for continuous employee occupancy.
The closure of a line, duct, or pipe by closing and locking or tagging two in-line valves and by opening and locking or tagging a drain or vent valve between the two closed valves.

The surrounding and effective capture of a person by a liquid or finely divided solid substance that can be aspirated to cause death by filling or plugging the respiratory system, or that can exert enough force on the body to cause death by strangulation, constriction, or crushing.

The action by which any part of a person's body breaks the plane of an opening into a confined space. Entry is considered to have occurred as soon as any part of the entrant's body breaks the plane of the opening.
The written or electronic document provided by [Company Name] that authorizes entry into a specific permit-required confined space under defined conditions for a specified period of time.

The qualified person responsible for determining whether acceptable entry conditions are present, authorizing entry, overseeing operations, and terminating entry as required. May also serve as attendant if duties can be effectively performed simultaneously.

An atmosphere that may expose employees to the risk of death, incapacitation, impairment of ability to self-rescue, injury, or acute illness from one or more of the following: flammable gas/vapor/mist >10% LEL, airborne combustible dust at or above its LEL, oxygen <19.5% or >23.5%, atmospheric concentration of any substance above its PEL, or any other atmospheric condition that is immediately dangerous to life or health (IDLH).
Operations involving welding, cutting, brazing, grinding, soldering, or any other activity that produces sparks, open flame, or sufficient heat to ignite flammable or combustible materials.

The process by which a permit space is removed from service and completely protected against the release of energy and material into the space by methods such as blanking or blinding, lockout/tagout, double block and bleed, disconnecting or securing mechanical linkages, and draining, purging, or flushing.
A confined space that does not contain or, with respect to atmospheric hazards, has the potential to contain any hazard capable of causing death or serious physical harm.

A confined space that has one or more of the following characteristics: contains or has a potential to contain a hazardous atmosphere, contains a material that has the potential for engulfing an entrant, has an internal configuration such that an entrant could be trapped or asphyxiated, or contains any other recognized serious safety or health hazard.
The process of retrieving an entrant from a confined space when the entrant cannot self-rescue due to physical incapacitation, atmospheric conditions, or entrapment.
The process by which the atmospheric conditions within a confined space are evaluated using calibrated direct-reading instruments to determine whether acceptable entry conditions exist.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Administer the Confined Space Entry Program. Maintain the confined space inventory and classification records. Develop and review entry procedures. Conduct or coordinate training. Evaluate rescue capabilities. Audit permit quality and program compliance. Investigate incidents. Update this SOP when regulations, operations, or lessons learned require changes.

Ensure employees under their supervision are trained before performing confined space work. Ensure entry permits are completed and approved before entry begins. Verify that equipment, PPE, and rescue resources are available. Report deficiencies to EHS. Ensure contractor coordination requirements are met.

Verify that all conditions on the entry permit are met before authorizing entry. Know the hazards of the space. Ensure that testing, isolation, ventilation, PPE, communication, and rescue provisions are in place. Monitor entry operations. Cancel the permit and order evacuation when unacceptable conditions arise. Close out the permit when work is complete.

Know the hazards of the space. Properly use equipment and PPE. Maintain communication with the attendant. Alert the attendant of any warning signs or symptoms. Exit the space immediately when ordered by the attendant or entry supervisor, when an evacuation alarm activates, or when the entrant recognizes any dangerous condition.

Know the hazards of the space. Maintain an accurate count of entrants. Maintain continuous communication with entrants. Monitor conditions inside and outside the space. Order evacuation when necessary. Summon rescue and emergency services when needed. Keep unauthorized persons from entering the space. Never enter the space for any reason, including rescue.

Maintain rescue readiness and equipment. Participate in rescue drills at least annually. Respond to confined space rescue emergencies. Be trained in CPR, first aid, and the use of rescue equipment including retrieval systems and SCBA. Be familiar with the specific spaces they may be called upon to enter.

Do not enter any confined space unless authorized and trained. Report confined spaces that are improperly posted, unsecured, or being entered without permits. Understand the hazards of confined spaces in their work area.

Comply with [Company Name]'s Confined Space Entry Program when performing work on [Company Name] property. Coordinate entry activities with [Company Name] EHS and the host entry supervisor. Provide documentation of their confined space entry program and training. Inform [Company Name] of any hazards created during contractor entry operations.

training: Confined space recognition and hazard awareness for all affected employees
Authorized entrant training: hazards, duties, PPE use, atmospheric testing awareness, communication, self-rescue, evacuation triggers
Attendant training: monitoring duties, headcount, communication, access control, emergency notification, prohibition on entry
Entry supervisor training: permit review, hazard evaluation, authorization, monitoring, cancellation, closure
Rescue personnel training: rescue techniques, retrieval system operation, SCBA use, CPR/first aid, practice entries into representative spaces at least annually
Atmospheric monitoring equipment operation, pre-use checks, calibration verification, and bump testing
Hazard isolation procedures including lockout/tagout, blanking/blinding, and double block and bleed
Ventilation setup, airflow verification, and retesting requirements
Communication equipment use and protocols
Contractor coordination requirements for confined space entry
Supervisor and manager awareness training on program requirements and their oversight duties
Refresher training annually and when procedures change, new hazards are identified, or performance deficiencies are observed
ppe: Full-body harness with D-ring attachment for retrieval line connection
Retrieval line, tripod, davit, or other mechanical retrieval device as required by the space configuration
Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA) or supplied air respirator for rescue and for entry into IDLH atmospheres
Calibrated multi-gas detector (O₂, LEL, CO, H₂S at minimum — additional sensors as dictated by space hazards)
Hard hat rated for the work environment
Safety glasses or chemical splash goggles as appropriate
Gloves appropriate to the hazards (chemical-resistant, cut-resistant, or thermal as applicable)
Steel-toe boots or chemical-resistant footwear
Hearing protection when noise levels exceed 85 dBA
Communication equipment — two-way radio, hard-line phone, or approved system maintaining continuous contact
Lighting — intrinsically safe lighting for flammable or explosive atmospheres
Chemical-resistant clothing when exposure to corrosive or toxic materials is possible
Fall protection equipment when vertical entry exceeds four feet
hazards: Oxygen deficiency (below 19.5%) or oxygen enrichment (above 23.5%) causing asphyxiation or increased fire risk
Flammable gases, vapors, or mists exceeding 10% of the Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) creating explosion and fire hazards
Toxic atmospheric contaminants (H₂S, CO, chlorine, ammonia, solvents, etc.) exceeding permissible exposure limits
Engulfment by stored materials such as grain, sand, gravel, coal, or liquids
Entrapment due to converging walls, sloping floors, or internal configurations that taper to a smaller cross-section
Mechanical hazards from unguarded agitators, augers, mixers, conveyors, or other equipment within the space
Electrical hazards from energized equipment, wiring, or components inside the space
Thermal hazards including extreme heat, steam, hot surfaces, or cryogenic materials
Chemical exposure from residues, coatings, reaction products, or cleaning agents within the space
Noise exposure amplified by the enclosed space geometry
Gravity hazards from falls during vertical entry or from elevated internal surfaces
Hazardous energy release from pressurized lines, hydraulic systems, or stored energy sources not properly isolated
procedure: IDENTIFICATION AND CLASSIFICATION OF CONFINED SPACES: [Company Name] shall identify all confined spaces at [Site Name] and maintain a current inventory. A space is classified as a confined space if it: (1) is large enough for a person to enter and perform work, (2) has limited or restricted means for entry or exit, and (3) is not designed for continuous employee occupancy. Confined spaces shall be further evaluated and classified as non-permit or permit-required. A confined space is permit-required if it: contains or has a potential to contain a hazardous atmosphere, contains a material with the potential to engulf an entrant, has an internal configuration that could trap or asphyxiate, or contains any other recognized serious safety or health hazard. Permit-required confined spaces shall be posted with signage stating: 'DANGER – PERMIT-REQUIRED CONFINED SPACE – DO NOT ENTER WITHOUT AUTHORIZATION.' The confined space inventory shall be reviewed and updated at least annually and whenever workplace changes create new spaces or alter existing ones.

ENTRY PROHIBITION AND PRE-PLANNING: No person shall enter a permit-required confined space without an approved entry permit unless alternate entry procedures or reclassification have been properly documented per Sections 7.9 and 7.10 of this SOP. Before any entry, the entry supervisor shall evaluate: all known and potential hazards of the space, isolation requirements for energy sources and material flow, atmospheric testing protocol, ventilation needs, PPE requirements, rescue arrangements, communication methods, simultaneous operations in or near the space, and time limits for the entry. Pre-entry planning shall be documented on the entry permit.

PERMIT-REQUIRED CONFINED SPACE ENTRY PERMIT: The entry permit shall include at minimum: identification of the space, purpose of entry, date and authorized duration, names of authorized entrants and attendants, name of entry supervisor, hazards of the space, measures taken to isolate and control hazards, acceptable entry conditions (atmospheric readings), results of initial and periodic atmospheric tests, rescue and emergency services and how to summon them, communication procedures, equipment and PPE required, special precautions or hot work permit references, and any other information necessary for safe entry. The completed permit shall be posted at the entry point or readily available to all entrants and attendants. The entry supervisor signs to authorize entry.

HAZARD ISOLATION: Before entry, all energy sources and material flow paths shall be isolated. Isolation methods include: lockout/tagout of mechanical, electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, and other energy sources per [Company Name]'s LOTO Procedure; blanking or blinding of pipes and ducts carrying hazardous materials; double block and bleed for lines that cannot be blanked; draining, venting, purging, or flushing of residual materials; disconnecting and securing mechanical linkages. Isolation shall be verified by a qualified person before entry is authorized. Verification methods shall include zero-energy checks, valve position confirmation, line break confirmation, and atmospheric testing downstream of isolation points.

ATMOSPHERIC TESTING: Atmospheric testing shall be performed before entry and as often as necessary during entry to ensure acceptable conditions are maintained. Testing shall be performed in the following order: (1) Oxygen — acceptable range is 19.5% to 23.5%, (2) Flammable gases and vapors — must be below 10% LEL, (3) Toxic contaminants — must be below applicable PEL/TLV. Testing shall be performed using calibrated direct-reading instruments that have been bump-tested before use. Where the space has depth, testing shall be performed at multiple elevations (top, middle, bottom) to detect stratification. Readings shall be recorded on the entry permit. If hazardous atmospheric conditions are detected, entry is prohibited until the hazard is corrected and retesting confirms acceptable conditions.

VENTILATION AND ATMOSPHERIC CONTROL: Forced-air ventilation shall be used to maintain acceptable atmospheric conditions during entry. The air source shall be from a clean, uncontaminated location. Airflow shall be directed to ensure adequate circulation throughout the space and displacement of contaminants. Continuous atmospheric monitoring shall be maintained during mechanically ventilated entries. Ventilation alone shall not be assumed effective without testing and verification. If ventilation fails or is interrupted, entrants shall immediately exit and entry shall not resume until acceptable conditions are re-established and verified.

ENTRY CONDITIONS AND AUTHORIZATION: Entry may begin only when: all hazards have been evaluated and documented on the permit, isolation is complete and verified, atmospheric testing confirms acceptable conditions, all entrants and attendants are present, trained, and briefed, required PPE and equipment are available and in use, rescue arrangements are confirmed and documented, communication systems are tested and operational, and the entry supervisor has signed the permit authorizing entry.

DUTIES DURING ENTRY: The attendant shall remain stationed outside the space at all times during entry. Continuous communication shall be maintained between entrants and the attendant. Atmospheric conditions shall be continuously monitored. Only the work authorized on the entry permit shall be performed. Work shall stop and entrants shall exit immediately when: the attendant or entry supervisor orders evacuation, an atmospheric alarm activates, any entrant recognizes symptoms of exposure or a dangerous condition, ventilation fails, communication is lost, or any other prohibited condition arises.

ALTERNATE ENTRY PROCEDURES: Alternate entry procedures may be used only when the sole hazard posed by the space is atmospheric and that hazard can be controlled by forced-air ventilation alone. Before using alternate entry, the employer must demonstrate with monitoring data that forced-air ventilation is sufficient to maintain the space safe for entry. Continuous ventilation and continuous atmospheric monitoring are required throughout the entry. An attendant is not required for alternate entry, but a means to summon rescue must be available. Documentation supporting the use of alternate entry shall be maintained. If conditions change and the space cannot be maintained safe by ventilation alone, alternate entry shall be discontinued and full permit procedures shall apply.

RECLASSIFICATION OF PERMIT SPACES: A permit-required confined space may be reclassified as a non-permit confined space only when ALL hazards within the space have been eliminated without entry into the space. Ventilation alone does not constitute hazard elimination for the purposes of reclassification. The reclassification shall be documented, including the date, the space, the hazards eliminated, and how elimination was verified. The documentation shall be made available to entrants. If hazards are reintroduced, the space shall revert to permit-required status immediately.

HOT WORK IN CONFINED SPACES: When hot work (welding, cutting, brazing, grinding, soldering) is performed inside a confined space, a Hot Work Permit shall be obtained in addition to the Confined Space Entry Permit. Continuous atmospheric monitoring for flammable gases and vapors shall be maintained during and after hot work. A fire watch shall be posted per [Company Name]'s Hot Work Procedure. Adequate ventilation shall be maintained to control fumes, gases, and heat. Respiratory protection requirements shall be reviewed and upgraded as necessary. All combustible and flammable materials shall be removed from the space or protected from ignition. Rescue planning shall be enhanced to account for the additional hazards of hot work.

RETRIEVAL AND RESCUE PLANNING: A rescue plan shall be established before any permit-required confined space entry. Non-entry rescue shall be used when feasible. Retrieval systems (tripods, davits, winches, full-body harnesses, retrieval lines) shall be set up and ready for use during entry where the space configuration allows. Emergency services shall not be assumed capable of confined space rescue unless they have been specifically contacted, have confirmed capability for the specific space type, and have agreed to respond. Where on-site rescue teams are used, they shall practice rescue operations at least annually using representative spaces. Rescue equipment shall be inspected before each use.

COMMUNICATION: Reliable communication shall be maintained between entrants and attendants at all times during entry. Acceptable communication methods include voice, two-way radio, hard-line telephone, or other approved systems appropriate to the space and work conditions. Communication systems shall be tested before entry begins. Loss of communication shall trigger evaluation by the attendant and may require evacuation of all entrants until communication is restored.

PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT: PPE shall be selected based on the hazards of the specific space and the work to be performed. At minimum, consider: hard hat, eye protection, appropriate gloves, protective footwear, and fall protection/retrieval harness for vertical entry exceeding four feet. Chemical-resistant PPE shall be worn when exposure to corrosive, toxic, or irritating chemicals is possible. Hearing protection shall be used when noise levels inside the space exceed 85 dBA. Respiratory protection shall be selected per the Respiratory Protection Program and the SDS for any contaminants present. PPE shall be inspected before each use.

CONTRACTOR ENTRY COORDINATION: When contractors perform confined space entry on [Company Name] property, [Company Name] shall inform the contractor of the permit spaces involved, the hazards and [Company Name]'s experience with those spaces, and the precautions and procedures required. The contractor shall inform [Company Name] of the contractor's confined space entry program and the hazards created by the contractor's work. Both parties shall coordinate entry operations to prevent interference and ensure safety. A debrief shall be conducted at the end of contractor entry work to identify any problems encountered and lessons learned.

SIGNAGE, BARRICADES, AND ACCESS CONTROL: Permit-required confined spaces shall be posted with warning signs. Before entry, barricades, covers, guardrails, or other access controls shall be established as needed to prevent unauthorized entry and protect entrants from adjacent operations, vehicle and pedestrian traffic, mobile equipment, falling objects, and other external hazards. Entry points shall be secured when the space is unattended.

PERMIT CLOSURE AND ENTRY TERMINATION: The entry permit shall be closed when: the authorized work is complete, conditions become unsafe, the permit time expires, or the work shift ends — whichever occurs first. The entry supervisor shall document any problems encountered during the entry. The space shall be returned to a safe condition and all entrants, equipment, and tools shall be accounted for. Cancelled permits shall be retained for at least one year for program review purposes.

INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW: All confined space incidents, near-misses, atmospheric alarms, unauthorized entries, rescue events, permit deviations, and abnormal conditions shall be reported and investigated per [Company Name]'s Incident Reporting and Investigation Procedure. EHS shall conduct periodic reviews of permit quality, training effectiveness, rescue readiness, equipment reliability, and lessons learned. The program shall be reviewed and updated at least annually.
emergencyResponse: If an entrant becomes incapacitated, shows symptoms of exposure, or signals for help, the attendant shall immediately initiate the rescue plan — summon the rescue team and call emergency services.
The attendant shall NOT enter the space for any reason, including rescue. Non-entry rescue using retrieval systems shall be attempted first if applicable.
If the rescue team responds, they shall enter only with proper PPE including SCBA and retrieval equipment, and only under the direction of a qualified rescue leader.
Call 911 and [Company Name] emergency number. Provide the location, nature of the emergency, number of persons involved, and known atmospheric conditions.
Once the victim is removed from the space, administer first aid and CPR as needed until emergency medical services arrive.
Secure the space — no further entry shall occur until the incident has been investigated, the cause identified, and corrective actions implemented.
Report the event immediately to EHS and management per [Company Name]'s incident reporting procedure.
Preserve the entry permit, atmospheric monitoring records, and all documentation related to the entry for investigation purposes.

revisionHistory.description: Initial release

Complete program rewrite — expanded to enterprise-grade confined space entry program covering identification, classification, permit procedures, isolation, atmospheric testing, ventilation, alternate entry, reclassification, hot work, rescue planning, contractor coordination, signage, incident reporting, and program auditing.

title: Working at Heights Program
summary: A comprehensive working at heights program covering fall hazard identification, hierarchy of controls, access methods, ladders, scaffolds, aerial lifts, personal fall protection, anchor points, dropped object prevention, rescue planning, suspension trauma, contractor coordination, and program auditing.

sections.purpose: This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, evaluating, planning, and controlling fall hazards and other risks associated with working at heights at [Company Name] facilities, field locations, and project sites. The program applies to all work where employees or contractors may be exposed to fall hazards, including falls from elevation, falling tools or materials, unstable work surfaces, misuse of ladders or access equipment, failure of anchor points or fall protection systems, and unsafe work practices during elevated work. The objective is to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, dropped object incidents, property damage, and operational disruption resulting from inadequately planned or controlled work at height.
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, construction activities, maintenance activities, field locations, test areas, production areas, warehouses, office support spaces, rooftops, platforms, mezzanines, structures, and non-routine work where employees or contractors may be exposed to fall hazards.

It applies to employees performing work at height, supervisors and managers, EHS personnel, maintenance personnel, technicians, operators, engineers and support personnel, contractors, and rescue or emergency response personnel as applicable.

It applies to work performed from or involving, but not limited to: fixed ladders, portable ladders, step ladders, extension ladders, scaffolds, mobile scaffolds, elevated platforms, mezzanines, roofs, catwalks, structural steel, work stands, aerial lifts, scissor lifts, boom lifts, man baskets where approved, tanks, vessels and process equipment, open-sided floors or platforms, pits or floor openings where fall exposure exists, temporary work platforms, and elevated equipment, vehicles, or machinery where personnel access is required.

This SOP applies to both routine and non-routine work at height and should be used with other required programs such as confined space, lockout/tagout, scaffolding, electrical safety, dropped object prevention, crane and rigging safety, and permit-to-work requirements where applicable.

definitions.definition: A secure point of attachment for lifelines, lanyards, or deceleration devices that is capable of supporting the loads applied during a fall arrest event as required by applicable standards.
A vehicle-mounted or self-propelled device used to position personnel, including boom lifts, scissor lifts, and articulating platforms.
A person who has been trained, evaluated, and approved to perform work at height using specified access methods and fall protection systems.

A person who is capable of identifying existing and predictable fall hazards, who has the authority to take prompt corrective action to eliminate or control them, and who meets applicable regulatory and company requirements for that designation.
Any tool, material, fastener, or piece of equipment that falls from elevation and has the potential to strike personnel, damage equipment, or disrupt operations below.
A system used to arrest a worker in a fall from a working level, consisting of a full-body harness, connecting device, anchorage, and deceleration device.
Any condition at a work area that exposes a worker to a risk of falling to a lower level, including unprotected edges, openings, unstable surfaces, and inadequate access.
Methods that physically prevent a fall from occurring, such as guardrails, covers, and permanent platforms.
The broad category of systems and methods used to prevent workers from falling or to arrest a fall after it begins, including fall prevention, travel restraint, positioning, and fall arrest.
A body support device consisting of connected straps distributed over the shoulders, chest, waist, and legs that distributes fall arrest forces over the thighs, pelvis, chest, and shoulders.
A flexible line of webbing, rope, or cable used to connect a harness to an anchor point or lifeline, typically incorporating an energy absorber for fall arrest applications.
A system used to support a worker on an elevated vertical surface and allow the worker to work hands-free while leaning back.
A deceleration device containing a drum-wound line that can be slowly extracted and retracts automatically, and which arrests a fall by locking the drum and applying braking force.
A system that limits a worker's travel so the worker cannot reach a fall hazard, preventing a fall from occurring rather than arresting one.
Any side or edge of a walking-working surface, platform, or structure where there is no guardrail, parapet, or other barrier and a fall to a lower level is possible.

Any work activity where a person could fall a distance sufficient to cause injury, including work on or from ladders, scaffolds, platforms, roofs, lifts, structures, equipment, or any elevated or open surface.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Develop and maintain the working at heights program. Conduct hazard assessments for elevated work. Evaluate and approve anchor points. Review fall protection plans and rescue arrangements. Conduct audits and inspections. Deliver or coordinate training. Review incidents, near misses, and corrective actions. Evaluate new equipment and methods. Maintain program records. Support contractor coordination.

Ensure fall hazards are identified and controlled before work begins. Verify workers are trained and authorized. Confirm correct access methods, fall protection, and dropped object controls are in place. Enforce compliance. Address deficiencies immediately. Ensure rescue planning is addressed where fall arrest systems are used. Review task risk assessments and permits. Report incidents and near misses.

Inspect fall protection equipment, scaffolds, access systems, and anchor points. Identify existing and predictable fall hazards and take prompt corrective action. Authorize work at height where designated. Verify compliance with regulatory and company requirements. Remove defective equipment from service. Support training and field coaching.

Follow all working at heights requirements. Inspect personal fall protection equipment before each use. Use only approved access methods, anchor points, and fall protection systems. Report damaged or defective equipment immediately. Do not bypass or alter fall protection systems. Report incidents, near misses, and unsafe conditions. Participate in training and rescue drills as required.

Design and maintain permanent fall prevention and access systems. Evaluate and certify anchor points. Support engineering solutions for elevated access, guardrails, covers, platforms, and tie-off systems. Review changes to structures, roofs, and elevated equipment that may affect fall protection requirements.

Comply with [Company Name] working at heights requirements. Provide and maintain their own fall protection equipment unless otherwise agreed. Communicate fall protection methods, rescue approach, and any incidents or unsafe conditions to the host employer. Participate in coordination meetings and follow permit and barricade requirements.

Maintain rescue capability, equipment, and training. Respond to fall arrest events. Conduct rescue within established time limits. Participate in drills and exercises. Communicate access or resource limitations to EHS and supervision.

Falls through openings, skylights, fragile surfaces, or unprotected floor holes
Falling objects striking workers or equipment below

Insufficient clearance below the work area for fall arrest deceleration

procedure:
**7.1 FALL HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND ASSESSMENT**

Before work at height begins, the work area and task shall be evaluated for fall hazards. The evaluation shall consider height of exposure, distance to lower level, unprotected edges, surface stability, task type, duration, access method, weather conditions, lighting, proximity to electrical or process hazards, need to handle tools and materials, swing fall potential, clearance requirements, rescue considerations, and dropped object exposure.

The evaluation shall also determine whether the task can be completed without exposing personnel to a fall hazard, such as by performing work at ground level, using remote methods, or redesigning access.

Higher-risk conditions include work near leading edges, on fragile surfaces, over water or hazardous materials, at significant heights, during adverse weather, involving multiple trades, or above active work areas.

**7.2 HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS FOR WORKING AT HEIGHTS**

[Company Name] shall apply the following hierarchy of controls in order of preference:

1. Eliminate the need to work at height — perform the task at ground level, redesign equipment for ground-level access, or use remote methods.
2. Use passive fall prevention — install guardrails, covers, permanent platforms, parapets, or other barriers that protect workers without requiring action.
3. Use travel restraint — limit worker travel so the worker cannot reach the fall hazard.
4. Use positioning systems — support the worker on a vertical surface where appropriate.
5. Use personal fall arrest systems — arrest a fall after it begins, used only where fall prevention and restraint are not feasible.
6. Use administrative controls — warning lines, safety monitors, controlled access zones, and signage used only as supplemental measures.

Fall prevention is always preferred over fall arrest whenever feasible. Personal fall arrest systems shall not be used as a substitute for reasonably available prevention methods.

**7.3 WORK PLANNING AND AUTHORIZATION**

All work at height shall be planned before the task begins. Planning shall address the nature of the task, exposure height, fall path, access equipment selection, walking-working surface condition, need for barricades or exclusion zones, type of fall protection, anchor suitability, clearance requirements, dropped object controls, weather conditions, coordination with other work, and rescue arrangements where fall arrest systems are used.

Higher-risk tasks shall include additional controls such as a Job Safety Analysis (JSA), task risk assessment, permit to work, supervisor approval, EHS review, engineering review, and simultaneous operations review as appropriate for the scope and complexity of the work.

**7.4 SELECTION OF ACCESS METHOD**

The access method shall be selected based on the task, duration, height, reach, load, work surface, and hazards. Options include ladders, scaffolds, aerial lifts, scissor lifts, fixed platforms, catwalks, and work stands.

The selected method shall be appropriate for the planned scope and duration of work. Improper use of one access method in place of another is prohibited. Ladders shall not be used where scaffolds or lifts are more appropriate for the scope and duration of work.

**7.5 PORTABLE LADDERS**

Portable ladders shall be inspected before each use. Damaged, modified, or makeshift ladders are prohibited. Ladders shall be placed on stable and level surfaces. Workers shall maintain three points of contact while climbing. Standing on the top step or top cap is prohibited unless the ladder is specifically designed for that use. Overreaching is prohibited.

Extension ladders shall be secured at the top where needed and positioned at the correct angle (approximately 4:1 ratio). Landing extension above the upper support point shall be maintained where applicable. Doorway and traffic exposure shall be controlled. Carrying items that prevent safe climbing is prohibited.

Metal ladders or conductive ladders shall not be used near exposed electrical hazards. Ladders are not work platforms unless specifically designed and rated for that purpose.

**7.6 FIXED LADDERS AND FIXED ACCESS SYSTEMS**

Fixed ladders and fixed access systems shall be maintained in safe condition with structural soundness, clean walking surfaces, functional guardrails, gates, covers, and fall protection connection points where required. Environmental conditions such as ice, corrosion, or damage shall be evaluated before use.

Defects or damage shall be reported and corrected before use. Access to damaged fixed systems shall be restricted until repairs are completed.

**7.7 SCAFFOLDS AND TEMPORARY WORK PLATFORMS**

Scaffolds shall be erected, modified, inspected, and used in accordance with applicable requirements and competent person oversight where needed. Requirements include approved components, adequate foundations, full decking where required, guardrails, toe boards, safe access, inspections before each shift and after events that could affect integrity, load limits, prohibition on unauthorized alterations, and good housekeeping.

Incomplete, tagged-out, or unapproved scaffolds shall not be used. Workers shall not modify scaffolds unless authorized and qualified.

**7.8 AERIAL LIFTS, BOOM LIFTS, AND SCISSOR LIFTS**

Only trained and authorized operators shall operate aerial lifts. Pre-use inspections shall be performed each shift. Ground and surface conditions shall be reviewed before positioning. Manufacturer limits for load, reach, wind, and slope shall be followed.

Fall protection (harness and lanyard) is required in boom lifts. Workers shall keep both feet on the platform floor. Climbing on rails or using boxes, planks, or ladders inside platforms is prohibited. Safe distance from electrical hazards shall be maintained. Rated load limits shall not be exceeded. Outriggers and stabilizers shall be deployed where required. Area protection below the lift shall be established. Unsafe movement while elevated is prohibited.

**7.9 UNPROTECTED EDGES, OPENINGS, AND FLOOR HOLES**

Employees exposed to unprotected edges, openings, skylights, floor holes, and similar hazards shall be protected by guardrails, covers, travel restraint, fall arrest, barricades, or temporary edge protection as appropriate.

Covers shall be secured, marked with the word HOLE or COVER - DO NOT REMOVE, and capable of supporting at least twice the maximum anticipated load.

Skylights and fragile surfaces shall not be assumed to support personnel unless specifically designed, rated, and approved for that purpose.

**7.10 PERSONAL FALL PROTECTION SYSTEMS**

Personal fall protection systems include full-body harnesses, lanyards, self-retracting lifelines (SRLs), lifelines, positioning devices, travel restraint systems, and anchor connectors. Only company-approved or otherwise authorized equipment shall be used.

All equipment shall be inspected before each use. Damaged or suspect equipment shall be immediately removed from service and not returned until repaired or replaced by a qualified person. Only approved anchor points shall be used. All hardware and connectors shall be compatible.

Swing fall hazards and clearance requirements shall be evaluated before selecting the attachment point. One hundred percent tie-off shall be maintained where required by site rules or conditions. Body belts shall not be used for fall arrest.

**7.11 ANCHOR POINTS AND ANCHORAGE SELECTION**

Anchor points shall be selected and approved for the intended use. Selection shall consider structural adequacy, load direction, edge distance, swing fall potential, clearance, number of users, compatibility with connecting hardware, and manufacturer or engineering approval.

Tying off to handrails, unapproved piping, conduit, unapproved guardrails, temporary structures, vehicles or mobile equipment (unless specifically designed and authorized), or unknown structural members is explicitly prohibited.

Where anchor adequacy is uncertain, engineering or competent person review is required before use.

**7.12 INSPECTION, CARE, AND STORAGE OF FALL PROTECTION EQUIPMENT**

All fall protection equipment shall be inspected before each use by the user. Inspection shall include harness webbing, stitching, hardware, D-rings, buckles, lanyards, energy absorbers, SRLs, connectors, anchor straps, and all load-bearing components. Look for cuts, burns, abrasion, fraying, corrosion, deformation, contamination, chemical exposure, UV degradation, and evidence of prior loading.

Equipment involved in a fall arrest event shall be immediately removed from service and not returned to use until inspected and approved by a competent person or the manufacturer.

Equipment shall be stored clean and dry, protected from heat, direct sunlight, chemicals, sharp edges, moisture, crushing, and deformation. Periodic documented inspections shall be performed where required by company policy or manufacturer recommendation.

**7.13 DROPPED OBJECT PREVENTION**

Dropped object evaluation and controls are required whenever work is performed above personnel, equipment, energized systems, roadways, or active work areas. Controls include tool tethering, securing materials, toe boards, debris nets where appropriate, covered work platforms, barricades, exclusion zones, controlled lifting and staging of materials, restricted access below, and housekeeping.

Dropped object prevention is a required part of every working at heights planning effort. Failure to control dropped object hazards is a stop-work condition.

**7.14 WEATHER AND ENVIRONMENTAL CONDITIONS**

Weather and environmental conditions shall be evaluated before and during elevated work. Factors include wind speed, rain, lightning, ice, snow, extreme heat, reduced visibility, dust, smoke, fog, slick surfaces, and any other condition that affects worker stability, equipment function, or safety.

Work at height shall be stopped and personnel shall descend when conditions deteriorate to unsafe levels. Reassessment is required whenever conditions change significantly.

**7.15 RESCUE PLANNING AND SUSPENSION TRAUMA**

Rescue planning is required wherever personal fall arrest systems are used. The rescue plan shall address work location, number of workers, access to a suspended worker, response time, rescue equipment, lift or ladder needs for access, communication, medical response, and site-specific complications.

Employees shall not rely solely on external emergency services for rescue without confirming their capability and response time for the specific location and scenario.

Suspension trauma shall be considered in every rescue plan. Prompt recovery of a worker suspended in a harness is critical. Workers shall be trained in self-rescue techniques where feasible and in recognizing suspension trauma symptoms.

**7.16 BARRICADES, EXCLUSION ZONES, AND AREA CONTROL**

Areas below or adjacent to elevated work shall be protected. Controls include barricades, warning tape, cones, rigid barriers, signage, spotters where appropriate, and temporary closure of walkways or work areas.

Exclusion zone size shall reflect height of work, tools and materials present, fall path, equipment footprint, and nearby traffic or pedestrian routes. Unauthorized personnel shall not enter controlled areas.

**7.17 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION**

[Company Name] shall coordinate with contractors before elevated work begins. The company shall communicate site fall protection rules, known hazards, rescue expectations, barricade and dropped object controls, permit requirements, and any restrictions related to roofs, platforms, lifts, or anchor points.

Contractors shall communicate access methods, fall protection systems, rescue approach, hazards introduced by their work, and any incident, near miss, or unsafe condition encountered. Coordination is required where multiple employers are working in the same area.

**7.18 INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW**

All falls, fall arrest events, dropped object incidents, near misses, equipment failures, anchor concerns, and unsafe conditions shall be reported immediately. Investigation shall address planning quality, control adequacy, equipment condition and use, surface and environmental conditions, supervision, rescue readiness, human factors, training adequacy, and engineering improvement opportunities.

Periodic EHS and operational review of the working at heights program shall be conducted. Reviews shall evaluate hazard assessments, equipment condition, training effectiveness, incident trends, rescue readiness, contractor performance, and program compliance.
emergencyResponse: If a fall arrest event occurs, activate the rescue plan immediately. Do not delay rescue. Contact site emergency services and provide the exact location, height, number of workers involved, and nature of injuries if known.

Rescue a suspended worker as quickly as possible to minimize suspension trauma risk. Ideally, rescue should be completed within minutes, not left until external emergency services arrive unless they are confirmed capable and nearby.
Prevent additional personnel from entering the fall area unless they are trained and equipped rescue responders.
If a worker is found down at a lower level after a fall, do not move the worker unless necessary to prevent further injury. Activate emergency medical response immediately.

After any fall arrest event or fall-related incident, isolate the area. Preserve all equipment involved for investigation. Remove equipment from service. Complete an incident report and conduct a thorough investigation including root cause analysis.
Report all dropped object incidents, near misses, and unsafe conditions even if no injury occurred.
