Fall hazard assessments or task risk assessments where required
Permits associated with elevated work where applicable
Inspection records for company-owned fall protection equipment where required
Lift or scaffold inspection records where applicable
Anchor certification or evaluation records where applicable
Incident, near miss, and investigation records
Corrective action records
Contractor coordination records
Audit findings and program review records

APPENDIX A - WORKING AT HEIGHTS PLANNING CHECKLIST: Task reviewed for fall hazards; Need to work at height minimized where possible; Correct access method selected; Walking-working surface stable; Unprotected edges and openings controlled; Fall protection available and inspected; Anchor points known and approved; Clearance and swing fall hazards evaluated; Dropped object controls in place; Barricades and exclusion zones established; Weather acceptable; Rescue planning addressed where fall arrest is used

APPENDIX B - LADDER USE QUICK CHECKLIST: Correct ladder type and size selected; Ladder in good condition; Stable and level surface; Correct positioning and angle; Doorway and traffic controls in place; Three points of contact maintained; No overreaching; Not used as a work platform unless designed for it; Electrical hazard review completed

APPENDIX C - PERSONAL FALL PROTECTION PRE-USE CHECKLIST: Harness webbing, stitching, and buckles inspected; D-rings and connectors inspected; Lanyards, SRLs, and energy absorbers inspected; Anchor straps and connectors inspected; No cuts, burns, fraying, deformation, or corrosion found; Manufacturer labels legible and present; Equipment compatibility confirmed; Approved anchor location identified; Clearance below work area evaluated; Rescue plan confirmed

APPENDIX D - DROPPED OBJECT PREVENTION CHECKLIST: Tools tethered where needed; Loose materials secured; Toe boards or containment methods in place where required; Personnel below protected; Exclusion zone established; Housekeeping adequate; Wind conditions reviewed; Heavy or awkward items staged safely; Communication between elevated workers and personnel below confirmed

APPENDIX E - CONDITIONS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE STOP WORK: Missing, damaged, or unusable fall protection; Unstable, damaged, or improperly used access equipment; Questionable or unapproved anchor point; Unsafe weather or environmental conditions; Barricades or exclusion zones cannot be maintained; Dropped object incident or near miss remains uncorrected; Rescue capability unavailable where needed; Supervisor, EHS, or competent person stops the work; Conditions change such that safe elevated work cannot be assured

COMPLIANCE AND DEVIATIONS: Failure to comply with this procedure may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination, and may expose personnel to serious injury or fatality. Falls from height remain one of the leading causes of workplace fatalities across all industries. Deviations from this procedure require review and written approval from EHS and operational management before work proceeds, unless emergency action is needed to protect life or health. No deviation may remove required fall prevention, fall protection, access control, dropped object controls, rescue preparedness, or other critical safeguards without equivalent or greater protection in place. [Company Name] reserves the right to apply stricter requirements than those set by regulation where conditions or experience justify additional protection.

revisionHistory.description: Initial release

Complete program rewrite — expanded to enterprise-grade working at heights program covering fall hazard identification, hierarchy of controls, access methods, ladders, scaffolds, aerial lifts, personal fall protection, anchor points, dropped object prevention, rescue planning, suspension trauma, barricades, contractor coordination, incident reporting, and program auditing.

title: Suspended Loads and Lifting Operations Program
summary: Establishes minimum requirements for planning, authorizing, and safely performing lifting operations involving suspended loads, including crane operations, rigging, load control, exclusion zones, critical lifts, and dropped object prevention.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for planning, authorizing, and safely performing lifting operations involving suspended loads at [Company Name] facilities and work locations.

The objective is to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, dropped load incidents, struck-by incidents, pinch point injuries, crushing injuries, equipment damage, property damage, and operational disruption resulting from improper lift planning, rigging failure, crane or hoist misuse, unstable loads, inadequate communication, poor load path control, inadequate ground or support conditions, and unsafe employee positioning during lifting operations.

This program applies to all lifting operations and requires that every lift be planned, every load be controlled, every person be protected, and every piece of equipment be inspected before use.

Document No.: [Document No.]
Effective Date: [Effective Date]
Revision: [Revision]
Owner: [Owner]
Approved By: [Approved By]
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, construction activities, maintenance activities, production areas, warehouses, test areas, field locations, launch or test support operations, and non-routine activities involving lifting operations or suspended loads.

It applies to employees involved in lifting operations, crane operators, hoist operators, riggers, signal persons, supervisors and managers, EHS personnel, engineers, technicians, maintenance personnel, operators, support personnel, and contractors performing lifting operations onsite.

It applies to lifting operations involving but not limited to:
- Overhead cranes, bridge cranes, gantry cranes, jib cranes, and monorails
- Hoists, chain falls, and come-alongs used for lifting where permitted
- Mobile cranes, truck cranes, and rough terrain cranes
- Forklifts used with approved lifting attachments where specifically authorized
- Telehandlers used with approved lifting attachments where specifically authorized
- Spreader bars, lifting beams, slings, shackles, eyebolts, hooks, and below-the-hook lifting devices
- Custom lifting fixtures
- Personnel exclusion zones around suspended loads
- Load transfers, load turning, installation lifts, equipment removals, and heavy picks

This SOP applies to both routine and non-routine lifting operations and should be used with other required programs such as working at heights, crane and rigging inspection requirements, mobile equipment safety, contractor safety, electrical safety, permit-to-work, dropped object prevention, and lockout/tagout where applicable.

This procedure does not authorize personnel lifting unless a separate approved procedure specifically permits and governs that activity.

definitions.definition: A person who has been trained, evaluated, and authorized to operate specific lifting equipment at [Company Name].
A device used to attach a load to a hoist or crane hook, including lifting beams, spreader bars, clamps, grabs, and custom fixtures not considered standard rigging.

A lift that meets site-defined criteria for higher risk based on load value, capacity percentage, environment, complexity, proximity to critical equipment or personnel, or other factors requiring additional planning and approval.
Forces imposed on rigging, equipment, or structures from sudden starts, stops, swinging, bouncing, or shock loading during a lift.
The planned route a load travels from pick point to set-down location, including all areas over which the load passes while suspended.
A person who by education, training, experience, or professional credential has demonstrated the ability to solve problems related to lifting operations, rigging design, or structural adequacy.
A person trained and authorized to select, inspect, and configure rigging hardware and to attach and detach loads for lifting.
A person trained and designated to direct crane or hoist movements using standard hand signals, radio, or other approved communication methods.
Any load that is supported by a crane, hoist, or other lifting device and is not resting on a stable surface or otherwise secured against falling.
A rope or line attached to a load to control its position, prevent rotation, and guide placement during a lift.
A controlled lift performed by raising the load a short distance off the surface to verify rigging security, load balance, brake function, and equipment response before proceeding with the full lift.

A documented plan specifying load characteristics, equipment, rigging configuration, personnel roles, load path, set-down location, communication, environmental conditions, and safety precautions for a lifting operation.
The maximum load that may be applied to rigging hardware or a lifting device during normal service, as specified by the manufacturer or a qualified person.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Provide technical guidance on lifting hazards, review critical lift plans, audit program compliance, investigate lifting incidents, maintain program documentation, coordinate training requirements, and update this procedure as regulations or conditions change.

Ensure lifting operations are planned and performed in accordance with this procedure. Verify that operators, riggers, and signal persons are trained and authorized. Enforce exclusion zones and stop-work authority. Approve lift plans where required. Review incidents and near misses. Ensure equipment inspections are completed and documented.

Operate lifting equipment only within rated capacity and design limitations. Perform pre-use inspections. Refuse to make a lift when conditions are unsafe. Maintain clear communication with the signal person and rigger. Stop the lift if signals are unclear, personnel enter the hazard zone, or conditions change. Report equipment deficiencies and incidents.

Select and inspect rigging hardware appropriate for the load. Configure rigging to maintain load stability and prevent slipping, shifting, or uncontrolled movement. Verify load weight and center of gravity where practicable. Ensure edge protection where needed. Remove damaged or questionable rigging from service. Participate in pre-lift briefings and communicate rigging concerns.

Direct crane or hoist movements using standard hand signals, radio, or other approved communication methods. Maintain visual contact with the load and operator. Ensure the load path is clear before signaling movement. Stop the operation if personnel enter the hazard zone or communication is lost. Only one designated signal person shall direct the operator unless an emergency stop is needed.

Stay clear of suspended loads, load paths, and exclusion zones. Do not walk or work under suspended loads. Report unsafe lifting conditions immediately. Follow all barricade and area control requirements. Participate in required training.

Provide engineering support for critical lift plans, anchor adequacy, structural review, custom lifting fixture design, and below-the-hook device approval. Review lift configurations that exceed standard parameters. Evaluate and approve lifting points where engineering review is required.

Comply with [Company Name] lifting and rigging requirements while onsite. Provide documentation of operator, rigger, and signal person qualifications where required. Submit lift plans for higher-risk lifts as required by site policy. Report all incidents, near misses, and unsafe conditions. Coordinate lifting activities with [Company Name] supervision.

training: Operators shall complete equipment-specific training and evaluation before operating cranes, hoists, or other lifting devices
Riggers shall complete rigging training covering hardware selection, inspection, sling angles, hitch types, WLL, and load control
Signal persons shall complete signal person training covering standard hand signals, radio protocol, and emergency stop
Supervisors and managers shall complete training on lift planning, critical lift requirements, exclusion zones, and stop-work authority
Employees working in areas exposed to suspended load hazards shall receive awareness training on exclusion zones, personnel positioning, and reporting
Contractors shall meet training requirements as defined by site policy before performing lifting operations

Training topics include recognition of lifting hazards, equipment capacity and limitations, rigging selection and inspection, load balance and center-of-gravity considerations, proper communication and signaling, exclusion zones and personnel positioning, pinch point and line-of-fire hazards, tag line use and load control, critical lift requirements, stop-work authority, and incident and near-miss reporting
Training records shall be maintained by [the safety department / training coordinator] and verified before personnel are assigned to lifting operations
ppe: Hard hat
Safety glasses or face shield as required
Steel-toe or safety-toe boots
High-visibility vest where required by site policy
Leather or cut-resistant gloves as appropriate for rigging tasks
Hearing protection where noise exposure warrants
Additional PPE as identified by task hazard assessment
hazards: Struck by falling, swinging, or shifting loads
Crush injuries from loads landing on personnel or pinch points between load and fixed objects
Crane or hoist structural failure, tip-over, or mechanical malfunction
Rigging hardware failure including sling breaks, shackle failure, and hook deformation
Electrocution from contact with overhead power lines
Pinch and crush points during rigging attachment, load landing, or load guiding
Dynamic loading and shock loading from sudden starts, stops, or swing
Wind-related load control loss
Unstable ground or support conditions causing equipment failure
Dropped tools or materials from elevated work associated with rigging setup
Stored energy release from tensioned rigging or binding components
procedure:
**7.1 LIFT HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND ASSESSMENT**

Before any lifting operation begins, the work area and task shall be evaluated to identify hazards and determine required controls. The evaluation shall consider load weight, size, shape, center of gravity, lifting point adequacy, equipment capacity, sling angles, load path, set-down area, structural or ground conditions, weather, personnel exposure, pinch points, visibility, communication limits, dynamic loading, tag line need, barricade need, and consequence of a dropped load or uncontrolled movement.

If the load weight, center of gravity, or attachment integrity cannot be reasonably confirmed, the lift shall not proceed until adequate information or engineering support is established.

**7.2 HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS FOR LIFTING OPERATIONS**

The following control hierarchy shall be applied:

1. Eliminate unnecessary lifting or redesign the task to avoid suspended loads
2. Use engineered lifting points, fixtures, and permanent handling systems
3. Use properly sized and configured equipment for the load and environment
4. Control the load path and exclude personnel from the hazard zone
5. Use administrative controls such as lift plans, permits, spotters, and communication as supplemental controls
6. Use PPE as the last layer of protection

Personnel exposure to the suspended load hazard zone should be minimized or eliminated whenever feasible.

**7.3 LIFT PLANNING AND AUTHORIZATION**

All lifting operations require planning before work begins. Planning shall include task purpose, load characteristics, equipment selection, rigging configuration, operator/rigger/signal person assignments, pick point, final set location, load path, support conditions, environmental conditions, barricades, communication method, test lift need, and documented lift plan or critical lift review when required.

Additional controls may include task risk assessment, JSA, permit to work, supervisor approval, EHS review, engineering review, and pre-lift briefing.

The level of planning shall be proportional to the complexity and risk of the lift.

**7.4 CRITICAL LIFTS**

Critical lifts are higher-risk lifts based on site-defined criteria or supervisory determination. Examples include lifts involving mission-critical equipment, limited clearances, tandem lifts, non-standard rigging, engineered lifting devices, high-capacity picks, lifts over operating equipment, lifts with catastrophic consequence potential, and lifts involving unstable or unusual center-of-gravity conditions.

Critical lifts require a formal lift plan, verified load data, rigging review, equipment configuration review, engineering review where needed, a pre-lift meeting, defined stop-work triggers, and management/EHS/engineering approval as required by site policy.

[Company Name] shall maintain site-specific criteria defining which lifts are classified as critical.

**7.5 SELECTION OF LIFTING EQUIPMENT**

Lifting equipment shall be selected based on load characteristics, load path, reach, environment, and support conditions. Selection criteria include capacity, reach, lift height, radius, configuration, surface support, overhead obstructions, indoor/outdoor use, precision requirements, and manufacturer limits.

Use beyond rated capacity or outside intended operating conditions is prohibited.

Forklifts, telehandlers, or other mobile equipment shall not be used to suspend loads unless specifically designed, equipped, and approved for that use under an approved procedure.

Improvised lifting with unapproved equipment is prohibited.

**7.6 RIGGING EQUIPMENT SELECTION AND USE**

Rigging equipment includes wire rope slings, synthetic slings, chain slings, metal mesh slings, shackles, eyebolts, hoist rings, hooks, spreader bars, lifting beams, clamps, and other approved below-the-hook devices.

Rigging shall be selected based on WLL, hitch type, sling angle, edge protection needs, temperature and chemical exposure, abrasion potential, hardware compatibility, and load balance.

Unmarked, damaged, modified, or makeshift rigging is prohibited.

Rigging shall maintain load stability, avoid side loading where not allowed, prevent slipping or shifting, minimize shock loading, protect against sharp edges, and ensure hooks are loaded in the bowl and not on the tip.

**7.7 INSPECTION OF LIFTING EQUIPMENT AND RIGGING**

Pre-use inspections and periodic inspections shall be performed per site policy, applicable standards, and manufacturer instructions.

Inspection items include crane and hoist controls, brakes, hooks, latches, wire ropes, chains, hoist drums, reeving, limit switches, slings, shackles, lifting beams, spreader bars, below-the-hook devices, leaks, loose parts, structural damage, and identification and load markings.

Equipment and rigging with cuts, broken wires, kinks, birdcaging, abrasion, heat damage, corrosion, bent or cracked hardware, missing identification, deformation, or other defects affecting safe use shall be removed from service until repaired or replaced.

**7.8 LOAD WEIGHT, CENTER OF GRAVITY, AND ATTACHMENT POINTS**

Actual or calculated load weight shall be confirmed where practicable before proceeding with the lift. Understanding the center of gravity, condition and adequacy of lifting points, load distribution to rigging, stability of internal contents or components, and potential shifting during lift or rotation is required.

Unsupported assumptions about weight or center of gravity are prohibited.

Lifting points shall be designed or approved for lifting, inspected, compatible with hardware, and used within design orientation and load path.

A test lift and reevaluation shall be performed when load behavior is uncertain.

**7.9 PRE-LIFT BRIEFING AND TEST LIFT**

A pre-lift briefing appropriate to the lift complexity shall be conducted before the operation. The briefing shall include scope, roles, weight and configuration, lift path, final placement, communication method, exclusion zones, stop-work triggers, environmental concerns, and pinch point or line-of-fire hazards.

A test lift shall be performed when appropriate by raising the load a short distance off the surface to confirm rigging security, load balance, brake function, clearance, stability, and equipment response.

If the load shifts or behaves abnormally, the load shall be landed safely and the lift reevaluated before proceeding.

**7.10 COMMUNICATION AND SIGNALING**

Clear, reliable communication between operator, rigger, and signal person is required for all lifting operations. Acceptable methods include standard hand signals, radio communication, hard-line or headset systems, or direct verbal communication where practical.

Only one designated signal person should direct the operator unless an emergency stop is needed. The operator shall stop if signals are unclear, conflicting, or lost.

Emergency stop may be initiated by anyone observing an unsafe condition. The operator shall not move the load unless the path is clear and the instruction is understood.

**7.11 LOAD CONTROL AND SUSPENDED LOAD RESTRICTIONS**

Loads shall be moved in a controlled manner to prevent sudden movement, swinging, rotation, or contact with structures or personnel. Controls include smooth lifting, avoiding shock loading, keeping the load as low as practical while traveling where applicable, using tag lines where needed, avoiding sudden starts and stops, maintaining awareness of pinch points and crush zones, and prohibiting personnel under the load.

Riding loads, hooks, or rigging is prohibited. Using suspended loads to drag, pry, or side pull is prohibited unless specifically allowed by equipment design and plan. Leaving suspended loads unattended is prohibited unless safely secured and site rules permit it.

Personnel shall remain clear of the load path, areas beneath the load, pinch points, swing radius, and rigging tension-release hazards.

**7.12 BARRICADES, EXCLUSION ZONES, AND AREA CONTROL**

Controlled lift areas are required whenever personnel could be exposed to suspended load hazards, load swing, falling objects, or equipment movement. Controls include barricades, warning tape, cones, hard barriers, signage, spotters, and temporary closure of walkways or work areas.

Exclusion zone size shall reflect load size, lift height, fall path, swing radius, nearby operations, equipment footprint, and dropped-object potential.

Unauthorized personnel are prohibited from entering lift areas.

**7.13 ENVIRONMENTAL AND SITE CONDITIONS**

Wind, rain, lightning, reduced visibility, uneven or unstable ground, congested areas, temperature effects, lighting, nearby energized lines, process hazards, and simultaneous operations shall be evaluated before and during lifting operations.

Ground-bearing pressure, floor loading, structural support, and setup stability shall be confirmed where applicable, especially for mobile cranes and heavy lifts.

Lifting operations shall be suspended when environmental conditions exceed safe limits as determined by the operator, supervisor, or site policy.

**7.14 OVERHEAD CRANES, HOISTS, AND FIXED LIFTING SYSTEMS**

Overhead cranes, hoists, and fixed lifting systems shall be operated only by trained and authorized personnel.

Requirements include pre-use inspections, brake and control verification, rated capacity compliance, centering the hook over the load where required, avoiding side pull unless equipment is designed and approved for it, proper reeving and hook loading, controlled movement, warning affected personnel in shared areas, and safe storage of hooks and blocks when not in use.

Equipment with known defects shall not be used until corrected or removed from service.

**7.15 MOBILE CRANES AND MOBILE LIFTING EQUIPMENT**

Mobile cranes and mobile lifting equipment shall be operated only by trained and authorized personnel.

Requirements include setup location review, ground stability, outrigger and stabilizer deployment, boom length and angle, operating radius, configuration-based capacity reduction, power line clearance, travel path, tail swing, wind conditions, and operator visibility.

Crane mats, cribbing, spotters, or utility coordination shall be used where needed. Load charts shall be available and followed for the specific configuration.

**7.16 SPECIAL HAZARDS: PINCH POINTS, LINE-OF-FIRE, AND STORED ENERGY**

Pinch points, crush hazards, line-of-fire exposures, and stored energy involving tensioned rigging, shifting loads, binding components, and release of retained force during removal or landing shall be evaluated for every lift.

Placement of hands, feet, or body parts between the load and fixed objects, under the load, between tensioned rigging components, or at hazardous connection points is prohibited.

Mechanical means, tag lines, and controlled staging shall be used to minimize manual positioning of loads where feasible.

**7.17 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION**

Company and contractor coordination is required before contractor lifting begins. [Company Name] shall communicate site lifting rules, known hazards, permit and critical lift requirements, exclusion zone expectations, restrictions near structures, utilities, or occupied areas, and reporting and stop-work expectations.

Contractors shall communicate planned lifting methods, equipment and rigging, operator/rigger/signal person qualifications where required, lift plan details for higher-risk lifts, hazards introduced by their work, and any incident, near miss, or unsafe condition encountered.

Coordination is required where multiple employers are working in the same area.

**7.18 INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW**

Dropped loads, uncontrolled movement, rigging failures, near misses, equipment malfunctions, communication failures, line-of-fire exposures, and unsafe conditions shall be reported immediately.

Investigation shall address lift planning, equipment and rigging selection, load information, communication effectiveness, area control, inspection adequacy, site and environmental conditions, supervision, training adequacy, and need for engineering or procedural improvements.

Periodic EHS and operational review of this program is required to verify adequacy of training, inspections, procedures, equipment, and corrective actions.
emergencyResponse: If a load becomes unstable or shifts unexpectedly, stop the operation, warn nearby personnel, and set the load down in the safest available location if possible.
If a dropped load incident occurs, evacuate the affected area, deny entry, notify supervision and EHS immediately, and do not disturb the scene until investigation is authorized.
If personnel are injured by a struck-by, crush, or pinch point incident, activate emergency medical response immediately, secure the area, and preserve equipment and rigging in the condition found.

If crane or equipment structural failure is suspected, evacuate the area immediately, establish a large exclusion zone, and do not re-enter until the equipment has been evaluated by a qualified person.

If contact with power lines occurs or is suspected, keep all personnel away from the equipment and load, do not touch the crane or rigging, and contact the utility provider and emergency services immediately.
Report all incidents, near misses, and equipment failures to supervision and EHS for investigation and corrective action.
documentation: Training records for operators, riggers, signal persons, supervisors, and affected personnel
Lift plans where required, including critical lift documentation
Equipment inspection records for cranes, hoists, and fixed lifting systems
Rigging inspection records for slings, shackles, below-the-hook devices, and hardware
Engineering reviews, calculations, or approvals where applicable
Incident, near miss, and investigation records
Corrective action records
Contractor coordination records
Audit findings and program review records
Permits or task risk assessments for higher-risk lifting work

**COMPLIANCE AND DEVIATIONS**

Failure to comply with this procedure may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination. More importantly, failure to follow safe lifting and rigging practices creates serious risk of injury, fatality, and catastrophic equipment or property damage.

Deviations from this procedure require EHS and operational management review and approval unless emergency action is needed to protect life or health. No deviation may remove required lift planning, equipment inspection, load control, exclusion zones, personnel clearance, or other critical safeguards without equivalent or greater protection.

**APPENDIX A -- LIFT PLANNING CHECKLIST**

- Load weight known or reasonably confirmed
- Center of gravity understood
- Lifting points adequate
- Proper lifting equipment selected
- Rigging configuration appropriate
- Operator, rigger, and signal person assigned
- Load path clear
- Final set location prepared
- Exclusion zone established
- Communication method confirmed
- Environmental conditions acceptable
- Critical lift review completed if required

**APPENDIX B -- RIGGING PRE-USE CHECKLIST**

- Slings checked for cuts, wear, broken wires, heat damage, or deformation
- Shackles, hooks, and hardware checked for cracks, bends, or missing parts
- Identification tags and ratings verified
- Lifting beams and below-the-hook devices checked for damage or missing markings
- Edge protection available where needed
- Connector fit and compatibility confirmed
- Hitch configuration and sling angle suitability verified
- Remove from service any damaged, questionable, modified, or unmarked rigging equipment

**APPENDIX C -- PRE-LIFT BRIEFING CHECKLIST**

- Scope of the lift reviewed
- Roles and responsibilities confirmed
- Load weight and shape discussed
- Rigging method reviewed
- Load path and landing area identified
- Exclusion zones established
- Communication method agreed
- Test lift need determined
- Stop-work triggers defined
- Environmental concerns addressed
- Pinch point or line-of-fire hazards discussed

**APPENDIX D -- SUSPENDED LOAD SAFETY RULES**

- Never stand under a suspended load
- Stay out of the load path
- Keep clear of pinch points and crush zones
- Use tag lines where needed
- Do not ride the load or hook
- Do not place hands on the load where shifting could trap them
- Stop the lift if communication is lost or conditions change
- Keep unauthorized personnel out of the lift area

**APPENDIX E -- CONDITIONS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE STOP WORK**

- Load weight or center of gravity uncertain beyond acceptable limits
- Rigging damaged, unmarked, or unsuitable
- Equipment defects affecting safe operation
- Communication unclear, conflicting, or lost
- Personnel enter the suspended load hazard zone
- Environmental conditions become unsafe
- Load shifts unexpectedly or becomes unstable
- Ground or support conditions become questionable
- Operator, supervisor, signal person, rigger, EHS, or other responsible person stops the work
- Conditions change such that safe lifting cannot be assured

title: Powered Industrial Trucks and Mobile Equipment Safety Program
summary: Establishes minimum requirements for safely operating, inspecting, maintaining, and controlling powered industrial trucks and mobile equipment including pedestrian safety, traffic management, dock operations, and load handling.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for safely operating, inspecting, maintaining, and controlling powered industrial trucks and mobile equipment at [Company Name] facilities and work locations.

The objective is to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, struck-by incidents, caught-between incidents, tip-overs, dropped load incidents, property damage, equipment damage, and operational disruption resulting from unsafe operation, inadequate training, poor visibility, pedestrian interaction, unstable loads, excessive speed, improper parking, inadequate maintenance, poor traffic management, and failure to control equipment hazards.

This program applies to all powered industrial truck and mobile equipment operations and requires that every operator be trained and authorized, every piece of equipment be inspected before use, and every work area be evaluated for hazards before operations begin.

Document No.: [Document No.]
Effective Date: [Effective Date]
Revision: [Revision]
Owner: [Owner]
Approved By: [Approved By]
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, warehouses, production areas, test areas, maintenance activities, field locations, loading docks, storage yards, launch or test support areas, construction support activities, and non-routine operations involving powered industrial trucks or mobile equipment.

It applies to employees who operate or work around PITs or mobile equipment, forklift operators, telehandler operators, tugger and tow motor and pallet jack operators where powered, supervisors and managers, EHS personnel, maintenance personnel, engineers, technicians, operators, support personnel in affected areas, and contractors performing work involving or affected by PITs or mobile equipment onsite.

It applies to but is not limited to:
- Forklifts (stand-up and sit-down)
- Reach trucks and order pickers
- Powered pallet jacks and electric pallet trucks
- Tow tractors and tuggers
- Telehandlers
- Yard tractors where applicable
- Utility vehicles and site service carts
- Material handling vehicles
- Equipment used for loading, unloading, moving, stacking, or positioning materials

It applies to both indoor and outdoor operations including warehouses, production and assembly areas, docks and loading areas, yards and laydown areas, road crossings within site boundaries, congested pedestrian areas, and work near structures, utilities, pits, or elevated edges.

This SOP should be used with other required programs such as suspended loads and lifting operations, working at heights, lockout/tagout, contractor safety, permit-to-work, traffic control, and battery charging or fuel handling requirements where applicable.

This procedure does not authorize the use of PITs or mobile equipment for personnel lifting unless a separate approved procedure and approved equipment specifically permit and govern that activity.

definitions.definition: A device added to a PIT or mobile equipment to extend its function, such as a clamp, rotator, push-pull, side-shift, or fork extension, which may affect rated capacity and handling characteristics.
A person who has been trained, evaluated, and authorized by [Company Name] to operate specific powered industrial trucks or mobile equipment.
Any area around the equipment where the operator cannot see personnel, objects, or obstructions without additional aids such as mirrors, cameras, or spotters.
Weight built into the rear of a forklift or similar equipment to offset the load on the forks and maintain stability.
A bridge device used to span the gap between a loading dock and a trailer or vehicle, rated for specific loads and equipment.
The horizontal distance from the face of the forks to the center of gravity of the load, which directly affects rated capacity.
Any powered vehicle or machine used for transporting, lifting, pushing, pulling, or positioning materials or personnel within a worksite.
An area designated to separate pedestrians from equipment travel paths and operating zones to prevent struck-by and caught-between hazards.

A mobile, power-driven vehicle used to carry, push, pull, lift, stack, or tier materials, including forklifts, reach trucks, order pickers, powered pallet jacks, and similar equipment as defined by OSHA.
A visual and operational check of equipment performed by the operator before use to identify defects or unsafe conditions.
The maximum load the equipment is designed to handle at a specified load center and configuration, as shown on the data plate.
A designated person who assists the operator by providing guidance, warning pedestrians, and ensuring the path is clear, especially during backing or limited-visibility operations.
The geometric area formed by the front axle pivot points and the rear steer axle center that determines the tipping threshold of a counterbalanced forklift.
Loss of equipment stability resulting in the unit overturning laterally or longitudinally, which is a leading cause of forklift fatalities.
The designated or planned route for equipment movement, including aisles, lanes, intersections, ramps, and outdoor routes.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Provide technical guidance on PIT and mobile equipment hazards. Audit program compliance. Investigate incidents involving mobile equipment. Maintain program documentation. Coordinate training requirements. Review traffic management plans. Update this procedure as regulations or conditions change.

Ensure only trained and authorized personnel operate PITs and mobile equipment. Enforce traffic rules, speed limits, and pedestrian controls. Verify pre-use inspections are completed. Remove unsafe equipment from service. Review incidents and near misses. Ensure work areas are evaluated for hazards before operations begin. Approve deviations where authorized.

Operate equipment only within rated capacity and design limitations. Perform pre-use inspections before each shift or use. Follow all traffic rules, speed limits, and pedestrian separation requirements. Refuse to operate unsafe equipment. Maintain clear visibility or use spotters. Report equipment deficiencies, incidents, and near misses immediately. Wear seat belt or restraint as required.

Stay in designated walkways and pedestrian zones. Do not approach operating equipment unless the operator has clearly stopped and acknowledged. Comply with exclusion zones, barricades, and traffic controls. Report unsafe conditions, near misses, and traffic control deficiencies.

Perform maintenance and repairs on PITs and mobile equipment using approved methods and parts. Apply LOTO where maintenance exposes personnel to hazardous energy. Verify equipment is safe before returning to service. Maintain service records.

Design and maintain traffic routes, pedestrian controls, dock configurations, and equipment storage areas. Evaluate floor loading, ramp grades, and surface conditions. Support traffic management planning and layout improvements.

Comply with [Company Name] traffic rules and mobile equipment requirements while onsite. Provide documentation of operator training and authorization where required. Report all incidents, near misses, and unsafe conditions. Coordinate equipment operations with [Company Name] supervision.

training: PIT operators shall complete equipment-specific or class-specific training including classroom instruction, practical training, and operator evaluation before independent operation
Telehandler operators shall complete training specific to telehandler controls, capacity, stability, and attachment use
Powered pallet jack or tugger operators shall complete training appropriate to the equipment type and operating environment
Supervisors and managers shall complete training on program requirements, operator authorization, traffic management, and incident response
Employees working in areas exposed to mobile equipment hazards shall receive pedestrian awareness training covering walkways, exclusion zones, traffic controls, and reporting
Contractors shall meet training requirements as defined by site policy before operating equipment onsite
Maintenance personnel shall receive training relevant to inspection, energy isolation, and safe service procedures

Training topics include recognition of PIT and mobile equipment hazards, equipment controls and operating characteristics, stability and capacity principles, load handling and visibility, pedestrian interaction and traffic rules, intersections and backing and blind spot controls, dock and trailer safety, ramp and incline and surface hazards, parking and shutdown, battery charging or refueling hazards, attachments and special handling, pre-use inspections, incident and near-miss reporting, and stop-work authority
Refresher training or reevaluation is required after unsafe operation, incidents, assignment to different equipment, significant workplace changes, or as required by policy or regulation
ppe: Hard hat where required by site policy or overhead hazard exposure
Steel-toe or safety-toe boots
High-visibility vest where required by site rules or outdoor/yard operations
Seat belt or operator restraint system as required for sit-down equipment
Hearing protection where noise exposure warrants
Eye protection where flying debris, dust, or splash hazards exist
Acid-resistant gloves and face shield for battery maintenance where applicable
Additional PPE as identified by task hazard assessment
hazards: Tip-over from overloading, uneven surfaces, sharp turns, or elevated loads
Struck-by at intersections, blind corners, doorways, and pedestrian crossings
Pedestrian struck-by in shared traffic areas, docks, and congested zones
Caught-between or crush injuries between equipment and fixed objects
Falling or shifting loads from improper stacking, overloading, or unstable materials
Dock edge falls or trailer separation during loading/unloading
Carbon monoxide exposure from internal combustion equipment in enclosed areas
Battery acid exposure, hydrogen gas accumulation, or electrical hazards during charging
LPG, diesel, or gasoline fuel hazards during refueling
Ramp and incline loss of control
Contact with overhead power lines, utilities, or structures
Equipment malfunction from inadequate maintenance or uninspected defects
procedure:
**7.1 HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND ASSESSMENT**

Before PIT or mobile equipment operations begin, the work area and task shall be evaluated. The evaluation shall consider equipment type, load characteristics, travel route, congestion, pedestrian exposure, blind spots, surface conditions, dock edges, ramps, pits, lighting, intersections, charging or fueling areas, ventilation for internal combustion units, outdoor weather, attachment use, simultaneous operations, and need for spotters, barricades, or signage.

Higher-risk activities include long or unstable loads, congested pedestrian areas, trailer loading and unloading, work near suspended loads, use near utilities, yard operations with poor visibility, slopes, uneven surfaces, and special attachments.

If hazards cannot be adequately controlled, the operation shall not proceed.

**7.2 HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS FOR PIT AND MOBILE EQUIPMENT OPERATIONS**

The following control hierarchy shall be applied:

1. Eliminate unnecessary equipment movement or redesign material flow
2. Separate pedestrians and equipment by physical barriers or controlled routes
3. Use equipment appropriate to the task and environment
4. Improve visibility, layout, lighting, and traffic control
5. Use administrative controls such as speed limits, spotters, signage, and work rules as supplemental controls
6. Use PPE as a final layer of protection

Pedestrian exposure to travel paths, backing zones, and load handling areas should be minimized or eliminated whenever feasible.

**7.3 EQUIPMENT AUTHORIZATION AND OPERATOR QUALIFICATION**

Only trained and authorized personnel may operate PITs or mobile equipment. Authorization shall be equipment-specific or class-specific as required by [Company Name] policy.

Training and evaluation shall cover controls, instrumentation, capacity, stability, visibility, load handling, site-specific hazards, charging or refueling, parking, pedestrian interaction, dock and trailer operations, attachments, emergency actions, and inspection requirements.

Operators shall demonstrate competency before independent operation.

Refresher training or reevaluation is required after unsafe operation, incidents, assignment to different equipment, significant workplace changes, or when required by policy or regulation.

**7.4 EQUIPMENT SELECTION AND USE LIMITATIONS**

Equipment shall be selected based on load, path, environment, surface, and intended task. Selection criteria include rated capacity, mast height, fork length, turning radius, indoor or outdoor use, fuel type, floor loading, aisle width, attachments, tire type, ventilation for internal combustion units, and precision positioning needs.

Use beyond rated capacity, with unapproved attachments, on unsuitable surfaces, in hazardous atmospheres unless specifically approved, for towing or lifting or pushing beyond design limits, or for personnel lifting unless separately approved and governed is prohibited.

Improvised modifications are prohibited.

**7.5 PRE-USE INSPECTION**

Pre-use inspections shall be performed before operation at shift start or as otherwise required by site rules. Inspection items include forks, mast, chains, rollers, carriage, tires, hydraulic hoses and cylinders, horn, warning devices, lights, mirrors, cameras, alarms, steering, brakes, seat belt or restraint, leaks, gauges, battery or fuel condition, overhead guard, structural components, attachments, parking brake, backup alarm if equipped, and fire extinguisher where required.

Equipment with defects affecting safe operation shall be removed from service and reported.

Unsafe equipment shall be tagged or controlled to prevent use until repaired.

**7.6 GENERAL OPERATING RULES**

Equipment shall be operated in a controlled and predictable manner. Requirements include seat belt or restraint use, smooth starts and stops and turns, safe speeds, slowing at intersections and blind corners and ramps and congested areas, horn or warning use where required, safe following distance, keeping limbs inside the compartment, facing direction of travel when practicable, maintaining clear visibility, operating only from the designated operator position, no phone use or distractions, and no passengers unless the equipment is designed for them.

If visibility is blocked by the load, reverse travel where safe and allowed or use of a spotter or alternate control method is required.

Stunt driving, horseplay, abrupt maneuvers, and unsafe speed are prohibited.

**7.7 LOAD HANDLING AND STABILITY**

The load shall not exceed rated capacity. Capacity is affected by load center, mast height, attachment use, and configuration.

Loads shall be stable and balanced, forks fully positioned where applicable, load against backrest where practical, proper mast tilt for travel where permitted, loads kept as low as practical while traveling, no sudden lifting or lowering or braking or turning with elevated loads, and no movement of unstable or unsecured loads unless specifically controlled.

Additional securement may be needed for odd-shaped materials. Personnel under elevated loads is prohibited. Raised loads change center of gravity and increase instability risk.

**7.8 ATTACHMENTS AND SPECIAL HANDLING**

Attachments shall be approved for the equipment, capacity impact known and reflected, operators trained, and the task evaluated for added hazards.

Special handling considerations include long loads, suspended attachments, drums, high-center-of-gravity loads, fragile or valuable equipment, shifting contents, and wide or oversized materials.

Reduced speed, spotters, barricades, alternate routes, additional supervision, and lift planning may be required.

Operators shall receive specific training when attachments change equipment handling characteristics.

**7.9 PEDESTRIAN SAFETY AND TRAFFIC CONTROL**

Pedestrian interaction shall be treated as a serious risk. Controls include designated walkways, marked crossings, physical barriers, one-way routes, speed limits, mirrors, horn use rules, warning lights, spotters, restricted access zones, and dock and yard traffic rules.

Pedestrians shall not approach operating equipment unless the operator has seen them and movement is clearly stopped or controlled.

Operators shall yield or stop when separation is uncertain.

Physical separation should be used whenever feasible in congested or high-risk areas.

**7.10 INTERSECTIONS, BLIND SPOTS, AND BACKING**

Extra caution is required at intersections, blind corners, doorways, racks, and restricted sight lines. Controls include slowing down, horn use, maintaining control, stopping if the path is not clear, and use of mirrors, cameras, or spotters.

For backing, operators shall check the path behind, use a spotter when needed, move slowly and deliberately, and stop immediately if communication with the spotter is lost.

**7.11 RAMPS, INCLINES, EDGES, AND UNEVEN SURFACES**

Reduced speed and safe orientation of the load are required on inclines. Turning on ramps is prohibited unless safe and specifically allowed.

Adequate distance from dock edges, trenches, pits, and drop-offs shall be maintained. Dock plates, dock boards, or ramps shall be rated and secured.

Use on unstable, muddy, soft, or damaged surfaces is prohibited unless evaluated and controlled. Approaching unprotected edges without confirming the surface is designed and safe for the equipment and load is prohibited.

**7.12 LOADING DOCKS, TRAILERS, AND VEHICLES**

Requirements include trailer brake set, wheel chocks or restraints or locking systems where required, properly positioned and rated dock plate or leveler, sound trailer floor, adequate lighting, clear path, no trailer separation during loading, and awareness of slippery floors, uneven loading, load shift, trailer creep, reefer units, hoses, and personnel inside trailers.

Work shall stop if trailer or loading conditions become unsafe.

**7.13 PARKING, SHUTDOWN, AND UNATTENDED EQUIPMENT**

Parking requirements include lowering forks or attachments to a safe position, neutralizing controls, setting parking brake, shutting off power, removing key or control access where required, chocking wheels where required, and prohibiting parking in aisles, exits, emergency access routes, or in front of fire protection equipment unless authorized.

Leaving elevated forks, loads, or attachments in hazardous positions is prohibited.

Site rules may define when equipment is considered unattended based on distance and visibility.

**7.14 BATTERY CHARGING, REFUELING, AND ENERGY SOURCES**

Charging and refueling shall occur only in designated or approved areas.

For batteries: ventilation shall be adequate, no ignition sources permitted, required PPE worn, acid exposure protection available, proper charger connection and disconnection followed, equipment parked securely, cable and connector inspections performed, and spill response materials available.

For fueling: approved fueling areas used, engine shutdown where required, ignition source control maintained, spill prevention in place, and bonding or grounding applied where applicable.

Operators shall be trained on battery, LPG, diesel, gasoline, or other energy source hazards specific to their equipment.

**7.15 MAINTENANCE, REPAIRS, AND OUT-OF-SERVICE CONDITIONS**

Equipment needing repair shall be removed from service. Only qualified personnel shall perform maintenance or repair.

Requirements include defect reporting, tagging or locking out equipment where needed, proper replacement parts and service methods, LOTO when maintenance exposes personnel to hazardous energy, and verification before return to service.

Common out-of-service conditions include brake or steering problems, hydraulic leaks, damaged forks or mast, inoperative horn or warning device, tire damage, nonfunctional restraint, structural damage, unsafe attachments, and uncontrolled fuel or battery hazards.

**7.16 OUTDOOR, YARD, AND SHARED-ROUTE OPERATIONS**

Outdoor hazards include weather, uneven terrain, poor lighting, road crossings, and interaction with larger vehicles.

Controls include designated lanes, pedestrian exclusion zones, speed limits, high-visibility clothing where required, radios or communication protocols, spotters, lighting requirements, and weather restrictions for poor visibility, wind, or slick surfaces.

Where routes are shared with trucks, cranes, or other vehicles, traffic plans addressing right-of-way, crossing controls, and restricted access are required.

**7.17 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION**

Coordination is required before contractor PIT or mobile equipment operations begin. [Company Name] shall communicate traffic rules, pedestrian controls, restricted areas, speed limits, dock and yard procedures, incident reporting and stop-work expectations, and site-specific hazards.

Contractors shall communicate equipment types, operator qualifications where required, travel routes, operating areas, hazards introduced, and any incident, near miss, or unsafe condition encountered.

Coordination is required where multiple employers are working in the same area.

**7.18 INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW**

Collisions, near misses, struck-by events, dropped loads, tip-overs, pedestrian exposures, equipment malfunctions, trailer incidents, property damage, and unsafe conditions shall be reported immediately.

Investigation shall address operator actions, training adequacy, equipment condition, load characteristics, traffic control and layout, pedestrian exposure, visibility, communication, surface and environmental conditions, supervision, and need for improvements.

Periodic EHS and operational review of this program is required.
emergencyResponse: If a tip-over occurs, the operator should stay in the cab, hold on firmly, brace against the seat, and lean away from the direction of fall. Do not attempt to jump from the equipment.

If a struck-by or crush injury occurs, activate emergency medical response immediately. Secure the area and do not move the equipment until instructed by emergency responders or investigation personnel.
If a pedestrian is struck or a near-miss with a pedestrian occurs, stop equipment immediately, render assistance, notify supervision and EHS, and secure the scene.
If a load falls or becomes unstable, stop the equipment, warn nearby personnel, establish an exclusion zone, and do not attempt to recover the load until conditions are evaluated.
If a fuel or battery spill, fire, or hazardous atmosphere condition occurs, evacuate the area, notify emergency response, and do not re-enter until cleared.
Report all incidents, near misses, and equipment failures to supervision and EHS for investigation and corrective action.
documentation: Operator training and evaluation records
Equipment authorization records where required
Pre-use inspection records where required by site policy
Maintenance and repair records
Incident, near miss, and investigation records
Corrective action records
Contractor coordination records
Traffic control or site layout review records where applicable
Audit findings and program review records

**COMPLIANCE AND DEVIATIONS**

Failure to comply with this procedure may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination. More importantly, failure to follow safe operating practices for powered industrial trucks and mobile equipment creates serious risk of injury, fatality, and property damage.

Deviations from this procedure require EHS and operational management review and approval unless emergency action is needed to protect life or health. No deviation may remove required operator authorization, equipment inspection, pedestrian separation, traffic control, capacity limits, or other critical safeguards without equivalent or greater protection.

**APPENDIX A -- PRE-USE INSPECTION CHECKLIST**

- Forks, mast, and carriage condition
- Tires and wheels
- Steering and brakes
- Horn and warning devices
- Lights, mirrors, and cameras where equipped
- Hydraulic leaks
- Seat belt or restraint system
- Battery, fuel, or power source
- Attachments secure and appropriate
- No obvious structural damage
- Data plate or capacity information available
- Equipment safe to operate
- Remove from service any equipment with defects affecting safe operation

**APPENDIX B -- PEDESTRIAN AND TRAFFIC CONTROL CHECKLIST**

- Travel route known
- Pedestrian walkways identified
- Blind spots and intersections understood
- Speed limits known
- Barricades or exclusion zones in place where needed
- Spotters assigned where visibility is limited
- Dock and trailer controls in place if applicable
- Lighting adequate
- Horn or warning expectations understood
- Shared-route hazards controlled

**APPENDIX C -- LOAD HANDLING CHECKLIST**

- Load within rated capacity
- Load stable and balanced
- Forks properly positioned
- Load center understood
- Visibility adequate or spotter used
- Travel path clear
- Load as low as practical for travel
- Attachment use approved if applicable
- No personnel under or near load path
- Surface and clearance suitable

**APPENDIX D -- DOCK AND TRAILER SAFETY CHECKLIST**

- Trailer secured against movement
- Dock plate or leveler in place and rated
- Trailer floor sound
- Lighting adequate
- Path clear of obstructions
- Edge conditions controlled
- Weather or slippery conditions considered
- Communication with dock personnel clear
- Operation can proceed without pedestrian conflict

**APPENDIX E -- CONDITIONS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE STOP WORK**

- Operator not authorized or not fit to operate
- Equipment defects affecting safe operation
- Load exceeds capacity or becomes unstable
- Visibility lost and cannot be restored safely
- Pedestrian separation cannot be maintained
- Dock, trailer, ramp, or surface conditions become unsafe
- Communication with a spotter is lost where required
- Weather or environmental conditions become unsafe
- A near miss, collision, or uncontrolled equipment movement occurs and conditions remain uncorrected
- A supervisor, EHS, or other responsible person stops the work
- Conditions change such that safe operation cannot be assured

title: Machine Guarding and Tooling Safety Program
summary: Comprehensive enterprise-grade program for identifying, evaluating, controlling, and monitoring hazards associated with machinery, machine guarding, and tooling used in workplace operations. Covers point-of-operation protection, interlocks, safeguarding devices, tooling controls, setup and changeover, jam clearing, automated and robotic cell safety, and amputation prevention.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, evaluating, controlling, and monitoring hazards associated with machinery, machine guarding, and tooling used in workplace operations at [Company Name]. The purpose is to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, amputation, laceration, crushing injury, entanglement, struck-by injury, caught-in or caught-between injury, equipment damage, product damage, and operational disruption resulting from unguarded or inadequately guarded machines, unsafe tooling, unexpected machine movement, bypassed safety devices, exposure to moving parts, improper setup or changeover, inadequate inspection, and unsafe work practices.

This program applies the hierarchy of controls to machine guarding and tooling hazards and establishes clear expectations for safeguarding, inspection, safe operation, maintenance, and incident response. All personnel involved in or exposed to machine operations shall understand and comply with this procedure.

Document No.: [Document No.] | Effective Date: [Effective Date] | Revision: [Revision]
Owner: [Owner] | Approved By: [Approved By]
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, production areas, manufacturing cells, laboratories, test areas, maintenance activities, workshops, fabrication areas, machine shops, assembly areas, field support activities, and non-routine operations involving machinery, powered equipment, machine tooling, or exposure to hazardous moving parts at [Site Name] and all company-controlled locations.

This procedure applies to:
- Employees who operate, set up, adjust, maintain, clean, service, inspect, or work near machinery or tooling
- Supervisors and managers responsible for machine operations
- EHS personnel
- Engineers, technicians, operators, assemblers, machinists, maintenance personnel, and support personnel
- Contractors performing work on or around machinery onsite

This procedure applies to machinery and tooling involving, but not limited to:
- Saws, grinders, drill presses, milling machines, lathes, presses, shears, punches, and rollers
- Conveyor systems, mixers, fans and blowers, and power transmission equipment
- Automated or semi-automated equipment, robotic cells, and CNC machines
- Fixtures and jigs, dies, cutting tools, forming tools, clamps, and workholding devices
- Guards, interlocks, light curtains, presence-sensing devices, two-hand controls, adjustable guards, barrier guards, and point-of-operation protection systems

This procedure applies to both routine and non-routine machine use, including production, testing, setup, changeover, troubleshooting, jam clearing, maintenance support, inspection, and cleaning.

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with other required programs such as lockout/tagout, electrical safety, confined space, hazard communication, PITs/mobile equipment, working at heights, permit-to-work, and contractor safety requirements where applicable.

[Applicable State / Local Requirements]
[Related Company Procedures]

definitions.definition: A physical enclosure or fence that prevents personnel from reaching into a hazard zone during machine operation.
The act of defeating, disabling, or circumventing a guard, interlock, sensor, or other safeguarding device, whether intentionally or unintentionally.
A condition where a body part is caught in, pulled into, compressed, or crushed between moving machine parts, between a machine part and a fixed object, or between two or more moving components.
The area in, on, or around a machine where exposure to hazardous motion, energy, or material exists during operation.
A permanent barrier attached to the machine that requires tools for removal and provides consistent protection against access to hazardous parts.
A device that automatically stops hazardous machine motion or prevents startup when a guard is opened, removed, or when personnel enter a safeguarded zone.
A device used to hold, support, locate, or guide a workpiece during machining or assembly operations to improve accuracy, repeatability, and safety.
A barrier, device, or system designed to prevent personnel from contacting hazardous moving parts, points of operation, or other machine hazards during operation.
The area of a machine where work is actually performed on the material being processed, such as cutting, shaping, boring, forming, or assembling.

A device that detects the presence of a person or body part within a defined zone and initiates a stop command or prevents hazardous motion. Examples include light curtains, area scanners, and safety mats.
The minimum distance between a safeguarding device and the nearest hazard point, calculated to ensure that hazardous motion has ceased before a person can reach the danger zone.
Cutting tools, forming tools, dies, blades, bits, inserts, fixtures, jigs, clamps, workholding devices, and other accessories used to perform machining or processing operations.

The unintended or uncontrolled activation of a machine or release of stored energy that could cause injury. This includes startup from control circuit faults, software commands, or accidental activation.

A safeguarding device that requires the simultaneous and sustained use of both hands to initiate and maintain machine operation, preventing the operator from having hands in the danger zone during the hazardous portion of the cycle.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Provide technical guidance on machine guarding standards, safeguarding methods, and regulatory requirements. Conduct or support machine hazard assessments and guarding reviews. Assist with selection and evaluation of safeguarding devices, interlocks, and presence-sensing systems. Review bypass authorization requests and temporary safeguard exception conditions. Investigate machine-related incidents, near misses, and guarding deficiencies. Conduct periodic audits of guarding compliance, interlock function, and tooling condition. Maintain program documentation and support corrective action tracking. Coordinate regulatory updates and industry best practice integration.

Ensure all machines under their area of responsibility are properly guarded and safeguarded before operation. Verify that operators, setup personnel, and maintenance workers are trained, competent, and authorized as applicable. Enforce compliance with guarding, interlock, tooling, and lockout/tagout requirements. Authorize and control temporary guard removal, bypass conditions, and safeguard exceptions. Conduct pre-task briefings for higher-risk machine activities, setup, or changeover. Stop work when guarding deficiencies, unsafe tooling, or bypass conditions are identified. Support incident investigation and corrective action implementation.

Perform pre-use inspections of machines, guards, interlocks, tooling, and workholding devices before operation. Operate machines only with all required guards and safeguards in place and functioning. Keep hands, body parts, and loose clothing out of danger zones. Use push sticks, feed tools, holders, and other aids where required. Do not remove, bypass, or defeat guards, interlocks, or safety devices unless specifically authorized under a controlled condition. Report damaged, missing, loose, or ineffective guards, interlocks, or tooling immediately. Stop work and notify supervision when unsafe conditions are identified.

Follow controlled setup, adjustment, and changeover procedures. Apply lockout/tagout where required during tooling changes, die changes, blade changes, or fixture adjustments. Confirm tooling alignment, securement, and compatibility before restart. Restore all guards, covers, interlocks, sensors, and access panels after changeover. Verify safety functions and conduct trial operation per approved procedure before releasing to production. Report recurrent setup issues, guard interference, or tooling compatibility problems.

Perform maintenance and repair under proper hazardous energy control including lockout/tagout. Replace guards, interlocks, safety components, and tooling with approved parts and materials. Restore all guards, panels, covers, and safeguarding devices after maintenance. Test safety functions, interlocks, emergency stops, and sensors before returning equipment to service. Document maintenance, repairs, and inspections as required. Report safety deficiencies discovered during maintenance and support corrective action.

Design or specify machines, tooling, and safeguarding systems that minimize exposure to hazardous motion and energy. Conduct or support machine hazard assessments, guarding reviews, and safe-distance calculations. Review and approve new machine installations, modifications, tooling changes, and safeguarding upgrades. Evaluate bypass requests, alternative guarding proposals, and temporary safeguard conditions. Support investigation of machine-related incidents and design-related corrective actions. Maintain engineering documentation for guarding configurations, interlock logic, and tooling specifications.

Comply with all [Company Name] machine guarding, tooling, lockout/tagout, and safeguarding requirements while onsite. Communicate scope of work, required machine access, and any planned guard removal, bypass, testing, or energized troubleshooting. Provide evidence of training and competency for machine-related tasks where required. Report all incidents, near misses, unsafe conditions, or discovered deficiencies to the company representative. Follow stop-work directives and do not resume work until conditions are corrected.

hazards: Amputation from unguarded or inadequately guarded point-of-operation, rotating parts, or power transmission components
Caught-in or caught-between injuries from rollers, gears, belts, chains, rotating shafts, or reciprocating parts
Lacerations from cutting tools, sharp edges, blades, or burrs
Crushing injuries from presses, shears, punches, clamps, or closing dies
Entanglement of hair, clothing, gloves, or cleaning materials in rotating parts
Struck-by injuries from ejected parts, chips, fragments, broken tooling, or workpiece kickback
Burns from hot surfaces, friction, or heat-generating operations
