procedure:
7.1 HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND ASSESSMENT

Before operation, setup, modification, or new installation, evaluate the machine and associated tooling for hazards including: point-of-operation hazards, rotating/reciprocating/cutting/shearing/crushing/punching/drawing-in hazards, power transmission components, tooling movement, workholding, unexpected startup, ejected parts/chips/scrap, hot surfaces, sharp edges, pinch points, access needs during operation/setup/jam clearing/cleaning/maintenance, manual feeding exposures, need to reach into danger zones, interference between production needs and safeguarding, and human factors such as repetitive access and shortcut behavior.

Documented risk assessment, engineering review, or additional control strategy shall be completed for higher-risk machines or operations where needed. The machine or operation shall not proceed if hazards cannot be adequately controlled.

7.2 HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS FOR MACHINE GUARDING AND TOOLING

Apply the following control order:
1. Eliminate the hazard through design or process change
2. Use engineered guards, barriers, enclosures, or fixed separation
3. Use interlocks, presence-sensing devices, or other safeguarding controls
4. Use safe tooling, fixtures, clamps, and workholding methods that reduce exposure
5. Use administrative controls such as procedures, signs, and training as supplemental controls
6. Use PPE as a final layer of protection

Safeguarding should prevent access to the hazard whenever feasible rather than rely on operator reaction time.

7.3 MACHINE GUARDING REQUIREMENTS

All machines shall be guarded against point of operation, ingoing nip points, rotating parts, reciprocating motion, flying chips/fragments/debris, transverse motion, power transmission components, and exposed blades/cutters/rolls/tooling.

Guarding methods include: fixed guards, adjustable guards, self-adjusting guards, interlocked guards, barrier enclosures, light curtains, area scanners, safety mats, two-hand controls, pull-backs or restraints where specifically designed and allowed, presence-sensing devices, and perimeter guarding for automated equipment.

Guards shall: prevent or adequately restrict access, be secure and durable, not create additional hazards, not interfere unnecessarily with work, allow visibility where needed without exposing personnel, not be easily bypassed, and withstand expected operating conditions.

Improvised barriers, cardboard shields, taped-open guards, or temporary blocking methods shall not be used as safeguards.

7.4 TOOLING, FIXTURES, AND WORKHOLDING

Tooling, fixtures, and workholding methods shall be selected and maintained to reduce exposure and support safe operation. Requirements include: correct tooling selection, secure installation, safe condition of jigs/fixtures/clamps/workholding, minimizing need for hands in danger zones, use of push sticks/blocks/feed tools/holders where appropriate, verification of correct dies/cutters/blades/bits/inserts/forming tools, replacement of worn or damaged tooling, and adequate support/restraint of workpieces to prevent kickback, shifting, or ejection.

Guarding and procedures shall be reevaluated when tooling changes affect hazard exposure.

7.5 INTERLOCKS, SENSORS, AND SAFEGUARDING DEVICES

Interlocks, sensors, and safeguarding devices shall be maintained in functional condition and tested as required. Operation with nonfunctional or defeated interlocks is prohibited unless under an approved controlled condition.

Verify guard switches, sensors, light curtains, safety stops, access door/gate operation, emergency stop access, and protection of cables/wiring/control components. Investigate recurring faults, nuisance trips, or repeated bypass attempts.

Interlocks and sensors are not a substitute for proper hazard assessment and must have suitable placement, response time, and safe distance for the hazard.

7.6 PRE-USE INSPECTION

Operators or authorized users shall inspect machinery, guards, and tooling before use, at shift start, or as otherwise required. Inspect: guard presence and condition, secure mounting, interlock/sensor condition, emergency stop access, tooling installation and condition, workholding device condition, visible damage/looseness/cracks/wear, leaks, unusual noise or vibration, cleanliness affecting safe operation, control panel integrity, labels, and warning markings.

Remove from service or control to prevent use when defects affecting safe operation are found.

7.7 STARTUP, OPERATION, AND NORMAL USE

Operate only from the designated control position and in accordance with approved procedures. Requirements include: keeping guards and safeguards in place, keeping hands/body parts out of danger zones, prohibiting reaching into moving machinery, prohibiting clearing jams/repositioning parts/removing scraps during hazardous motion unless an approved safeguarded method exists, using tools/brushes/devices for chip or scrap removal where appropriate, monitoring for abnormal machine behavior, stopping for unusual noise/vibration/part ejection/guard shift/unexpected motion, not distracting operators performing hazardous tasks, controlling loose clothing/jewelry/entanglement hazards, and only using intended materials/processes unless reviewed and approved.

7.8 SETUP, ADJUSTMENT, CHANGEOVER, AND TOOLING CHANGES

Controlled conditions are required for setup, alignment, adjustment, die changes, blade changes, fixture changes, and other tooling activities. Requirements include: stopping hazardous motion before access, use of lockout/tagout where required, safe support of raised components or suspended parts, use of proper tools for installation/removal, confirmation of tooling alignment/securement/compatibility before restart, restoration of guards/covers/sensors/access panels after changeover, verification of safety functions before return to production, and cautious trial operation per approved procedure.

Limited movement for setup, troubleshooting, or testing may only occur under an approved controlled method with qualified personnel and additional safeguards.

7.9 JAM CLEARING, TROUBLESHOOTING, AND ABNORMAL CONDITIONS

Jam clearing, fault response, sensor alignment, and troubleshooting require strict control due to high exposure risk. Requirements include: stopping the machine before clearing jams unless a safeguarded method specifically allows otherwise, using lockout/tagout where exposure exists, using tools instead of hands where feasible, controlling stored energy/residual motion, preventing unexpected restart, prohibiting unauthorized bypass to restore production, and reevaluating repeated jams or chronic abnormal conditions for root cause and design correction.

Operators shall report recurrent jams, bypass attempts, or guard interference issues.

7.10 LOCKOUT/TAGOUT AND HAZARDOUS ENERGY CONTROL

The Company Lockout/Tagout Procedure shall be followed whenever servicing, maintenance, jam clearing, blade changes, tooling replacement, or other tasks expose personnel to hazardous energy. Applicable energy sources include: electrical, mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, thermal, gravity, spring tension, stored pressure, and residual motion.

Employees shall not rely solely on control circuit stops, software commands, or normal stop buttons where hazardous energy exposure exists unless an approved alternative control method is specifically allowed and equivalent protection is demonstrated.

7.11 TEMPORARY GUARD REMOVAL OR SAFEGUARD BYPASS

Temporary removal or bypass is allowed only under tightly controlled conditions. Requirements include: necessity for setup, troubleshooting, testing, maintenance, or approved engineering work; authorization by supervision and where required maintenance, engineering, or EHS; equivalent or greater protection; minimized exposure; qualified personnel only; documentation or formal control per site requirements; and restoration/verification of all guards and safeguards before return to normal production.

Routine production with guards removed or interlocks defeated is prohibited.

7.12 AUTOMATED EQUIPMENT, ROBOTIC CELLS, AND PERIMETER GUARDING

Guarding of automated or robotic equipment shall prevent personnel from entering hazard zones during automatic operation. Safeguarding includes: perimeter fencing, interlocked gates, light curtains, area scanners, safe-speed modes, setup modes with hold-to-run controls, presence sensing, restricted access zones, and visual warning indicators.

Approved access and hazardous energy control procedures are required for entry into safeguarded cells for setup, teaching, maintenance, or inspection. Collaborative systems or reduced-speed modes must have specific safety functions and allowed exposure conditions defined and controlled.

7.13 HOUSEKEEPING, CHIPS, SCRAPS, AND SECONDARY HAZARDS

Machine areas shall be maintained to support safe operation and access. Requirements include: controlled removal of chips/scrap/debris, use of brushes/vacuums/hooks rather than hands where needed, control of hot chips/sharp scrap/ejected material, slip/trip prevention, prevention of buildup interfering with guards/sensors/motion, control of cutting fluid/oil/coolant residues, and proper storage/disposal of sharp tooling.

Housekeeping is an important supporting control but does not replace guarding.

7.14 MAINTENANCE, REPAIR, AND RETURN TO SERVICE

Maintenance and repair shall be performed only by qualified personnel under proper hazardous energy control. Requirements include: approved replacement of guards/interlocks/safety components, prohibition on defeating or downgrading safeguarding during repair, reinstallation of removed panels/covers/guards, testing of safety functions before return to service, confirmation of tooling/alignment/machine condition before release, and documentation of repairs/inspections as required.

Machines shall not be returned to service with known safety deficiencies unless specifically controlled under an approved temporary condition with equivalent protection and defined corrective timeline.

7.15 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION

Company/contractor coordination is required before work begins. [Company Name] shall communicate: machine hazards, safeguarding expectations, lockout/tagout requirements, restricted-access areas, permit/approval requirements, incident reporting and stop-work expectations, and known issues related to guarding, tooling, automation, or abnormal conditions.

Contractors shall communicate: scope of work, required machine access, any guard removal/bypass/testing/energized troubleshooting expected, hazards introduced, and any incident, near miss, unsafe condition, or discovered deficiency. Coordination shall include situations where multiple employers are working nearby.

7.16 INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW

Immediate reporting is required for: guarding deficiencies, tooling failures, bypass events, unexpected startup, part ejection, near misses, amputation-risk events, equipment malfunctions, or unsafe machine conditions.

Investigation shall address: hazard assessment quality, guarding adequacy, tooling condition and selection, interlock/control function, operator actions, setup/maintenance/changeover practices, training adequacy, supervision, human factors, and need for design/procedural/training improvements.

Periodic EHS and operational review of the program shall be conducted.
emergencyResponse: Press emergency stop immediately if an entanglement, caught-in, or amputation-risk event occurs.
Do NOT attempt to reverse the machine to free a trapped worker unless specifically trained and the reversal will not worsen the injury.

Call 911 and administer first aid including bleeding control. If an amputation occurs, recover the amputated part, wrap it in clean moist material, place in a sealed bag on ice, and transport with the patient.
Preserve the scene for investigation. Do not restart the machine until authorized by supervision, EHS, and/or engineering.
Report all machine-related incidents, near misses, and unsafe conditions immediately to supervision and EHS.
If a guard, interlock, or safety device has failed, control access to the machine and do not allow operation until repaired and verified.
documentation:
APPENDIX A - MACHINE GUARDING PRE-USE CHECKLIST

[ ] Guards present and secure
[ ] Guard openings do not allow unsafe access
[ ] Interlocks or sensors intact and functional
[ ] Emergency stop devices accessible
[ ] Tooling installed correctly
[ ] Workholding devices secure
[ ] No obvious cracks, looseness, or damage
[ ] No excessive leaks, debris, or contamination affecting safe operation
[ ] Warning labels and control markings present where required
[ ] Machine appears safe to operate
[ ] Remove from service or control any machine with defects affecting safe operation

APPENDIX B - TOOLING AND WORKHOLDING CHECKLIST

[ ] Correct tooling installed for the task
[ ] Blades, bits, cutters, dies, or forming tools in acceptable condition
[ ] Tooling secured properly
[ ] Jigs, fixtures, clamps, and workholding stable and functional
[ ] Workpiece support adequate
[ ] No sharp edges, broken components, or excessive wear
[ ] Guarding remains effective with installed tooling
[ ] Additional aids such as push tools available where needed

APPENDIX C - SETUP / CHANGEOVER CHECKLIST

[ ] Hazardous motion controlled during setup
[ ] Lockout/tagout applied where required
[ ] Tooling aligned and secured
[ ] Guards, covers, and access panels reinstalled
[ ] Interlocks and sensors restored
[ ] Workholding devices correctly positioned
[ ] Trial run or verification completed safely
[ ] No loose tools, materials, or components remain in the machine
[ ] Machine safe for normal operation

APPENDIX D - JAM CLEARING AND ABNORMAL CONDITION CHECKLIST

[ ] Machine motion stopped
[ ] Hazardous energy controlled as required
[ ] Stored energy considered
[ ] Proper tools available
[ ] Hands will not enter unsafe areas without protection
[ ] Guards or interlocks will not be bypassed unless specifically authorized and controlled
[ ] Recurrent issues being reported for correction
[ ] Machine will be verified safe before restart

APPENDIX E - CONDITIONS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE STOP WORK

- Guard missing, loose, damaged, or ineffective
- Interlock, sensor, or emergency stop not functioning as intended
- Tooling cracked, loose, worn, or improperly installed
- Unexpected machine motion
- Part, chip, or fragment ejected unsafely
- Jam or abnormal condition cannot be cleared safely
- Unauthorized bypass without equivalent protection
- Supervisor, maintenance, engineering, EHS, or other responsible person stops the work
- Conditions change such that safe machine operation cannot be assured

SECTION 9 - RECORDS

The following records shall be maintained as required:
- Training and competency records
- Machine hazard assessments where required
- Guarding review records where applicable
- Inspection records where required by site policy
- Maintenance and repair records
- Bypass authorization or safeguard exception records where applicable
- Incident, near miss, and investigation records
- Corrective action records
- Contractor coordination records
- Audit findings and program review records

SECTION 10 - COMPLIANCE AND DEVIATIONS

Failure to comply with this procedure may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination and may result in serious injury or fatality. All personnel are expected to understand and follow these requirements.

Deviations from this procedure require review and approval by EHS and operational management unless emergency action is needed to protect life or health. No deviation may remove required guarding, interlocks, tooling controls, hazardous energy controls, or other critical safeguards without equivalent or greater protection.

Willful bypass of machine guards, interlocks, or safeguarding devices without authorization and equivalent protection is a serious safety violation subject to immediate corrective action.

title: Hazardous Materials SOP
summary: Establishes safe handling, storage, and spill response procedures for hazardous chemicals and materials in the workplace.

sections.purpose: To establish safe procedures for the handling, storage, use, and disposal of hazardous materials and chemicals, and to ensure compliance with hazard communication requirements.
sections.scope: Applies to all personnel who handle, store, transport, or may be exposed to hazardous chemicals and materials.

definitions.definition: Safety Data Sheet — a document providing hazard, handling, storage, and emergency information for a chemical product.
Globally Harmonized System — a standardized system for classifying and labeling chemicals.
Permissible Exposure Limit — the maximum amount of a chemical a worker can be exposed to over a work shift.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Maintain SDS library, approve new chemicals, manage inventory.
Read SDS before use, follow safe handling procedures, report spills immediately.
Ensure workers are trained, PPE is available, and storage areas are compliant.

training: HazCom / GHS training
Chemical-specific handling procedures
SDS interpretation
Spill response and cleanup
Emergency eyewash and shower use
ppe: Chemical splash goggles
Chemical-resistant gloves (material-specific)
Chemical-resistant apron or suit
Respirator (type per SDS requirements)
Face shield
Steel-toe boots
hazards: Skin or eye chemical burns
Inhalation of toxic vapors or fumes
Fire or explosion from flammable chemicals
Environmental contamination from spills
Chronic health effects from repeated exposure
procedure: Review the SDS for each chemical before handling.
Ensure proper labeling on all chemical containers — no unlabeled containers.
Use required PPE as specified by the SDS.
Store chemicals in designated areas with proper segregation.
Keep flammable materials in approved flammable storage cabinets.
Use chemicals only in well-ventilated areas or with local exhaust ventilation.
Never mix chemicals unless specifically authorized and trained.
Clean up spills immediately using proper spill kits and procedures.
Dispose of hazardous waste per local, state, and federal regulations.
Maintain an up-to-date chemical inventory and SDS library.
emergencyResponse: For chemical exposure: flush affected area with water for at least 15 minutes.
For inhalation: move to fresh air immediately — call 911 if symptoms persist.
For spills: contain the spill, prevent it from reaching drains or waterways.
Use appropriate spill kit materials (absorbents, neutralizers, etc.).
Report all spills to the supervisor and EHS department.

Chemical inventory records
Training records
Spill response reports
Exposure monitoring records

title: Hazardous Energy Control and Lockout/Tagout Program
summary: A comprehensive Hazardous Energy Control and Lockout/Tagout Program covering energy identification, machine-specific procedures, isolation, lockout/tagout application, stored energy control, verification, group LOTO, shift change, contractor coordination, temporary energization, periodic inspection, training, and program review.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, isolating, controlling, verifying, and restoring hazardous energy sources during servicing, maintenance, setup, adjustment, inspection, cleaning, troubleshooting, construction support, and other work activities where unexpected energization, startup, or release of stored energy could cause injury or damage at [Company Name].

The purpose is to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, amputation, crushing injury, electrical shock, arc flash exposure, burns, release of pressure, uncontrolled motion, equipment damage, and operational disruption resulting from failure to control hazardous energy.

Hazardous energy incidents are a leading cause of serious injuries and fatalities in general industry and construction. This program requires systematic identification, positive isolation, personal lockout/tagout, stored energy control, verification of zero energy state, training, supervision, and periodic program review to protect all personnel.

This procedure supports compliance with OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147, applicable electrical safety standards, and company policies.
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, production areas, maintenance activities, laboratories, test areas, machine shops, fabrication areas, assembly areas, utility systems, field locations, construction support activities, and non-routine operations where hazardous energy may be present and employee exposure could occur during servicing or related work at [Site Name].

This procedure applies to:
• Employees who perform servicing, maintenance, inspection, adjustment, repair, setup, cleaning, or troubleshooting
• Authorized employees who apply locks and tags
• Affected employees who operate or work near equipment under lockout/tagout
• Supervisors and managers
• EHS personnel
• Engineers, technicians, operators, maintenance personnel, and support personnel
• Contractors performing work involving hazardous energy control onsite

Hazardous energy sources covered include, but are not limited to:
• Electrical — line voltage, control circuits, stored charge, capacitors, batteries
• Mechanical — rotating, reciprocating, cutting, pressing, conveying, lifting
• Hydraulic — pressurized lines, cylinders, accumulators
• Pneumatic — compressed air, pressurized systems, air-operated equipment
• Thermal — steam, hot surfaces, heated fluids, cryogenic systems
• Chemical — reactive materials, process chemicals, corrosive fluids
• Gravity — elevated parts, suspended loads, counterweights, springs
• Pressurized systems — vessels, piping, vacuum, process lines
• Stored electrical charge — capacitors, UPS systems, battery banks
• Residual motion — flywheels, conveyors, rotating assemblies

Equipment and systems covered include, but are not limited to:
• Manufacturing equipment, test stands, pumps, compressors, conveyors, mixers, fans and blowers
• Hydraulic presses, pneumatic systems, robotic cells, CNC machines
• Material handling systems, electrical panels and control systems
• Utilities and facility systems, process piping and vessels
• Mechanical assemblies with moving parts or stored force

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with Electrical Safety, Machine Guarding and Tooling, Confined Space Entry, Suspended Loads and Lifting Operations, Permit-to-Work, Contractor Safety, and Process Safety requirements where applicable.

definitions.definition: An employee whose job requires them to operate or use a machine or equipment on which servicing or maintenance is being performed under lockout/tagout, or whose job requires them to work in an area in which such servicing or maintenance is being performed.

A person who locks out or tags out machines or equipment in order to perform servicing or maintenance on that machine or equipment. An authorized employee and an affected employee may be the same person when the authorized employee also operates the equipment.

An energy-isolating device is capable of being locked out if it has a hasp or other means of attachment to which, or through which, a lock can be affixed, or it has a locking mechanism built into it, or it can be locked without dismantling, rebuilding, or replacing the device or permanently altering its energy control capability.

A mechanical device that physically prevents the transmission or release of energy, including but not limited to a manually operated electrical circuit breaker, a disconnect switch, a manually operated valve, a block, or any similar device. Push buttons, selector switches, and other control circuit devices are NOT energy-isolating devices.
A procedure used when servicing or maintenance is performed by a crew, craft, department, or other group where multiple authorized employees apply lockout/tagout to the same equipment or system.
Any electrical, mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, chemical, thermal, gravitational, or other energy that could cause injury to personnel if unexpectedly released, activated, or started up.

The placement of a lockout device on an energy-isolating device, in accordance with an established procedure, ensuring that the energy-isolating device and the equipment being controlled cannot be operated until the lockout device is removed.
A device that uses a positive means such as a lock to hold an energy-isolating device in a safe position and prevent the energization of a machine or equipment. Includes key or combination padlocks.

A written procedure identifying the specific energy sources, isolation points, lockout/tagout steps, stored energy control methods, and verification requirements for a particular machine or piece of equipment.

Energy that remains in a system after the energy source has been isolated, including electrical charge in capacitors, pressure in hydraulic or pneumatic systems, spring tension, gravitational potential energy in elevated parts, thermal energy, and residual mechanical motion.

The placement of a tagout device on an energy-isolating device, in accordance with an established procedure, to indicate that the energy-isolating device and the equipment being controlled may not be operated until the tagout device is removed.

A prominent warning device, such as a tag and a means of attachment, that can be securely fastened to an energy-isolating device to indicate that the equipment may not be operated until the tagout device is removed.
The process of testing or checking to confirm that all energy sources have been effectively isolated and the equipment is at a zero energy state before work begins.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Administer the Hazardous Energy Control and Lockout/Tagout Program. Develop and maintain program policies, procedures, and machine-specific LOTO procedures. Coordinate and track training for authorized and affected employees. Conduct or coordinate periodic inspections and program audits. Investigate LOTO-related incidents and implement corrective actions. Review and approve alternative control methods where applicable. Maintain program records. Update the program as equipment, standards, or operations change.

Ensure employees are trained and authorized before performing LOTO. Enforce compliance with LOTO procedures. Coordinate group lockout, shift change, and contractor LOTO activities. Ensure machine-specific procedures are available and current. Notify affected employees before LOTO is applied. Verify that equipment is properly isolated and restored. Report incidents, near misses, and unsafe conditions. Support periodic inspections and audits.

Follow applicable LOTO procedures and machine-specific instructions. Identify all hazardous energy sources before work begins. Apply personal locks and tags to energy-isolating devices. Control stored and residual energy. Verify zero energy state before beginning exposed work. Maintain exclusive control of personal lockout keys. Remove personal locks and tags only when work is complete and conditions are safe. Report hazardous conditions, procedure deficiencies, and incidents.

Understand the purpose and restrictions of LOTO. Do not attempt to restart, reenergize, or operate equipment that is locked or tagged out. Follow instructions from authorized employees and supervisors regarding LOTO status. Report locked/tagged equipment that appears to be improperly controlled.

Perform servicing and maintenance under proper LOTO controls. Verify energy isolation before beginning exposed work. Use machine-specific procedures where required. Control stored energy. Participate in group LOTO and shift change procedures. Report equipment deficiencies and unsafe conditions.

Design new equipment and systems to be capable of lockout. Identify energy sources and isolation points for machine-specific procedures. Support development and revision of LOTO procedures when equipment changes. Evaluate alternative control methods where standard LOTO is not feasible. Support incident investigation and corrective actions.

Comply with [Company Name] LOTO requirements while onsite. Ensure contractor employees are trained and authorized for LOTO as required. Coordinate lockout/tagout activities with company personnel. Communicate hazards introduced by contractor work. Report incidents, near misses, and unsafe conditions to [Company Name] supervision and EHS.

Initial training for affected employees on the purpose and restrictions of LOTO
Retraining when job assignments change, equipment changes, procedures change, periodic inspections reveal deficiencies, or inadequate knowledge is observed

This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, production areas, maintenance activities, laboratories, test areas, machine shops, fabrication areas, assembly areas, utility systems, field locations, construction support activities, and non-routine operations where hazardous energy may be present at [Site Name].

Personnel covered include authorized employees, affected employees, supervisors, managers, EHS personnel, engineers, technicians, operators, maintenance personnel, support personnel, and contractors.

Hazardous energy sources include electrical, mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, thermal, chemical, gravity, spring tension, pressurized systems, stored electrical charge, rotational energy, residual motion, fluid pressure, process line pressure, vacuum systems, capacitors, batteries, and counterweights or suspended components.

• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.333 — Selection and Use of Work Practices where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.269 — Electric Power Generation, Transmission, and Distribution where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.146 — Permit-Required Confined Spaces where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910 Subpart O — Machinery and Machine Guarding where applicable
• NFPA 70E — Standard for Electrical Safety in the Workplace where applicable
• ANSI/ASSE Z244.1 — Control of Hazardous Energy where applicable
• Manufacturer instructions for machinery, electrical systems, hydraulic systems, pneumatic systems, and control equipment
• [Company Name] Electrical Safety Procedure
• [Company Name] Machine Guarding and Tooling Procedure
• [Company Name] Confined Space Entry Procedure
• [Company Name] Permit to Work Procedure
• [Company Name] Contractor Safety Management Procedure
• [Company Name] Incident Reporting and Investigation Procedure
• Site-specific machine-specific LOTO procedures and energy isolation standards
• [Applicable State / Local Requirements]
• [Related Company Procedures]

**4. DEFINITIONS**

Affected Employee: An employee whose job requires them to operate or use equipment on which servicing or maintenance is being performed under lockout/tagout, or whose job requires them to work in an area where such work is being performed.

Authorized Employee: A person who locks out or tags out machines or equipment to perform servicing or maintenance. An authorized and affected employee may be the same person.

Capable of Being Locked Out: An energy-isolating device that has a hasp or other means to which a lock can be affixed, has a built-in locking mechanism, or can be locked without dismantling or permanently altering the device.

Energy-Isolating Device: A mechanical device that physically prevents the transmission or release of energy — includes disconnect switches, circuit breakers, valves, blocks, and similar devices. Push buttons, selector switches, and control circuit devices are NOT energy-isolating devices.

Group Lockout/Tagout: A procedure used when multiple authorized employees apply lockout/tagout to the same equipment or system.

Hazardous Energy: Any electrical, mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, chemical, thermal, gravitational, or other energy that could cause injury if unexpectedly released or activated.

Lockout: Placement of a lockout device on an energy-isolating device ensuring the equipment cannot be operated until the lockout device is removed.

Lockout Device: A device using a positive means (lock) to hold an energy-isolating device in a safe position.

Machine-Specific Procedure: A written procedure identifying specific energy sources, isolation points, lockout steps, stored energy control, and verification for a particular machine.

Stored Energy: Energy remaining in a system after the source has been isolated — includes capacitor charge, hydraulic/pneumatic pressure, spring tension, gravity, thermal energy, and residual motion.

Tagout: Placement of a tagout device on an energy-isolating device to warn against operation.

Tagout Device: A prominent warning tag with attachment means that can be securely fastened to an energy-isolating device.

Tryout / Verification: Testing or checking to confirm all energy sources are effectively isolated and equipment is at zero energy state.

**5. POLICY**

[Company Name] will identify and control hazardous energy through planning, isolation, lockout/tagout, stored energy release, verification, training, supervision, and periodic review.

No servicing, maintenance, setup, adjustment, cleaning, jam clearing, inspection, testing, or other activity exposing personnel to hazardous energy may be performed unless hazardous energy has been controlled in accordance with this procedure and any required machine-specific instructions.

The company will prioritize use of positive energy isolation through lockout whenever the equipment is capable of being locked out.

Employees shall not rely solely on normal stop buttons, selector switches, software commands, interlocks, control circuit devices, or emergency stops as protection against hazardous energy unless a specific approved alternative control method is allowed by regulation and company procedure and equivalent or greater protection is demonstrated.

Employees shall not remove, bypass, ignore, defeat, or tamper with lockout/tagout devices or attempt to operate locked or tagged equipment.

Violation of LOTO requirements is a serious safety infraction that may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination.

**6. RESPONSIBILITIES**

**EHS / Program Administrator:**
Administer the Hazardous Energy Control and Lockout/Tagout Program. Develop and maintain program policies, procedures, and machine-specific procedures. Coordinate training. Conduct periodic inspections and audits. Investigate incidents and implement corrective actions. Review and approve alternative control methods. Maintain records. Update the program as needed.

**Managers and Supervisors:**
Ensure employees are trained and authorized. Enforce LOTO compliance. Coordinate group lockout, shift change, and contractor activities. Ensure machine-specific procedures are available and current. Notify affected employees. Verify proper isolation and restoration. Report incidents and support audits.

**Authorized Employees:**
Follow LOTO procedures and machine-specific instructions. Identify all energy sources. Apply personal locks and tags. Control stored energy. Verify zero energy state. Maintain exclusive key control. Remove personal locks only when work is complete. Report hazardous conditions and incidents.

**Affected Employees:**
Understand LOTO purpose and restrictions. Do not operate locked/tagged equipment. Follow supervisor instructions regarding LOTO status. Report improperly controlled equipment.

**Maintenance / Qualified Service Personnel:**
Perform work under proper LOTO controls. Verify isolation. Use machine-specific procedures. Control stored energy. Participate in group and shift change LOTO. Report deficiencies.

**Engineering / System Owners:**
Design equipment to be capable of lockout. Identify energy sources and isolation points. Support procedure development and revision. Evaluate alternative methods. Support investigations.

**Contractors:**
Comply with site LOTO requirements. Ensure employees are trained and authorized. Coordinate with company personnel. Communicate hazards. Report incidents and unsafe conditions.

**7.1 HAZARDOUS ENERGY IDENTIFICATION AND ASSESSMENT**

Machines, equipment, systems, and processes shall be evaluated before work begins to identify all hazardous energy sources.

Identification shall consider:
• All primary and secondary energy sources
• Normal operating conditions and control configurations
• Stored energy — capacitors, accumulators, springs, elevated parts, pressurized lines
• Interconnected systems and multiple feed points
• Gravity, suspended parts, and counterweights
• Pressurized vessels, piping, and vacuum systems
• Remote or automatic startup capability
• Interlocks that may create false confidence
• Need for bleed-off, venting, blocking, blanking, or dissipation
• Need for machine-specific procedure
• Number of employees involved and need for group LOTO
• Temporary energization or testing needs

Work shall not proceed until adequate information, engineering support, or procedure review is completed if all hazardous energy sources cannot be clearly identified and isolated.

**7.2 HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS FOR HAZARDOUS ENERGY**

The preferred order of control is:
1. Eliminate the need for exposure through design or planning
2. Use positive energy-isolating devices capable of lockout
3. Release, block, restrain, or dissipate stored energy
4. Verify zero energy state before exposure
5. Use administrative controls — procedures, permits, coordination, supervision — as supplemental controls
6. Use PPE as a supporting measure, not as a substitute for isolation

Exposure to hazardous energy should be eliminated whenever feasible before personnel enter a hazard zone.

[Company Name] will prioritize positive isolation and lockout over administrative controls or operator vigilance.

**7.3 MACHINE-SPECIFIC LOTO PROCEDURES**

Written machine-specific procedures are required where regulation, risk, complexity, or site policy requires them.

Machine-specific procedures shall include:
• Equipment name, identifier, and location
• All energy sources — type, magnitude, and location
• Each isolation point and the corresponding energy-isolating device
• Shutdown sequence
• Lockout device locations and requirements
• Tagout requirements
• Stored energy control methods — bleeding, venting, blocking, draining, discharging, restraining
• Verification steps — tryout method, test instruments, indicators
• Restart and release sequence
• Special precautions — temperature cool-down, chemical drainage, multiple feeds, interlocked systems
• Group lockout steps if applicable

Simple equipment may use a documented simplified procedure only when:
• The equipment has a single energy source that can be readily identified and isolated
• The lockout completely deenergizes and deactivates the equipment
• The equipment is isolated from the energy source and locked out during servicing
• A single lockout device achieves full isolation
• The lockout does not create hazards for other employees
• There is no history of incidents or near misses related to the equipment

Procedures shall be updated when equipment changes, isolation points change, incidents/near misses occur, inspections identify deficiencies, or process modifications affect energy control.

**7.4 PREPARATION FOR SHUTDOWN**

Before lockout/tagout is applied, the authorized employee shall:
• Review the applicable machine-specific procedure or general LOTO procedure
• Identify all hazardous energy sources and isolation points
• Identify all affected employees and notify them of the planned lockout
• Gather required locks, tags, hasps, blocks, blanks, and other hardware
• Confirm the scope of work and expected duration
• Coordinate with operations, supervision, and other work groups as needed
• Determine whether group lockout, shift change, or contractor coordination is required

Work shall not proceed until all necessary personnel understand the isolation scope, their responsibilities, and the planned sequence.

**7.5 EQUIPMENT SHUTDOWN**

Machines or equipment shall be shut down using normal stopping procedures before energy isolation is applied, unless the equipment and procedure require a different sequence for safety.

Shutdown includes:
• Operating stop controls in the proper sequence
• Confirming moving parts have come to a complete stop
• Allowing safe completion of operating cycles where applicable
• Disconnecting or closing feeds, supplies, and utilities as required

Shutdown alone does not constitute hazardous energy control. Energy isolation and lockout/tagout must follow.

**7.6 ISOLATION OF ENERGY SOURCES**

Each hazardous energy source shall be isolated using appropriate energy-isolating devices.

Isolation methods include:
• Opening and locking electrical disconnects in the OFF position
• Racking out circuit breakers where applicable
• Closing and locking valves in closed position
• Applying blanks, blinds, or spectacle blinds to piping
• Double block and bleed for process lines where required
• Disconnecting or misaligning lines
• Mechanically blocking motion — pins, blocks, chocks, braces
• Securing elevated components against falling
• Pinning or chocking moving parts
• Disconnecting battery sources where required
• Discharging capacitors using approved methods
• Depressurizing hydraulic or pneumatic systems
• Draining, venting, or purging process systems

Control circuit devices, software stop functions, or selector switches are NOT substitutes for energy isolation unless specifically allowed under an approved alternative control method with equivalent or greater protection.

**7.7 APPLICATION OF LOCKOUT AND TAGOUT DEVICES**

Each authorized employee exposed to the hazard shall apply a personal lock and tag to the energy-isolating device or group lockout device as applicable.

Requirements:
• Locks shall be individually assigned and uniquely identifiable to the authorized employee
• Tags shall identify the employee, date, and warn against operation
• Locks and tags shall be durable and standardized as required by site policy
• Locks shall hold the energy-isolating device in a safe/off/isolated position
• Only lockout-rated devices shall be used

Tagout-only methods are permitted only where lockout is not possible and applicable additional protections are implemented per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147.

Employees shall maintain exclusive control of their assigned lockout keys. Keys shall not be loaned, shared, or left accessible to others.

**7.8 CONTROL OF STORED OR RESIDUAL ENERGY**

All stored, residual, or secondary energy shall be controlled before exposure begins.

Methods include:
• Releasing pressure from lines, vessels, accumulators, and cylinders
• Bleeding hydraulic or pneumatic systems
• Discharging capacitors using approved methods and equipment
• Allowing hot surfaces to cool where required
• Releasing spring tension
• Blocking or pinning elevated parts
• Draining liquids from lines and vessels
• Venting gas from closed systems
• Waiting for rotating parts to stop fully
• Verifying vacuum release
• Restraining parts against movement

Stored energy may reaccumulate during the work activity. Ongoing monitoring or repeated control measures may be necessary, and employees shall remain aware of reaccumulation potential.

**7.9 VERIFICATION OF ISOLATION / ZERO ENERGY STATE**

Verification is mandatory before work begins and may not be skipped.

Verification methods include:
• Attempting to start the equipment using normal operating controls (tryout)
• Testing for absence of voltage using properly rated test instruments (electrical hazards)
• Checking pressure gauges, indicators, and bleed points
• Observing bleed or vent completion
• Confirming blocked or restrained parts remain stable
• Verifying lines are drained, disconnected, or blanked
• Confirming zero mechanical movement — no rotation, reciprocation, or drift

After tryout, controls shall be returned to the neutral or off position.

For electrical hazards, absence-of-voltage testing shall follow the [Company Name] Electrical Safety Procedure, NFPA 70E, and applicable standards. Test instruments shall be verified before and after use.

**7.10 GROUP LOCKOUT/TAGOUT**

Group LOTO methods shall be used when multiple authorized employees work on the same equipment or system.

Methods include:
• Multiple locks applied directly to energy-isolating devices
• Hasps allowing multiple locks on a single isolation point
• Lock boxes securing keys from isolation locks
• Group isolation boards or stations
• Primary authorized employee coordination

Requirements:
• A primary authorized employee or coordinator shall be designated
• Each exposed employee shall apply a personal lock before beginning work and remove it when their work is complete
• Clear communication of isolation scope, responsibilities, and status to all involved
• Equivalent protection for each employee — no employee shall be exposed without personal lockout protection
• Documentation or tracking as required by site policy

**7.11 SHIFT CHANGE AND PERSONNEL CHANGES**

When LOTO extends across shifts or personnel change during the work:
• Incoming personnel shall apply their personal locks before outgoing personnel remove theirs
• Work and isolation status, hazard information, and scope shall be communicated during handoff
• Group lock transition coordination shall ensure continuous protection
• Confirmation that all energy sources remain properly isolated

Gaps in protection during shift handoff are prohibited. At no time shall all locks be removed while exposed work continues or may resume.

**7.12 OUTSIDE PERSONNEL / CONTRACTOR COORDINATION**

Coordination is required before work begins when contractors and company employees work on the same equipment or in the same area.

Coordination shall address:
• Scope of work and expected duration
• All energy sources and isolation points
• Shutdown and restoration responsibilities
• Use of locks, tags, hasps, group methods, and lock boxes
• Communication of LOTO status and work progress
• Verification expectations
• Temporary energization/testing needs
• Shift change or extended project duration requirements

Each employer shall ensure its employees are protected and understand the coordinated arrangement. Contractors shall not rely solely on the host employer's LOTO without personal lockout protection where required.

**7.13 TEMPORARY RELEASE OF LOCKOUT FOR TESTING OR POSITIONING**

Temporary energization or repositioning is allowed only under controlled conditions for testing, alignment, positioning, or troubleshooting.

Procedure:
1. Clear all tools, materials, and non-essential items from the machine
2. Ensure all employees are safely positioned clear of hazard zones
3. Remove locks and tags only per the controlled procedure
4. Energize only for the minimum duration necessary
5. Perform the required testing, positioning, or observation
6. Deenergize, reisolate, and reapply lockout/tagout
7. Reverify zero energy state before continuing exposed work

Routine repeated energization without proper hazard review for each cycle is prohibited.

Only authorized personnel shall participate in temporary energization procedures.

**7.14 REMOVAL OF LOCKOUT/TAGOUT AND RETURN TO SERVICE**

Before removing locks and tags:
• Verify that work is complete or at a safe stopping point
• Confirm that guards and safety devices are reinstalled
• Remove all tools, materials, and temporary equipment from the machine
• Verify that the equipment is intact and ready for operation
• Confirm that all employees are safely positioned or accounted for
• Notify affected employees that LOTO is being removed and equipment is returning to service

Each authorized employee shall remove their own lock and tag.

After lock removal:
• Restore energy in a controlled manner following the established sequence
• Observe for abnormal conditions during initial operation where appropriate
• Confirm that the equipment is operating normally before releasing to production

**7.15 REMOVAL OF ANOTHER EMPLOYEE'S LOCK**

A lock or tag shall only be removed by the employee who applied it except under a formal lock removal exception procedure approved by site management.

The exception procedure requires:
• Verification that the employee is not onsite or available
• Reasonable efforts to contact the employee
• Authorization by management or designated responsible authority
• Verification that the work area is safe and the employee is not at risk
• Documentation of the removal, including reason, authorization, and verification steps
• Notification to the employee before they resume work, if possible

Unauthorized removal of another person's lock or tag is strictly prohibited and is a serious safety violation.

**7.16 SPECIAL SITUATIONS AND ALTERNATIVE CONTROL METHODS**

Certain activities may require special consideration:
• Minor tool changes or minor adjustments during normal production operations that are routine, repetitive, and integral to the use of the equipment, provided the work is performed using alternative measures that provide effective protection
• Plug-and-cord connected equipment under exclusive control of the employee who unplugged it
• Testing or diagnostic tasks requiring energized conditions under controlled procedures
• Specialized electrical work governed by the Electrical Safety Procedure and NFPA 70E
• Process systems requiring engineered alternatives to traditional LOTO

Any alternative control method shall be:
• Specifically evaluated for the task and equipment
• Documented where required
• Demonstrated to provide equivalent or greater protection
• Approved by responsible supervision and, where required, EHS or engineering

Alternative methods shall not be used as shortcuts around proper LOTO.

**7.17 TAGS, HARDWARE, AND LOTO EQUIPMENT CONTROL**

Lockout/tagout devices and related hardware shall be:
• Standardized by size, shape, color, or format as required by site policy
• Durable enough to withstand the environment in which they are used
• Clearly legible with warning language prohibiting equipment operation
• Used only for lockout/tagout purposes unless a site system allows otherwise with equal clarity
• Maintained in usable condition and inspected periodically

Damaged or illegible devices shall be removed from service and replaced.

Lockout keys and devices shall not be loaned, shared, or misused in any way that compromises hazardous energy control.

**7.18 PERIODIC INSPECTIONS AND PROGRAM AUDITS**

Periodic inspections of the program and selected machine-specific procedures shall be conducted at required intervals (at least annually per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147).

Inspections shall evaluate:
• Employee understanding of LOTO requirements and their responsibilities
• Accuracy and completeness of machine-specific procedures
• Proper use of locks, tags, and hardware
• Verification practices
• Group LOTO coordination effectiveness
• Shift change control adequacy
• Contractor coordination practices
• Need for procedure updates or equipment modifications

Deficiencies shall be corrected promptly.

Periodic EHS and operational management review of the overall program shall be conducted to verify effectiveness and regulatory compliance.

**7.19 INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW**

Immediate reporting is required for:
• Hazardous energy exposure or unexpected energization
• Unexpected startup during servicing
• Isolation failure or verification failure
• Missing, damaged, or compromised lockout device
• Unauthorized restart or removal of LOTO devices
• Near misses involving hazardous energy
• Injuries related to hazardous energy
• Unsafe conditions related to LOTO

Investigation shall address:
• Hazard identification adequacy
• Procedure quality and accuracy
• Isolation effectiveness
• Verification methods used
• Stored energy control adequacy
• Group lock coordination
• Training adequacy
• Supervision and planning
• Human factors and shortcut behavior
• Need for engineering, procedural, or training improvements

Findings and corrective actions shall be documented and tracked to completion.

**8. TRAINING REQUIREMENTS**

Training is required for:
• Authorized employees — before performing LOTO
• Affected employees — on the purpose and restrictions of LOTO
• Other employees who work in areas where LOTO is used
• Supervisors and managers
• Maintenance personnel
• Contractors as required by site controls

Training topics:
• Recognition of hazardous energy sources
• Purpose and scope of the LOTO program
• Difference between authorized and affected employee responsibilities
• Use of machine-specific procedures
• Lockout and tagout device application
• Stored energy control
• Verification of zero energy state
• Group lockout and shift change requirements
• Contractor coordination
• Temporary energization/testing controls
• Restrictions on starting or using locked/tagged equipment
• Lock removal exception procedures
• Incident and near-miss reporting requirements
• Stop-work authority

**9. RECORDS**

The following records shall be maintained:
• Training and competency records for authorized and affected employees
• Machine-specific LOTO procedures where required
• Periodic inspection records (at least annual)
• Program audit records
• Incident, near miss, and investigation records
• Corrective action records
• Contractor coordination records
• Alternative control method documentation where applicable
• Lock removal exception records where applicable
• Program review records

**10. COMPLIANCE AND DEVIATIONS**

All employees and contractors shall comply with the requirements of this Hazardous Energy Control and Lockout/Tagout Program. Failure to comply may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination and may create serious injury or fatality risk.

Deviations from this procedure require review and approval by EHS and operational management unless emergency action is needed to protect life or health.

No deviation may remove required energy isolation, personal lock application, stored energy control, verification, or other critical safeguards without equivalent or greater protection.

Emergency deviations shall be documented and reviewed as soon as practicable after the event.

**11. APPENDICES**

**APPENDIX A — LOTO PRE-JOB PLANNING CHECKLIST**

☐ Equipment and scope of work identified
☐ All hazardous energy sources known
☐ Machine-specific procedure available if required
☐ Isolation points identified
☐ Required locks, tags, hasps, and devices available
☐ Affected employees notified
☐ Stored energy sources considered
☐ Group lockout, shift change, or contractor coordination needs addressed
☐ Verification method understood
☐ Temporary energization needs identified and planned if applicable

**APPENDIX B — LOCKOUT/TAGOUT APPLICATION CHECKLIST**

☐ Equipment shut down using normal stopping procedures
☐ All energy-isolating devices operated to safe/off/isolated position
☐ Personal locks and tags applied to each isolation point
☐ Stored energy released, blocked, restrained, or dissipated
☐ Verification / tryout completed — attempted to start using normal controls
☐ Controls returned to neutral/off after tryout
☐ Zero energy state confirmed
☐ Work area safe to begin servicing

**APPENDIX C — GROUP LOTO / SHIFT CHANGE CHECKLIST**

☐ Responsible coordinator identified
☐ All exposed employees applied personal locks
☐ Isolation scope understood by all involved
☐ Lock box or hasp control clear
☐ Shift change handoff completed — incoming locks applied before outgoing locks removed
☐ Continuous protection maintained at all times
☐ Communication documented or otherwise controlled as required

**APPENDIX D — RELEASE FROM LOCKOUT CHECKLIST**

☐ Work complete or at safe stopping point
☐ Employees clear of hazard zones and accounted for
☐ Guards and safety devices reinstalled
☐ Tools and materials removed from machine and work area
☐ Equipment intact and ready for service
☐ Each employee removed their own lock and tag
☐ Affected employees notified of return to service
☐ Controlled restart can proceed safely

**APPENDIX E — CONDITIONS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE STOP WORK**

☐ Energy sources cannot be clearly identified
☐ An isolation point cannot be controlled as planned
☐ Stored energy cannot be safely released or restrained
☐ Verification cannot be completed
☐ A lock, tag, or key control issue is identified
☐ A lockout device is missing, damaged, or compromised
☐ Unexpected motion, pressure, voltage, or other energy is detected
☐ Affected personnel attempt to operate locked/tagged equipment
☐ Group or shift handoff protection is uncertain
☐ A supervisor, EHS, or other responsible person stops the work
☐ Conditions change such that safe hazardous energy control cannot be assured
