title: Pressurized Systems Safety Program
summary: A comprehensive Pressurized Systems Safety Program covering hazard identification, pressure boundary integrity, pressurization and depressurization controls, hydrostatic and pneumatic testing, keep-out zones, line breaking, hose and whip hazard controls, relief device management, restraint and barrier requirements, contractor coordination, incident reporting, and program review.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, evaluating, controlling, and safely managing hazards associated with pressurized systems, stored pressure, pressure testing, pressurized components, and related operations at [Company Name].

The purpose is to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, struck-by incidents, lacerations, hearing damage, blast or overpressure exposure, component rupture, hose whip, line failure, projectile hazards, chemical release, cryogenic release, equipment damage, property damage, and operational disruption resulting from unexpected release of stored pressure, failure of pressurized components, improper testing methods, inadequate restraints, poor isolation, inadequate pressure relief, defective hardware, incorrect assembly, or unsafe employee positioning.

Pressurized system incidents can cause catastrophic injuries with extremely short reaction times. This program requires systematic hazard identification, engineering review, pressure boundary integrity, controlled pressurization, isolation and depressurization, barriers and keep-out zones, restraints, verification, training, supervision, and periodic program review to protect all personnel.

This procedure supports compliance with applicable OSHA standards, ASME codes, CGA guidance, industry standards, and company policies.
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, laboratories, production areas, fabrication areas, maintenance activities, field locations, test areas, utilities, assembly areas, ground support systems, process systems, and non-routine operations involving pressurized systems or exposure to stored pressure hazards.

It applies to:
• Employees who work on, around, or with pressurized systems
• Engineers, technicians, operators, assemblers, mechanics, and maintenance personnel
• Supervisors and managers responsible for pressurized system work
• EHS personnel
• Pressure test personnel
• Inspectors and quality personnel involved in pressure-related activities
• Contractors performing work involving pressurized systems onsite

This procedure applies to pressurized systems and components including, but not limited to:
• Pressure vessels
• Piping systems
• Hoses and tubing
• Regulators
• Relief devices
• Accumulators
• Tanks and cylinders
• Hydraulic systems
• Pneumatic systems
• Gas distribution systems
• Cryogenic systems
• Vacuum systems where collapse or release hazards exist
• Manifolds
• Test articles
• Quick disconnects and fittings
• Valve assemblies
• Instrumentation lines
• Pumps and compressors
• Pressurized tooling or fixtures
• Ground support equipment and test stands
• Temporary pressurized assemblies
• Pressure test setups

This procedure applies to activities including, but not limited to:
• Normal operation of pressurized systems
• Assembly and installation
• Pressure testing and leak testing
• Charging and pressurization
• Depressurization and venting
• Purging and inerting
• Disconnection or line breaking
• Troubleshooting, inspection, and maintenance
• Modification and rework
• Transportation or movement of pressurized equipment where hazard exists

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with other required programs such as Hazardous Energy / LOTO, Asphyxiants, Confined Space Entry, Suspended Loads and Lifting Operations, Hazard Communication, Respiratory Protection, Electrical Safety, Permit-to-Work, and Contractor Safety requirements where applicable.

definitions.definition: The pressure at which a component, vessel, hose, tube, or fitting is expected to fail catastrophically. Components must never be used at or near burst pressure during normal operations or testing.
A system, component, or assembly that contains stored pressure from gas, liquid, or other media and remains pressurized whether actively supplied or isolated.
The rupture, separation, cracking, deformation, or loss of integrity of a pressurized component, resulting in uncontrolled release of pressure, media, or fragments.
The controlled or uncontrolled release of pressure from a system, component, or assembly to bring it to a safe, zero-pressure state before work begins.

A pressure test conducted using a liquid medium, typically water, to verify the integrity of a pressure boundary. Hydrostatic tests store significantly less energy than pneumatic tests at equivalent pressures.
A defined area around a pressurized system, test setup, or hazard zone from which non-essential personnel are excluded to reduce exposure to rupture, blast, whip, spray, or projectile hazards.
The intentional opening, disconnection, or disassembly of a system that contains or may contain pressure, residual media, or stored energy, exposing personnel to potential release hazards.
The maximum pressure at which a pressure vessel, system, or component is rated or certified to operate under normal service conditions at a designated temperature.
The physical envelope of components — including vessels, piping, fittings, hoses, seals, gaskets, and closures — that contains the pressurized medium and prevents uncontrolled release.
A device designed to open automatically at a set pressure to relieve excess pressure and prevent overpressurization of a system or component. Includes relief valves, rupture discs, and burst discs.

A controlled procedure in which a system, component, or assembly is subjected to a specified pressure to verify structural integrity, leak tightness, or performance. Includes hydrostatic, pneumatic, and proof tests.

Any assembly of components — including vessels, piping, hoses, fittings, valves, regulators, and instruments — designed to contain, convey, control, or release fluids or gases under pressure above atmospheric.

A pressure test conducted using a gas medium such as air, nitrogen, or helium. Pneumatic tests store significantly more energy than hydrostatic tests at equivalent pressures and require additional controls.

Pressure retained within a system, component, line, accumulator, cylinder, cavity, or dead leg after isolation from the supply source. Stored pressure can remain hazardous even after the supply is disconnected.
A component, assembly, system, or hardware item that is subjected to a pressure test to verify integrity, performance, or acceptance criteria.

The hazard created when a pressurized hose, tube, line, or flexible connection separates or fails under pressure, causing the freed section to move rapidly and violently, potentially striking personnel or equipment.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Develop and maintain this program. Establish pressure hazard assessment requirements and keep-out zone standards. Review and approve controls for higher-risk pressurized work and pressure testing. Verify training, inspection, and compliance programs. Support incident investigation and corrective action. Conduct periodic program audits and reviews. Provide technical guidance on pressure hazards, barrier requirements, and safe work practices.

Ensure personnel under their direction understand and comply with this procedure. Verify that hazard assessments, isolation, barriers, keep-out zones, and controls are in place before work begins. Authorize pressure-related work, line breaks, and pressure tests as required. Stop work when conditions are unsafe or controls are inadequate. Ensure only trained and authorized personnel perform pressurized system work. Report incidents, failures, leaks, and near misses. Support training, planning, and program improvements.

Ensure pressurized systems are designed, installed, modified, and maintained in compliance with applicable codes and this procedure. Specify pressure ratings, materials, components, relief provisions, and test requirements. Support hazard assessments and procedure development for pressure testing and non-routine pressurized work. Review temporary pressurized assemblies and test setups. Support incident investigations and engineering corrective actions as needed.

Follow all requirements of this procedure. Inspect components, fittings, hoses, and gauges before use. Operate pressurized systems only within rated limits and approved procedures. Maintain keep-out zones during pressurization. Stop work and report abnormal conditions, leaks, movement, noise, or equipment concerns. Participate in pre-job briefings, hazard assessments, and training. Do not improvise pressurized assemblies or bypass controls.

Verify isolation and depressurization before maintenance, disassembly, or line breaking. Inspect pressure boundaries, relief devices, gauges, hoses, fittings, and support hardware. Replace damaged or questionable components with approved replacements. Verify reassembly integrity and conduct leak checks or pressure checks as required before return to service. Report deficiencies and support corrective actions.

Remain clear of keep-out zones and pressurized hazard areas unless authorized. Follow warnings, barriers, and access restrictions. Report unsafe conditions, leaks, abnormal sounds, or missing controls. Do not operate, adjust, or tamper with pressurized systems or equipment without authorization and training.

Comply with this procedure and all applicable site safety requirements. Communicate scope of pressure-related work, test methods, pressures, and hazards introduced. Use components, test equipment, and methods appropriate for the service conditions and approved by the host employer where required. Report all incidents, leaks, failures, near misses, and unsafe conditions to the host employer. Participate in coordination meetings and pre-job briefings.

training: Recognition of pressurized system hazards — pressure release, rupture, whip, projectile, overpressure, spray, and cryogenic exposure
Stored pressure and line-of-fire hazards — understanding that stored energy can remain after isolation and that personnel positioning is critical
Difference between hydrostatic and pneumatic testing — energy levels, risk differences, and when each method is appropriate
Isolation and depressurization requirements — valves, disconnects, lockout/tagout, venting, bleeding, gauge verification, and dead leg awareness
Pressure test planning and approval requirements — procedures, engineering review, test setup, acceptance criteria, and stop-work triggers
Barrier, restraint, and keep-out zone expectations — defining hazard boundaries, barricade use, remote pressurization, and personnel exclusion
Hose, fitting, tubing, and whip hazard controls — inspection, routing, restraints, whip checks, and replacement criteria
Relief device, gauge, and pressure control equipment awareness — function, limitations, prohibition on tampering, and reporting defects
Line breaking and opening pressurized systems — verification, PPE, positioning, controlled loosening, and stop-work triggers
Pressurization and charging operations — incremental approach, monitoring, fitting verification, and abnormal condition response
Leak and abnormal condition response — stop pressurization, depressurize remotely if possible, clear the area, report, and investigate
Component compatibility and selection — pressure rating, temperature, medium, thread and connection compatibility, and prohibition on unrated or damaged parts
Incident and near-miss reporting requirements
Stop-work authority — any person may stop work when pressurized system safety cannot be assured
ppe: Face shield or safety glasses with side shields as required by the hazard assessment
Hearing protection where pressure release, venting, or testing creates noise exposure
Chemical-resistant gloves appropriate for the medium and temperature
Cryogenic gloves where cryogenic exposure exists
Hard hat where overhead hazards, falling objects, or component failure risk exists
Steel-toe or safety-toe boots
FR clothing where flammable media, hot systems, or arc flash hazards exist
Respiratory protection where toxic, oxygen-deficient, or asphyxiant atmospheres may result from release — managed under the Respiratory Protection Program
Body protection or blast protection as specified by hazard assessment for high-energy pressure testing
Additional PPE as determined by task-specific hazard assessment
hazards: Catastrophic vessel, fitting, or component rupture producing blast, overpressure, and fragmentation
High-pressure fluid injection injuries from leaks at fittings, hoses, or seals
Hose whip or tube separation striking personnel or equipment
Projectile hazards from failed closures, caps, plugs, fittings, or end connections
Chemical release, toxic exposure, or asphyxiant displacement from pressurized gas or liquid systems
Cryogenic burns, frostbite, or rapid gas expansion from cryogenic system failure
Burns from steam, hot fluids, or hot pressurized surfaces
Noise and hearing damage from high-pressure venting, relief activation, or rupture events
Slip, trip, and contamination hazards from leaked fluids, oils, or cryogenic liquids
Oxygen-deficient atmospheres from venting inert gases in enclosed areas
Stored pressure in isolated sections, dead legs, accumulators, or cavities creating unexpected release
Reaction forces, movement, or instability of pressurized assemblies during charging or testing
Incompatible components, improvised assemblies, or unrated hardware used under pressure
procedure:
7.1 PRESSURE HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND ASSESSMENT

Before work involving pressurized systems begins, the task, system, and environment shall be evaluated to identify hazards and determine required controls.

Evaluation shall include:
• Pressure level, energy content, type of medium, and system volume
• Component rating and compatibility — pressure, temperature, material, and medium
• Test or operating pressure relative to design limits
• Failure consequences — fragment, overpressure, whip, spray, toxic release, or cryogenic exposure
• Hose and tube routing, fitting type, and connection integrity
• Pressure relief provisions and isolation points
• Stored residual pressure in lines, cavities, accumulators, or dead legs
• Need for barriers, restraints, shields, remote pressurization, or remote monitoring
• Exposure of adjacent personnel, equipment, or operations
• Environmental conditions, simultaneous operations, and access needs
• Whether hydrostatic or pneumatic methods are being considered
• Whether line breaking, disassembly, or maintenance after pressurization is involved

If the hazard cannot be adequately understood or controlled, the operation shall not proceed until engineering support, procedure review, or alternative methods are established.

7.2 HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS FOR PRESSURIZED SYSTEMS

Controls shall be applied in the following preferred order:

1. Eliminate the pressure hazard or avoid exposure through design or planning
2. Use lower-energy methods, such as hydrostatic testing instead of pneumatic testing, where feasible
3. Use pressure-rated components, relief devices, barriers, restraints, and engineered controls
4. Use remote pressurization, remote monitoring, and keep-out zones to separate personnel from hazards
5. Use administrative controls such as written procedures, permits, communication, and supervision as supplemental controls
6. Use PPE as a supporting measure, not as a substitute for pressure control and separation

Personnel exposure to the pressurized hazard zone shall be minimized or eliminated whenever feasible. No person shall be placed in the line of fire when lower-risk alternatives exist.

7.3 PRESSURIZED SYSTEM DESIGN AND COMPONENT COMPATIBILITY

Only components suitable for intended pressure, medium, temperature, and service conditions shall be used.

Considerations shall include:
• Pressure rating and maximum allowable working pressure
• Temperature rating and thermal effects
• Material compatibility with the medium
• Thread, connection, and fitting compatibility
• Seal and gasket suitability
• Vibration, movement, and fatigue considerations
• Support, restraint, and routing needs
• Manufacturer limitations and instructions

Unrated, damaged, modified, or incompatible components are prohibited. Temporary assemblies and test setups shall receive additional review to verify component selection, assembly integrity, and restraint adequacy.

7.4 WORK PLANNING AND AUTHORIZATION

Before work involving pressurized systems begins, adequate planning shall be completed.

Planning shall include:
• Scope of work and system/component identification
• Pressure source and medium
• Normal operating pressure and maximum planned pressure
• Isolation points and venting/depressurization methods
• Required barriers, restraints, and keep-out zone boundaries
• Permits, approvals, and engineering review needs
• Communication methods and emergency actions
• Simultaneous operations and adjacent hazards
• Post-work return-to-service requirements

Additional controls may include JSA, task risk assessment, permit to work, documented test procedure, supervisor approval, EHS review, engineering review, and pre-job briefing. Higher-risk or non-routine pressurized work shall not proceed without adequate planning and authorization.

7.5 ISOLATION AND DEPRESSURIZATION BEFORE WORK

Before maintenance, disassembly, line breaking, or other intrusive work, the system shall be isolated and depressurized.

Isolation methods shall include:
• Closing and locking valves
• Disconnecting pressure sources
• Double block and bleed
• Blanking or blinding
• Disconnecting regulators or supply cylinders
• Lockout/tagout of compressors, pumps, and pressure sources
• Mechanical blocking where movement hazards exist

Depressurization shall include:
• Controlled venting through approved vent paths
• Draining liquid media
• Bleeding trapped pressure in lines, hoses, accumulators, and dead legs
• Gauge verification of zero pressure
• Checking cavities, low points, and isolated sections for residual pressure

Employees shall not assume a system is depressurized based solely on shutdown. Verification is mandatory before opening any pressure boundary.

7.6 LINE BREAKING AND OPENING PRESSURIZED SYSTEMS

Opening a pressurized system or one that may retain residual pressure is a hazardous activity requiring strict controls.

Requirements include:
• Confirmation of isolation and depressurization
• Identification of contents and hazards
• Verification of venting and draining
• Appropriate PPE for the medium and conditions
• Positioning personnel out of the line of fire of fittings, flanges, and closures
• Cautious loosening of connections where residual pressure is possible
• Shielding or controlled methods where residual pressure is suspected
• Exclusion zones where needed to protect adjacent personnel

Work must stop immediately if pressure, product release, or unexpected energy is detected during opening.

7.7 PRESSURIZATION AND CHARGING OPERATIONS

Pressurization shall be performed slowly and in a controlled manner using appropriate equipment and procedures.

Before introducing pressure:
• Verify that components, fittings, and closures are correctly installed
• Confirm gauges and control devices are appropriate and functional
• Verify relief devices are installed where required
• Ensure the system or test article is restrained or supported as needed
• Establish keep-out zones before pressure is introduced

During pressurization:
• Increase pressure incrementally where appropriate
• Monitor for leaks, abnormal noise, movement, or pressure instability
• Stop immediately if abnormal conditions occur

Personnel shall not stand in front of pressurized closures, fittings, regulators, hoses, or likely failure points during charging.

7.8 PRESSURE TESTING — GENERAL REQUIREMENTS

Pressure testing shall be performed only under an approved procedure or controlled work instruction.

The test plan shall include:
• Test article identification
• Test medium — liquid or gas
• Test pressure and pressurization rate
• Hold duration and acceptance criteria
• Relief and control devices
• Gauge requirements and placement
• Barrier and keep-out zone requirements
• Personnel assignments and communication
• Leak detection method
• Controlled depressurization steps
• Stop-work triggers

Pressure tests shall use the lowest hazard method feasible. Pneumatic tests require additional controls because compressed gas stores significantly more energy than liquid at equivalent pressures.

7.9 HYDROSTATIC TESTING

Hydrostatic testing shall be used where feasible and appropriate.

Requirements include:
• Approved liquid test medium compatible with the system
• Removal or control of trapped air where possible to minimize stored gas energy
• Suitable pressure measurement equipment with adequate range and resolution
• Gradual, controlled pressurization to the specified test pressure
• Monitoring for leaks, movement, deformation, or abnormal behavior
• Controls for splash, slip, and contamination from test fluid
• Controlled depressurization and draining after the test

Even during hydrostatic testing, personnel shall remain clear of potential failure points and rupture zones. Hydrostatic tests are preferred over pneumatic tests because the stored energy at equivalent pressures is substantially lower.

7.10 PNEUMATIC TESTING

Pneumatic testing is allowed only when justified by system design, cleanliness requirements, functional requirements, or other valid technical reason, and when approved controls are in place.

Additional controls for pneumatic testing shall include:
• Formal written procedure
• Engineering review where required by site policy or risk level
• Defined keep-out zone appropriate for the energy level
• Remote pressurization where feasible
• Remote monitoring where feasible
• Barriers or shielding where appropriate for the hazard
• Secure restraint of hoses, fittings, and the test article
• Incremental pressurization with hold points for leak checks and observation
• Restricted personnel access — authorized test personnel only in controlled areas
• Clear stop-work triggers

Compressed gas shall not be used casually for integrity testing where lower-hazard methods are practical.

7.11 KEEP-OUT ZONES, BARRIERS, AND PERSONNEL POSITIONING

Keep-out zones shall be established whenever pressurized work or testing creates rupture, projectile, whip, spray, overpressure, or release hazards.

Requirements include:
• Defining the hazard boundary before work begins based on pressure, volume, medium, and failure potential
• Use of barricades, cones, hard barriers, tape, signage, or physical separation as appropriate
• Restricting access to authorized personnel only
• Positioning personnel outside the predicted line of fire and failure path
• Avoiding placement of personnel in front of closures, caps, plugs, regulators, fittings, flanges, or end connections

Keep-out zone size shall be reevaluated when pressure, volume, media, or setup changes. Personnel shall remain out of the hazard zone during active pressurization unless specific approved procedures and adequate protection allow limited access.

7.12 HOSES, TUBING, FITTINGS, AND WHIP HAZARD CONTROLS

Hoses, tubes, and flexible connections shall be treated as severe hazards if they separate or fail under pressure.

Controls shall include:
• Use of service-rated components appropriate for the pressure, medium, and temperature
• Inspection before each use for cuts, abrasion, bulging, cracking, corrosion, or degradation
• Routing to minimize personnel exposure and physical damage
• Securing or restraining lines where whip could occur
• Use of whip checks or other retention devices where appropriate and required by site policy
• Avoiding kinks, twists, abrasion points, and sharp bends
• Keeping personnel out of the line of fire during pressurized operations
• Prompt replacement of damaged or degraded flexible components

Quick disconnects and temporary connections require additional caution — verify secure engagement and compatibility before pressurization.

7.13 RELIEF DEVICES, GAUGES, AND PRESSURE CONTROL EQUIPMENT

Pressure relief and control devices shall be appropriate for the system and maintained in functional condition.

Requirements include:
• Properly rated regulators and pressure control devices for the intended service
• Relief devices installed where overpressure is possible
• Prohibition on isolating a pressure boundary from required relief protection unless specifically controlled and approved
• Suitable gauges positioned for safe reading
• Verification of relief device setpoints where required by policy or code
• Prohibition on tampering with relief settings unless specifically authorized and qualified

Defective, damaged, or suspect gauges, regulators, or relief devices shall be removed from service and replaced before the system is returned to operation.

7.14 RESTRAINTS, SUPPORTS, AND TEST SETUP INTEGRITY

Pressurized components, test articles, hoses, and temporary assemblies shall be restrained, supported, or anchored as needed to prevent movement, separation, or instability.

Methods include:
• Mechanical supports, clamps, and stands
• Restraint cables and whip restraints
• Shields and blast barriers
• Cradles and load-rated fixtures
• Remote mounting of test articles where feasible

Setup evaluation shall include stability, movement during pressurization, reaction forces, vibration, hose and line routing, support point adequacy, and potential component separation.

Improvised setups without adequate support or restraint are prohibited.

7.15 ABNORMAL CONDITIONS, LEAKS, AND EMERGENCY RESPONSE

Leaks, unexpected pressure behavior, abnormal sounds, visible movement, vibration, unexpected frost formation, bulging, or component distress shall be treated as serious conditions.

If abnormal conditions occur:
• Stop pressurization immediately if possible
• Do not approach the hazard zone unless safe and authorized
• Depressurize remotely or by approved method if feasible
• Warn affected personnel and secure the area
• Notify supervision and EHS
• Prohibit resumption until the condition is evaluated and corrected

Employees shall not attempt to tighten, reposition, or repair pressurized fittings or components while under hazardous pressure unless a specific approved procedure allows it and equivalent protection is in place.

7.16 MAINTENANCE, INSPECTION, AND RETURN TO SERVICE

Maintenance and inspection shall be performed by qualified personnel using appropriate isolation and depressurization methods.

Requirements include:
• Inspection for wear, damage, corrosion, cracking, deformation, leakage, or improper assembly
• Replacement of damaged or questionable components with approved replacements
• Verification of reassembly and connection integrity
• Leak checks or pressure checks as required before return to service
• Restoration of guards, shields, restraints, and labeling as applicable

Systems shall not be returned to service with known pressure boundary deficiencies unless specifically controlled under an approved temporary condition with equivalent protection and a defined corrective timeline.

7.17 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION

Coordination between the company and contractors shall occur before pressurized system work begins.

The company shall communicate:
• Known pressure hazards and system conditions
• Site pressure testing and pressurization rules
• Isolation, depressurization, and lockout/tagout expectations
• Barrier, keep-out zone, and personnel exclusion requirements
• Permit and approval requirements
• Incident reporting and stop-work expectations
• Known issues related to system condition or prior failures

Contractors shall communicate:
• Scope of pressure-related work
• Test methods and pressures to be used
• Hazards introduced by their work
• Equipment and components to be used
• Any incidents, leaks, failures, or unsafe conditions encountered

Coordination shall include situations where multiple employers are working nearby.

7.18 INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW

All incidents, near misses, and unsafe conditions related to pressurized systems shall be reported immediately.

Reportable events include:
• Pressure release, leak, rupture, or component failure
• Hose whip event or fitting separation
• Test failure or abnormal test result
• Abnormal pressure behavior or unexpected pressurization
• Equipment malfunction or relief device activation
• Near miss involving personnel exposure
• Injury or property damage

Investigation shall evaluate:
• Hazard assessment adequacy
• Procedure quality and compliance
• Component selection and compatibility
• Pressure boundary integrity
• Pressurization and depressurization practices
• Keep-out zone effectiveness
• Personnel positioning
• Relief and control device performance
• Training adequacy
• Supervision and planning
• Need for engineering, procedural, or training improvements

Periodic EHS and operational review of the program shall be conducted.
emergencyResponse: If an uncontrolled pressure release, rupture, or hose whip occurs, evacuate the immediate area, warn others, and do not attempt to approach the hazard zone until it is confirmed safe
If a leak, spray, or abnormal condition is detected during pressurization or testing, stop pressurization immediately if safe to do so, secure the area, and notify supervision
If a high-pressure fluid injection injury occurs, treat as a medical emergency — call 911 immediately regardless of how minor the wound appears
If cryogenic release occurs, evacuate the area, prevent contact with spilled cryogenic liquid, ventilate enclosed areas, and do not re-enter until the atmosphere is verified safe
If oxygen-deficient or toxic atmospheres may result from a gas release, evacuate, prevent entry, and activate the emergency response system — do not attempt unauthorized rescue
If component failure, projectile hazard, or blast is suspected, keep all personnel clear and notify supervision, engineering, and EHS before any assessment
Report all pressurized system incidents, failures, leaks, near misses, and unsafe conditions immediately — do not resume work until investigated and corrected
Follow the site emergency response plan, confined space rescue procedures, and medical emergency procedures where applicable
documentation: Pressure hazard assessments where required
Pressure test procedures and plans where required
Training and competency records
Inspection and maintenance records for pressure vessels, hoses, fittings, and relief devices
Calibration or verification records for gauges and related pressure measurement equipment where applicable
Incident, near miss, and investigation records
Corrective action records
Contractor coordination records

APPENDIX A — PRESSURIZED SYSTEM PRE-JOB PLANNING CHECKLIST

□ System or component identified
□ Pressure source and medium known
□ Pressure rating and limits understood
□ Components appropriate for service — pressure, temperature, medium, and connections verified
□ Isolation points identified
□ Venting and depressurization methods defined
□ Required barriers, restraints, and keep-out zones identified
□ Procedure or permit requirements addressed
□ Required gauges, regulators, and relief devices available and suitable
□ Personnel roles and communication clear
□ Emergency response and stop-work triggers understood
□ Pre-job briefing completed

APPENDIX B — PRESSURE TEST SETUP CHECKLIST

□ Test article identified and documented
□ Test medium correct — liquid or gas as specified
□ Test pressure defined and approved
□ Components, fittings, and hoses rated for the test pressure and in good condition
□ Relief and control devices installed as required
□ Gauges suitable, readable, and positioned safely
□ Test article and lines restrained or supported as needed
□ Keep-out zone established and marked
□ Nonessential personnel clear of the hazard area
□ Pressurization method controlled — incremental approach planned
□ Leak check method and acceptance criteria understood
□ Stop-work triggers reviewed with all personnel

APPENDIX C — ISOLATION AND DEPRESSURIZATION CHECKLIST

□ Pressure source isolated — valves, disconnects, or supply removed
□ Lockout/tagout applied where required
□ Valves, blinds, or disconnects in the correct position
□ Residual pressure vented or drained through approved path
□ Gauges indicate zero pressure where applicable
□ Trapped pressure in hoses, dead legs, accumulators, or cavities considered and addressed
□ Stored mechanical or gravitational energy addressed
□ System verified safe to open or work on

APPENDIX D — KEEP-OUT ZONE AND LINE-OF-FIRE CHECKLIST

□ Hazard boundary defined based on pressure, volume, and medium
□ Barricades, cones, tape, or hard barriers in place
□ Only authorized personnel inside the controlled area
□ Personnel positioned out of the direct line of fire of fittings, closures, hoses, and end connections
□ Remote pressurization or monitoring used where feasible
□ Adjacent operations assessed and protected
□ Emergency stop or depressurization response known by all personnel in the area

APPENDIX E — CONDITIONS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE STOP WORK

• Pressure rating or design limit uncertain
• Component appears damaged, incompatible, or improperly installed
• Leak, abnormal sound, movement, bulging, or vibration detected
• Hose, fitting, restraint, or support becomes questionable
• Keep-out zone cannot be maintained
• Required gauges, regulators, or relief devices are missing or defective
• Isolation or depressurization cannot be verified
• Personnel enter the hazard zone without authorization
• A supervisor, engineering, EHS, or other responsible person stops the work
• Conditions change such that safe pressurized system control cannot be assured

SIGNATURE AND APPROVAL

Prepared By: _______________________ Date: ___________
Reviewed By: _______________________ Date: ___________
Approved By: _______________________ Date: ___________

10. COMPLIANCE AND DEVIATIONS

Failure to comply with this procedure may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination and may create serious risk of injury, illness, or fatality.

Deviations from this procedure require review and approval by EHS and operational management unless immediate emergency action is needed to protect life or health. No deviation may remove required pressure control, isolation, barriers, restraints, keep-out zones, verification, or other critical safeguards without equivalent or greater protection.

revisionHistory.description: Initial release — comprehensive Pressurized Systems Safety Program covering hazard identification, pressure boundary integrity, pressurization and depressurization controls, hydrostatic and pneumatic testing, keep-out zones, line breaking, hose and whip hazard controls, relief device management, restraint and barrier requirements, contractor coordination, incident reporting, and program review.

title: Health Exposure to Hazardous Materials Program
summary: A comprehensive Health Exposure to Hazardous Materials Program covering hazard identification, exposure assessment, hierarchy of controls, engineering controls and ventilation, administrative controls and safe work practices, PPE, respiratory protection, hygiene and decontamination, air monitoring, medical response, spill and contamination control, non-routine task planning, contractor coordination, incident reporting, and program review.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, evaluating, controlling, and monitoring employee health exposures to hazardous materials in the workplace at [Company Name].

The purpose is to prevent injury, illness, serious injury, fatality, chronic occupational disease, acute toxic effects, skin and eye injury, respiratory injury, sensitization, chemical absorption, contamination events, and operational disruption resulting from exposure to hazardous materials through inhalation, skin contact, eye contact, ingestion, injection, or other occupational exposure pathways.

Health exposures to hazardous materials can cause immediate harm or result in delayed, progressive, or irreversible effects including respiratory disease, organ damage, cancer, reproductive harm, and neurological impairment. This program requires systematic hazard identification, exposure assessment, engineering controls, administrative controls, hygiene practices, PPE, respiratory protection, exposure monitoring, medical response, training, supervision, and periodic program review to protect all personnel.

This procedure supports compliance with applicable OSHA standards, ACGIH guidance, NIOSH recommendations, industry standards, and company policies.
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, laboratories, production areas, fabrication areas, maintenance activities, warehouses, field locations, test areas, assembly areas, utilities, storage areas, and non-routine operations where employees or contractors may be exposed to hazardous materials that present health risks.

It applies to:
• Employees who work with, around, or may be exposed to hazardous materials
• Supervisors and managers responsible for work involving hazardous materials
• EHS personnel
• Industrial hygiene personnel or other qualified exposure assessors
• Engineers, technicians, operators, assemblers, mechanics, maintenance personnel, inspectors, and support personnel
• Contractors performing work involving hazardous materials onsite

This procedure applies to hazardous materials including, but not limited to:
• Toxic liquids, solids, and gases
• Vapors, dusts, fumes, mists, and aerosols
• Corrosives, sensitizers, and irritants
• Carcinogens, reproductive hazards, and neurotoxins
• Asphyxiants with health effects
• Cryogenic materials with health exposure concerns
• Solvents, paints, coatings, adhesives, and resins
• Acids and bases
• Metals and metal compounds
• Cleaning chemicals and process chemicals
• Fuels, propellants, and combustion byproducts
• Laboratory chemicals
• Hazardous waste where exposure potential exists

This procedure applies to exposure scenarios including, but not limited to:
• Normal handling, use, mixing, and dispensing
• Transferring, sampling, cleaning, and flushing
• Coating, painting, or solvent use
• Grinding, sanding, cutting, or heating of hazardous materials
• Leak or spill response support where authorized
• Maintenance, inspection, or disassembly
• Waste handling and line breaking
• Confined or enclosed area work
• Non-routine tasks involving hazardous residue or contamination

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with other required programs such as Hazard Communication, Respiratory Protection, Asphyxiants, Confined Space Entry, Pressurized Systems, Hazardous Energy / LOTO, Permit-to-Work, Emergency Response, and Contractor Safety requirements where applicable.

definitions.definition: A single exposure or series of exposures to a hazardous material occurring over a short period — typically minutes to hours — that may produce immediate or rapidly developing health effects.

Work practices, procedures, scheduling, training, signage, restricted access, and other organizational measures used to reduce employee exposure to hazardous materials. Administrative controls supplement but do not replace engineering controls.

The collection and analysis of air samples to measure the concentration of airborne contaminants in the breathing zone or work area. Includes personal sampling, area sampling, and real-time direct-reading instrument measurements.

Repeated or prolonged exposure to a hazardous material over weeks, months, or years that may produce delayed, progressive, or cumulative health effects such as organ damage, respiratory disease, cancer, or neurological impairment.
The presence of hazardous material residue on skin, clothing, surfaces, tools, equipment, or in the environment at levels that may cause health effects or spread exposure to others.
Contact between hazardous materials and the skin, which may result in local effects such as irritation, burns, or sensitization, or systemic effects through absorption into the body.

Physical changes to the workplace or process that eliminate or reduce employee exposure at the source. Examples include local exhaust ventilation, enclosures, isolation, closed transfer systems, automation, and wet methods.

The systematic process of evaluating the nature, magnitude, frequency, duration, and route of employee exposure to hazardous materials through observation, process knowledge, monitoring data, and industrial hygiene methods.

Any chemical, substance, mixture, or agent that may cause injury, illness, or adverse health effects through exposure by inhalation, skin contact, eye contact, ingestion, injection, or other occupational exposure pathways.

The science and practice of anticipating, recognizing, evaluating, and controlling workplace environmental hazards that may cause injury, illness, or impairment. Industrial hygienists use monitoring, sampling, and professional judgment to assess and manage health exposures.
The maximum concentration of an airborne contaminant to which an employee may be exposed over an 8-hour work shift as established by OSHA regulation. PELs are legally enforceable limits.

The pathway by which a hazardous material enters the body. Primary occupational routes include inhalation, dermal (skin) contact, eye contact, and ingestion. Injection through cuts or punctures is an additional route in some operations.

A substance that may cause an allergic response in a significant number of exposed individuals after initial sensitization. Subsequent exposures, even at low levels, can trigger severe reactions including dermatitis, asthma, or anaphylaxis.

The maximum concentration of an airborne contaminant to which an employee may be exposed during a continuous 15-minute period, not to be exceeded at any time during the work day, as established by OSHA or recommended by ACGIH.

The average concentration of an airborne contaminant measured over a specified time period — typically 8 hours — used to compare against occupational exposure limits and assess cumulative exposure risk.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Develop and maintain this program. Establish health exposure assessment requirements and control standards. Review and approve controls for higher-risk or non-routine hazardous material work. Verify training, PPE, ventilation, monitoring, and compliance programs. Support incident investigation and corrective action. Conduct periodic program audits and reviews. Provide technical guidance on exposure hazards, control strategies, and regulatory requirements.

Conduct or support exposure assessments, air monitoring, wipe sampling, and biological monitoring where applicable. Evaluate engineering and administrative control effectiveness. Interpret monitoring results and recommend controls. Advise on PPE and respiratory protection selection based on exposure data. Support new material review, process changes, and incident investigation. Maintain monitoring records and exposure data.

Ensure personnel under their direction understand and comply with this procedure. Verify that hazard assessments, controls, PPE, ventilation, and hygiene measures are in place before work begins. Authorize hazardous material work and ensure only trained and equipped personnel perform tasks with health exposure potential. Stop work when conditions are unsafe or controls are inadequate. Report incidents, exposures, symptoms, and near misses. Support training, planning, and program improvements.

Follow all requirements of this procedure. Attend required training and maintain awareness of health hazards associated with materials used in their work. Use engineering controls, PPE, respiratory protection, and hygiene practices as required. Keep containers closed and labeled. Report leaks, spills, exposures, symptoms, equipment malfunctions, and unsafe conditions immediately. Do not handle hazardous materials without understanding associated hazards, required controls, and emergency response actions.

Verify hazards and controls before performing maintenance, line breaking, or disassembly that may involve hazardous material residues or contamination. Use required PPE and decontamination practices. Report contamination, unexpected residue, exposure symptoms, or equipment concerns. Coordinate with operations and EHS before work on contaminated systems.

Design, install, and maintain ventilation, containment, and other engineering controls. Evaluate health exposure implications of new materials, processes, or changes. Support material approval reviews and hazard assessments. Ensure closed systems, local exhaust ventilation, and isolation methods function as intended. Support incident investigation and engineering corrective actions.

Comply with this procedure and all applicable site safety requirements. Communicate hazardous materials introduced by their work, exposure controls to be used, and PPE/respirator requirements. Report all incidents, exposures, contamination events, and unsafe conditions to the host employer. Participate in coordination meetings and pre-job briefings. Follow all site hygiene, decontamination, and emergency response requirements.

hazards: Respiratory illness, irritation, or injury from inhalation of dusts, fumes, vapors, mists, gases, or aerosols
Skin irritation, burns, dermatitis, or systemic absorption from dermal contact with hazardous liquids, solids, or residues
Eye injury, irritation, or permanent damage from splash, spray, vapor, or dust contact
Sensitization leading to allergic dermatitis, occupational asthma, or anaphylaxis from repeated exposure to sensitizing agents
Chronic organ damage — liver, kidney, lung, neurological, or hematological — from prolonged or repeated exposure
Cancer from occupational exposure to carcinogens including certain metals, solvents, dusts, and process chemicals
Reproductive and developmental harm from exposure to reproductive toxins
Acute poisoning, chemical burns, or systemic toxicity from high-concentration or uncontrolled exposure
Ingestion of hazardous materials through contaminated hands, food, or surfaces
Cross-contamination and secondary exposure to coworkers, family members, or the environment
Combined or synergistic exposure effects from simultaneous use of multiple hazardous materials
Contamination of work areas, tools, PPE, or personal items
Oxygen-deficient or toxic atmospheres from vapor accumulation, gas release, or chemical reaction in enclosed areas
procedure:
7.1 HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND HEALTH EXPOSURE ASSESSMENT

Before work involving hazardous materials begins, the task, material, and environment shall be evaluated to identify health hazards and determine required controls.

Evaluation shall include:
• Material identity, composition, and health hazard classification
• Physical form — liquid, solid, gas, vapor, dust, fume, mist, or aerosol
• Routes of exposure — inhalation, skin, eye, ingestion, or injection
• Quantity used, concentration, and volatility
• Temperature and pressure conditions affecting release potential
• Airborne release, splash, mist, residue, or contamination generation potential
• Task frequency, duration, and normal vs non-routine conditions
• Existing ventilation, containment, or isolation controls
• Surface contamination potential and dermal exposure risk
• Combined exposure potential from simultaneous materials
• Acute and chronic health effects — irritation, burns, sensitization, organ damage, carcinogenicity, reproductive toxicity, and systemic toxicity
• Need for air monitoring, wipe sampling, biological monitoring, or medical surveillance

Information sources shall include SDSs, labels, manufacturer data, process knowledge, monitoring data, incident history, regulatory guidance, and industrial hygiene review.

Work shall not proceed until additional evaluation or controls are established if the hazard cannot be adequately characterized or controlled.

7.2 HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS FOR HAZARDOUS MATERIAL HEALTH EXPOSURE

Controls shall be applied in the following preferred order:

1. Eliminate the hazardous material or exposure-generating activity
2. Substitute a less hazardous material, form, or process
3. Use engineering controls such as enclosure, local exhaust ventilation, isolation, closed transfer, splash shielding, or automation
4. Use administrative controls such as scheduling, exposure time limits, restricted access, procedural controls, and training
5. Use PPE and respiratory protection as supporting measures where required

Exposures shall be controlled at the source whenever feasible rather than relying solely on worker behavior or PPE. No employee shall be unnecessarily exposed to health hazards when engineering or process controls can reduce or eliminate the exposure.

7.3 MATERIAL APPROVAL AND INTRODUCTION OF NEW HAZARDS

New hazardous materials, new processes, or changes that may affect employee health exposure shall be reviewed before introduction where required by site policy.

Review shall include:
• Health hazard profile and exposure routes
• Existing and required controls
• PPE and respiratory protection needs
• Ventilation, containment, and storage requirements
• Spill, release, and emergency response implications
• Waste handling requirements
• Medical surveillance implications where applicable
• Availability of safer alternatives

No new material or process with significant health exposure implications shall be introduced until appropriate controls are defined and implemented.

7.4 WORK PLANNING AND AUTHORIZATION

Before work involving hazardous materials with health exposure potential begins, adequate planning shall be completed.

Planning shall include:
• Material hazards and exposure routes
• Task duration, frequency, and quantity/concentration involved
• Ventilation, containment, and engineering controls in place
• Need for PPE and respiratory protection
• Hygiene and decontamination measures
• Need for air monitoring or exposure assessment
• Spill, splash, or release potential
• Emergency response, eyewash/shower access, and medical actions
• Waste handling and disposal
• Operations that may increase exposure — line breaking, heating, agitation, spraying, or abrasive work
• Need for permits, reviews, or additional approvals

Higher-risk controls may include JSA, task risk assessment, permit to work, industrial hygiene review, EHS review, supervisor approval, and engineering review.

7.5 ENGINEERING CONTROLS AND VENTILATION

Engineering controls shall be used where needed to reduce employee exposure to hazardous materials.

Examples include:
• Local exhaust ventilation and fume hoods
• Enclosures and glove boxes
• Closed systems and sealed dispensing
• Isolation barriers and splash guards
• Wet methods to suppress dust
• Remote transfer methods and automated systems
• Negative pressure enclosures where appropriate

Ventilation systems shall be:
• Suitable for the specific hazard and contaminant
• Maintained in functional condition
• Exhausting contaminants away from employees where appropriate
• Not creating additional hazards such as recirculation or cross-contamination
• Reevaluated when task conditions or materials change

Employees shall not assume ventilation is adequate without basis. Industrial hygiene evaluation or process knowledge should support control adequacy where necessary.

7.6 ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS AND SAFE WORK PRACTICES

Administrative controls and safe work practices shall supplement engineering controls and reduce exposure.

Practices shall include:
• Limiting time in exposure areas where needed
• Restricting access to hazardous material work zones
• Performing work during lower occupancy periods where feasible
• Using designated hazardous material work areas
• Prohibiting unnecessary personnel in exposure zones
• Following specific transfer, dispensing, mixing, and cleanup procedures
• Keeping containers closed when not actively in use
• Minimizing disturbance of residues, powders, or dried deposits
• Using tools or devices to reduce direct contact
• Avoiding compressed air for cleaning contaminated surfaces unless specifically controlled and approved

Safe work practices shall address the specific route of exposure and material hazards involved.

7.7 PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT

PPE shall be selected based on the hazardous material, route of exposure, concentration, task demands, and likelihood of contact or release.

Selection shall consider:
• Safety glasses, chemical splash goggles, or face shields based on splash and vapor risk
• Chemical-resistant gloves — selected for material compatibility, breakthrough time, and duration of use
• Lab coats, aprons, coveralls, chemical suits, or protective sleeves based on contamination risk
• Chemical-resistant footwear or boot covers
• Respiratory protection where airborne exposure hazards exist

PPE selection shall consider material compatibility, breakthrough potential, splash or immersion risk, contamination potential, duration of use, and compatibility with other PPE.

PPE shall not be used as the sole control where engineering or administrative controls are feasible and needed.

7.8 RESPIRATORY PROTECTION

Respiratory protection shall be used when airborne exposure hazards cannot be adequately controlled by other means or when required by procedure, permit, or hazard assessment.

The Company Respiratory Protection Program applies. Employees shall be medically qualified, fit tested, trained, and authorized as required. The selected respirator shall be appropriate for the specific hazard, concentration, and conditions. Air-purifying respirators shall not be used in oxygen-deficient or IDLH atmospheres unless specifically authorized by applicable program requirements.

Respirator use shall be coordinated with industrial hygiene assessment where applicable to ensure the correct type, cartridge, and protection factor are selected.

7.9 HYGIENE PRACTICES AND DECONTAMINATION

Hygiene practices shall prevent ingestion, skin absorption, cross-contamination, and secondary exposure.

Requirements include:
• Hand washing after handling hazardous materials and before breaks, eating, or leaving the work area
• Removal of contaminated gloves or PPE before touching clean surfaces
• Prohibition on eating, drinking, chewing gum, smoking, or applying cosmetics in contaminated areas
• Use of designated changing or decontamination areas where required
• Segregation of contaminated clothing or PPE
• Cleaning of tools and reusable PPE after use
• Surface decontamination where residues or contamination are present

Work areas with significant dermal or contamination hazards shall have defined decontamination expectations.

7.10 STORAGE, HANDLING, AND TRANSFER

Hazardous materials shall be stored, handled, and transferred in a manner that minimizes health exposure risk.

Requirements include:
• Keeping containers labeled and closed when not in active use
• Using compatible containers and transfer equipment
• Preventing spills, splashes, and uncontrolled releases
• Segregating incompatible materials
• Using designated storage areas where required
• Using transfer methods that reduce vapor release, misting, and contact potential
• Avoiding heating, agitating, or atomizing hazardous materials without appropriate controls
• Verifying secondary containment or spill controls where needed

Material handling methods shall reduce both routine exposure and abnormal release potential.

7.11 AIR MONITORING, WIPE SAMPLING, AND EXPOSURE EVALUATION

Exposure monitoring shall be conducted when needed to evaluate airborne concentrations, confirm control effectiveness, assess employee risk, or meet regulatory or program requirements.

Monitoring methods include:
• Personal air sampling using calibrated pumps and appropriate media
• Area air sampling to characterize general work environment
• Real-time direct-reading instruments for screening or short-term assessment
• Surface wipe sampling for contamination assessment
• Biological monitoring where applicable and approved

Monitoring shall be considered when:
• New materials or tasks are introduced
• Exposure levels are uncertain
• Ventilation or controls are modified
• Symptoms or incidents suggest a problem
• Regulatory requirements apply
• Historical data is no longer representative

Monitoring results shall be reviewed by qualified personnel and used to determine whether additional controls, PPE, or medical follow-up are needed. Results shall be communicated to affected employees as required.

7.12 MEDICAL RESPONSE AND EXPOSURE REPORTING

Actual or suspected health exposures shall be reported immediately.

Reportable exposures include:
• Inhalation of excessive fumes, vapors, dust, or gas
• Skin contamination or chemical burns
• Eye exposure or splash
• Ingestion of hazardous material
• Injection or puncture involving hazardous material
• Symptoms such as dizziness, nausea, breathing difficulty, irritation, headache, confusion, rash, or unusual taste or odor

Initial response shall include:
• Leaving the exposure area and moving to fresh air
• Using eyewash or safety shower as needed
• Removing contaminated clothing
• Flushing affected areas per SDS or medical guidance
• Obtaining medical evaluation promptly
• Providing SDS or exposure information to medical personnel when available

Exposure events shall be documented and investigated to identify root causes and prevent recurrence.

7.13 SPILLS, LEAKS, AND CONTAMINATION EVENTS

Spills, leaks, emissions, or contamination events shall be managed in accordance with site emergency and spill response procedures.

Employees may only respond within the limits of their training, PPE, and authorization.

Considerations shall include:
• Material hazard and health effects
• Amount released and spread of contamination
• Airborne exposure potential
• Skin or eye contact potential
• Ventilation effects and vapor migration
• Adjacent personnel and need for evacuation or area isolation
• Need for decontamination of personnel, surfaces, or equipment
• Waste handling and disposal requirements

Contaminated areas, tools, or surfaces shall be controlled until cleaned, decontaminated, or otherwise addressed.

7.14 NON-ROUTINE TASKS AND RESIDUAL HAZARD WORK

Additional planning shall be required for non-routine tasks involving hazardous materials or contaminated equipment.

Examples include:
• Opening process equipment with chemical residue
• Cleaning tanks, vessels, or piping
• Heating coated or contaminated materials
• Disturbing dried residue or powder deposits
• Maintenance on equipment with internal contamination
• Sampling unknown or partially characterized materials
• Handling hazardous waste or spent media

Planning shall address residual hazards, hidden contamination, airborne exposure potential, decontamination needs, special PPE or respiratory protection, monitoring needs, and emergency response measures.

Non-routine tasks with significant health exposure potential shall not proceed without adequate planning and authorization.

7.15 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION

Coordination between the company and contractors shall occur before hazardous material work begins.

The company shall communicate:
• Known hazardous materials and exposure hazards
• Site PPE, respiratory protection, and hygiene requirements
• Ventilation and containment expectations
• Emergency response and decontamination requirements
• Incident reporting and stop-work expectations
• Restricted areas or permit requirements where applicable

Contractors shall communicate:
• Hazardous materials introduced by their work
• Exposure controls to be used
• PPE and respirator requirements
• Any incident, leak, contamination event, or unsafe condition encountered

Coordination shall include situations where multiple employers work nearby.

7.16 INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW

All incidents, near misses, and unsafe conditions related to hazardous material health exposure shall be reported immediately.

Reportable events include:
• Exposure events and symptom reports
• Contamination events
• Monitoring exceedances
• Control failures — ventilation, containment, or engineering control malfunction
• PPE failures — breakthrough, tear, or inadequate protection
• Spills, leaks, or uncontrolled releases
• Medical treatment or referral resulting from exposure

Investigation shall evaluate:
• Hazard identification adequacy
• Exposure assessment quality
• Material handling practices
• Engineering control effectiveness
• Administrative control effectiveness
• PPE selection and use
• Hygiene practices and decontamination
• Training adequacy
• Supervision and work planning
• Need for engineering, procedural, or training improvements

Periodic EHS and operational review of the program shall be conducted.
emergencyResponse: If acute symptoms develop — dizziness, nausea, breathing difficulty, irritation, confusion, or burning — leave the exposure area immediately, move to fresh air, and notify supervision
If skin contamination occurs, remove contaminated clothing and flush affected skin with water for at least 15 minutes or per SDS guidance — seek medical evaluation
If eye exposure occurs, flush eyes immediately at the eyewash station for at least 15 minutes or per SDS guidance — seek medical evaluation promptly
If ingestion occurs, do not induce vomiting unless directed by medical personnel or Poison Control — provide SDS information to responders
If a spill or release creates airborne exposure or contamination, evacuate the affected area, warn others, and do not re-enter until conditions are verified safe
If oxygen-deficient or toxic atmospheres are suspected from vapor accumulation or gas release, evacuate and do not re-enter — activate emergency response
Do not attempt cleanup or response beyond the limits of your training, PPE, and authorization
Report all exposure events, symptoms, contamination, and near misses immediately — document the event and provide SDS or material information to medical personnel when available
Follow the site emergency response plan, spill response procedures, and medical emergency procedures where applicable

APPENDIX A — HAZARDOUS MATERIAL EXPOSURE PRE-JOB PLANNING CHECKLIST

□ Material identity known
□ Health hazards understood — acute, chronic, sensitization, carcinogenic, reproductive, and systemic effects
□ Routes of exposure identified — inhalation, dermal, eye, ingestion
□ Quantity and concentration known
□ SDS or hazard information available
□ Engineering controls in place and functional — ventilation, enclosure, containment
□ Administrative controls and procedures defined
□ Required PPE and respirators available and appropriate
□ Hygiene and decontamination needs addressed
□ Emergency response and medical actions understood — eyewash, shower, first aid
□ Spill or contamination response expectations defined
□ Pre-job briefing completed

APPENDIX B — EXPOSURE CONTROL CHECKLIST

□ Ventilation or enclosure functioning as intended
□ Containers labeled and closed when not in use
□ Transfer methods minimize release potential
□ Splash, mist, dust, or vapor generation controlled
□ Required PPE worn correctly and in good condition
□ Respiratory protection used where required
□ Personnel exposure time controlled where applicable
□ Contaminated tools and surfaces managed appropriately
□ Unnecessary personnel kept clear of the exposure area
□ Monitoring data supports control adequacy where available

APPENDIX C — HYGIENE AND DECONTAMINATION CHECKLIST

□ Hands and exposed skin washed as required
□ Contaminated gloves or PPE removed appropriately
□ Contaminated clothing or disposable PPE handled properly
□ Tools and reusable PPE cleaned or segregated
□ Food, drink, and personal items have not been contaminated
□ Work surfaces cleaned or controlled as needed
□ Waste and residues placed in the correct containers
□ Decontamination area or wash station used where required

APPENDIX D — EXPOSURE INCIDENT RESPONSE CHECKLIST

□ Work stopped
□ Affected person moved to a safe location — fresh air, away from contamination
□ Eyewash, shower, fresh air, or skin flushing used as needed
□ Contaminated clothing removed where appropriate
□ Supervision and EHS notified
□ Medical evaluation initiated where needed
□ SDS or material information available for medical responders
□ Area secured if contamination remains
□ Event documented and investigation initiated

APPENDIX E — CONDITIONS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE STOP WORK

• Material identity or hazard is uncertain
• Required controls are missing or not functioning — ventilation, containment, or engineering controls
• PPE or respirator requirements cannot be met
• Ventilation or containment fails during the task
• A leak, spill, or contamination event creates uncontrolled exposure
• Employees experience symptoms of exposure — dizziness, nausea, irritation, breathing difficulty, rash, or confusion
• Monitoring results or observations indicate unsafe exposure potential
• Eyewash, safety shower, or emergency equipment is inaccessible
• A supervisor, EHS, or other responsible person stops the work
• Conditions change such that safe health exposure control cannot be assured

SIGNATURE AND APPROVAL

Prepared By: _______________________ Date: ___________
Reviewed By: _______________________ Date: ___________
Approved By: _______________________ Date: ___________

10. COMPLIANCE AND DEVIATIONS

Failure to comply with this procedure may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination and may create serious risk of injury, illness, or fatality.

Deviations from this procedure require review and approval by EHS and operational management unless immediate emergency action is needed to protect life or health. No deviation may remove required hazard evaluation, exposure controls, hygiene practices, PPE requirements, medical response measures, or other critical safeguards without equivalent or greater protection.

revisionHistory.description: Initial release — comprehensive Health Exposure to Hazardous Materials Program covering hazard identification, exposure assessment, hierarchy of controls, engineering controls and ventilation, administrative controls, PPE, respiratory protection, hygiene and decontamination, air monitoring, medical response, spill and contamination control, non-routine task planning, contractor coordination, incident reporting, and program review.

title: Contractor Safety Management Program
summary: A comprehensive Contractor Safety Management Program covering prequalification, risk classification, scope definition, safety documentation and bridging, orientation, pre-job planning, hazard communication, permit-to-work coordination, simultaneous operations, PPE and equipment requirements, field oversight, subcontractor control, scope change and stop-work authority, incident reporting, emergency preparedness, performance monitoring, closeout, and periodic program review.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for selecting, onboarding, coordinating, monitoring, and evaluating contractors and subcontractors performing work on [Company Name]-controlled sites or operations. The objective is to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, property damage, environmental incidents, security issues, operational disruption, and regulatory exposure resulting from inadequate contractor qualification, poor work planning, unclear responsibilities, lack of hazard communication, insufficient supervision, noncompliance with site rules, and ineffective coordination between host employer personnel and contractor personnel.

Effective contractor safety management requires shared responsibility between the host employer and the contractor employer. This program defines the company's minimum expectations, host-employer controls, and coordination requirements for all contractor work.

Document No.: [Document No.]
Effective Date: [Effective Date]
Revision: [Revision]
Owner: [Owner]
Approved By: [Approved By]
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, production areas, laboratories, maintenance areas, construction activities, fabrication areas, assembly areas, warehouses, yards, utilities, field locations, test areas, launch or test support areas, and any other company-controlled location where contractors or subcontractors perform work.

It applies to:
• Contractors and subcontractors performing work onsite or under company operational control
• Company employees who manage, sponsor, oversee, or coordinate contractor work
• Supervisors and managers responsible for contractor interfaces
• EHS personnel
• Procurement, supply chain, or contract administrators involved in contractor selection or management
• Project managers, engineers, maintenance planners, operations personnel, and permit issuers involved in contractor work

It applies to contractor activities including, but not limited to:
• Construction
• Maintenance and repair
• Equipment installation
• Inspections and testing
• Calibration
• Commissioning and decommissioning
• Housekeeping or janitorial services where industrial hazards exist
• Waste management services
• Electrical work
• Mechanical work
• Welding, cutting, and brazing
• Confined space work
• Working at heights
• Crane and rigging activities
• PIT or mobile equipment operations
• Pressure testing
• Utilities work
• Hazardous material handling
• Specialty technical services
• Temporary labor performing hands-on field work
• Shutdown, turnaround, or outage support
• Emergency support services where applicable

This procedure applies to both planned and non-routine contractor work and shall be used in conjunction with other required programs such as Permit-to-Work, Hazard Communication, Confined Space Entry, Hazardous Energy / LOTO, Working at Heights, Suspended Loads, PITs / Mobile Equipment, Electrical Safety, Pressurized Systems, Respiratory Protection, Emergency Response, and site-specific operational controls where applicable.

This SOP does not eliminate contractor responsibility for complying with applicable laws, regulations, consensus standards, and their own internal safety requirements. It defines the company's minimum expectations and host-employer controls.

definitions.definition: Any individual, firm, or company engaged to perform work or provide services on a company-controlled site who is not a direct employee of [Company Name].
A contractor engaged by a primary contractor to perform a portion of the contracted work scope.
The employer who owns, operates, or controls the worksite where contractor work is performed and who has the responsibility to communicate known hazards and coordinate safety requirements.
The company employee, department, or project function responsible for requesting, planning, and overseeing contractor work at the site level.
The designated on-site representative of the contractor employer responsible for directing contractor work and ensuring compliance with safety requirements.

A person who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the work environment and who has the authority and knowledge to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate or control them.
The process of evaluating a contractor's safety capability, experience, regulatory history, training practices, and program maturity before approving them for work.
The process of categorizing contractor work scope according to the level of hazard, complexity, SIF potential, and required controls to determine appropriate oversight and approval.
A formal written authorization system that ensures specific safety requirements are met before hazardous work begins and that controls remain in place during the work.
The right and responsibility of any person to stop work when an unsafe condition, uncontrolled hazard, or program violation is observed or suspected.
The defined boundaries of work that a contractor is approved to perform, including location, tasks, timing, and conditions.
A site-specific safety briefing that communicates hazards, rules, emergency procedures, and expectations to contractor personnel before they begin work.
A process or document used to align contractor and host employer safety management systems, clarify responsibilities, and resolve gaps or conflicts between overlapping programs.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Develop and maintain this program. Define contractor safety management standards. Support prequalification review where required. Develop and deliver orientation content. Conduct or support field inspections and audits of contractor work. Support incident investigation involving contractor personnel. Track contractor safety performance trends. Coordinate corrective actions. Conduct periodic program reviews. Provide technical guidance on permits, hazard communication, and regulatory requirements.

Include safety requirements in procurement documents, contracts, and purchase orders. Support prequalification processes where required. Communicate safety expectations during contractor selection. Verify insurance and licensing requirements. Coordinate with EHS and sponsoring departments on contractor safety documentation. Include performance clauses in contracts where appropriate.

Define the work scope clearly. Identify hazards associated with the work location and task. Determine risk classification and required controls. Coordinate orientation, permits, access, and communication. Serve as the primary company contact for contractor coordination. Monitor contractor work progress. Report incidents, unsafe conditions, and scope changes. Participate in pre-job meetings and post-job reviews where applicable.

Ensure contractors under their area of responsibility are properly qualified, oriented, and coordinated. Verify that required permits, isolations, and controls are in place before contractor work begins. Stop work when conditions are unsafe or not in compliance. Ensure company personnel understand their roles in contractor coordination. Support incident response, investigation, and corrective action. Ensure closeout and post-work reviews are completed.

Provide qualified, trained, and equipped personnel for the work scope. Maintain an effective safety program. Comply with applicable laws, regulations, consensus standards, and company requirements. Designate a competent on-site supervisor. Communicate hazards introduced by contractor work. Report all incidents, near misses, injuries, and unsafe conditions immediately. Manage and oversee subcontractors under their control. Ensure workers complete required orientation. Maintain documentation as required.

Direct and oversee contractor personnel on site. Ensure compliance with site safety requirements, permits, and procedures. Participate in pre-job briefings. Communicate changes in scope, conditions, or hazards to the company representative. Stop work when conditions are unsafe. Ensure housekeeping, PPE, barricades, and area controls are maintained. Report incidents and near misses immediately.

Follow all site safety requirements, permits, and procedures. Attend required orientation and training. Use required PPE and equipment. Report incidents, near misses, unsafe conditions, and injuries immediately. Exercise stop-work authority when unsafe conditions exist. Do not exceed the authorized work scope. Participate in pre-task briefings.

Comply with all requirements of this program as applicable. Coordinate with the primary contractor and company representative. Provide qualified personnel. Complete required orientation. Report incidents and unsafe conditions. Operate within the defined work scope and controls.

training: Contractor safety management expectations — roles, responsibilities, and accountability structure
