Hazard communication responsibilities — host-employer and contractor obligations for sharing hazard information
Scope definition, scope change control, and reauthorization requirements
Field oversight expectations — observations, inspections, permit compliance checks, and corrective actions
Subcontractor control requirements
Incident, near-miss, and unsafe condition reporting requirements
Stop-work authority and escalation pathways
Emergency response expectations for contractor personnel
Performance monitoring, corrective action, and contractor evaluation
Closeout and post-work review requirements
ppe: Minimum site PPE as defined by site requirements — typically hard hat, safety glasses, steel-toe footwear, high-visibility vest
Task-specific PPE as determined by hazard assessment, permit requirements, or work procedure
Respiratory protection where required by exposure assessment or permit — managed under the Respiratory Protection Program
Fall protection where required by working at heights procedures
Chemical-resistant gloves, goggles, or protective clothing where required by material hazards
Hearing protection in designated high-noise areas
Arc-rated clothing or electrical PPE where required by electrical safety procedures
Welding or cutting PPE where required by hot work procedures
NOTE: Contractors are responsible for providing appropriate PPE for their employees unless otherwise specified by contract or site requirements.
hazards: Unfamiliarity with site-specific hazards, equipment, and operating conditions
Communication gaps between host employer, contractor, and subcontractor personnel
Inconsistent safety standards, training levels, and supervision quality between organizations
Multi-employer worksite coordination challenges — overlapping work scopes, shared hazard zones, and conflicting schedules
Scope creep — unauthorized expansion of work without review of additional hazards or controls
Inadequate prequalification — contractors lacking capability, equipment, training, or supervision for the work
Permit noncompliance — working without required permits, exceeding permit conditions, or continuing after permit expiration
Inadequate isolation, lockout/tagout, or hazardous energy control during contractor maintenance or servicing
Exposure to host-employer hazards not adequately communicated — chemicals, energized systems, pressurized lines, moving equipment, confined spaces
Contractor-introduced hazards not communicated to the host employer — chemicals, heat, sparks, fumes, noise, mobile equipment, temporary utilities
Emergency response confusion — contractor personnel unfamiliar with alarms, evacuation routes, assembly areas, or medical response
Fatigue, time pressure, and production-driven shortcuts during shutdown, turnaround, or outage contractor work
Subcontractor work performed without adequate qualification, coordination, or oversight
procedure:
**7.1 CONTRACTOR PREQUALIFICATION AND SELECTION**

Before awarding work, [Company Name] shall evaluate the contractor's suitability appropriate to the risk, scope, and site requirements. Evaluation shall include review of relevant work experience and technical capability, safety program maturity and written safety plans, training and qualification practices, regulatory history where appropriate such as OSHA citations or enforcement actions, incident and injury performance history including EMR and OSHA recordable rates, insurance coverage and licensing, references from comparable work, ability to supervise subcontractors if applicable, and ability to meet site-specific requirements.

Higher-risk scopes may require more detailed review, including on-site capability assessments, pre-award safety meetings, or submission of task-specific safety plans. Contractors shall not be selected solely on cost where competency, supervision, equipment, or safety capability is inadequate for the work.

[Company Name] shall maintain a record of contractor prequalification status where required by site policy.

**7.2 RISK CLASSIFICATION OF CONTRACTOR WORK**

Contractor work shall be classified according to risk so that appropriate controls, approvals, and oversight can be applied. Classification shall consider the nature of the work, potential for serious injury or fatality, need for permits such as hot work, confined space, LOTO, excavation, or working at heights, hazardous energy exposure, electrical exposure, pressurized system exposure, hazardous material exposure, lifting operations, interaction with ongoing operations or occupied areas, number of contractor personnel, duration of work, use of subcontractors, and impact on critical systems.

Lower-risk work such as office moves, routine service calls, or low-hazard maintenance may require simplified onboarding and oversight. Higher-risk work such as confined space entry, crane operations, electrical work, turnaround support, or work near active process systems requires expanded planning, engineering or EHS review, formal permits, and increased field monitoring.

[Company Name] shall define risk classification categories and required controls for each level.

**7.3 SCOPE DEFINITION AND WORK AUTHORIZATION**

The work scope shall be clearly defined before work begins. Scope definition shall include what work is authorized, where it will occur, when it will occur, which contractor or subcontractor will perform it, required interfaces with operations or maintenance, expected hazards and required controls, required permits or isolations, required access controls, equipment, utility, or system impacts, and limitations on scope changes without approval.

Contractors shall not expand the work scope, begin adjacent tasks, or perform added work without review and authorization by the company representative. Scope changes that introduce new hazards or require additional permits, controls, or approvals shall be treated as new work and shall receive appropriate planning and authorization before proceeding.

**7.4 CONTRACTOR SAFETY DOCUMENTATION AND BRIDGING**

The company shall review contractor safety documentation as appropriate to the work scope and risk level. Documentation may include safety program summary, JHA or task risk assessment, site-specific work plan, training records for specialized tasks such as LOTO authorization, confined space entry, crane operation, or working at heights, equipment inspection records, respiratory protection qualifications where applicable, crane/rigging/operator qualifications where applicable, permit support documents, emergency contact information, and subcontractor management plan.

When both contractor and host employer have applicable safety management systems, a bridging process shall be used to align requirements, clarify responsibilities, and resolve gaps or conflicts. Bridging shall address which procedures apply, who issues permits, who performs isolations, how incidents are reported, and how communication and coordination will be managed.

**7.5 CONTRACTOR ORIENTATION AND SITE ACCESS**

All contractor personnel shall complete site orientation before beginning work unless alternative emergency provisions are specifically authorized by site management. Orientation shall include site hazards relevant to the work location, emergency alarms and evacuation procedures, incident reporting requirements, restricted areas, PPE requirements, permit requirements, hazard communication information as applicable, traffic and equipment movement rules, access and security rules, stop-work authority, environmental and housekeeping expectations, and contact and escalation pathways.

Site access shall be controlled based on completion of orientation, approval status, work location, work timing, security requirements, and escort needs where applicable. Personnel who have not completed required onboarding shall not begin work.

[Company Name] shall maintain records of contractor orientation completion.

**7.6 PRE-JOB PLANNING AND PRE-TASK BRIEFING**

A pre-job review or pre-task briefing appropriate to the scope and risk shall be conducted before work begins. The briefing shall include scope of work, work sequence, hazards and controls, roles and responsibilities, permit requirements, interfaces with operations, maintenance, or other contractors, utility or isolation status, emergency actions, area restrictions, stop-work triggers, communication methods, and changes since prior planning.

Higher-risk work may require a formal pre-job meeting involving EHS, operations, engineering, permit issuers, and contractor supervision. All personnel involved in the work shall participate in or be briefed on the pre-task review.

Pre-task briefings shall be repeated when conditions change, work is interrupted and resumed, or new personnel join the work.

**7.7 HAZARD COMMUNICATION AND HOST-EMPLOYER INFORMATION SHARING**

The company shall communicate known workplace hazards relevant to contractor work. Information shall include chemical hazards present in the work area, confined spaces, electrical hazards, pressurized systems, moving equipment, noise, radiation or special hazards where applicable, operational restrictions, emergency systems, simultaneous operations, and process or residual energy hazards.

Contractors shall communicate hazards introduced by their work, including chemicals brought onsite, heat, sparks, and fumes, dust generation, noise, pressurization or testing, lifting operations, mobile equipment, and temporary utilities or connections.

Hazard communication shall be updated whenever conditions change, new hazards are identified, or the work scope is modified.

**7.8 PERMIT-TO-WORK AND SPECIALIZED AUTHORIZATIONS**

Contractor work requiring permits shall not begin until the applicable permit is issued and understood by all involved personnel. Permits may be required for hot work, confined space entry, line breaking, lockout/tagout or hazardous energy coordination, excavation, working at heights, electrical work, pressure testing, crane or critical lifts, hazardous material operations, and out-of-service impairments or system bypasses.

The permit process shall define scope, hazard controls, time limits, area restrictions, required approvals, coordination with operations, and stop-work or reevaluation triggers. Contractors shall comply with all permit conditions and shall not continue work after a permit expires, changes materially, or becomes invalid.

Permit renewal or reissuance shall follow site procedures and shall include reverification of conditions.

**7.9 INTERFACE WITH OPERATIONS AND SIMULTANEOUS WORK**

Contractor work shall be coordinated with ongoing operations and with other contractors or work groups operating in the same area. Coordination shall address overlapping work scopes, shared access routes, utility impacts, equipment isolation impacts, airborne hazards, noise, vehicle movement, crane swing or lift paths, restricted areas, fire protection impacts, testing or startup impacts, and occupied-area exposure.

Where simultaneous operations create elevated risk, the work shall be sequenced, separated, or otherwise controlled to prevent combined hazards from exceeding acceptable levels. The company representative shall determine whether additional controls, communication, or scheduling changes are needed.

**7.10 PPE, EQUIPMENT, AND TOOL REQUIREMENTS**

Contractor personnel shall use PPE, tools, and equipment appropriate to the work and in compliance with site requirements. Requirements include minimum site PPE, task-specific PPE, inspection and condition of tools and equipment, approved ladders, lifts, and access equipment, appropriate guarding on portable tools, correct electrical ratings, rated rigging and lifting devices, proper hose, fitting, and pressure equipment selection, and fire prevention and housekeeping provisions.

Damaged, unsafe, or unapproved tools and equipment shall not be used. Contractors are responsible for providing and maintaining their tools and equipment unless otherwise specified.

**7.11 SUPERVISION AND FIELD OVERSIGHT**

Contractor work shall be supervised at a level appropriate to the complexity and risk. Oversight shall include contractor supervisor presence on site, company field observations and inspections, EHS inspections, permit compliance checks, pre-task verification, spot checks of training or qualifications, and compliance monitoring for barricades, PPE, permits, housekeeping, and area controls.

Oversight is not a substitute for contractor responsibility. However, the company shall actively address unsafe behavior, unsafe conditions, and noncompliance through coaching, corrective action, work stoppage, or escalation as appropriate.

Field observation frequency shall increase for higher-risk work, new contractors, or contractors with prior performance issues.

**7.12 SUBCONTRACTOR CONTROL**

Primary contractors shall not use subcontractors on company work without authorization where required by contract or site requirements. When subcontractors are used, the primary contractor shall ensure their role is identified, scope is defined, qualifications are appropriate, orientation is completed, work is coordinated with the host employer and primary contractor, and safety performance is managed and monitored.

Primary contractors remain responsible for subcontractors under their control unless explicitly defined otherwise by the company. The company may require direct oversight, orientation, or coordination with subcontractors for higher-risk work.

**7.13 SCOPE CHANGES, STOP-WORK, AND REAUTHORIZATION**

Work shall stop and be reevaluated when conditions change, hazards increase, or the contractor wishes to alter the work scope. Examples include change in location, different task than planned, unexpected utilities or residual hazards, additional subcontractors, equipment or permit changes, new hazardous materials, additional lifting, cutting, entry, or energized work, and weather or operational changes.

Work shall not resume until the revised scope is reviewed and required approvals or controls are in place. Any company or contractor employee may stop work when unsafe conditions or uncontrolled hazards exist. Stop-work actions shall be supported by management without retaliation.

**7.14 INCIDENT, NEAR MISS, AND UNSAFE CONDITION REPORTING**

Contractors shall report incidents, near misses, injuries, spills, equipment damage, property damage, permit deviations, and unsafe conditions immediately to the company representative and contractor supervision. Response shall include area stabilization, medical response where needed, preservation of scene when appropriate, initial fact gathering, participation in investigation, and corrective action development.

Failure to report significant events promptly is a serious program violation and may result in contractor removal from site or disqualification from future work.

[Company Name] shall include contractor incidents in site incident tracking and trending.

**7.15 EMERGENCY PREPAREDNESS AND RESPONSE**

Contractors shall understand site emergency procedures before beginning work. Orientation and pre-job briefings shall cover alarm recognition, evacuation routes and assembly areas, medical response process, emergency contacts, fire extinguisher or spill response limitations, rescue limitations for contractor personnel, shutdown actions if needed, and accountability expectations.

Contractors shall not perform specialized emergency response or rescue beyond their authorization, training, and site approval. Contractor personnel shall follow all site emergency instructions and shall not reenter evacuated areas without authorization.

**7.16 PERFORMANCE MONITORING AND CORRECTIVE ACTION**

Contractor safety performance shall be monitored during the work and evaluated at completion as appropriate. Performance indicators include permit compliance, field observations, housekeeping, PPE use, supervision quality, incident and near miss reporting, responsiveness to corrective actions, work planning quality, and communication and coordination effectiveness.

Corrective actions may include coaching, rebriefing, additional supervision, permit suspension, stop-work, removal of personnel from site, formal corrective action requests, and contractor disqualification for future work where warranted.

[Company Name] shall maintain contractor performance records for use in future prequalification and selection decisions.

**7.17 CLOSEOUT AND POST-WORK REVIEW**

At completion of work, the company representative and contractor supervisor shall confirm that the scope is complete, permits are closed, equipment or systems are returned to appropriate status, temporary controls are removed appropriately, housekeeping is acceptable, tools, materials, and waste are removed or managed, outstanding hazards are communicated, incidents, near misses, and lessons learned are captured, and performance issues are documented for future contractor selection or oversight.

Higher-risk or higher-value projects may require a formal post-job review meeting with EHS, operations, engineering, and contractor management.

**7.18 PERIODIC PROGRAM REVIEW**

EHS and operational leadership shall periodically review this program for effectiveness. Review shall include incident trends involving contractor work, audit findings, contractor performance trends, repeat deficiencies, permit compliance, orientation effectiveness, prequalification effectiveness, sponsor and supervisor feedback, and changes in regulatory or operational needs.

Deficiencies shall be corrected and tracked to closure. Program improvements shall be communicated to affected personnel.
emergencyResponse: Contractors shall follow the host employer's emergency action plan at all times while on site.
All contractor personnel shall know the location of emergency exits, assembly areas, alarms, eyewash stations, fire extinguishers, and emergency contacts before beginning work.
Contractor incidents, injuries, spills, fires, or hazardous releases shall be reported immediately to the company representative and contractor supervision.
Serious incidents may result in contractor work stoppage, area isolation, and joint investigation between host employer and contractor.

Contractors shall not perform rescue, confined space entry rescue, hazardous material response, or other specialized emergency response unless specifically trained, equipped, and authorized by the site.
Contractor personnel shall participate in all site evacuations, drills, and emergency exercises when directed.
The host employer shall include contractor personnel in emergency accountability processes.

title: Non-Routine Activities Safety Program
summary: A comprehensive Non-Routine Activities Safety Program covering identification and classification of non-routine work, hazard identification and risk assessment, hierarchy of controls, work planning and scope definition, cross-functional review, permit coordination, pre-job briefing, simultaneous operations, temporary conditions and bypasses, equipment verification, execution monitoring, scope change control, emergency preparedness, contractor coordination, completion and restoration, incident reporting, and periodic program review.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, evaluating, planning, authorizing, and controlling non-routine activities that may expose employees, contractors, equipment, property, or operations to elevated risk at [Company Name] facilities and work locations. The objective is to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, environmental release, equipment damage, property damage, operational disruption, and regulatory exposure resulting from work that falls outside normal operating conditions, standard production tasks, or established routine procedures.

Non-routine activities carry elevated risk because they often involve unfamiliar conditions, incomplete procedures, novel hazards, time pressure, simultaneous operations, temporary configurations, and reduced margin for error. This program requires that every non-routine activity be identified, assessed, planned, authorized, communicated, monitored, and closed out with the same rigor applied to any high-risk work.

Document No.: [Document No.]
Effective Date: [Effective Date]
Revision: [Revision]
Owner: [Owner]
Approved By: [Approved By]
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, laboratories, production areas, fabrication areas, maintenance activities, test areas, field locations, warehouses, utilities, assembly areas, launch or test support areas, and any company-controlled location where non-routine work may occur.

It applies to:
• Employees involved in planning, approving, supervising, or performing non-routine work
• Supervisors and managers responsible for operational control of non-routine activities
• EHS personnel
• Engineers, technicians, operators, mechanics, maintenance personnel, inspectors, and support personnel
• Contractors and subcontractors performing non-routine activities onsite or under company operational control
• Permit issuers, area owners, and project leads involved in non-routine work planning

It applies to non-routine activities including, but not limited to:
• One-time tasks
• Infrequently performed tasks
• Startup, shutdown, commissioning, and decommissioning work
• Troubleshooting under abnormal conditions
• Equipment moves or reconfiguration
• Temporary systems or connections
• Line breaking or opening process equipment
• Work involving simultaneous operations
• Work involving new equipment, processes, or materials
• Manual interventions in systems not designed for routine access
• Emergency repairs
• Abnormal maintenance or recovery activities
• Unusual testing
• Temporary bypasses or impairments
• Work in unfamiliar areas or under unfamiliar conditions
• Work requiring multiple permits or multiple specialty controls
• Tasks with incomplete procedure coverage
• Work following incidents, failures, or abnormal events

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with other required programs such as Permit-to-Work, Hazardous Energy / LOTO, Confined Space Entry, Working at Heights, Suspended Loads and Lifting Operations, PITs / Mobile Equipment, Electrical Safety, Pressurized Systems, Hazard Communication, Respiratory Protection, Contractor Safety Management, and Emergency Response requirements where applicable.

definitions.definition: A state of equipment, process, environment, or operations that differs from normal, expected, or designed conditions and may introduce new or elevated hazards.
The person or function responsible for the operational control, safety conditions, and access management of a defined work area or system.
The defined boundaries of work that have been reviewed, approved, and communicated, including tasks, locations, timing, and conditions.

A structured review involving representatives from multiple disciplines such as operations, engineering, EHS, maintenance, or project management to evaluate the hazards, controls, and coordination requirements for a non-routine activity.
A position or location where a person is exposed to the path of a moving object, released energy, falling material, pressurized release, or other hazard trajectory.

A task that is not performed frequently enough to be considered routine, is outside normal operating conditions, is not fully covered by an established procedure, or involves increased uncertainty, changing conditions, or elevated consequence of failure.
A formal written authorization system that ensures specific safety requirements are met before hazardous work begins and that controls remain in place during the work.
The risk that remains after all identified controls have been applied. Residual risk must be understood and accepted before proceeding.
A structured process for identifying hazards, evaluating the likelihood and severity of potential harm, and determining appropriate controls before work begins.
Any modification to the authorized work scope, including changes in task, method, location, timing, personnel, equipment, or conditions.
Two or more work activities occurring in the same area or affecting the same systems at the same time, creating combined hazards that may exceed the risk of either activity performed independently.
The right and responsibility of any person to stop work when an unsafe condition, uncontrolled hazard, or program violation is observed or suspected.
A state of equipment, system, or area configuration that differs from normal and is intended to exist for a limited time, such as temporary utilities, bypasses, barriers, supports, or impairments.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Develop and maintain this program. Define criteria for identifying and classifying non-routine activities. Provide technical guidance on risk assessment, control selection, and permit coordination. Support cross-functional reviews for higher-risk work. Conduct or support field observations and inspections during non-routine activities. Support incident investigation and corrective action development. Track trends in non-routine work incidents and near misses. Conduct periodic program audits and reviews.

Ensure non-routine activities under their responsibility are identified, planned, and controlled in accordance with this procedure. Verify that risk assessments, permits, and controls are in place before work begins. Assign qualified personnel. Ensure pre-job briefings are conducted. Monitor work execution and stop work when conditions change or hazards are uncontrolled. Ensure completion, restoration, and closeout are performed. Report incidents and near misses. Support corrective action and lessons learned.

Communicate area-specific hazards, operational conditions, system status, and restrictions relevant to the non-routine work. Coordinate with work performers on timing, access, isolations, and SIMOPS. Verify that area conditions are consistent with the work plan. Communicate changes in area or system status that may affect the work. Support pre-job and post-job reviews.

Provide technical support for risk assessment, control selection, system evaluation, and temporary condition review. Support cross-functional reviews for complex or high-consequence work. Evaluate temporary bypasses, impairments, or alternate configurations for adequacy and risk. Assist with incident investigation and root cause analysis. Review and update procedures as needed based on lessons learned.

Follow all requirements of this procedure. Participate in risk assessments and pre-job briefings. Perform work within the authorized scope. Use required PPE, tools, and controls. Monitor conditions during execution and report changes immediately. Stop work when conditions are unsafe, controls are lost, or scope is unclear. Report incidents, near misses, abnormal conditions, and scope deviations immediately.

Verify that applicable permits are issued, conditions are met, and controls are in place before work begins. Coordinate with area owners, operations, and work performers. Monitor permit validity during work. Suspend or close permits when conditions change or work is complete.

Comply with this procedure and all applicable site requirements. Participate in risk assessments, pre-job briefings, and coordination meetings. Perform work within the authorized scope. Report incidents, near misses, unsafe conditions, and scope changes immediately. Do not begin non-routine work without required review, briefing, and authorization. Follow the Contractor Safety Management Program.

ppe: PPE as determined by the task-specific risk assessment and applicable permits
Minimum site PPE where required — typically hard hat, safety glasses, steel-toe footwear, high-visibility vest
Task-specific PPE such as chemical-resistant gloves, face shields, arc-rated clothing, fall protection, respiratory protection, or hearing protection as identified by the hazard assessment
Specialized PPE where required by permit conditions, material hazards, or environmental conditions

NOTE: PPE shall not be used as the sole control where engineering or administrative controls are feasible. PPE requirements for non-routine work should be specifically evaluated, not assumed from routine task defaults.
hazards: Unfamiliarity with the task, equipment, area, or operating conditions — leading to errors, misjudgments, or unrecognized hazards
Inadequate or nonexistent procedures — tasks with no written procedure, partial procedures, or procedures not covering actual conditions
Simultaneous operations creating combined hazards exceeding individual task risk
Temporary conditions, bypasses, or impairments reducing normal safeguards without equivalent protection
Scope creep — unauthorized expansion of work introducing unplanned hazards without additional controls
Time pressure and production urgency overriding proper planning, review, and approval
Abnormal equipment or system condition — residual energy, contamination, unstable structures, partial disassembly, or degraded integrity
Human factors — fatigue, complacency, assumption-based decision-making, overconfidence, communication gaps
Interactions with energized systems, pressurized systems, hazardous materials, confined spaces, or elevated work
Inadequate communication between work performers, area owners, operations, maintenance, contractors, and permit issuers
Loss of controls during execution due to condition changes, equipment failure, or environmental shifts
Emergency response complexity when non-routine work creates unusual hazard profiles
Incomplete restoration after work — leaving temporary conditions, missing guards, or unverified system status
procedure:
**7.1 IDENTIFICATION AND CLASSIFICATION OF NON-ROUTINE ACTIVITIES**

Work shall be identified as non-routine when it is not performed frequently enough to be considered routine, is outside normal operating conditions, is not fully covered by an established procedure, or involves increased uncertainty or changing conditions.

Indicators that a task is non-routine include no existing procedure or only partial procedure coverage, one-time or rare performance, abnormal equipment condition, temporary system or configuration, unusual access/tools/methods required, interaction with multiple hazards or permits, work near ongoing operations or critical systems, new material/equipment/process, elevated consequence of error or failure, recovery from upset/failure/emergency event, and need for special approvals or technical review.

Once identified as non-routine, the work shall be planned and controlled under this SOP and any applicable specialized procedures. The level of planning and review shall be proportional to the complexity, hazard level, and consequence of the work.

**7.2 HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND RISK ASSESSMENT**

A structured hazard review shall be conducted before non-routine work begins. The assessment shall address work scope and sequence, energy sources including electrical/mechanical/hydraulic/pneumatic/thermal/chemical/gravity/stored pressure, hazardous materials, access and egress requirements, SIMOPS and interface conditions, equipment condition and reliability, environmental conditions, pressure/temperature/motion/stored energy hazards, line-of-fire and struck-by hazards, fall hazards, lifting or load movement, confined space or enclosed area conditions, electrical hazards, exposure to residues/contamination/process contents, human factors such as fatigue/time pressure/unfamiliarity/communication challenges, and consequences of failure or loss of control.

The level of risk assessment shall match the complexity and hazard level of the work. Options include pre-task hazard review, JSA, task risk assessment, formal cross-functional review, engineering review, or permit-specific hazard assessment.

If hazards cannot be adequately controlled, the activity shall not proceed until additional controls, alternative methods, or engineering support are established.

**7.3 HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS FOR NON-ROUTINE ACTIVITIES**

Controls shall be applied in the following preferred order:

1. Eliminate the task or eliminate the hazard through redesign or alternate method
2. Substitute lower-risk methods, materials, tools, or timing
3. Use engineering controls such as barriers, remote operation, isolation, ventilation, restraints, or shielding
4. Use administrative controls such as permits, limited access, sequencing, spotters, procedures, and communication
5. Use PPE as a supporting measure, not as a substitute for proper planning and hazard control

Work should be redesigned to reduce exposure to the highest-consequence hazards whenever feasible before execution. Reliance solely on worker behavior or PPE is not acceptable when engineering or administrative controls are practical.

**7.4 WORK PLANNING AND SCOPE DEFINITION**

The scope of non-routine work shall be clearly defined before work begins. Scope definition shall include what work is to be performed, why the work is needed, where it will occur, who will perform it, when it will occur, what equipment/systems/areas are affected, what conditions must exist before work begins, and what tasks are explicitly excluded unless separately approved.

The scope shall be specific enough to prevent unauthorized expansion or assumptions during execution. If the scope cannot be clearly defined, the work shall be paused until planning is sufficient.

Scope documentation shall be appropriate to the risk level — ranging from a documented pre-task briefing for lower-risk work to a formal work plan with approvals for higher-risk activities.

**7.5 CROSS-FUNCTIONAL REVIEW AND APPROVAL**

Higher-risk, complex, or high-consequence non-routine activities shall be reviewed by necessary stakeholders before work begins. Review may involve operations, EHS, engineering, maintenance, quality, security, facilities, project management, contractor supervision, permit issuers, and emergency response support as appropriate.

The review shall confirm that hazards are understood, controls are appropriate, interfaces are addressed, permits and isolation needs are known, roles and communication are clear, emergency actions are defined, and stop-work triggers are established.

Higher-risk work may require documented approval from designated authorities before execution. The level of approval shall be defined by site policy based on risk classification.

**7.6 PERMIT-TO-WORK AND SPECIALIZED CONTROLS**

Required permits or formal authorizations shall be identified before work begins. Permits may be required for hot work, confined space entry, lockout/tagout or hazardous energy coordination, excavation, line breaking, working at heights, electrical work, pressure testing, critical lifting, hazardous material operations, impairment or bypass approvals, and specialized test or startup authorizations.

All permit conditions shall be incorporated into the work plan and communicated during the pre-job briefing. Non-routine work shall not continue when required permits are missing, expired, invalid, or inconsistent with actual conditions.

Permit renewal or reissuance shall follow site procedures and shall include reverification of conditions.

**7.7 PRE-JOB BRIEFING**

A pre-job briefing shall be conducted with all involved personnel before non-routine work begins. The briefing shall cover work scope, work sequence, hazards and controls, roles and responsibilities, permit conditions, isolation status, area restrictions, communication methods, emergency response actions, stop-work triggers, scope change triggers, and SIMOPS or nearby work interactions.

All involved personnel shall understand the plan before work begins. Questions or concerns shall be resolved before starting. Pre-job briefings shall be repeated when conditions change, work is interrupted and resumed, or new personnel join the work.

The briefing shall be documented where required by site policy or risk level.

**7.8 CONTROL OF INTERFACES AND SIMULTANEOUS OPERATIONS**

Non-routine work that interacts with nearby operations, equipment, systems, or work groups shall be evaluated for SIMOPS risk. Controls may include sequencing work to avoid overlap, temporarily isolating adjacent operations, barricading areas, restricting access, scheduling during low-occupancy periods, assigning spotters or coordinators, pausing incompatible work, and defining hold points before simultaneous steps.

Where simultaneous operations create elevated risk, they shall be actively coordinated and, if necessary, separated in time or space. The area owner and work supervisor shall jointly determine whether concurrent work is acceptable.

SIMOPS controls shall be documented and communicated to all affected personnel.

**7.9 TEMPORARY CONDITIONS, BYPASSES, AND ABNORMAL CONFIGURATIONS**

Non-routine work may involve temporary utilities, temporary supports or restraints, temporary wiring or instrumentation, temporary guards or shielding, system bypasses, impairment of alarms/interlocks/protective devices, alternate process routing, and open systems or exposed components.

These conditions shall be specifically reviewed and controlled before use. Controls shall include clear documentation of what is temporary, defined duration and ownership, equivalent or greater protection where normal safeguards are impaired, communication to affected personnel, and removal or restoration when the work is complete.

Routine use of temporary conditions as permanent solutions is prohibited. Temporary conditions that extend beyond their original duration shall be formally reviewed and reauthorized.

**7.10 EQUIPMENT CONDITION, VERIFICATION, AND READINESS**

Equipment, tools, and systems involved in non-routine work shall be verified before work begins. Verification shall include equipment identification, isolation status, pressure or zero-energy confirmation, guarding or access condition, structural support condition, calibration or test equipment status, tool condition, temporary system integrity, cleanliness or contamination condition, utility status, and environmental readiness.

Assumptions shall not be used in place of verification for critical conditions. Verification results shall be communicated to work performers and documented where required.

**7.11 EXECUTION MONITORING AND DYNAMIC REASSESSMENT**

Active monitoring shall be maintained during execution of non-routine work. Monitoring shall ensure work remains within approved scope, conditions match the approved plan, hazards remain controlled, personnel stay within defined roles, barriers/permits/isolations remain valid, and unexpected changes are identified quickly.

Dynamic reassessment shall be triggered when conditions change, new hazards are discovered, task sequence changes, equipment behaves unexpectedly, weather or environment changes, additional personnel or contractors are introduced, or a near miss, abnormal condition, or control failure occurs.

When significant changes occur, work shall stop and be reevaluated before continuing. The decision to resume shall be made by the responsible supervisor or authority and shall be documented where required.

**7.12 SCOPE CHANGES AND REAUTHORIZATION**

Review shall be required before continuation whenever the approved activity changes. Changes requiring reauthorization include change in task or method, change in work location, additional equipment or systems affected, new hazards or conditions discovered, different personnel performing the task, additional contractor involvement, new permit needs, loss of a planned control, and extension of work duration beyond original approval.

Revised work shall not proceed until required controls, permits, and approvals are updated. The original risk assessment and pre-job briefing shall be revisited to address the changed conditions.

**7.13 EMERGENCY PREPAREDNESS AND RESPONSE**

Emergency response actions shall be defined before non-routine work begins at a level appropriate to the task. Planning shall address alarm and notification methods, area evacuation, medical response, spill or release response, rescue limitations, shutdown actions if needed, emergency contacts, access for responders, and special hazards such as pressure, toxic materials, electrical hazards, or confined space conditions.

Personnel shall not perform emergency response or rescue beyond their authorization, training, and site approval. Emergency actions shall be communicated during the pre-job briefing and shall be understood by all involved personnel.

**7.14 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION**

Contractor work classified as non-routine shall be coordinated in accordance with the Contractor Safety Management Program and this SOP. Coordination shall include scope and limits of work, hazards and controls, permits and approvals, area ownership and communication, SIMOPS, equipment and utility impacts, emergency actions, scope change and stop-work expectations, and performance monitoring.

Contractors shall not begin non-routine work without the required review, briefing, and authorization. Contractor supervision shall be present at a level appropriate to the risk. The company representative shall verify that contractor planning and controls are consistent with this program.

**7.15 COMPLETION, RESTORATION, AND RETURN TO NORMAL CONDITIONS**

At completion, verification shall confirm that work is complete or at a safe stopping point, temporary controls/bypasses/supports/barriers are removed or formally transferred under control, equipment/systems/areas are restored to an acceptable condition, guards/labels/protective devices are reinstalled or returned to service, permits are closed as required, housekeeping is acceptable, tools/materials/waste are removed or controlled, and affected personnel are informed of system or area status.

If restoration cannot be completed immediately, the remaining temporary condition shall be documented, communicated, and controlled under the temporary condition management provisions of this procedure.

Higher-risk or complex non-routine work may require a formal post-job review to capture lessons learned.

**7.16 INCIDENT, NEAR MISS, AND ABNORMAL CONDITION REPORTING**

Any incident, near miss, abnormal condition, control failure, permit deviation, scope deviation, or unsafe condition related to non-routine work shall be reported immediately. Investigations shall evaluate planning quality, scope definition, risk assessment adequacy, control selection, permit coordination, communication effectiveness, supervision and oversight, human factors, scope change control, and need for engineering/procedural/training improvements.

Lessons learned shall be captured and used to improve future planning, training, and program effectiveness. Trends in non-routine work incidents and near misses shall be tracked and reviewed periodically.

**7.17 PERIODIC PROGRAM REVIEW**

EHS and operational leadership shall periodically review this program for effectiveness. Review shall include incident trends, near miss trends, permit deviations, repeat planning failures, contractor performance during non-routine work, effectiveness of pre-job briefings, scope change control effectiveness, temporary condition management, audit findings, and feedback from supervisors, planners, and work performers.

Deficiencies shall be corrected and tracked to closure. Program improvements shall be communicated to affected personnel and incorporated into training.
emergencyResponse: Emergency response actions shall be defined and communicated before non-routine work begins.
All personnel involved in non-routine work shall know the location of emergency exits, assembly areas, alarms, eyewash stations, fire extinguishers, and emergency contacts.
Any worker may invoke stop-work authority at any time when unsafe conditions exist.
If conditions change unexpectedly during non-routine work, work shall stop immediately and conditions shall be reevaluated before resuming.
Personnel shall not perform rescue, confined space entry rescue, hazardous material response, or other specialized emergency response unless specifically trained, equipped, and authorized.
Incidents, injuries, spills, releases, or near misses during non-routine work shall be reported immediately.
The area owner and work supervisor shall coordinate emergency response actions specific to the non-routine work scope and location.
documentation: Risk assessments and planning documents where required
Permit records — hot work, confined space, LOTO, line break, working at heights, and other applicable permits
Pre-job briefing records where applicable
Cross-functional review records where applicable
Scope authorization records
Incident, near miss, and investigation records
Corrective action records
Contractor coordination records
Temporary condition or bypass documentation where applicable
Audit findings and program review records

APPENDIX A — NON-ROUTINE ACTIVITY PRE-PLANNING CHECKLIST
✓ Work confirmed as non-routine
✓ Scope clearly defined
✓ Hazards and interfaces identified
✓ Risk assessment completed
✓ Required permits or approvals identified
✓ Required stakeholders engaged
✓ Area conditions and operational impacts reviewed
✓ Temporary conditions or bypasses identified
✓ Roles and responsibilities assigned
✓ Emergency actions defined
✓ Scope change and stop-work triggers understood

APPENDIX B — PRE-JOB BRIEFING CHECKLIST
✓ Scope and sequence of work reviewed
✓ Hazards and controls discussed
✓ Roles and responsibilities clear
✓ Permit conditions understood
✓ Isolation or equipment status confirmed
✓ Area restrictions and barricades discussed
✓ SIMOPS and interfaces addressed
✓ Communication methods established
✓ Emergency actions understood
✓ Stop-work and scope change triggers reviewed

APPENDIX C — DYNAMIC REASSESSMENT CHECKLIST
✓ Work remains within approved scope
✓ Conditions match the approved plan
✓ Controls remain effective
✓ Permits remain valid
✓ No new hazards have emerged
✓ Area access remains controlled
✓ Temporary systems or barriers remain intact
✓ Communication remains effective
✓ Unexpected conditions escalated promptly
✓ Work stops for reevaluation if conditions change materially

APPENDIX D — COMPLETION AND RESTORATION CHECKLIST
✓ Work complete or at a safe stopping point
✓ Temporary controls removed or formally transferred under control
✓ Equipment and systems returned to acceptable status
✓ Guards and protective devices restored
✓ Tools, materials, and waste removed or managed
✓ Housekeeping acceptable
✓ Permits closed as required
✓ Affected personnel notified of status
✓ Outstanding hazards or incomplete restoration items documented and communicated

APPENDIX E — CONDITIONS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE STOP WORK
• Work scope is unclear or changes without approval
• Hazards are not adequately controlled
• Required permits are missing, expired, or invalid
• A critical control is lost or impaired
• Conditions differ materially from the approved plan
• SIMOPS create uncontrolled risk
• Temporary conditions become unstable or ineffective
• An abnormal condition, near miss, or incident occurs and conditions remain uncorrected
• A supervisor, area owner, EHS, permit issuer, or other responsible person stops the work
• Conditions change such that safe execution cannot be assured

title: Mobile Equipment Safety in Mining SOP
summary: Safe operating procedures for haul trucks, loaders, dozers, graders, and other mobile equipment in surface and underground mining operations.

sections.purpose: To establish safe operating requirements for mobile equipment in mining environments, preventing collisions, rollovers, struck-by incidents, and pedestrian interactions.
sections.scope: Applies to all operators, maintenance personnel, and pedestrians working in or near mobile equipment operating areas at mine sites.

definitions.definition: Self-propelled equipment used for mining, hauling, grading, loading, or support operations.
A ridge of material along the edge of an elevated roadway or dump point to prevent equipment from going over the edge.
An area around equipment where the operator's direct line of sight is obstructed.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Conduct pre-shift inspections, follow traffic rules, maintain situational awareness, and report defects.
Ensure operators are trained and competent, review traffic management plans, and enforce speed limits.
Monitor compliance, investigate incidents, and coordinate traffic management improvements.

training: MSHA Part 46 or Part 48 training (as applicable)
Equipment-specific operator training and competency evaluation
Traffic management and right-of-way rules
Pre-shift inspection procedures
Emergency shutdown and evacuation
ppe: Hard hat
High-visibility clothing
Safety glasses
Steel-toe boots
Hearing protection
Seatbelt (mandatory in all mobile equipment)
hazards: Collision with other equipment or light vehicles
Struck-by for pedestrians in equipment operating areas
Rollover on slopes, berms, or unstable ground
Loss of braking on grades
Blind spot incidents during reversing or turning
Tire or component failure
procedure: Conduct a thorough pre-shift inspection and document any defects before operating.
Wear seatbelt at all times while equipment is in motion.
Obey posted speed limits and adjust speed for road and weather conditions.
Maintain safe following distances based on equipment size and road grade.
Use headlights, strobes, and backup alarms at all times.
Yield right-of-way to loaded equipment and follow established traffic patterns.
Do not operate equipment near personnel unless radio confirmation is received.
Report all berms, road conditions, and hazard changes to supervision immediately.
Park equipment on level ground with parking brake applied and bucket/blade lowered.
Never leave running equipment unattended.
emergencyResponse: In case of equipment fire, safely stop equipment, engage parking brake, and evacuate. Use onboard fire suppression if equipped.
For rollovers, stay in the cab with seatbelt fastened until equipment stops. Do not attempt to jump from rolling equipment.
Report all collisions, near misses, and equipment failures immediately.
documentation: Pre-shift inspection records
Training and competency records
Incident and near-miss reports
Traffic management plan
references: MSHA 30 CFR Part 56/57 — Mobile Equipment
MSHA 30 CFR Part 77 — Surface Coal
Company Traffic Management Plan

title: Ground Control / Highwall Safety SOP
summary: Procedures for managing ground stability hazards including highwall monitoring, bench maintenance, and geotechnical controls in surface mining operations.

sections.purpose: To establish requirements for safe work practices near highwalls, pit walls, and areas with ground instability potential in surface mining operations.
sections.scope: Applies to all personnel working in or near open pit, quarry, and surface mining areas where ground control hazards exist.

definitions.definition: The unexcavated face of exposed overburden and ore in a surface mine.
A horizontal ledge cut into the wall of a pit to arrest falling rock and material.
A documented plan identifying geotechnical risks and controls for ground stability.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Develop and maintain the ground control plan, conduct stability assessments, and recommend controls.
Ensure daily inspections are completed, enforce exclusion zones, and report changes in ground conditions.
Report cracks, movement, or unusual ground conditions immediately. Follow all exclusion zone restrictions.

training: Ground control hazard awareness
Visual inspection for signs of instability
Exclusion zone procedures
Emergency evacuation for ground failure events
ppe: Hard hat with chin strap
High-visibility clothing
Steel-toe boots
Safety glasses
Communication radio
hazards: Highwall collapse or bench failure
Rockfall from unstable faces
Tension crack development
Water saturation weakening ground stability
Blast damage to pit walls
Equipment operating too close to edges
procedure: Maintain a current ground control plan approved by a competent geotechnical professional.
Conduct visual inspections of highwalls and benches at the start of each shift.
Monitor for tension cracks, seepage, bulging, or unusual movement.
Maintain catch benches at design widths and clear of excessive debris.
Establish and enforce exclusion zones at the base of active highwalls.
Do not operate equipment closer than the designated safe distance from edges and crests.
Implement slope monitoring systems (prisms, radar) where ground conditions warrant.
Suspend operations during and after heavy rainfall until ground conditions are assessed.
After blasting, inspect affected highwalls and benches before resuming work.
emergencyResponse: Evacuate all personnel from the affected area immediately upon signs of ground movement.
Establish an expanded exclusion zone and prevent re-entry until assessed by geotechnical personnel.
Report all ground failures, rockfalls, and near misses to MSHA as required.
documentation: Ground control plan
Daily inspection records
Slope monitoring data
Incident and near-miss reports
references: MSHA 30 CFR 56.3130 — Wall, Bank, and Slope Stability
MSHA 30 CFR 77.1000 — Surface Coal Ground Control
Company Geotechnical Management Plan

title: Conveyor / Crushing / Screening Safety SOP
summary: Safe work procedures for maintenance, operation, and access around conveyors, crushers, and screening equipment in mining and processing operations.

sections.purpose: To prevent injuries from entanglement, caught-between, and struck-by hazards associated with conveyors, crushers, and screening equipment.
sections.scope: Applies to all personnel operating, maintaining, or working near conveyor systems, crushers, screens, and associated processing equipment.

definitions.definition: The point where two rotating parts meet or where a belt contacts a pulley, creating a pinch hazard.
A device that immediately stops equipment operation when activated.
Physical barriers preventing contact with moving parts.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Conduct pre-start inspections, monitor equipment during operation, and report deficiencies.
Follow LOTO procedures, inspect guarding, and repair defects before returning equipment to service.
Verify guards are in place, LOTO is followed, and personnel are trained.

training: Conveyor and crusher hazard awareness
LOTO procedures for processing equipment
Emergency stop location and operation
Guard inspection and reporting
ppe: Hard hat
Safety glasses
Hearing protection
Steel-toe boots
Close-fitting clothing (no loose items)
Dust mask or respirator as needed
hazards: Entanglement in conveyor belts, rollers, and pulleys
Caught-between at nip points and pinch points
Struck-by from material falling off conveyors
Crusher feed and discharge point hazards
Dust exposure from crushing and screening operations
procedure: Verify all guards are in place and secure before starting equipment.
Test emergency stop pull cords and buttons before each shift.
Never reach into, under, or across a moving conveyor.
Use LOTO procedures for all maintenance, cleanup, and unjamming tasks.
Maintain required clearances around crusher feed and discharge areas.
Use walkways and crossovers — never walk on or climb over conveyors.
Monitor dust levels and ensure suppression systems are functioning.
Report any unusual noise, vibration, or belt tracking issues immediately.
emergencyResponse: Activate emergency stop immediately if anyone is in contact with moving equipment.
Do NOT attempt to free an entangled person by reversing equipment without direction from emergency personnel.
Call emergency services and mine rescue if entrapment occurs.
documentation: Pre-shift inspection records
LOTO verification records
Guard inspection logs
Training records
references: MSHA 30 CFR 56.14107 — Moving Machine Parts
MSHA 30 CFR 56.14112 — Construction and Maintenance of Guards
Company LOTO Program

title: Mine Ventilation / Air Quality SOP
summary: Procedures for maintaining adequate ventilation and air quality in mining operations, including dust control, gas monitoring, and ventilation system management.

sections.purpose: To establish requirements for maintaining safe atmospheric conditions in mining operations through ventilation, dust control, and air quality monitoring.
sections.scope: Applies to all surface and underground mining operations where ventilation, dust, or atmospheric hazards may be present.

definitions.definition: A documented plan describing air flow requirements, fan capacity, and monitoring protocols.
Fine particulate matter small enough to be inhaled deep into the lungs.
Permissible Exposure Limit — the maximum allowable concentration of a substance in air.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Design and maintain ventilation systems, monitor air quality, and update ventilation plans.
Ensure ventilation systems are operational, dust suppression is active, and monitoring is current.
Use respiratory protection as required, report air quality concerns, and follow dust control procedures.

training: Mine ventilation fundamentals
Dust sampling and monitoring
Respiratory protection use and fit testing
Gas detection equipment operation
ppe: Respiratory protection (dust mask or respirator as required)
Safety glasses
Hard hat
Hearing protection
hazards: Respirable dust (silica, coal dust) exposure
Methane accumulation (coal mines)
Oxygen deficiency or enrichment
Diesel particulate matter exposure
Toxic gas exposure (CO, NO2, SO2)
procedure: Maintain ventilation systems per the approved ventilation plan.
Conduct air quality monitoring at the start of each shift and at regular intervals.
Ensure dust suppression systems (water sprays, foam, enclosures) are operational.
Monitor personal dust exposure with sampling equipment as required by MSHA.
In underground mines, test for methane before and during operations in applicable areas.
Report ventilation system failures or air quality concerns immediately.
Maintain ventilation records and dust sampling results per MSHA requirements.
emergencyResponse: Evacuate the area if atmospheric monitoring indicates unsafe conditions.
Activate emergency ventilation fans if available.
Report atmospheric hazards to mine control/dispatch immediately.
documentation: Ventilation plan
Air monitoring records
Dust sampling results
Training records
Equipment calibration logs

MSHA 30 CFR Part 70/71 — Coal Dust Standards

title: Emergency Response / Evacuation for Mining SOP
summary: Emergency response and evacuation procedures for mining operations including mine rescue, fire response, ground failure events, and atmospheric emergencies.

sections.purpose: To establish emergency response and evacuation procedures for mine sites to protect all personnel during fire, ground failure, atmospheric emergency, or other critical events.
sections.scope: Applies to all mine personnel, contractors, and visitors at the mine site.

definitions.definition: A trained team equipped to perform rescue operations in mine emergency situations.
A sealed chamber providing breathable air, communication, and supplies for trapped miners.
A personal emergency breathing device carried by underground miners.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Ensure emergency plans are current, rescue teams are trained, and drills are conducted.
Coordinate emergency response training, maintain equipment, and lead evacuation drills.
Know evacuation routes, carry self-rescuers (underground), and follow emergency procedures.

training: Emergency evacuation procedures and routes
Self-rescuer donning and use (underground)
Fire suppression equipment operation
Mine rescue awareness
First aid and CPR
ppe: Self-rescuer (underground mines)
Hard hat with cap lamp
Communication device
First aid kit
hazards: Mine fire or explosion
Ground failure or entrapment
Atmospheric emergency (gas, dust)
Flooding or inundation
Equipment-related emergencies
procedure: Maintain a current emergency response plan approved by mine management.
Post evacuation routes and assembly areas at all mine locations.
Conduct emergency evacuation drills at least quarterly.
Ensure mine rescue teams are trained and equipped per MSHA requirements.
Maintain communication systems (phones, radios, tracking) for emergency use.
In underground mines, ensure self-rescuers are available and personnel are trained in their use.
Stage fire suppression equipment at high-risk areas.
emergencyResponse: Activate the mine alarm and notify mine control/dispatch.
Evacuate all personnel via the designated escape routes.
Account for all personnel at the assembly area.
Do not re-enter the mine until authorized by mine management and rescue teams.
Contact MSHA and local emergency services as required.

title: Hazard Communication Program (HAZCOM) SOP
summary: A complete Hazard Communication SOP covering chemical inventory, labeling, SDS management, training, storage, handling, spill response, contractor communication, and program auditing.

sections.purpose: This procedure establishes the requirements for [Company Name]'s Hazard Communication Program in compliance with OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1200 (General Industry) and 29 CFR 1926.59 (Construction). The purpose is to ensure that all employees, contractors, and visitors are informed about the hazardous chemicals present in the workplace, understand the risks associated with those chemicals, and know how to protect themselves. This program defines the methods used to identify, communicate, and control chemical hazards through proper labeling, Safety Data Sheet (SDS) management, employee training, and safe work practices.
sections.scope: This SOP applies to all [Company Name] operations at [Site Name] where hazardous chemicals are used, stored, handled, transported, or may be encountered during routine and non-routine work activities. This includes all employees, temporary workers, contractors, subcontractors, and visitors who may be exposed to hazardous chemicals. The program covers all chemical products as defined by the GHS-aligned OSHA Hazard Communication Standard, including raw materials, intermediates, finished products, by-products, waste chemicals, and any substance for which a Safety Data Sheet exists or is required. Exemptions include articles, food and beverages for employee consumption, consumer products used in the workplace in the same manner as normal consumer use, and pharmaceuticals in final form for direct patient administration.

definitions.definition: Any chemical classified as a physical hazard or a health hazard per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1200, including chemicals that are carcinogenic, toxic, irritating, corrosive, sensitizing, or act on the blood, nervous system, or organs.
A 16-section document provided by the chemical manufacturer or importer containing detailed hazard, handling, storage, emergency, and disposal information for a hazardous chemical product.
Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals — the internationally adopted system for hazard classification, labeling, and SDS formatting.
The original manufacturer's container in which the chemical is received, bearing the manufacturer's label.
Any container into which a chemical is transferred from its original (primary) container for use or storage at the workplace.
A GHS-standard symbol on a white diamond with a red border used on labels and SDSs to represent specific hazard categories (e.g., flame, skull and crossbones, exclamation mark).
A word used on GHS labels to indicate the relative severity of a hazard — either 'Danger' (more severe) or 'Warning' (less severe).

The maximum allowable airborne concentration of a chemical to which workers may be exposed, expressed as a PEL (Permissible Exposure Limit), TLV (Threshold Limit Value), or REL (Recommended Exposure Limit).
A set of standard operating procedures and controls designed to minimize employee exposure to hazardous chemicals during laboratory or production activities.
A task not regularly performed during normal operations that may expose employees to hazardous chemicals not covered under standard procedures.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Administer the Hazard Communication Program. Maintain the chemical inventory and SDS library. Conduct or coordinate HAZCOM training. Review and approve new chemical requests. Conduct program audits and inspections. Ensure regulatory compliance and update this SOP as standards change.

Ensure employees under their supervision are trained on chemical hazards before initial assignment and when new chemicals are introduced. Verify that containers are properly labeled, SDSs are accessible, and PPE is available and used. Communicate chemical hazards during pre-shift briefings and toolbox talks. Report labeling deficiencies and SDS gaps to EHS.

Attend and complete all required HAZCOM training. Read and understand labels and SDSs for chemicals used in their work area. Use required PPE as specified on the SDS and by area controls. Report unlabeled or damaged containers, missing SDSs, and chemical spills to their supervisor immediately. Never use a chemical without understanding its hazards.

Ensure that Safety Data Sheets are obtained from suppliers before any new chemical product is purchased or brought onsite. Include HAZCOM requirements in purchase orders and vendor agreements. Notify EHS of any new chemical products or changes to existing products.

Verify that all incoming chemical containers have proper GHS-compliant labels. Confirm that an SDS accompanies each new chemical shipment or is available electronically. Reject or quarantine shipments that arrive without proper labels or SDSs. Route SDSs to EHS for review and filing.

Comply with [Company Name]'s Hazard Communication Program while on site. Provide SDSs for any chemicals they bring onsite and notify [Company Name] EHS before use. Ensure their employees are trained on the hazards of chemicals they use and the chemicals present in [Company Name] work areas they may access.

training: Initial HAZCOM training for all new employees before assignment to work involving chemical exposure
Annual refresher training on the Hazard Communication Program and any changes to the chemical inventory or SDS library
Job-specific chemical hazard training when new chemicals are introduced or when work processes change
GHS label reading and interpretation — understanding pictograms, signal words, hazard statements, and precautionary statements
How to access, read, and use a 16-section Safety Data Sheet
Location and use of the chemical inventory and SDS library (physical and/or electronic)
Proper selection and use of personal protective equipment (PPE) for chemical handling
Emergency procedures for chemical spills, leaks, releases, and exposure incidents
Safe chemical storage and segregation practices
Secondary container labeling requirements
Non-routine task chemical hazard assessment procedures
Contractor chemical communication requirements
ppe: Chemical-resistant gloves appropriate to the chemical being handled (consult SDS Section 8)
Safety glasses with side shields or chemical splash goggles as indicated by the SDS
Face shield for splash or spray hazards involving corrosives or irritants
Chemical-resistant apron or full-body suit for handling corrosive, toxic, or large-volume chemicals
Respiratory protection — type selected based on the chemical, concentration, and exposure duration (consult SDS Section 8 and the Respiratory Protection Program)
Steel-toe boots or chemical-resistant footwear as appropriate to the work area
Emergency eyewash and safety shower within the work area where chemicals are used or stored
hazards: Acute toxicity — exposure to chemicals causing immediate health effects including irritation, burns, or poisoning through inhalation, skin contact, or ingestion
Chronic health effects — long-term or repeated exposure leading to organ damage, reproductive harm, or cancer
Flammable and combustible liquids — fire or explosion risk from improper storage, handling, or ignition sources near flammable chemicals
Corrosive materials — severe skin, eye, or tissue damage from contact with acids, bases, or oxidizing chemicals
Reactive or unstable chemicals — risk of violent reaction, decomposition, or explosion when exposed to heat, shock, friction, or incompatible materials
Oxygen-displacing gases — asphyxiation risk from inert gases (nitrogen, argon, helium) in enclosed or poorly ventilated areas
Environmental release — contamination of air, water, or soil from uncontrolled spills, leaks, or improper waste disposal
Sensitizers and allergens — development of allergic responses (skin or respiratory) from repeated exposure to certain chemicals
procedure: HAZARDOUS CHEMICAL APPROVAL: No new chemical product shall be introduced to any [Company Name] facility without prior review and approval by EHS. Requestors must submit the SDS to EHS for hazard evaluation, compatibility review, and PPE/storage determination before the chemical is ordered. EHS will assess whether existing controls are adequate or whether additional safeguards are required.

CHEMICAL INVENTORY: EHS shall maintain a current inventory of all hazardous chemicals present at [Site Name]. The inventory shall include the product name, manufacturer, SDS availability status, location(s) of use and storage, and the approximate quantity on hand. The chemical inventory shall be reviewed and updated at least [annually/semi-annually]. Departments shall notify EHS when chemicals are added, removed, or substituted.

SAFETY DATA SHEETS (SDSs): SDSs shall be maintained for every hazardous chemical on the inventory. SDSs shall be readily accessible to all employees during their work shift — electronically via [system name] and/or in hard-copy binders located at [location]. EHS is responsible for ensuring SDSs are current and complete. When a new or updated SDS is received, EHS will review it and update the inventory and any affected procedures, controls, or training materials.

LABELING — PRIMARY CONTAINERS: All containers of hazardous chemicals received from manufacturers or distributors must bear a GHS-compliant label including: product identifier, signal word (Danger or Warning), hazard statement(s), precautionary statement(s), GHS pictogram(s), and supplier identification. Do not remove, deface, or obscure manufacturer labels. Damaged or illegible labels must be replaced immediately.

LABELING — SECONDARY CONTAINERS: Any container into which a chemical is transferred from its original container must be labeled with at a minimum: the product name (matching the SDS and inventory), and the applicable hazard warnings. Acceptable secondary labels include GHS-formatted workplace labels, HMIS labels, NFPA labels, or printed labels generated from the SDS system. Pre-printed label stock shall be available in [location].

LABELING — PORTABLE CONTAINERS: A portable container (e.g., spray bottle, bucket) filled by an employee for immediate use during their shift does not require a label only if: (a) the employee who filled the container maintains continuous possession and control of the container, and (b) the contents are used during and disposed of by the end of the same work shift. If any of these conditions are not met, the container must be labeled per secondary container requirements.

LABELING — PIPING, TANKS, AND PROCESS VESSELS: Fixed piping systems, tanks, and process vessels containing hazardous chemicals shall be identified using labels, color coding, or signage per ANSI/ASME A13.1 standards. Flow direction arrows are recommended. Identification shall be placed at valves, flanges, changes in direction, and penetrations through walls or floors.

LABELING — UNLABELED CONTAINERS: Any container found without a label — or with an illegible label — must be immediately removed from service, segregated, and reported to the supervisor. The contents must be identified before the container is returned to use. If the contents cannot be identified, the container must be treated as unknown hazardous waste and disposed of per [Company Name]'s waste management procedures.

EMPLOYEE INFORMATION AND TRAINING: All employees who may be exposed to hazardous chemicals shall receive initial HAZCOM training before their first work assignment and refresher training at least annually. Training shall cover: the requirements of 29 CFR 1910.1200, location and availability of the Written Hazard Communication Program, the chemical inventory, and SDSs. It shall also address how to read and interpret GHS labels and SDSs, the physical and health hazards of chemicals in the work area, protective measures and PPE requirements, and emergency procedures for spills, exposures, and releases. Training records shall be maintained by EHS.

NON-ROUTINE TASKS: Before any non-routine task involving potential chemical exposure, the supervisor shall conduct a hazard assessment with EHS support. The assessment shall identify all chemicals that may be encountered, review the applicable SDSs, determine required PPE and controls, and brief all involved workers. Examples of non-routine tasks include tank cleaning, vessel entry, piping modifications, decommissioning equipment, and maintenance in chemical storage areas. A written non-routine task plan shall be completed using the form in Appendix D.

CHEMICAL STORAGE AND SEGREGATION: Chemicals shall be stored according to their hazard class and compatibility. Incompatible chemicals (e.g., oxidizers and flammables, acids and bases) must be segregated by physical distance, secondary containment, or separate cabinets. Flammable liquids exceeding [threshold quantity] must be stored in approved flammable storage cabinets or rooms. Storage areas shall be labeled, ventilated, and equipped with appropriate spill containment. Chemicals shall not be stored in aisles, exit routes, near ignition sources, or above eye level without secondary containment.

SAFE HANDLING REQUIREMENTS: Employees shall read and understand the SDS before handling any chemical for the first time. Use the minimum quantity of chemical necessary for the task. Transfer chemicals carefully to avoid spills — use funnels, pumps, or approved dispensing equipment. Keep containers closed when not actively dispensing. Wash hands thoroughly after handling chemicals and before eating, drinking, or smoking. Do not eat, drink, smoke, or apply cosmetics in chemical use or storage areas.

PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT: PPE for chemical handling shall be selected based on the hazards identified in SDS Section 8. EHS shall provide guidance on the proper type and rating of gloves, eye protection, respiratory protection, and body protection for each chemical. PPE shall be inspected before each use and replaced when damaged. Employees shall be trained on the proper donning, doffing, use, limitations, and care of chemical PPE.

EXPOSURE CONTROLS: Engineering controls are the primary method for reducing chemical exposures. These include local exhaust ventilation, fume hoods, enclosed processes, and substitution with less hazardous chemicals. Administrative controls include limiting exposure duration, rotating tasks, and restricting access to chemical areas. When engineering and administrative controls are insufficient to reduce exposure below the applicable PEL/TLV, respiratory protection and/or additional PPE shall be provided.

SPILLS, LEAKS, AND RELEASES: In the event of a chemical spill or release, employees shall evacuate the immediate area if the spill poses an inhalation, fire, or reactive hazard. Notify the supervisor and EHS immediately. Only trained and properly equipped personnel may perform spill cleanup. Spill kits appropriate to the chemicals used shall be maintained in [locations]. Spills exceeding reportable quantities shall be reported to the appropriate regulatory agencies per [Company Name]'s spill reporting procedure. All spills shall be documented using the incident reporting system.

EXPOSURE INCIDENTS AND MEDICAL RESPONSE: Any employee who experiences symptoms of chemical exposure (irritation, dizziness, difficulty breathing, burns, etc.) shall immediately stop work and move to fresh air. Notify the supervisor and seek medical attention. Provide the SDS to the treating medical provider. EHS shall investigate all chemical exposure incidents, document findings, and implement corrective actions to prevent recurrence. Exposure records shall be maintained per OSHA recordkeeping requirements.

CONTRACTOR HAZARD COMMUNICATION: Before starting work, contractors shall be informed of the hazardous chemicals present in or near their work areas and the location of the SDS library. Contractors shall provide [Company Name] with SDSs for any chemical products they bring onsite. Contractor chemicals must be properly labeled and stored per this SOP. Contractor compliance with the HAZCOM program shall be verified during contractor orientations and periodic inspections.

WASTE CHEMICALS AND DISPOSAL: Waste chemicals, empty containers, and contaminated materials shall be disposed of in accordance with federal, state, and local regulations and [Company Name]'s waste management procedures. Do not pour chemicals down drains, into trash, or onto the ground. Waste containers must be labeled with the contents and the words 'Hazardous Waste' or as otherwise required. Contact EHS for disposal guidance for unknown or mixed waste streams.

PROGRAM AUDITING AND REVIEW: EHS shall audit the Hazard Communication Program at least annually. Audits shall verify that: the chemical inventory is accurate and current, SDSs are accessible for all listed chemicals, container labels are present and legible, employee training records are complete and current, storage and segregation practices meet requirements, and contractor HAZCOM compliance is being verified. Audit findings shall be documented, and corrective actions shall be tracked to completion. This SOP shall be reviewed and updated at least annually or when regulatory changes, new operations, or audit findings require revision.
emergencyResponse: In the event of a large or uncontrolled chemical spill, activate the area alarm and evacuate all personnel to the designated assembly point upwind of the release.
Call emergency services (911) and [Company Name] Emergency Response Team at [emergency number] immediately for fires, explosions, or releases involving highly toxic or reactive chemicals.
Do not attempt to clean up large or unknown spills without proper training, PPE, and authorization. Secure the area and restrict access.
For skin contact with corrosives or toxic chemicals, flush the affected area with water for at least 15 minutes using the nearest safety shower or eyewash station. Remove contaminated clothing.
For eye contact, flush eyes with water at the nearest eyewash station for at least 15 minutes while holding eyelids open. Seek immediate medical attention.

For inhalation exposure, move the affected person to fresh air immediately. If the person is not breathing, call 911 and begin CPR if trained. Do not enter a potentially contaminated atmosphere without respiratory protection.
Provide the SDS for the chemical involved to first responders and medical personnel.
Document all emergency responses, exposures, and near-misses using [Company Name]'s incident reporting system and notify EHS within [timeframe].
