title: Respiratory Protection Program
summary: A comprehensive Respiratory Protection Program covering hazard identification, respirator selection, medical evaluation, fit testing, use and maintenance, cartridge change-out, voluntary use, IDLH controls, breathing air quality, contractor coordination, training, and program review.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, evaluating, selecting, using, maintaining, and administering respiratory protection in the workplace at [Company Name]. The purpose is to prevent injury, illness, serious injury, fatality, and operational disruption resulting from exposure to airborne contaminants, oxygen-deficient atmospheres, hazardous dusts, fumes, mists, vapors, gases, biological hazards, and other respiratory hazards that cannot be adequately controlled by elimination, substitution, engineering controls, or administrative controls alone.

Respiratory hazards can cause acute injury, chronic illness, permanent disability, and death. This program requires systematic hazard assessment, proper respirator selection, medical evaluation, fit testing, training, inspection, maintenance, and program administration to protect all personnel from respiratory exposures.

This procedure supports compliance with OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134, applicable substance-specific standards, and company policies. It applies to both mandatory and voluntary respirator use.
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, laboratories, production areas, maintenance activities, field locations, test areas, fabrication areas, assembly areas, confined space work, emergency response support activities, and non-routine operations where employees or contractors may be required or permitted to use respiratory protection.

This procedure applies to:
• Employees required to wear respirators
• Employees who voluntarily wear respirators where allowed
• Supervisors and managers responsible for work involving respiratory hazards
• EHS personnel
• Industrial hygiene personnel or other qualified hazard assessors
• Maintenance personnel responsible for respirator upkeep where applicable
• Fit test administrators and trainers
• Contractors performing work requiring respirator use onsite
• Emergency response personnel where respiratory protection is required

This procedure applies to respiratory protection used for, but not limited to:
• Dusts, fumes, mists, vapors, gases, smoke, and aerosols
• Asphyxiants and oxygen-deficient atmospheres
• Cleaning chemicals
• Painting, coating, or solvent use
• Welding, cutting, and brazing operations
• Grinding, sanding, or abrasive work
• Chemical transfers
• Spill response support where authorized
• Confined space operations where respiratory protection is required
• Maintenance, inspection, and non-routine work with airborne exposure potential

Respirator types covered include, but are not limited to:
• Filtering facepiece respirators (N95, P100, etc.)
• Elastomeric half-mask respirators
• Elastomeric full-face respirators
• Powered air-purifying respirators (PAPRs)
• Supplied-air respirators (SARs)
• Self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA), where applicable and authorized
• Escape respirators where specifically designated

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with other required programs such as Hazard Communication, Confined Space Entry, Asphyxiants, Welding/Cutting/Brazing, Chemical Safety, Emergency Response, and Permit-to-Work requirements where applicable.

definitions.definition: A respirator that removes specific air contaminants by passing ambient air through a filter, cartridge, or canister. APRs do not supply oxygen and shall not be used in oxygen-deficient or IDLH atmospheres.

The workplace level of respiratory protection that a respirator or class of respirators is expected to provide to employees who have been properly fitted and trained, as specified by OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134.
A respirator that supplies the user with breathing air from a source independent of the ambient atmosphere. Includes supplied-air respirators (SARs) and self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA).
A container filled with sorbent material designed to remove specific gases and vapors from inspired air. Cartridges are used on half-mask and full-face air-purifying respirators.

A negative-pressure particulate respirator with a filter as an integral part of the facepiece or with the entire facepiece composed of the filtering medium. Common examples include N95, N99, N100, R95, P95, and P100 designations.

A quantitative estimate of the fit of a particular respirator to a specific individual, typically expressed as the ratio of the concentration of a substance outside the respirator to the concentration inside.
The use of a protocol to qualitatively or quantitatively evaluate the fit of a specific make, model, style, and size of respirator on an individual.

definitions.term: Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health (IDLH)
definitions.definition: Any condition that poses an immediate or delayed threat to life, would cause irreversible adverse health effects, or would interfere with an individual's ability to escape unaided from a dangerous atmosphere.
An assessment performed by a physician or other licensed health care professional (PLHCP) to determine whether an employee is medically able to use a respirator.

A respirator in which the air pressure inside the facepiece is negative during inhalation relative to the ambient air pressure outside the facepiece. Includes filtering facepiece and elastomeric APRs without powered assistance.
A respirator in which the pressure inside the facepiece exceeds the ambient air pressure outside during inhalation. Includes PAPRs and pressure-demand SARs/SCBAs.
An air-purifying respirator that uses a blower to force ambient air through air-purifying elements to the inlet covering (hood, helmet, or facepiece).
A pass/fail fit test that relies on the individual's sensory detection of a test agent (e.g., Bitrex, saccharin, irritant smoke) leaking into the respirator facepiece.
A fit test that uses an instrument to measure the amount of leakage into the respirator facepiece and produces a numerical fit factor.
A device designed to protect the wearer from inhalation of hazardous atmospheres, including air-purifying and atmosphere-supplying devices.
An action conducted by the respirator user to determine if the respirator is properly seated on the face each time it is donned. A seal check is not a substitute for a fit test.
Respirator use by an employee who chooses to wear a respirator when not required by the employer or regulation. Voluntary use is subject to specific requirements under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Administer the Respiratory Protection Program. Identify respiratory hazards and determine when respirators are required. Select appropriate respirator types based on hazard assessment. Coordinate medical evaluations, fit testing, and training. Maintain program records, equipment inventories, and change-out schedules. Conduct or coordinate exposure assessments and industrial hygiene sampling. Review and approve respirator selection for new tasks, chemicals, or conditions. Conduct program audits and update the program as standards, hazards, or operations change. Investigate respiratory incidents and implement corrective actions.

Ensure employees required to use respirators have completed medical evaluation, fit testing, and training before respirator use. Monitor compliance with respirator use, seal checks, maintenance, and change-out requirements. Enforce facial hair and PPE compatibility requirements. Remove employees from respirator-required tasks if medical clearance, fit testing, or training is not current. Report respiratory hazard concerns, equipment issues, and incidents to EHS. Support periodic workplace evaluations and exposure assessments. Ensure contractors comply with site respiratory protection requirements.

Use assigned or approved respirators as trained and when required. Perform seal checks each time a tight-fitting respirator is donned. Inspect respirators before each use and report defects. Clean, maintain, and store respirators per training and manufacturer instructions. Comply with facial hair restrictions and PPE compatibility requirements. Replace cartridges and filters per change-out schedules or when conditions require. Leave the work area if the respirator malfunctions, seal is lost, breathing becomes difficult, or breakthrough is suspected. Report respiratory concerns, equipment problems, and incidents to the supervisor immediately. Complete required medical evaluations, fit testing, and training on time. Do not modify, alter, or misuse assigned respiratory equipment.

Conduct medical evaluations per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134 using the required questionnaire or examination. Determine whether the employee is medically able to use the type of respirator assigned under expected work conditions. Provide written medical clearance or restrictions to the employer as required. Conduct reevaluations when indicated by medical signs, symptoms, workplace changes, or program requirements. Maintain medical records in accordance with applicable privacy and retention requirements.

Conduct fit testing using approved protocols per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134 Appendix A. Verify facial hair compliance before testing. Record fit test results and maintain records. Provide training on donning, doffing, seal checks, limitations, and proper use. Identify employees who cannot achieve an adequate fit and coordinate alternatives. Report recurring fit issues or equipment problems to the Program Administrator.

Clean, disinfect, inspect, and maintain reusable respirators per manufacturer instructions and company requirements. Replace worn or damaged components with manufacturer-approved parts. Maintain emergency-use respirators in designated locations and inspect them at required intervals. Track respirator inventory and cartridge/filter stock. Report equipment condition issues to EHS.

Comply with [Company Name] respiratory protection requirements while onsite. Ensure contractor employees required to wear respirators are medically evaluated, fit tested, and trained per OSHA and site requirements. Communicate respiratory hazards introduced by contractor work. Report incidents, near misses, and unsafe conditions to [Company Name] supervision and EHS.

procedure:
**1. PURPOSE**

This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, evaluating, selecting, using, maintaining, and administering respiratory protection in the workplace at [Company Name]. The purpose is to prevent injury, illness, serious injury, fatality, and operational disruption resulting from exposure to airborne contaminants, oxygen-deficient atmospheres, hazardous dusts, fumes, mists, vapors, gases, biological hazards, and other respiratory hazards that cannot be adequately controlled by elimination, substitution, engineering controls, or administrative controls alone.

Document No.: [Document No.]
Effective Date: [Effective Date]
Revision: [Revision]
Owner: [Owner]
Approved By: [Approved By]

**2. SCOPE**

This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, laboratories, production areas, maintenance activities, field locations, test areas, fabrication areas, assembly areas, confined space work, emergency response support activities, and non-routine operations where employees or contractors may be required or permitted to use respiratory protection at [Site Name].

Personnel covered include employees required to wear respirators, employees who voluntarily wear respirators where allowed, supervisors and managers, EHS personnel, industrial hygiene personnel, maintenance personnel, fit test administrators, trainers, contractors, and emergency response personnel.

Respirator types covered include filtering facepiece respirators, elastomeric half-mask and full-face respirators, powered air-purifying respirators (PAPRs), supplied-air respirators (SARs), self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA) where applicable, and escape respirators where designated.

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with Hazard Communication, Confined Space Entry, Asphyxiants, Welding/Cutting/Brazing, Chemical Safety, Emergency Response, and Permit-to-Work programs where applicable.

**3. REFERENCES**

• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134 — Respiratory Protection
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1200 — Hazard Communication
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.146 — Permit-Required Confined Spaces where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910 Subpart I — Personal Protective Equipment
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1000 — Air Contaminants where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1025 — Lead where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1048 — Formaldehyde where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1053 — Respirable Crystalline Silica where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.252 — Welding, Cutting, and Brazing where applicable
• NIOSH respirator selection and certification guidance where applicable
• ANSI/ASSP Z88.2 — Practices for Respiratory Protection where applicable
• Manufacturer instructions for respirators, cartridges, filters, PAPRs, SCBA, and related equipment
• [Company Name] Hazard Communication Program
• [Company Name] Confined Space Entry Procedure
• [Company Name] Asphyxiants Safety Procedure
• [Company Name] Emergency Response Plan
• [Company Name] Medical Surveillance Program where applicable
• [Company Name] Incident Reporting and Investigation Procedure
• Site-specific industrial hygiene assessment procedures where applicable
• [Applicable State / Local Requirements]
• [Related Company Procedures]

**4. DEFINITIONS**

Air-Purifying Respirator (APR): A respirator that removes specific air contaminants by passing ambient air through a filter, cartridge, or canister. APRs do not supply oxygen.

Assigned Protection Factor (APF): The workplace level of respiratory protection that a respirator or class of respirators is expected to provide to properly fitted and trained employees, as specified by OSHA.

Atmosphere-Supplying Respirator: A respirator that supplies the user with breathing air from a source independent of the ambient atmosphere, including SARs and SCBAs.

Cartridge / Canister: A container filled with sorbent material designed to remove specific gases and vapors from inspired air.

Filtering Facepiece Respirator: A negative-pressure particulate respirator with a filter as an integral part of the facepiece (e.g., N95, P100).

Fit Factor: A quantitative estimate of the fit of a particular respirator to a specific individual.

Fit Test: A protocol to qualitatively or quantitatively evaluate the fit of a specific make, model, style, and size of respirator on an individual.

Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health (IDLH): Any condition posing an immediate or delayed threat to life, irreversible health effects, or interference with the ability to escape.

Medical Evaluation: An assessment by a PLHCP to determine whether an employee is medically able to use a respirator.

Negative-Pressure Respirator: A respirator where air pressure inside the facepiece is negative during inhalation relative to ambient.

Positive-Pressure Respirator: A respirator where pressure inside the facepiece exceeds ambient during inhalation (PAPRs, pressure-demand SARs/SCBAs).

Powered Air-Purifying Respirator (PAPR): An APR using a blower to force ambient air through air-purifying elements.

Qualitative Fit Test (QLFT): A pass/fail fit test relying on sensory detection of a test agent leaking into the facepiece.

Quantitative Fit Test (QNFT): A fit test using an instrument to measure leakage and produce a numerical fit factor.

Respirator: A device designed to protect the wearer from inhalation of hazardous atmospheres.

Seal Check / User Seal Check: An action by the user to determine if the respirator is properly seated on the face each time it is donned. Not a substitute for a fit test.

Voluntary Use: Respirator use by an employee who chooses to wear a respirator when not required.

**5. POLICY**

[Company Name] will identify and control respiratory hazards through elimination, substitution, engineering controls, administrative controls, and, where necessary, properly selected and managed respiratory protection.

Respirators shall be used only when:
• Respiratory hazards have been evaluated
• Engineering and administrative controls are not feasible or sufficient
• The respirator has been selected for the specific hazard and exposure
• The employee has completed required medical evaluation, fit testing, and training
• The respirator is in safe condition and properly maintained
• Program requirements for use, inspection, storage, and change-out are followed

The company will prioritize control of airborne hazards at the source before relying on respirators.

No employee shall be required or permitted to use a respirator in a manner inconsistent with its certification, limitations, or manufacturer instructions.

Air-purifying respirators shall not be used in oxygen-deficient or IDLH atmospheres unless specifically approved by regulation, hazard assessment, and company procedure.

Unauthorized emergency entry or rescue using respirators is prohibited.

Employees shall not remove, modify, or misuse assigned respiratory protection equipment.

**6. RESPONSIBILITIES**

**EHS / Respiratory Program Administrator:**
Administer the Respiratory Protection Program. Identify respiratory hazards and determine when respirators are required. Select appropriate respirator types based on hazard assessment. Coordinate medical evaluations, fit testing, and training. Maintain program records, equipment inventories, and change-out schedules. Conduct or coordinate exposure assessments and industrial hygiene sampling. Review and approve respirator selection for new tasks, chemicals, or conditions. Conduct program audits and update the program as needed. Investigate respiratory incidents and implement corrective actions.

**Managers and Supervisors:**
Ensure employees have completed medical evaluation, fit testing, and training before respirator use. Monitor compliance with respirator use, seal checks, maintenance, and change-out requirements. Enforce facial hair and PPE compatibility requirements. Remove employees from tasks if clearances are not current. Report concerns and incidents to EHS. Support workplace evaluations. Ensure contractor compliance.

**Employees / Respirator Users:**
Use assigned respirators as trained and when required. Perform seal checks each time a tight-fitting respirator is donned. Inspect respirators before use and report defects. Clean, maintain, and store respirators properly. Comply with facial hair restrictions. Replace cartridges/filters per schedule. Leave the area if the respirator malfunctions or breakthrough is suspected. Report concerns immediately. Complete medical evaluations, fit testing, and training on time.

**Medical Provider / Occupational Health Support:**
Conduct medical evaluations per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134. Determine medical ability to use assigned respirator types. Provide written clearance or restrictions. Conduct reevaluations as indicated. Maintain records per applicable privacy requirements.

**Fit Test Administrators / Trainers:**
Conduct fit testing using approved protocols. Verify facial hair compliance. Record results. Provide training on donning, doffing, seal checks, limitations, and use. Identify employees who cannot achieve adequate fit and coordinate alternatives.

**Maintenance / Equipment Custodians:**
Clean, disinfect, inspect, and maintain reusable respirators per manufacturer instructions. Replace worn or damaged components with approved parts. Maintain emergency-use respirators in designated locations. Track inventory and supplies.

**Contractors:**
Comply with site respiratory protection requirements. Ensure employees are medically evaluated, fit tested, and trained per OSHA and site requirements. Communicate respiratory hazards introduced by contractor work. Report incidents and unsafe conditions.

**7.1 RESPIRATORY HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND ASSESSMENT**

Work tasks and environments shall be evaluated before respirators are assigned or used. Hazard identification shall consider:
• Contaminant type — dust, fume, mist, vapor, gas, smoke, aerosol, biological agent
• Contaminant concentration and applicable exposure limits (PEL, TLV, REL, STEL, ceiling)
• Duration and frequency of exposure
• Physical form of the contaminant and particle size where applicable
• Oxygen deficiency potential
• IDLH potential
• Work location — indoors, outdoors, enclosed, confined space
• Simultaneous activities that may add or change airborne hazards
• Temperature, humidity, and physical work demands
• Feasibility and adequacy of engineering controls
• Emergency release potential or upset conditions

Methods include SDS review, process knowledge, air monitoring, industrial hygiene sampling, manufacturer/regulatory guidance, observation of work practices, ventilation review, and incident history.

If the hazard cannot be adequately characterized, a conservative interim approach shall be used, and the assessment shall be completed before finalizing respirator selection and work authorization.

**7.2 HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS**

The preferred order of control is:
1. Eliminate the hazard — remove the source of airborne contamination
2. Substitute less hazardous materials or processes
3. Use engineering controls — local exhaust ventilation, enclosures, isolation, wet methods, automation
4. Use administrative controls — limit exposure time, restrict access, schedule work, work practice controls
5. Use respiratory protection — when other controls are not feasible, not adequate, or are being installed

Respirators shall not be used as the sole long-term control when feasible engineering controls can adequately reduce exposure.

[Company Name] will prioritize source control and hazard reduction over reliance on personal respiratory protection wherever practicable.

**7.3 RESPIRATOR SELECTION**

Respirator selection shall be based on the specific hazard, exposure level, task demands, work environment, and respirator limitations.

Selection factors include:
• Contaminant type and concentration
• Applicable exposure limits and required protection factor
• Warning properties of the contaminant
• Oxygen content of the atmosphere
• IDLH potential
• Duration and nature of the task
• Physical work demands
• Facial fit needs and facial characteristics
• Eye and face protection needs
• Mobility and communication needs
• Heat stress burden
• PPE compatibility (hard hat, hearing protection, face shield, welding hood)

Respirator types include:
• Filtering facepiece (N95, P100, etc.) — particulates within APF limits
• Half-mask elastomeric APR — particulates and/or gases/vapors within APF limits
• Full-face elastomeric APR — higher protection factor, eye protection, particulates and/or gases/vapors
• PAPR — reduced breathing resistance, higher APF for some configurations, accommodates facial hair for loose-fitting models
• SAR — atmosphere-supplying, higher protection, continuous or pressure-demand modes
• SCBA — highest protection, self-contained air supply, IDLH and emergency use

Air-purifying respirators shall NOT be selected for:
• Oxygen-deficient atmospheres
• Atmospheres above respirator or cartridge limits
• IDLH atmospheres unless specifically approved
• Contaminants without appropriate cartridges or filters
• Conditions where breakthrough, saturation, or warning-property control is inadequate

**7.4 MEDICAL EVALUATION**

Medical evaluation is required before fit testing or required respirator use.

The evaluation determines whether the employee is physically able to use the assigned respirator under expected conditions. Evaluation shall be conducted by a physician or other licensed health care professional (PLHCP) using the OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134 Appendix C questionnaire or an equivalent medical examination.

Medical reevaluation is required when:
• The employee reports symptoms affecting respirator use
• A provider, supervisor, or program administrator identifies a need
• Workplace conditions change significantly
• Respirator type changes significantly (e.g., from APR to SCBA)
• Observations during fit testing or use indicate a problem
• Policy or regulation requires periodic reevaluation

Employees shall not use required respirators until medically cleared.

Medical records shall be maintained in accordance with applicable privacy and retention requirements.

**7.5 FIT TESTING**

Fit testing is required for tight-fitting respirators:
• Before initial use
• Whenever a different facepiece (make, model, style, or size) is assigned
• At least annually thereafter, or more frequently if required

Repeat testing is required when:
• Facial changes (weight change, dental work, scarring, surgery) may affect fit
• The employee reports poor fit or difficulty obtaining a seal
• A different size, model, or style is needed

Qualitative fit testing (QLFT) or quantitative fit testing (QNFT) may be used depending on respirator type, required protection factor, and program requirements.

Employees shall be clean-shaven in the sealing area for tight-fitting respirators unless a specific approved device allows otherwise.

Employees who cannot obtain an adequate fit shall be evaluated for alternatives such as different models, sizes, or PAPRs where appropriate.

Fit test results shall be documented and maintained.

**7.6 TRAINING AND USER QUALIFICATION**

Training is required before initial respirator use and when conditions, respirator type, or performance deficiencies indicate a need for retraining.

Training shall include:
• Why the respirator is necessary and why engineering/administrative controls alone are insufficient
• Respirator capabilities and limitations
• Proper donning, doffing, positioning, and strap adjustment
• Positive-pressure and negative-pressure seal checks per manufacturer instructions
• Maintenance, cleaning, disinfection, inspection, and storage
• Cartridge and filter replacement schedules and criteria
• Recognition of malfunction, breakthrough, or fit loss
• Medical signs and symptoms affecting safe respirator use
• Emergency procedures and when to leave the work area
• Voluntary use rules where applicable
• Facial hair and PPE compatibility requirements
• Incident and near-miss reporting
• Stop-work authority

Employees shall demonstrate understanding and ability to use assigned respirators before independent use.

**7.7 USE OF RESPIRATORS**

Respirators shall be worn whenever required by hazard assessment, work procedure, permit, or supervisor direction.

Requirements include:
• Use only the assigned or approved respirator for the task
• Inspect the respirator before each use
• Perform seal checks each time a tight-fitting respirator is donned
• Wear the respirator for the full duration of the required exposure period
• Leave the area immediately if the respirator malfunctions, seal is lost, breathing becomes difficult, or warning signs of breakthrough occur
• Maintain the respirator per training and manufacturer instructions
• Do not share individually assigned tight-fitting respirators without proper cleaning and control

Prohibited practices:
• Use inconsistent with training or manufacturer instructions
• Use with facial hair that interferes with the seal of tight-fitting respirators
• Unauthorized modifications to the respirator
• Use of damaged respirators or respirators with missing critical components
• Continued use after detecting breakthrough, odor, taste, or irritation where applicable

**7.8 USER SEAL CHECKS**

Positive-pressure and/or negative-pressure user seal checks shall be performed per manufacturer instructions each time a tight-fitting respirator is donned.

If a proper seal cannot be achieved:
• Readjust the facepiece and straps
• Inspect for damage, obstruction, or improper assembly
• Try a different size or model if needed
• Do not enter the hazardous area until a proper seal is confirmed

A seal check does not replace fit testing. Seal checks verify proper donning; fit testing verifies facepiece compatibility with facial characteristics.

**7.9 VOLUNTARY USE OF RESPIRATORS**

Voluntary respirator use is allowed only when it does not create a hazard.

Conditions for voluntary use:
• The respirator itself must not create a medical or safety hazard
• The employee must receive required information on use and limitations (OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134 Appendix D for filtering facepieces)
• The respirator selected must be appropriate for the conditions
• Tight-fitting voluntary-use respirators may require medical evaluation depending on type and program policy
• Filtering facepiece voluntary use may have simplified requirements where allowed by regulation

Voluntary use does NOT authorize employees to:
• Enter hazardous atmospheres requiring mandatory respirator use
• Perform respirator-required tasks without full program compliance
• Exceed the limitations of the respirator selected

**7.10 FACIAL HAIR, EYEWEAR, AND OTHER INTERFERENCES**

Facial hair that comes between the sealing surface of the facepiece and the face, or that interferes with valve function, is prohibited for tight-fitting respirators. This includes stubble, beards, mustaches extending past the sealing area, and sideburns that interfere with the seal.

Clothing, jewelry, head coverings, or other conditions that interfere with the seal or valve function are also prohibited.

Prescription glasses, face shields, hard hats, hearing protection, welding hoods, and other PPE must be compatible with the assigned respirator and shall not break the seal or interfere with respirator function.

Where facial hair or other limitations prevent effective use of tight-fitting respirators, alternative approved respiratory protection (e.g., PAPRs with loose-fitting hoods or helmets) shall be evaluated.

**7.11 CARTRIDGE, CANISTER, AND FILTER CHANGE-OUT**

Replacement is required based on:
• Manufacturer recommendations
• End-of-service-life indicators (ESLI) where applicable
• Established change-out schedules
• Task duration and exposure conditions
• Increased breathing resistance
• Damage, contamination, or saturation
• Company program requirements

Particulate filter replacement is required when:
• Breathing resistance increases noticeably
• The filter is damaged, wet, soiled, or contaminated

For gas/vapor cartridges without ESLI:
• Approved change-out schedules shall be established based on contaminant type, concentration, humidity, breathing rate, and cartridge capacity
• Employees shall not rely solely on odor or taste for change-out unless specifically allowed by hazard characteristics and program controls

Replacement cartridges and filters shall be available at the work location or readily accessible.

**7.12 CLEANING, DISINFECTION, AND STORAGE**

Reusable respirators shall be cleaned, disinfected, dried, and stored per manufacturer instructions and company requirements.

Cleaning frequency:
• As needed to maintain sanitary condition
• Before reassignment to another user
• After contamination, heavy soiling, or chemical exposure
• After emergency or special-use exposure where required

Storage requirements:
• Protect from dust, sunlight, heat, freezing, moisture, chemicals, and physical damage
• Store to prevent deformation of facepiece, valves, and straps
• Emergency-use respirators shall be stored in designated, clearly marked, accessible locations

Dirty, damaged, or improperly stored respirators shall not be used.

**7.13 INSPECTION AND MAINTENANCE**

Inspection is required before each use and during cleaning.

Inspect:
• Facepiece for cracks, tears, distortion, or deterioration
• Straps and harness for elasticity, fraying, or damage
• Inhalation and exhalation valves for function and condition
• Lens or visor for scratches, cracks, or fogging
• Cartridges/filters for proper installation, damage, or expiration
• Connectors, gaskets, and seals for integrity
• PAPR blower, battery, and hose for function where applicable
• SAR hoses, regulators, fittings, and airline connections where applicable
• SCBA cylinder condition and pressure where applicable

Defective respirators shall be removed from service until repaired or replaced.

Repairs shall be performed only by qualified personnel using manufacturer-approved parts and methods.

Emergency-use respirators shall be inspected at intervals consistent with regulatory and site requirements and after each use.

**7.14 BREATHING AIR QUALITY AND ATMOSPHERE-SUPPLYING SYSTEMS**

Breathing air shall meet applicable quality requirements (OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134(i), CGA Grade D or equivalent) and come from an approved source.

Requirements include:
• Breathing air purity testing at required intervals
• Compressor maintenance, monitoring, and alarm systems
• Carbon monoxide monitoring or high-temperature alarm on oil-lubricated compressors
• Breathing-air couplings incompatible with non-respirable gas systems
• Cylinder maintenance, inspection, hydrostatic testing, and filling per applicable standards
• Airline protection from contamination, kinking, and physical damage
• Air supply adequate for duration and demand of the task

Only authorized personnel shall use SARs or SCBA, and only for tasks and conditions approved by the program.

**7.15 IDLH, OXYGEN-DEFICIENT, AND EMERGENCY CONDITIONS**

Entry into IDLH or oxygen-deficient atmospheres requires special controls and is prohibited except under approved procedures.

Requirements include:
• SCBA or approved atmosphere-supplying respirator with escape provisions
• Trained and authorized personnel only
• Standby/rescue provisions — at least one standby person equipped and trained for rescue where required
• Communication and retrieval planning
• Entry authorization under confined space, emergency response, or specialty procedures

Air-purifying respirators shall NOT be used in oxygen-deficient or IDLH atmospheres.

Unauthorized emergency response or rescue entry is prohibited.

Personnel discovering a hazardous atmosphere or a person in distress shall not enter but shall activate the emergency response system and prevent others from entering.

**7.16 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION**

[Company Name] and contractor employers shall coordinate before work requiring respiratory protection begins.

[Company Name] shall communicate:
• Known respiratory hazards at the work location
• Site respirator requirements and restrictions
• Permit, confined space, and emergency restrictions
• Decontamination or cartridge change-out requirements where applicable
• Incident reporting and stop-work expectations

Contractors shall communicate:
• Respiratory hazards introduced by their work
• Respirator types to be used onsite
• Whether employees are medically cleared, fit tested, and trained per OSHA and site requirements
• Any incident, deficiency, or unsafe condition encountered

Coordination shall be documented and include cases where multiple employers work in proximity.

**7.17 INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW**

Immediate reporting is required for:
• Respirator malfunction or failure
• Poor fit, seal loss, or breakthrough
• Medical issues during or after respirator use
• Overexposure concerns or symptoms of exposure
• Improper respirator use or program non-compliance
• Air supply quality or pressure issues
• Training deficiencies discovered during use
• Unsafe respiratory hazard conditions

Investigation shall address:
• Hazard assessment adequacy
• Respirator selection appropriateness
• Medical qualification and fit testing status
• Training adequacy
• Change-out schedule effectiveness
• Respirator condition and maintenance
• Work practices and supervision
• Environmental and exposure conditions
• Need for engineering, procedural, or training improvements

Periodic EHS and operational review of the program shall be conducted to verify effectiveness and compliance.

**8. TRAINING REQUIREMENTS**

Training is required for:
• All employees required to use respirators — before initial use
• Voluntary users where required by program conditions
• Supervisors and managers responsible for respirator-required work
• Fit test administrators and support personnel where appropriate
• Maintenance or equipment custodians where applicable
• Contractors as required by site controls

Training topics:
• Recognition of respiratory hazards
• Why the respirator is necessary
• Proper respirator selection and limitations
• Donning, doffing, and seal checks
• Maintenance, storage, and inspection
• Cartridge and filter change-out requirements
• Medical and fit testing requirements
• Emergency procedures and restrictions
• Limitations of air-purifying respirators
• Voluntary use requirements where applicable
• Incident and near-miss reporting requirements
• Stop-work authority

**9. RECORDS**

The following records shall be maintained:
• Respiratory hazard assessments
• Medical clearance records (maintained per applicable privacy requirements)
• Fit test records (make, model, size, date, method, result)
• Training records
• Respirator issuance records where applicable
• Inspection and maintenance records where required
• Breathing air quality records where applicable
• Cartridge change-out schedules and documentation
• Incident, near miss, and investigation records
• Corrective action records
• Contractor coordination records
• Audit findings and program review records

**10. COMPLIANCE AND DEVIATIONS**

All employees and contractors shall comply with the requirements of this Respiratory Protection Program. Failure to comply may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination and may create serious injury, illness, or fatality risk.

Deviations from this procedure require review and approval by EHS and operational management unless emergency action is needed to protect life or health.

No deviation may remove required respiratory hazard evaluation, medical qualification, fit testing, respirator selection controls, maintenance requirements, or other critical safeguards without equivalent or greater protection.

Emergency deviations shall be documented and reviewed as soon as practicable after the event.

**11. APPENDICES**

**APPENDIX A — RESPIRATOR PRE-USE INSPECTION CHECKLIST**

☐ Facepiece clean and in acceptable condition
☐ Straps and harness intact, elastic, and not frayed
☐ Inhalation and exhalation valves present and functioning
☐ Correct cartridges or filters installed and properly seated
☐ Lens clear and undamaged (full-face respirators)
☐ Gaskets, seals, and connectors intact
☐ PAPR blower, battery, hose, or airline components functional where applicable
☐ No visible damage, deformation, contamination, or missing parts
☐ Respirator appears safe to use
☐ Remove from service any respirator with defects affecting safe operation

**APPENDIX B — DONNING AND SEAL CHECK CHECKLIST**

☐ Correct respirator selected for the task and hazard
☐ Facepiece seated properly on the face
☐ Straps adjusted — snug but not over-tightened
☐ Facial hair does not interfere with the seal (tight-fitting respirators)
☐ Required PPE is compatible and in place
☐ Positive-pressure or negative-pressure seal check completed per manufacturer instructions
☐ User can breathe normally and communicate adequately
☐ No gaps, leaks, or discomfort that would prevent safe use
☐ Do not enter the work area until an adequate seal is achieved

**APPENDIX C — CARTRIDGE AND FILTER CHANGE-OUT CHECKLIST**

☐ Correct cartridge or filter type installed for the contaminant
☐ Change-out schedule known for the current task
☐ Cartridge or filter not expired where applicable
☐ Filter not damaged, wet, or excessively loaded
☐ No odor, taste, irritation, or signs of breakthrough where applicable
☐ Breathing resistance not excessive
☐ Replacement cartridges or filters available at the work location
☐ Replace cartridges or filters immediately if required by conditions or schedule

**APPENDIX D — CLEANING AND STORAGE CHECKLIST**

☐ Respirator cleaned and disinfected per manufacturer instructions
☐ Components inspected during cleaning — valves, straps, lens, connectors
☐ Damaged parts replaced or respirator removed from service
☐ Respirator completely dry before storage
☐ Stored in clean, dry, protected location away from heat, sunlight, chemicals, and damage
☐ Facepiece and valves not distorted during storage
☐ Emergency-use respirators returned to designated storage locations if applicable

**APPENDIX E — CONDITIONS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE STOP WORK**

☐ Respirator damaged or malfunctioning
☐ Adequate seal cannot be maintained
☐ Breathing becomes difficult beyond expected limits
☐ User smells or tastes contaminants, or experiences irritation or breakthrough
☐ User experiences dizziness, nausea, distress, or other medical symptoms
☐ Required cartridge or filter change-out cannot be maintained
☐ Air supply quality or pressure is questionable (atmosphere-supplying respirators)
☐ Oxygen-deficient or IDLH conditions are encountered without required equipment and authorization
☐ Supervisor, EHS, or other responsible person stops the work
☐ Conditions change such that safe respirator use cannot be assured

title: Spill Prevention, Control, and Countermeasure (SPCC) Program
summary: A comprehensive Spill Prevention, Control, and Countermeasure (SPCC) Program covering oil storage inventory management, secondary containment, transfer and overfill prevention, inspection and maintenance, drainage evaluation, spill response, regulatory reporting, contractor coordination, rainwater management, and periodic program review aligned with 40 CFR Part 112 and company environmental policy.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for preventing, controlling, containing, reporting, and responding to oil spills and oil-related discharges that could impact navigable waters, adjoining shorelines, stormwater systems, drainage pathways, soil, or other environmental receptors at [Company Name] facilities.

The purpose is to prevent environmental damage, regulatory noncompliance, fire hazards, property damage, operational disruption, and safety risks resulting from spills, overfills, leaks, equipment failures, transfer errors, incompatible storage conditions, poor inspection practices, inadequate secondary containment, and ineffective spill response.

This program supports compliance with 40 CFR Part 112, applicable state environmental regulations, and company environmental policy. It establishes the framework for identifying oil storage and handling risks, implementing prevention and containment controls, maintaining spill readiness, and ensuring prompt reporting and corrective action when spill events or near-miss conditions occur.

Document No.: [Document No.]
Effective Date: [Effective Date]
Revision: [Revision]
Owner: [Owner]
Approved By: [Approved By]
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, storage areas, maintenance areas, utilities, test areas, yards, loading and unloading areas, waste accumulation areas, and any company-controlled location where oil or oil-containing equipment is stored, transferred, used, or managed in quantities or configurations requiring SPCC controls or equivalent spill prevention measures.

This procedure applies to:
• Employees involved in managing, operating, maintaining, inspecting, or responding to oil storage systems or spill hazards
• Supervisors and managers responsible for areas with oil storage or oil-handling activities
• EHS personnel
• Facilities, maintenance, utilities, operations, and environmental personnel
• Engineers and project personnel involved in oil storage system design, modification, or decommissioning
• Contractors performing work involving oil storage, transfers, maintenance, construction, waste oil management, or spill response support onsite

This procedure applies to oil and oil-containing materials including, but not limited to:
• Petroleum oils, fuel oil, diesel fuel, gasoline where applicable to SPCC-related oil storage controls
• Hydraulic oil, lubricating oil, gear oil, compressor oil, turbine oil
• Waste oil and used oil
• Transformer oil, insulating oil, heat transfer oil
• Oily wastewater where applicable
• Oil-containing equipment and systems

This procedure applies to oil storage and handling systems including, but not limited to:
• Aboveground storage tanks and mobile or portable oil storage containers
• Drums and totes, day tanks, generator fuel systems
• Hydraulic power units, oil-filled transformers and electrical equipment
• Process equipment containing oil, waste oil accumulation containers
• Oil transfer piping and hoses, loading/unloading and fueling areas
• Emergency generators, spill kits and containment equipment, secondary containment systems

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with other required programs such as Hazard Communication, Hazardous Energy / LOTO, Contractor Safety Management, Permit-to-Work, Emergency Response, Waste Management, Stormwater Pollution Prevention, Fire Prevention, and site-specific environmental compliance procedures where applicable.

definitions.definition: A container used for oil storage with a capacity of 55 gallons or greater, including tanks, drums, totes, and other fixed or portable vessels.
Any spill, leak, emission, release, or overflow of oil into or upon navigable waters, adjoining shorelines, stormwater systems, drainage pathways, soil, or other environmental receptors.

Any route — including ditches, culverts, storm drains, curbs, surface slopes, swales, or subsurface features — through which spilled oil could travel from the spill location to navigable waters, shorelines, or other protected receptors.
Waters of the United States as defined by the Clean Water Act and associated regulatory guidance, including rivers, streams, lakes, wetlands, coastal waters, and their adjoining shorelines.

Oil of any kind or in any form, including but not limited to petroleum, fuel oil, sludge, oil refuse, oil mixed with waste, and any other substance meeting the definition of oil under 40 CFR Part 112 or applicable regulations.

Equipment that includes an oil storage container or containers in which the oil is present solely to support the function of the equipment, such as hydraulic systems, transformers, lubricating systems, heat transfer systems, and compressors.

Devices, procedures, and practices designed to prevent oil from exceeding the safe fill level of a container, including alarms, automatic shutoffs, gauge monitoring, and operator attendance during filling.
Oil-filled operational equipment that meets the criteria for qualified status under 40 CFR Part 112 based on size, design, inspection, and risk, potentially allowing alternative containment measures.

A structure, device, or system — such as berms, dikes, curbs, double-wall tanks, spill pallets, or containment trenches — designed to contain oil in the event of a primary container leak, spill, or failure, preventing discharge to the environment.

A collection of spill response materials — such as absorbent pads, booms, granular absorbent, drain covers, disposal bags, and PPE — staged in areas where oil spills could occur for rapid initial response.

A Spill Prevention, Control, and Countermeasure Plan prepared in accordance with 40 CFR Part 112 that documents facility oil storage, spill prevention measures, containment, inspection programs, and spill response procedures.
The process of moving oil from one container, tank, vehicle, or system to another, including filling, dispensing, unloading, fueling, and pumping activities.
The largest foreseeable discharge from any single container or system at the facility, used for containment sizing, response planning, and risk assessment.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Develop, implement, and maintain this program. Ensure SPCC plan documentation is current and meets applicable regulatory requirements. Coordinate oil storage inventories and drainage evaluations. Establish inspection schedules and verify completion. Provide training and technical guidance. Coordinate regulatory reporting for spill events. Support incident investigation and corrective action. Conduct periodic program reviews and audits. Coordinate with engineering and facilities on storage system changes, containment design, and decommissioning. Maintain program records as required.

Ensure personnel under their direction understand and comply with this procedure. Verify oil storage, containment, inspection, and transfer controls are in place in their areas. Report spills, leaks, near misses, and containment issues promptly. Ensure only trained personnel perform oil transfers, inspections, or spill response actions. Stop work when conditions create uncontrolled spill risk. Support corrective actions and program improvements.

Conduct inspections of oil storage containers, containment, transfer equipment, and spill kits as assigned. Perform oil transfers in compliance with this procedure. Report leaks, staining, damaged containers, containment deficiencies, and abnormal conditions. Maintain housekeeping in oil storage and handling areas. Use spill response equipment within their training. Report all spills and near-miss conditions immediately.

Support oil storage inventories, drainage evaluations, and containment assessments. Assist with inspection programs and documentation. Support rainwater management and containment drainage decisions. Coordinate with EHS on regulatory reporting requirements. Support environmental monitoring and spill tracking as applicable.

Evaluate oil storage and containment requirements during design, modification, and construction. Ensure new installations meet applicable regulatory and company containment standards. Notify EHS when projects add, remove, or modify oil storage capacity, drainage patterns, or containment systems. Support decommissioning and system removal per this procedure.

Follow all requirements of this procedure. Attend required training and maintain awareness of oil storage hazards, spill prevention practices, and response expectations in their work areas. Report all leaks, spills, staining, damaged containers, containment failures, and near-miss conditions immediately. Do not bypass secondary containment, leave oil transfers unattended where prohibited, or discharge oil to drains, soil, or the environment.

Comply with this procedure and all applicable site environmental and safety requirements. Do not bring bulk oil storage or perform oil transfers onsite without required review and approval. Use containment, spill kits, and transfer controls as required. Report all spills, leaks, and near-miss conditions to the company representative immediately. Remove contractor-generated waste oil and materials from the site as required. Participate in coordination meetings and pre-job briefings.

Oil storage locations, container types, and spill hazards specific to the site

Container management and housekeeping — labeling, closure, damaged container handling, and waste oil management
Security and access control for oil storage areas

Incident, near-miss, and abnormal condition reporting requirements
ppe: Chemical-resistant gloves appropriate for the oil or petroleum product handled
Safety glasses or chemical splash goggles as required by task
Face shield where splash risk exists during transfers or spill response
Chemical-resistant apron or coveralls where contamination risk exists
Chemical-resistant boots or overshoes where standing liquid or contaminated surfaces are present
Respiratory protection where vapor, mist, or confined space conditions exist — managed under the Respiratory Protection Program
Non-sparking tools where fire or ignition hazards exist near flammable oils
Nitrile or other chemically compatible gloves for spill cleanup and waste handling
hazards: Oil spills to navigable waters, adjoining shorelines, stormwater systems, or soil — environmental damage, regulatory consequences, and cleanup costs
Overfilling of tanks, drums, or containers — discharge, fire risk, and containment failure
Equipment or container failure — leaks, corrosion, structural failure, and catastrophic release
Transfer errors — hose failure, fitting incompatibility, valve misalignment, and unattended operations
Secondary containment failure — cracks, breaches, open drain valves, and inadequate capacity
Drainage to storm drains, surface water, or sensitive areas — uncontrolled discharge pathway
Fire and explosion — flammable oil vapors, ignition sources near storage or transfer areas
Slip, trip, and fall hazards from oil residues, spill conditions, and wet surfaces
Skin and eye contact with petroleum products, waste oil, or contaminated materials
Inhalation of oil mist, vapors, or fumes during transfer, heating, or spill cleanup
Vehicle or equipment impact — collision with tanks, containers, piping, or containment structures
Unauthorized access, vandalism, or tampering with oil storage systems
Rainwater contamination in secondary containment — uncontrolled discharge of oily water
Contractor-introduced oil storage, fueling, or waste oil without required controls
procedure:
**7.1 SPCC APPLICABILITY AND PROGRAM BOUNDARIES**

[Company Name] shall evaluate whether SPCC requirements under 40 CFR Part 112 apply based on oil storage capacity, location, and discharge potential. This evaluation shall consider:
• Type and quantity of oil stored onsite
• Aggregate aboveground oil storage capacity
• Presence of completely buried tanks where applicable
• Oil-filled operational equipment
• Drainage patterns, pathways to stormwater systems, ditches, soil, surface waters, or adjoining shorelines
• Exemptions or special applicability provisions where permitted

Even where full SPCC regulatory applicability does not apply, equivalent spill prevention and response controls may still be required by company policy or site risk.

Program boundaries shall be documented and updated when storage capacity, site drainage, or system configuration changes.

**7.2 OIL STORAGE INVENTORY AND SYSTEM IDENTIFICATION**

All relevant oil storage containers, equipment, and oil-filled systems shall be identified and documented as required by site policy and regulatory applicability.

The inventory shall include:
• Container or equipment identification and location
• Contents and capacity
• Container type (AST, drum, tote, transformer, hydraulic system, generator, etc.)
• Associated containment
• Drainage pathway considerations
• Operational status
• Ownership or responsible department

Storage and equipment changes shall be communicated to EHS for inventory and SPCC review. Unknown, unlabeled, or abandoned oil containers shall be addressed promptly.

[Oil Storage Inventory Table]

**7.3 SPILL HAZARD ASSESSMENT AND DRAINAGE EVALUATION**

Each oil storage and handling area shall be evaluated for spill risk and discharge potential.

Assessment factors include:
• Container size and condition
• Transfer frequency and overfill potential
• Hose and piping routing, connection points, and valves
• Vehicle strike potential
• Weather exposure
• Nearby drains, culverts, ditches, and waterways
• Surface slope and runoff direction
• Secondary containment adequacy
• Proximity to ignition sources where relevant
• Consequence of worst-case or maximum credible release

Drainage pathways shall be understood so containment, response, and reporting decisions can be made quickly. Updates are required when site grading or infrastructure changes affect runoff.

[Facility Drainage Description]

**7.4 SECONDARY CONTAINMENT REQUIREMENTS**

Appropriate secondary containment shall be provided for oil storage and transfer activities as required by regulation, site design, or risk.

Acceptable methods include berms, curbs, dikes, spill pallets, double-wall tanks, drip pans, sumps, containment trenches, portable containment during transfers, and other engineered or approved means.

Secondary containment shall be:
• Compatible with the stored material
• Maintained in functional condition
• Free of uncontrolled cracks, holes, or breaches
• Sufficient in capacity as required by applicable criteria (generally 110% of the largest container or 10% of total capacity, whichever is greater, or as specified by regulation)
• Able to prevent oil from escaping in the event of a discharge
• Managed for precipitation accumulation in a controlled manner

Containment drains or valves shall remain closed except during controlled draining under an approved inspection and release process.

**7.5 STORAGE, CONTAINER MANAGEMENT, AND HOUSEKEEPING**

Oil storage areas shall be maintained in an orderly condition that minimizes spill risk and supports inspection and response.

Requirements include:
• Compatible, properly labeled, sound containers
• Upright storage unless designed otherwise
• Removal from service of damaged, corroded, leaking, or bulging containers
• Segregation of incompatible materials where needed
• Maintained access to spill kits, valves, and containment
• Prompt cleanup of oil residues, drips, and small leaks
• Designated, labeled waste oil containers
• Minimizing open containers

Poor housekeeping that obscures leaks, damages containment, or delays response is prohibited.

**7.6 TRANSFER OPERATIONS AND OVERFILL PREVENTION**

Oil transfer activities shall be performed under controlled conditions.

Pre-transfer requirements include:
• Source and destination identification
• Hose and fitting compatibility
• Secondary or portable containment at transfer points
• Overfill prevention measures (gauging, alarms, automatic shutoffs, or operator monitoring)
• Personnel attendance requirements
• Communication between involved personnel
• Vehicle positioning and stability where applicable
• Weather and surface conditions
• Spill kit availability
• Drain protection where needed

During transfer:
• Inspect hoses and fittings before use
• Ensure secure connections
• Monitor transfer as required — do not overfill
• Follow proper valve sequence
• Be ready to stop transfer immediately if abnormal conditions arise

Unattended transfer operations are prohibited where site rules or risk conditions require active attendance.

**7.7 LOADING, UNLOADING, AND MOBILE OIL HANDLING**

Controlled loading and unloading of fuel, waste oil, lubricants, or other oil-containing materials shall include:
• Wheel chocks or vehicle securement where needed
• Hose routing to avoid strain or traffic exposure
• Use of drip pans or portable containment
• Drain inlet protection where appropriate
• Defined operator attendance
• Clear emergency shutdown method
• Adequate lighting and access
• Segregation from incompatible nearby work

Receiving containers shall be verified to have sufficient capacity before transfer begins.

**7.8 INSPECTION AND PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE**

Oil storage systems, containers, transfer equipment, and containment structures shall be inspected at defined intervals consistent with regulatory requirements, manufacturer guidance, and site risk.

Inspection items include:
• Container exterior condition
• Evidence of leaks, staining, or corrosion
• Tank supports and foundations
• Valves and fittings
• Hoses and transfer lines
• Secondary containment condition
• Drain valves and plugs (closed and secure)
• Security and impact protection
• Overfill prevention devices
• Gauges and indicators where applicable
• Spill kit condition and availability

Inspection findings shall be documented where required and deficiencies corrected in a timely manner. Preventive maintenance on critical oil-handling systems shall be performed to reduce release risk.

[Inspection Frequency]

**7.9 RAINWATER MANAGEMENT IN SECONDARY CONTAINMENT**

Rainwater or other accumulated liquids in containment areas shall be managed in a controlled manner.

Before draining containment, the following shall be confirmed:
• Visual inspection for oil sheen, discoloration, odor, or contamination
• Confirmation that discharge is allowable under site procedures and applicable permits
• Use of controlled drain points or removal methods
• Documentation or logging of drainage actions where required
• Stopping and escalating if contamination is suspected

Uncontrolled discharge of oily water from containment is prohibited.

**7.10 SPILL RESPONSE EQUIPMENT AND READINESS**

Appropriate spill response equipment shall be provided and maintained in areas where oil spills could occur.

Spill response materials include:
• Absorbent pads and booms
• Granular absorbent
• Drain covers and socks or berms
• Non-sparking tools where needed
• Disposal bags or containers
• PPE appropriate for the material
• Portable containment devices

Spill kits shall be:
• Located for quick access near oil storage and transfer areas
• Sized appropriately for the area risk
• Inspected periodically
• Restocked after use

Spill kits do not replace the requirement for prevention and containment controls.

**7.11 SPILL RESPONSE ACTIONS**

Personnel shall respond to oil spills within the limits of their training and authorization.

Initial response actions include:
• Stop the source if safe to do so — shut valves, upright containers, stop transfer pumps
• Protect drains and flow paths using drain covers, absorbents, or barriers
• Contain the spill using absorbent pads, booms, or berms
• Keep unneeded personnel away from the spill area
• Notify supervision and EHS immediately
• Escalate to emergency response if the spill exceeds onsite capabilities or presents fire, health, or environmental risk

Personnel shall not place themselves at risk during response and shall not attempt cleanup beyond their training or available controls.

**7.12 SPILL REPORTING AND REGULATORY NOTIFICATION**

All oil spills, releases, near misses with discharge potential, and containment failures shall be reported immediately through site reporting channels.

Reporting evaluation shall consider:
• Material type and estimated quantity
• Actual or potential impact to storm drains, soil, surface water, or adjoining shorelines
• Whether the spill was fully contained
• Whether the event triggers external reporting requirements under 40 CFR Part 112, Clean Water Act, or applicable state regulations

EHS or designated responsible personnel shall coordinate regulatory reporting where required. This includes reporting to the National Response Center, state environmental agencies, and local authorities as applicable.

Failure to promptly report a spill or release condition is a serious program violation.

[Reporting Thresholds / Notification Requirements]
[Emergency Contact Information]

**7.13 SECURITY AND ACCESS CONTROL**

Oil storage areas and critical oil-handling systems shall be protected from unauthorized access, vandalism, and accidental damage.

Security measures include:
• Locked valves or controls where appropriate
• Fencing, barriers, or restricted access areas
• Signage indicating oil storage and restricted access
• Vehicle impact protection (bollards, guardrails, barriers)
• Adequate lighting
• Controlled key or access systems

Unauthorized use, tampering, or connection to oil storage systems is prohibited.

**7.14 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION**

Coordination is required when contractors perform work involving oil storage, transfers, fueling, maintenance, tank work, waste oil handling, or spill response support onsite.

Coordination shall include:
• Scope of work and oil storage or use introduced by the contractor
• Secondary containment expectations
• Spill kit and response requirements
• Waste handling requirements
• Reporting and escalation procedures
• Permit requirements where applicable
• Drainage and environmental sensitivity of the area

Contractors shall not bring bulk oil storage or perform oil transfers onsite without required review and approval where applicable.

**7.15 CHANGES, MODIFICATIONS, AND DECOMMISSIONING**

Review is required before changes affecting oil storage, drainage, transfer, containment, or spill response are implemented.

Examples of changes requiring review include:
• Adding or removing tanks
• Increasing storage capacity
• Relocating containers
• Modifying containment structures
• Changing drainage patterns
• Installing new oil-filled equipment
• Changing fuel systems
• Taking systems out of service

Decommissioning shall include proper removal or isolation, residue management, labeling updates, and inventory revision.

SPCC documentation shall be updated when required by applicable rules and site policy.

**7.16 TRAINING AND SPILL AWARENESS IN THE FIELD**

Personnel working around oil storage or handling areas shall be trained to recognize:
• Spill hazards and signs of leaks or equipment failure
• Proper storage and transfer practices
• Secondary containment expectations
• Initial spill response steps
• Reporting requirements
• Area-specific environmental sensitivities

Training shall be practical and relevant to the employee's role and exposure. Refresher training shall be provided at defined intervals and when conditions, systems, or procedures change.

**7.17 INCIDENT INVESTIGATION AND CORRECTIVE ACTION**

Spills, significant leaks, overfills, containment failures, inspection deficiencies, and near misses with spill potential shall be evaluated and investigated as appropriate.

Investigations shall evaluate:
• Source and cause of the event
• Equipment condition
• Human factors
• Transfer practices
• Inspection effectiveness
• Containment adequacy
• Drainage impacts
• Reporting timeliness
• Response effectiveness
• Need for equipment, procedure, training, or design changes

Corrective actions shall be tracked to closure.

**7.18 PERIODIC PROGRAM REVIEW**

EHS and operational leadership shall periodically review the SPCC Program for effectiveness and compliance.

Review elements include:
• Inventory accuracy
• Spill trends
• Inspection completion and findings
• Containment adequacy
• Spill kit readiness
• Transfer control effectiveness
• Training completion
• Audit findings
• Regulatory changes
• Lessons learned from incidents or near misses

Deficiencies shall be corrected and tracked to closure.
emergencyResponse: If an oil spill occurs, stop the source if safe to do so — shut valves, upright containers, stop pumps or transfer equipment
Protect drains, flow paths, and environmental receptors using drain covers, absorbents, or barriers
Contain the spill using absorbent pads, booms, socks, or berms — do not allow oil to reach storm drains, waterways, or soil if preventable
Keep unneeded personnel away from the spill area
Notify supervision and EHS immediately — provide material type, estimated quantity, location, and whether containment has been breached
Escalate to emergency response if the spill exceeds onsite capabilities, presents fire or health risk, or threatens navigable waters
Do not wash oil into drains, ditches, or waterways
Do not use detergents or dispersants without EHS approval
Manage used absorbents and contaminated materials as waste in accordance with site waste management procedures
Preserve the spill area for investigation and documentation as needed
Report all spill events through the company incident reporting system
Follow site emergency response plan for fires, injuries, or large-scale releases

APPENDIX A — SPCC PRE-PLANNING CHECKLIST

☐ Oil source or container identified
☐ Capacity and contents known
☐ Spill hazards and drainage pathways understood
☐ Secondary containment present and functional
☐ Required inspections complete
☐ Transfer or handling method defined
☐ Spill kit available and stocked
☐ Reporting contacts known

☐ Area-specific environmental sensitivities understood

APPENDIX B — OIL TRANSFER CHECKLIST

☐ Source and receiving container correctly identified
☐ Receiving container has sufficient capacity
☐ Hoses and fittings compatible and in good condition
☐ Drip pans or containment in place as needed
☐ Valves correctly aligned
☐ Spill kit available
☐ Drain protection available where needed
☐ Transfer attended as required
☐ Communication established if multiple personnel are involved
☐ Transfer stops immediately if abnormal conditions occur

APPENDIX C — SECONDARY CONTAINMENT AND INSPECTION CHECKLIST

☐ Tanks and containers in good condition
☐ No visible leaks, staining, or corrosion present
☐ Containment walls, berms, or pallets intact
☐ Drain valves closed and secure
☐ Rainwater accumulation managed appropriately
☐ Hoses, fittings, and valves in acceptable condition
☐ Impact protection in place where needed
☐ Labels legible
☐ Spill kits available and stocked
☐ Deficiencies documented and corrected

APPENDIX D — SPILL RESPONSE CHECKLIST

☐ Source stopped if safe to do so
☐ Area protected
☐ Drains and pathways protected
☐ Spill contained if within capability
☐ Supervision and EHS notified
☐ Emergency response requested if needed
☐ Waste and used absorbents managed properly
☐ Area cleaned and restored appropriately
☐ Event documented and investigated

APPENDIX E — CONDITIONS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE STOP WORK

• Container or system integrity is questionable
• Secondary containment is missing, damaged, or ineffective
• Required spill response equipment is unavailable
• Transfer controls cannot be maintained
• A leak, overfill, or release occurs and conditions remain uncontrolled
• Drainage protection cannot be established where needed
• Inspection deficiencies create immediate spill risk
• A supervisor, EHS, or other responsible person stops the work
• Conditions change such that safe spill prevention and control cannot be assured
