title: Storm Water Pollution Prevention Plan (SWPPP) Program
summary: A comprehensive Storm Water Pollution Prevention Plan (SWPPP) Program covering pollutant source identification, drainage mapping, BMP selection and maintenance, erosion and sediment control, good housekeeping, outdoor storage controls, inspection and monitoring, illicit discharge prevention, spill coordination, corrective action, contractor coordination, and periodic program review aligned with EPA NPDES industrial storm water permit requirements.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, controlling, monitoring, and reducing pollutants in storm water runoff from [Company Name] facilities, operations, activities, and outdoor industrial areas. The purpose is to prevent environmental contamination, permit violations, unauthorized discharges, erosion, sediment migration, property damage, and operational disruption resulting from uncontrolled storm water contact with industrial materials, residues, waste, equipment, exposed soils, outdoor storage areas, fueling and maintenance activities, drainage failures, poor housekeeping, inadequate best management practices (BMPs), and ineffective response to storm-related conditions.

Storm water that contacts industrial materials, residues, or disturbed areas can transport pollutants — including oil, metals, sediment, chemicals, debris, and process residues — into storm drains, ditches, receiving waters, and the environment. This program requires systematic source identification, drainage evaluation, BMP implementation, good housekeeping, inspection, monitoring support, spill coordination, corrective action, training, and compliance management to protect water quality and meet applicable regulatory obligations.

This procedure supports compliance with applicable Clean Water Act requirements, EPA National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (NPDES) industrial storm water permits, state and local storm water regulations, and company environmental policies. It applies to industrial storm water discharge prevention and to equivalent pollution prevention controls where formal permit-driven requirements may not apply but environmental risk exists.
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, yards, storage areas, loading and unloading areas, maintenance areas, utilities, construction support areas, test areas, waste accumulation areas, outdoor industrial work zones, drainage infrastructure, and other company-controlled locations where storm water may contact industrial materials, residues, or activities and create pollutant discharge potential.

This procedure applies to:
• Employees involved in outdoor industrial activities that may affect storm water quality
• Supervisors and managers responsible for outdoor work areas, storage areas, drainage areas, and pollutant-generating activities
• EHS personnel
• Facilities, utilities, maintenance, operations, environmental, and site services personnel
• Engineers and project personnel involved in site layout, drainage modifications, containment, or outdoor equipment installation
• Contractors performing outdoor work, construction support, maintenance, fueling, waste handling, or material storage activities onsite

Storm water exposure sources include, but are not limited to:
• Outdoor raw material storage
• Scrap and waste storage
• Drums, totes, and containers
• Oil and fuel storage
• Vehicle and equipment fueling
• Vehicle and equipment maintenance
• Hydraulic equipment
• Outdoor process equipment
• Outdoor chemical storage
• Metal stock and fabrication residue
• Dust-generating materials
• Loading and unloading operations
• Waste dumpsters and roll-offs
• Construction support materials
• Exposed soils
• Sediment-producing activities
• Washout or wash-water related risks
• Leaks, spills, and residues in outdoor areas
• Outdoor battery storage or charging where exposure exists
• Drains, ditches, culverts, swales, catch basins, and discharge outfalls

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with other required programs such as Spill Prevention, Control, and Countermeasure (SPCC), Waste Management, Hazard Communication, Contractor Safety Management, Permit-to-Work, Emergency Response, Erosion and Sediment Control requirements, and site-specific environmental compliance procedures where applicable.

definitions.definition: A structural device, operational practice, maintenance procedure, or combination thereof used to prevent, control, or reduce the discharge of pollutants in storm water runoff.
A point at which storm water is discharged from the facility to a receiving water, storm sewer system, ditch, or other drainage pathway.
The portion of a site that drains to a specific discharge point or outfall based on topography, grading, and infrastructure.
The wearing away of soil, rock, or other surface material by water, wind, or other natural processes, which can generate sediment and transport pollutants.
The condition where outdoor industrial materials, activities, residues, or disturbed areas come into contact with precipitation or storm water runoff.
The practice of maintaining clean, orderly work areas, storage areas, and drainage features to minimize the potential for pollutant contact with storm water.

Any unauthorized non-storm water discharge to a storm drain, outfall, or drainage pathway, including but not limited to wash water, process fluids, oil, chemicals, sanitary waste, and cooling water where not permitted.

An activity or operation at a facility that involves the manufacture, processing, storage, handling, transport, or disposal of materials in a manner that exposes such materials to storm water contact.

A discharge to the storm water system that is not composed entirely of storm water, which may include authorized discharges such as uncontaminated air conditioning condensate, fire hydrant flushing, or landscape irrigation where permitted.
The point where storm water exits the site boundary or enters a receiving water, municipal storm sewer system, or other regulated or environmentally sensitive discharge location.

Any material, activity, condition, or area that has the potential to release pollutants into storm water runoff, including raw materials, waste, equipment leaks, chemical storage, exposed soils, and process residues.
A body of water such as a creek, stream, river, lake, wetland, estuary, or ocean that receives storm water discharge from the site, either directly or through a conveyance system.
Precipitation, snowmelt, or irrigation water that flows over the ground surface and does not infiltrate into the soil, collecting pollutants as it moves across outdoor areas.
Soil, sand, silt, clay, or other particulate material that is carried by storm water runoff and deposited in drainage systems, receiving waters, or other locations.
Precipitation-related runoff, including rain, snowmelt, and surface drainage that flows across outdoor areas and may collect pollutants before reaching drainage systems or receiving waters.

Storm Water Pollution Prevention Plan — a documented plan identifying pollutant sources, BMPs, inspection requirements, monitoring procedures, and corrective action processes for preventing and controlling storm water pollution from industrial activities.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Develop and maintain the SWPPP and this procedure. Coordinate pollutant source identification, BMP selection, drainage mapping, and outfall documentation. Establish and manage inspection schedules. Coordinate storm water monitoring and sampling support where required by permit. Review inspection findings and corrective actions. Track and report monitoring results, exceedances, and corrective action completion. Coordinate with regulatory agencies where required. Conduct periodic program reviews and audits. Provide technical guidance on storm water pollution prevention, BMP maintenance, and permit compliance. Manage SWPPP amendments when site conditions change.

Ensure personnel under their direction understand and comply with this procedure. Enforce good housekeeping, outdoor storage controls, and BMP requirements in their areas. Verify that pollutant sources are managed, drainage features are protected, and spill prevention measures are integrated with storm water controls. Report pollution risks, BMP failures, spills, illicit discharges, and drainage concerns. Support inspections, corrective actions, and training. Stop work when storm water controls are inadequate or when unauthorized discharges are occurring.

Maintain good housekeeping in outdoor work areas. Implement BMPs as assigned. Report leaks, stains, spills, and material exposure near drainage features. Maintain secondary containment, drain inlet protection, erosion controls, and structural BMPs. Repair or replace damaged BMPs promptly. Manage outdoor equipment, fueling, and transfer activities to minimize storm water exposure. Report inspection deficiencies and unusual conditions.

Conduct or support routine storm water inspections. Document findings and deficiencies. Monitor BMP condition and effectiveness. Support storm water sampling and monitoring where required. Assist with corrective action tracking. Maintain inspection records and documentation. Report potential permit deviations or exceedances promptly.

Evaluate storm water impacts during design, modification, and construction of outdoor areas, drainage systems, containment, storage, and equipment installations. Ensure new installations include appropriate BMPs and drainage controls. Notify EHS of changes affecting drainage patterns, outfalls, or pollutant exposure. Support SWPPP amendments for significant site changes.

Follow all requirements of this procedure. Maintain good housekeeping in outdoor work areas. Keep containers closed. Prevent pollutant contact with storm water. Report spills, leaks, illicit discharges, BMP damage, erosion, and drainage concerns immediately. Participate in required training. Do not discharge pollutants, wash water, oil, waste, sediment, chemicals, or residues to storm drains, ditches, outfalls, soil, or the environment.

Comply with this procedure and all applicable site storm water and environmental requirements. Manage materials, waste, and equipment brought onsite to prevent storm water contamination. Use BMPs, erosion controls, and containment as required. Report spills, leaks, illicit discharges, and BMP damage immediately. Do not remove storm water controls, discharge to drains, or create new outdoor pollutant exposure conditions without approval. Restore disturbed areas upon completion of work.

Spill and illicit discharge prevention — recognizing unauthorized discharges and reporting requirements
Erosion and sediment control basics — stabilization, silt fence, inlet protection, and stockpile management where relevant
Inspection responsibilities — what to look for, how to document findings, and how to escalate deficiencies
Reporting and escalation requirements — who to contact, what to report, and when to escalate
Corrective action expectations — responding to deficiencies, BMP failures, and monitoring exceedances
Outdoor transfer and fueling controls — drip pans, containment, attendance, drain protection, and spill kit readiness
Waste management and outdoor container controls — keeping containers closed, labeled, and in good condition
Contractor storm water responsibilities — outdoor work coordination, erosion control, and spill prevention
Storm water monitoring support — visual observation, sampling awareness, and chain-of-custody basics where applicable

ppe: Safety glasses or splash protection appropriate to the outdoor work and material exposure
Chemical-resistant gloves when handling chemicals, waste, or contaminated materials outdoors
Steel-toed or safety-rated footwear for outdoor industrial work areas
High-visibility vest or clothing where required by site traffic or heavy equipment areas
Hard hat where required by site rules or overhead hazard conditions
Hearing protection where noise levels in outdoor areas require it
Appropriate respiratory protection when handling dusty or volatile materials outdoors — managed under the Respiratory Protection Program
Rain gear and slip-resistant footwear for inspection and monitoring activities during wet weather

hazards: Oil, fuel, lubricant, and hydraulic fluid exposure to storm water from leaks, spills, transfers, and equipment maintenance
Chemical storage and handling — outdoor containers, drums, totes, and process chemicals exposed to precipitation
Sediment and erosion — disturbed soils, exposed stockpiles, unstabilized slopes, and construction areas generating sediment-laden runoff
Metal fabrication residues — grinding dust, cutting fluids, metal shavings, and scale exposed to storm water
Waste and debris — dumpsters, roll-offs, scrap piles, and waste containers allowing pollutant contact with rainfall and runoff
Vehicle and equipment traffic — tracking contaminants, fluid leaks, tire wash, and fueling area runoff
Loading and unloading — material spillage, container damage, and residue exposure during transfer operations
Wash water and washout — unauthorized discharge of cleaning water, concrete washout, or decontamination water to storm drains
Dust and particulates — airborne industrial dust settling on outdoor surfaces and washing into drainage with rainfall
Illicit discharges — process fluids, cooling water, sanitary waste, or chemical solutions improperly discharged to storm drains or drainage pathways
Drainage infrastructure failures — blocked catch basins, damaged inlet protection, collapsed culverts, and inadequate conveyance
Severe weather events — high-intensity rainfall, flooding, rapid snowmelt, and wind events overwhelming BMPs or exposing stored materials
procedure:
7.1 SWPPP APPLICABILITY AND PROGRAM BOUNDARIES

The site shall evaluate whether industrial storm water permit requirements or internal SWPPP controls apply based on operations, pollutant exposure, drainage pathways, and site-specific regulatory obligations. Factors include:
• Type of industrial activities conducted onsite
• Outdoor exposure of industrial materials or residues
• Applicable industrial sectors or permit categories
• Number and location of discharge outfalls — [Outfall Inventory Table]
• Site drainage patterns — [Drainage Area Map]
• Potential discharge to storm sewer systems, ditches, streams, or other receiving waters — [Receiving Waters]
• Construction or project interfaces where relevant
• Conditional exclusions such as no-exposure status where applicable
• Applicable permit number — [Permit Number]

Even where a full permit-driven SWPPP is not required, equivalent storm water pollution prevention controls may still be required by [Company Name] policy or environmental risk assessment. Program boundaries shall be documented and updated when site operations, drainage, storage practices, or infrastructure change.

7.2 SITE DESCRIPTION, MAPS, AND DRAINAGE IDENTIFICATION

The site shall maintain sufficient description and mapping to support storm water pollution prevention. Documentation shall include:
• Site boundaries, buildings, and operational areas
• Outdoor material storage locations
• Waste accumulation areas
• Fueling or maintenance areas
• Drainage features — culverts, swales, ditches, channels, storm drains, catch basins
• Outfall locations and receiving waters — [Receiving Waters]
• Structural BMP locations
• Areas of soil exposure or erosion concern
• Vehicle or equipment traffic areas
• Spill risk areas
• Loading, unloading, and transfer areas

Maps and drainage descriptions shall be updated when physical conditions, outfalls, or pollutant source locations change. Maps shall be maintained as part of the SWPPP documentation and available to inspection and environmental personnel.

7.3 POLLUTANT SOURCE IDENTIFICATION

The site shall identify actual and potential pollutant sources that could impact storm water runoff. Sources include:
• Outdoor raw materials, scrap, debris, and waste
• Oil and fuel handling areas
• Equipment leaks — hydraulic, lubricant, coolant, and fuel systems
• Outdoor chemical containers and process material storage
• Process residues — grinding dust, cutting fluids, metal shavings, coatings
• Powder, particulates, and dust-generating materials
• Sediment from disturbed soil, stockpiles, and construction activity
• Wash water or washout areas
• Drip areas beneath machinery, vehicles, or outdoor equipment
• Dumpsters, waste containers, and roll-offs
• Loading and unloading spill risk areas
• Temporary project materials and laydown areas
• Improperly managed spill cleanup materials

Source identification shall consider routine and non-routine conditions including spills, abnormal equipment conditions, severe weather, temporary work, and contractor activities.

7.4 STORM WATER HAZARD ASSESSMENT AND RISK EVALUATION

Each drainage area or relevant operational area shall be evaluated for storm water pollution risk. Assessment shall consider:
• Type and amount of exposed industrial material
• Likelihood of contact with precipitation or runoff
• Proximity to drainage features and outfalls
• Condition of storage and containment
• Potential for erosion or sediment transport
• Potential for leaks or overflows
• Vehicle traffic and equipment movement
• Housekeeping conditions
• Existing BMP effectiveness
• Consequences of discharge to receiving waters or drainage systems — [Receiving Waters]
• Seasonal or weather-related factors

If pollutant sources cannot be adequately controlled, the activity or storage arrangement shall be changed before continued operation. Risk evaluation results shall inform BMP selection, inspection frequency — [BMP Inspection Frequency] — and corrective action priorities.

7.5 BEST MANAGEMENT PRACTICES (BMPs)

Appropriate BMPs shall be selected and maintained to prevent or reduce pollutant discharge in storm water. BMPs include:
• Covering materials or activities to prevent precipitation contact
• Secondary containment — berms, curbs, barriers, spill pallets, dikes
• Drain inlet protection — covers, socks, berms, and filter devices
• Sediment barriers — silt fence, wattles, check dams, and inlet protection
• Stabilized entrances and exits
• Erosion control blankets and temporary stabilization
• Good housekeeping — prompt cleanup, orderly storage, container management
• Covered dumpsters and waste containers
• Leak prevention and equipment maintenance
• Storm water treatment devices where installed
• Vegetative stabilization for exposed soils
• Diversion structures — swales, berms, and channels directing clean water away from pollutant sources

BMP selection shall be based on pollutant source, drainage pathway, operating conditions, and permit or site requirements. BMPs shall be inspected at defined intervals — [BMP Inspection Frequency] — and repaired or replaced when ineffective.

7.6 GOOD HOUSEKEEPING AND MATERIAL MANAGEMENT

Outdoor industrial areas shall be maintained in an orderly condition that minimizes storm water contamination risk. Requirements include:
• Materials stored neatly and securely — upright, labeled, organized
• Minimizing outdoor exposure of pollutants to precipitation
• Removing trash, debris, and residues promptly
• Keeping containers closed when not in active use
• Using covered or otherwise protected storage where needed
• Managing scrap and waste to prevent runoff contact
• Cleaning up small leaks, drips, and staining promptly
• Maintaining access routes and drainage features free of obstructive debris where possible
• Preventing accumulation of contaminated solids or residues in exposed outdoor areas

Poor housekeeping that allows pollutants to migrate with runoff is prohibited. Supervisors shall monitor housekeeping conditions regularly and address deficiencies before they become pollution sources.

7.7 EROSION AND SEDIMENT CONTROL

Areas with exposed soil or potential soil disturbance shall be managed to minimize erosion and sediment transport. Controls include:
• Stabilizing exposed soils — seeding, mulching, matting, gravel, or chemical stabilization
• Covering disturbed areas — tarps, blankets, or temporary cover
• Using silt fence, wattles, check dams, and inlet protection where appropriate
• Managing stockpiles — covering, stabilizing, and providing perimeter controls
• Minimizing disturbed area size and duration
• Protecting slopes and drainage channels from concentrated flow
• Repairing erosion promptly
• Cleaning sediment from paved areas and drainage structures in a controlled manner — not flushing into storm drains

Sediment-producing activities shall not proceed without controls appropriate to the site and weather conditions. Erosion and sediment controls shall be inspected after significant rainfall events and repaired or replaced as needed.

7.8 INDUSTRIAL EQUIPMENT, VEHICLE, AND MAINTENANCE AREA CONTROLS

Outdoor industrial equipment, vehicles, and maintenance areas shall be managed to minimize pollutant exposure to storm water. Controls include:
• Drip pans under leaking equipment
• Covered maintenance or service areas where practical
• Prompt repair of leaks
• Storage of oily parts or waste under cover
• Proper used oil and fluid container management
• Absorbent use at recurring leak locations
• Segregation of clean and contaminated materials
• Storm drain protection during maintenance activities where needed
• Regular inspection of parking and staging areas for fluid staining

Equipment known to leak significantly shall not be left uncontrolled in storm water exposure areas. Maintenance areas shall be designed or managed to prevent contact between industrial fluids and storm water.

7.9 FUELING, TRANSFER, AND LIQUID HANDLING CONTROLS

Outdoor fueling and liquid transfer activities shall be controlled to prevent pollutant discharge to storm water. Controls include:
• Designated fueling or transfer areas with appropriate surface and containment
• Spill kits and absorbents staged at fueling and transfer locations
• Drip pans or portable containment at transfer points
• Hose and connection inspection before each transfer
• Attended transfer requirements — personnel present during fueling
• Drain inlet protection where fueling areas are near storm drains
• Good surface condition and cleanup practices
• Emergency shutoff readiness
• Weather consideration during transfers where risk increases

Fueling or transfer shall stop immediately if leaks, overfills, or abnormal conditions occur. Spill response equipment shall be immediately accessible during all transfer operations.

7.10 WASTE MANAGEMENT AND OUTDOOR CONTAINER CONTROLS

Waste materials stored outdoors shall be managed to prevent storm water contamination. Controls include:
• Labeled and closed waste containers — lids secured when not actively in use
• Good container condition — no cracks, corrosion, holes, or leaking
• Overpacking, repair, or removal of leaking containers
• Compatibility and segregation of incompatible waste
• Management of dumpsters, roll-offs, and waste bins to minimize precipitation exposure and runoff contact — close lids, cover loads
• Inspection and maintenance of waste storage areas
• Prevention of liquid accumulation and overflow
• Proper handling and management of washout and decontamination residues

Improper outdoor waste management that creates runoff contamination risk is prohibited.

7.11 SPILL PREVENTION INTERFACE AND RESPONSE COORDINATION

SWPPP controls shall be coordinated with spill prevention and spill response programs, including the [Company Name] SPCC Procedure. Coordination includes:
• Spill-prone areas having appropriate response equipment — absorbents, drain covers, containment
• Understanding spill pathways to storm drains, outfalls, or receiving waters
• Availability of drain protection measures where needed
• Prioritization of stormwater system protection during outdoor spill response
• Proper collection and management of spill cleanup residues
• Prompt reporting and evaluation of spill events with storm water impact potential

The site SWPPP and SPCC/spill response controls shall work together to reduce environmental release risk. Spill response actions shall include immediate protection of storm drains and drainage pathways whenever a spill occurs in an area draining to storm water infrastructure.

7.12 INSPECTIONS AND ROUTINE OBSERVATIONS

Storm water control areas, BMPs, outfalls, exposed industrial areas, and relevant site conditions shall be inspected at frequencies consistent with permit requirements, site risk, and seasonal conditions — [BMP Inspection Frequency]. Inspections shall include:
• BMP condition and effectiveness
• Housekeeping and outdoor storage conditions
• Secondary containment condition
• Leaks, staining, and residue accumulation
• Erosion and sediment control effectiveness
• Drainage features — catch basins, ditches, swales, and channels
• Inlet protection condition
• Outfall condition and appearance — [Outfall Inventory Table]
• Waste container condition and management
• Exposure of materials to precipitation
• Evidence of unauthorized discharges

Findings shall be documented where required and deficiencies corrected in a timely manner — [Corrective Action Timeframes]. Inspections shall also be conducted after significant storm events as required by applicable permits.

7.13 STORM EVENT, VISUAL, AND ANALYTICAL MONITORING SUPPORT

Storm water discharges shall be visually observed or sampled in accordance with applicable monitoring requirements where required by permit or site program. Monitoring includes:
• Outfall identification and access
• Storm event tracking — qualifying event criteria, timing, and documentation
• Visual assessment of discharge characteristics — color, clarity, odor, sheen, foam, floating solids
• Benchmark or effluent sample collection where required
• Documentation of qualifying events and sampling conditions
• Chain-of-custody support and proper sample handling
• Coordination with qualified samplers or analytical laboratories

Results shall be reviewed by qualified personnel and used to determine whether BMPs and controls remain effective or need improvement. Exceedances of applicable benchmarks or limits shall trigger corrective action review — [Corrective Action Timeframes].

7.14 ILLICIT DISCHARGE PREVENTION AND NON-STORM WATER CONTROLS

Unauthorized non-storm water discharges to storm drains, outfalls, or drainage pathways are prohibited. Potential illicit discharges include:
• Wash water — vehicle wash, floor wash, equipment wash
• Oil or fuel
• Chemical solutions
• Cooling water where not permitted
• Condensate or wastewater where not authorized
• Process fluids
• Sanitary waste
• Concrete washout
• Cleaning solutions
• Decontamination water

Personnel shall report suspected illicit discharges immediately to their supervisor and EHS. Where authorized non-storm water discharges exist, they shall be identified, documented, and managed consistent with applicable permits and site requirements.

7.15 CORRECTIVE ACTION AND BMP REPAIR

Corrective action is required when inspections, spills, observations, monitoring results, exceedances, or other findings indicate that storm water controls are ineffective or deficient. Corrective actions include:
• Repairing or replacing BMPs
• Improving containment or cover
• Changing storage practices
• Increasing housekeeping frequency
• Stabilizing soils
• Cleaning drainage structures
• Improving leak prevention
• Revising procedures
• Providing retraining
• Modifying site layout or drainage controls

Corrective actions shall be tracked to closure within required or reasonable timeframes — [Corrective Action Timeframes]. Where monitoring exceedances occur, the site shall evaluate whether additional or upgraded BMPs are needed and document the response.

7.16 CHANGES, MODIFICATIONS, AND CONSTRUCTION SUPPORT

Review is required before changes affecting drainage, outfalls, industrial exposure, outdoor storage, containment, or runoff flow are implemented. Changes requiring review include:
• New outdoor equipment installations
• New storage areas or relocation of existing storage
• Changes to site grading, paving, or excavation
• New utility work affecting drainage
• New process activities outdoors
• Temporary project laydown areas
• Decommissioning of structures or systems
• New impervious surfaces affecting runoff volume or direction
• Changes to waste accumulation areas or container types

SWPPP documentation, maps, BMPs, and inspection scopes shall be updated when changes materially affect storm water pollution risk. EHS shall be notified of planned changes affecting drainage or outdoor pollutant exposure before implementation.

7.17 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION

Coordination is required when contractors perform outdoor work that may affect storm water quality. Coordination shall address:
• Scope of outdoor work and expected duration
• Materials and waste introduced by the contractor
• Erosion and sediment controls
• Spill and leak prevention
• Containment expectations
• Housekeeping requirements
• Drain protection
• Spill reporting and escalation
• Restoration of disturbed areas
• Permit requirements where applicable

Contractors shall not remove storm water controls, discharge to drains, or create new outdoor pollutant exposure conditions without approval. Contractor outdoor activities shall be monitored for compliance with site storm water requirements.

7.18 INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW

Immediate reporting is required for any spill, illicit discharge, storm water permit deviation, visible discharge concern, BMP failure, erosion event, uncontrolled sediment release, monitoring exceedance, or unsafe condition related to storm water pollution prevention.

Investigations shall evaluate:
• Pollutant source identification adequacy
• BMP effectiveness
• Housekeeping and storage practices
• Inspection quality
• Spill prevention integration
• Drainage pathway understanding
• Contractor performance where applicable
• Training adequacy
• Need for engineering, procedural, or training improvements

EHS and operational leadership shall periodically review the program for effectiveness and update requirements as needed based on incidents, inspections, monitoring, operational changes, and lessons learned. Program review shall include inventory accuracy, spill trends, inspection completion and findings, containment adequacy, BMP readiness, training completion, audit findings, regulatory changes, and corrective action effectiveness.
emergencyResponse: Immediately report any spill, illicit discharge, BMP failure, uncontrolled sediment release, or storm water quality concern to your supervisor and EHS — do not attempt to discharge pollutants to storm drains, soil, or waterways

If a spill occurs outdoors: stop the source if safe, protect nearby storm drains using drain covers/socks/berms, contain the spill using available absorbents or barriers, and notify supervision and EHS immediately
Do not wash spilled materials into storm drains, ditches, or drainage pathways — contain and collect for proper disposal
If an illicit discharge is observed or suspected: stop the source if possible, document location and material, notify supervision and EHS, and protect downstream drainage if accessible
If erosion or sediment control failure occurs: stabilize or reinforce controls where safe, divert clean water away from eroded areas where possible, and report the condition for corrective action
During severe weather events: secure loose materials, close containers, verify BMPs are functioning, protect drain inlets where feasible, and report any failures or overflows promptly
If monitoring results indicate an exceedance: notify EHS, evaluate BMP effectiveness, initiate corrective action review, and document the response
Personnel shall not attempt environmental response actions beyond their training, authorization, and available equipment — escalate to EHS or emergency response as appropriate

Title: Storm Water Pollution Prevention Plan (SWPPP) Program
Document No.: [Document No.]

• SWPPP document and amendments where required

• Monitoring and sampling records where applicable

• Corrective action records
• Contractor coordination records
• BMP maintenance and repair records
• Audit findings and program review records

COMPLIANCE AND DEVIATIONS

Failure to comply with this procedure may result in disciplinary action, work suspension, contractor consequences where applicable, permit violations, regulatory exposure, and environmental risk. EHS and operational management review is required for deviations unless immediate action is required to protect life, health, property, or the environment. No deviation may remove required BMPs, inspection requirements, pollutant controls, reporting measures, or other critical safeguards without equivalent or greater protection.

Violations of storm water permits can result in significant regulatory penalties, enforcement actions, mandatory corrective measures, and reputational harm. All personnel share responsibility for protecting storm water quality.

APPENDIX A — SWPPP PRE-PLANNING CHECKLIST

☐ Outdoor pollutant sources identified
☐ Drainage pathways and nearby storm drains understood
☐ Applicable BMPs in place
☐ Required containment or cover available
☐ Spill kit or drain protection available where needed
☐ Waste management controls defined
☐ Inspection requirements known — [BMP Inspection Frequency]
☐ Area-specific environmental sensitivities understood — [Receiving Waters]
☐ Required permits or approvals in place where applicable
☐ Reporting contacts known — [Emergency Contact Information]

APPENDIX B — OUTDOOR EXPOSURE AND BMP CHECKLIST

☐ Materials stored to minimize precipitation exposure
☐ Containers labeled and closed when not in use
☐ Secondary containment functional where required
☐ Housekeeping acceptable
☐ Leaks and residues controlled
☐ Drain inlet protection used where needed
☐ Erosion and sediment controls in place where relevant
☐ Waste containers in good condition
☐ No unauthorized discharge to drains or soil occurring
☐ BMPs remain intact and effective

APPENDIX C — STORM WATER INSPECTION CHECKLIST

☐ No visible spills, leaks, or staining present
☐ Storage areas orderly and controlled
☐ BMPs intact
☐ Berms, pallets, covers, and barriers functional
☐ Catch basins, drains, and ditches free of excessive pollutant buildup
☐ Waste areas properly managed
☐ Erosion or sediment movement not occurring uncontrolled
☐ Outfalls appear free of unauthorized discharges where observable
☐ Corrective actions identified and documented when needed
☐ Post-storm event conditions assessed where required by permit

APPENDIX D — SPILL / ILLICIT DISCHARGE RESPONSE CHECKLIST

☐ Source stopped if safe to do so
☐ Storm drains and flow paths protected
☐ Area isolated as needed
☐ Supervision and EHS notified
☐ Spill response initiated within authorization limits
☐ Emergency response requested if needed
☐ Contaminated materials collected and managed properly
☐ Area restored appropriately
☐ Event documented and investigated

APPENDIX E — CONDITIONS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE STOP WORK

• Required BMPs are missing, damaged, or ineffective
• Storm drains or sensitive pathways cannot be protected where needed
• A spill, leak, sediment release, or illicit discharge occurs and remains uncontrolled
• Containers, waste systems, or storage areas create immediate runoff contamination risk
• Erosion or sediment controls fail under active work conditions
• A supervisor, EHS, or other responsible person stops the work
• Conditions change such that safe storm water pollution prevention cannot be assured

revisionHistory.description: Initial release — comprehensive Storm Water Pollution Prevention Plan (SWPPP) Program covering applicability determination, site description and drainage mapping, pollutant source identification, risk evaluation, BMP selection and maintenance, good housekeeping, erosion and sediment control, equipment and vehicle controls, fueling and transfer controls, waste management, spill coordination, inspections, monitoring support, illicit discharge prevention, corrective action, site changes, contractor coordination, incident reporting, and periodic program review.

title: Electrical Safety, Arc Flash, and Energized Electrical Work Program
summary: A comprehensive Electrical Safety, Arc Flash, and Energized Electrical Work Program covering electrical hazard identification, electrically safe work conditions, lockout/tagout interface, absence-of-voltage verification, energized work justification and permitting, shock protection boundaries, arc flash risk assessment, PPE, tools and test instruments, battery and stored energy controls, temporary wiring, contractor coordination, emergency response, and program review.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, evaluating, controlling, and safely managing electrical hazards, including shock, arc flash, arc blast, stored electrical energy, and hazards associated with energized electrical work at [Company Name] facilities.

The purpose is to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, electrical shock, electrocution, arc flash burns, arc blast trauma, secondary falls, fire, equipment damage, property damage, and operational disruption resulting from contact with exposed energized conductors or circuit parts, improper troubleshooting, inadequate isolation, failure to verify absence of voltage, use of improper tools or equipment, poor work planning, inadequate arc flash protection, and unsafe work practices around electrical systems.

This program requires systematic hazard identification, deenergization and isolation as the first priority, verification of absence of voltage, arc flash risk assessment, qualified worker requirements, safe work practices, PPE selection, tool and instrument controls, energized work permitting, supervision, training, and periodic program review.

This procedure supports compliance with applicable OSHA standards, NFPA 70E, NFPA 70, and company policies. It applies to all voltage classes present onsite and to all personnel who work on, near, or around electrical equipment or systems.
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, laboratories, production areas, maintenance activities, test areas, utilities, fabrication areas, assembly areas, field locations, construction support areas, and non-routine operations where employees or contractors may be exposed to electrical hazards at [Site Name].

It applies to:
• Employees who work on, near, or around electrical equipment or systems
• Qualified electrical workers
• Unqualified employees working in areas where electrical hazards may be present
• Supervisors and managers responsible for electrical work or electrical hazard exposure
• EHS personnel
• Engineers, technicians, electricians, mechanics, maintenance personnel, operators, and support personnel
• Contractors performing electrical work or working around energized electrical systems onsite

It applies to electrical systems and equipment including, but not limited to:
• Electrical panels and switchgear
• Motor control centers (MCCs)
• Disconnects, breakers, and control cabinets
• Junction boxes and bus bars
• Transformers and generators
• Batteries and battery systems
• Capacitors
• Test equipment and power distribution systems
• Extension cords and temporary wiring
• Portable electrical equipment
• Process equipment with electrical energy sources
• High-voltage and low-voltage systems where applicable
• Electrical components within machinery, test stands, and facilities infrastructure

It applies to activities including, but not limited to:
• Inspection, troubleshooting, testing, measurement, and voltage verification
• Installation, repair, maintenance, and adjustment
• Energized diagnostics where justified
• Temporary wiring use and panel access
• Component replacement and battery work
• Commissioning and decommissioning
• Startup and shutdown support where electrical hazard exists

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with the Company Hazardous Energy / LOTO Procedure, Machine Guarding and Tooling Procedure, Non-Routine Activities Procedure, Pressurized Systems Procedure, Contractor Safety Management Procedure, Permit-to-Work Procedure, Emergency Response Plan, and applicable facility operations controls where required.

definitions.definition: The pressure wave and shrapnel released during an arc flash event, capable of causing blunt force trauma, hearing damage, and secondary injuries from projectile debris.
A dangerous release of energy caused by an electric arc, producing intense light, heat, sound, and pressure that can cause severe burns and other injuries.
The distance from exposed energized conductors or circuit parts within which a person could receive a second-degree burn in the event of an arc flash.
A distance from an exposed energized conductor or circuit part within which there is an increased risk of shock. Includes limited and restricted approach boundaries.

A state in which an electrical conductor or circuit part has been disconnected from energized parts, locked and tagged, tested to verify the absence of voltage, and grounded if necessary, so that it is safe to work on.
Any work performed on exposed energized electrical conductors or circuit parts that have not been placed in an electrically safe work condition.

definitions.term: Exposed Energized Conductor or Circuit Part
definitions.definition: An energized electrical conductor or circuit part that is not suitably guarded, isolated, or insulated and is capable of being contacted by persons or objects.
Equipment, clothing, and work practices designed to protect personnel from arc flash hazards, including arc-rated clothing and face/head protection.
The closest distance an unqualified person may approach exposed energized conductors or circuit parts.

A person who has demonstrated skills and knowledge related to the construction and operation of electrical equipment and installations and has received safety training to identify and avoid the hazards involved.
The closest distance a qualified person may approach exposed energized conductors or circuit parts unless properly equipped and authorized.
A dangerous condition associated with the possible release of energy caused by contact or approach to energized electrical conductors or circuit parts.
Electrical wiring, cords, and connections installed for a limited time period during construction, maintenance, testing, or emergency conditions, not intended as permanent installation.
The process of confirming that a voltage-testing instrument is functioning properly by testing it on a known energized source before and after use for absence-of-voltage verification.
The distance between a person's face and chest area and the prospective arc source, used in arc flash hazard calculations and PPE selection.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Own and maintain the Electrical Safety Program. Coordinate arc flash studies, labeling, and equipment assessments. Review and approve energized work permits or equivalent authorizations. Conduct or support program audits, incident investigations, and corrective actions. Maintain training records and qualification tracking. Provide technical guidance on electrical hazard controls, boundaries, and PPE. Update the program based on regulatory changes, incidents, and lessons learned.

Ensure only qualified persons perform electrical work. Verify that electrical hazard assessments, LOTO, and absence-of-voltage verification are completed before work begins. Review and approve energized work justifications at the required level. Ensure personnel have required PPE, tools, and training. Enforce boundaries, access controls, and stop-work authority. Report and investigate electrical incidents and near misses. Allocate time and resources for proper electrical safety planning.

Perform electrical work within the scope of their training, qualification, and authorization. Establish electrically safe work conditions including isolation, LOTO, and absence-of-voltage verification. Determine and enforce shock and arc flash boundaries. Select and wear required PPE. Use properly rated and inspected tools and test instruments. Request energized work permits where required. Stop work when conditions change or hazards cannot be controlled. Report incidents, near misses, and unsafe conditions immediately.

Stay outside limited approach boundaries unless escorted and protected. Do not open electrical panels, remove covers, or interact with exposed energized parts. Report electrical hazards, damaged equipment, missing labels, or unsafe conditions to supervision. Comply with barricade, signage, and restricted area requirements. Complete required electrical awareness training.

Ensure electrical system design supports safe deenergization, isolation, and maintenance. Coordinate arc flash studies, label installation, and single-line diagram accuracy. Evaluate new equipment, modifications, and changes for electrical hazard impact. Ensure equipment labeling is maintained and updated after system changes.

Perform electrical maintenance, troubleshooting, and repair within the scope of their qualifications. Follow LOTO and absence-of-voltage verification procedures. Inspect and maintain test instruments, PPE, and insulated tools. Document completed electrical work and report deficiencies. Coordinate with operations on shutdown, isolation, and restart.

Comply with this procedure and site-specific electrical safety requirements. Provide evidence of electrical qualifications and training. Coordinate isolation, LOTO, boundaries, and energized work authorization with the host employer. Use proper PPE, tools, and test instruments. Report incidents, near misses, and hazards immediately. Do not begin electrical work without required review, coordination, and authorization.

training: Recognition of electrical shock and arc flash hazards associated with various voltage classes and equipment types
Qualified versus unqualified person responsibilities, boundaries, and access restrictions
Electrically safe work condition requirements including deenergization, isolation, LOTO, and absence-of-voltage verification
Lockout/tagout interface and coordination with the Company LOTO Procedure
Absence-of-voltage verification procedures including test instrument verification on known live sources before and after use
Shock protection boundaries — limited approach, restricted approach — and access control requirements
Arc flash boundary determination and arc flash PPE requirements including arc-rated clothing, face shields, hoods, and gloves
Energized work restrictions, justification criteria, and energized electrical work permit requirements
Safe use of test instruments, insulated tools, and portable electrical equipment
Temporary wiring and extension cord controls including inspection, GFCI protection, and routing requirements
Battery, capacitor, UPS, and stored electrical energy hazard awareness and controls
Emergency response expectations for electrical shock, arc flash, and arc blast events
Incident and near-miss reporting requirements and escalation procedures
Stop-work authority and obligation when electrical hazards cannot be controlled
ppe: Arc-rated clothing appropriate for the incident energy or PPE category of the task
Voltage-rated insulating gloves with leather protectors, class appropriate for the voltage
Arc-rated face shield or arc flash hood as required by hazard assessment
Safety glasses or goggles for electrical work
Hard hat rated for electrical work with arc-rated components where applicable
Hearing protection for work inside arc flash boundaries where required
Leather footwear or dielectric footwear as required by the task and environment
Insulating mats, blankets, or barriers where required to protect against contact with energized parts
Insulated tools rated for the voltage where work near or on energized parts is justified and authorized
Removal of conductive jewelry, watches, and accessories before performing electrical work
hazards: Electrical shock from contact with exposed energized conductors or circuit parts at any voltage class
Electrocution (fatal electrical shock) from direct contact, indirect contact through conductive objects, or step potential
Arc flash causing severe thermal burns, ignition of clothing, and blindness from intense UV and visible light
Arc blast producing high-pressure waves, projectile debris, shrapnel from molten metal, and hearing damage
Secondary falls resulting from involuntary muscle reaction to electrical shock or startle response near elevated surfaces
Fire and explosion from electrical faults, overloaded circuits, damaged wiring, or arc ignition of combustible materials
Stored electrical energy hazards from capacitors, batteries, UPS systems, solar feeds, and backfed circuits
Burns from contact with overheated conductors, terminals, or equipment operating beyond rated capacity
Improper isolation including failure to identify all energy sources, backfeed paths, or parallel supply circuits
Failure to verify absence of voltage by relying on indicators, control switches, or breaker handle position instead of proper testing
Use of improperly rated or damaged test instruments, tools, or PPE creating false confidence
Inadequate arc flash labeling, missing boundary information, or outdated arc flash study results
Temporary wiring hazards including damaged cords, missing grounds, overloaded circuits, and exposure to moisture or physical damage
Induced voltage, electromagnetic interference, or regenerative feedback from connected loads or adjacent circuits
procedure: 7.1 ELECTRICAL HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND ASSESSMENT — Before any electrical work begins, the task, equipment, and environment shall be evaluated. Assessment shall include: voltage level, available fault current where relevant, arc flash potential, shock exposure, equipment condition, whether exposed energized parts will be present, need to remove covers or barriers, stored electrical energy including capacitors and batteries, environmental conditions (wet areas, conductive dust, restricted space, heat, poor lighting), simultaneous operations nearby, equipment labeling and identification, availability of single-line or technical information, need for an energized work justification or permit, and need for barricades, attendants, or restricted access. If hazards cannot be adequately understood or controlled, the work shall not proceed until additional review, planning, or isolation is completed.

7.2 HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS FOR ELECTRICAL HAZARDS — The following control hierarchy shall be applied in order of preference: (1) Eliminate exposure by shutting down and deenergizing the equipment; (2) Isolate and lock out all energy sources; (3) Verify absence of voltage and establish an electrically safe work condition; (4) Use engineering controls such as barriers, insulated covers, remote racking, remote operation, or equipment design features; (5) Use administrative controls such as permits, qualified worker restrictions, signage, barricades, and work planning as supplemental controls; (6) Use PPE as a supporting measure, not as a substitute for deenergization and isolation. Whenever feasible, electrical work shall be performed in an electrically safe work condition.

7.3 ELECTRICALLY SAFE WORK CONDITION — Electrical conductors or circuit parts shall be placed in an electrically safe work condition before exposed work begins where hazard exposure exists. Steps include: identifying all energy sources, properly interrupting load current, opening disconnecting means, applying lockout/tagout, releasing stored electrical and associated energy (capacitors, batteries, springs, pressurized systems), verifying absence of voltage using an approved and functioning test instrument, and applying grounding where necessary and appropriate. Normal shutdown indicators, panel lights, HMI status, or breaker handle position alone do not establish an electrically safe work condition.

7.4 LOCKOUT/TAGOUT INTERFACE — Electrical isolation shall be coordinated with the Company Hazardous Energy / LOTO Procedure. LOTO shall address control of electrical power, stored charge, mechanical movement, hydraulic or pneumatic sources, thermal hazards, and gravity or spring tension where applicable when part of a broader system. Electrical LOTO shall not be considered complete until absence of voltage is verified where required. Group lockout, complex system isolation, and multi-trade coordination shall follow established LOTO protocols.

7.5 VERIFICATION OF ABSENCE OF VOLTAGE — Verification shall be performed by a qualified person using an appropriate, properly rated, and functioning test instrument. Steps include: confirming equipment identity before testing, verifying the test instrument on a known live source before use, testing each phase conductor and circuit part to confirm absence of voltage, testing phase-to-phase and phase-to-ground as applicable, reverifying the instrument on a known live source after use, and considering induced voltage, stored energy, backfeed, and multiple supply sources. Absence-of-voltage testing is mandatory where required and shall not be skipped, bypassed, or substituted with indicator-based confirmation.

7.6 ENERGIZED ELECTRICAL WORK JUSTIFICATION — Energized electrical work is permitted only when deenergizing introduces greater hazards, is infeasible due to equipment design or operational limitations, or the task involves justified diagnostics, testing, or measurement that can only be performed energized. Examples of potentially justified energized tasks include voltage testing, diagnostic troubleshooting, and certain commissioning checks. Production schedule pressure, convenience, avoiding minor interruption, or lack of shutdown planning are not valid justification by themselves. Enhanced review and control are required before energized work proceeds.

7.7 ENERGIZED ELECTRICAL WORK PERMIT AND APPROVAL — An approved energized work permit or equivalent documented authorization is required when required by site policy or risk level. The permit shall include: description of the task, equipment involved, why the work must be performed energized, shock hazard analysis, arc flash risk assessment, voltage level, boundaries, PPE requirements, tools and instruments to be used, restricted access measures, work procedures, emergency response considerations, qualified personnel assignments, and approval by [Energized Work Approval Authority] and other required reviewers. Certain routine diagnostic tasks may be governed by standing procedures if site policy allows, while still requiring full compliance with applicable controls.

7.8 SHOCK PROTECTION BOUNDARIES AND ACCESS CONTROL — Shock protection boundaries shall be established where exposed energized conductors or circuit parts are present. Requirements include: determining the limited approach boundary and restricted approach boundary where applicable, restricting access based on qualification and authorization, using barricades, attendants, signs, floor markings, or other controls where needed, preventing unqualified persons from entering controlled areas, and ensuring only qualified persons with appropriate precautions cross restricted boundaries. Personnel shall not cross approach boundaries without the required qualifications, controls, and PPE.

7.9 ARC FLASH RISK ASSESSMENT AND ARC FLASH BOUNDARY — An arc flash risk assessment shall be performed where employees may be exposed to arc flash hazards. Assessment shall consider: available incident energy information where applicable, equipment labeling, system voltage and equipment type, clearing time and protection characteristics where known, task to be performed, working distance, condition of maintenance and equipment integrity, and potential for human interaction with exposed energized parts. The arc flash boundary shall be respected and appropriate PPE and protective measures applied before work proceeds. Installed arc flash labels shall be reviewed where present and reevaluation performed if labeling is missing, unclear, outdated, or not applicable to the task. [Arc Flash Study Reference] and [Single-Line Diagram Reference] shall be consulted where available.

7.10 PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT FOR ELECTRICAL WORK — PPE selection shall be based on the shock and arc flash hazards associated with the task. Required PPE may include: arc-rated clothing, voltage-rated gloves with leather protectors, arc-rated face shield or arc flash hood, safety glasses or goggles, hard hat with arc-rated components, hearing protection, leather or dielectric footwear, and insulating mats or blankets. Selection shall consider incident energy or PPE category where applicable, shock hazard, task type, equipment condition, manufacturer instructions, and compatibility with other required PPE. Damaged, contaminated, expired, or improperly maintained electrical PPE shall not be used.

7.11 TOOLS, TEST INSTRUMENTS, AND ELECTRICAL EQUIPMENT — Only properly rated, suitable, and maintained tools and test instruments shall be used for electrical work. Requirements include: using meters and test instruments rated for the system voltage and environment (CAT rating), inspecting test leads, probes, cases, and accessories before use, using insulated tools where required, removing damaged tools or instruments from service, using only approved temporary jumpers or test devices where specifically allowed, and protecting tools and equipment from moisture, contamination, and physical damage. Improvised testing methods and use of unapproved tools are prohibited.

7.12 WORK PRACTICES FOR PANELS, CABINETS, AND EXPOSED ELECTRICAL PARTS — Evaluation is required before opening electrical panels, cabinets, or enclosures to determine exposure to energized parts and arc flash hazard. Requirements include: determining whether covers must be removed, verifying worker qualification, establishing boundaries and access control, wearing required PPE, using proper body positioning to reduce line-of-fire exposure, keeping unnecessary personnel clear, avoiding placing hands or tools into energized spaces unless specifically required and justified, and maintaining awareness of conductive objects, loose materials, and dropped tool hazards. Doors or covers shall not be opened casually in a manner that exposes personnel to uncontrolled electrical hazards.

7.13 BATTERIES, CAPACITORS, AND STORED ELECTRICAL ENERGY — Stored electrical energy hazards shall be addressed even after primary power is removed. Sources include: capacitor charge, battery voltage and short-circuit current, UPS or backup power systems, solar or alternate power feeds, and induced or backfed circuits. Controls include: isolation of stored energy sources, waiting periods where required, discharge procedures, testing for residual voltage, proper insulated tools, removal of conductive jewelry and objects, and arc flash and chemical hazard controls for batteries where required. Employees shall not assume stored electrical energy is absent without verification.

7.14 TEMPORARY WIRING, EXTENSION CORDS, AND PORTABLE ELECTRICAL EQUIPMENT — Temporary wiring and portable electrical equipment shall be used only in a controlled and safe manner. Requirements include: inspection of cords, plugs, insulation, and grounding features before use, prohibition on damaged cords, missing grounds, or taped repairs as permanent fixes, protecting temporary wiring from pinch points, traffic, moisture, sharp edges, and heat, using GFCI protection where required, routing cords to minimize trip and damage hazards, removing temporary wiring when no longer needed, and prohibiting overloads or unapproved daisy chaining. Temporary wiring shall not become a long-term uncontrolled condition.

7.15 HOUSEKEEPING AND ENVIRONMENTAL CONDITIONS — Electrical work areas shall be maintained to support safe access and control of hazards. Requirements include: adequate lighting, control of unnecessary conductive materials, control of water, moisture, dust, and debris, maintenance of access to disconnects, panels, and emergency egress, avoidance of work in wet conditions unless the task, equipment, and controls are specifically designed and approved, and use of appropriate ladders, platforms, or access equipment near electrical hazards. Poor housekeeping or adverse environmental conditions that increase electrical risk shall be corrected before work proceeds.

7.16 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION — Coordination is required when contractors perform electrical work or work near energized systems onsite. Requirements include: scope of electrical work, qualification requirements, shutdown and isolation responsibilities, energized work authorization requirements, arc flash and shock hazard information, site labeling and documentation, access control and boundaries, permit requirements, emergency response expectations, and incident reporting requirements. Contractors shall not begin electrical work without the required review, coordination, and authorization from the host employer.

7.17 EMERGENCY RESPONSE AND INCIDENT ACTIONS — Immediate stop work and emergency action is required if an electrical incident, shock, arc flash, or unexpected electrical condition occurs. Actions include: not touching an injured person who may still be energized until the hazard is controlled, deenergizing the source if safe and possible, activating emergency response, restricting access to the area, providing first aid or CPR only within training and after hazard control, preserving the scene where appropriate, and notifying supervision and EHS immediately. Potential electrical exposure incidents shall be medically evaluated even when outward injury appears limited, consistent with site medical response requirements.

7.18 INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW — Immediate reporting is required for any electrical shock, arc flash, arc blast, unexpected energization, near miss, equipment failure, label discrepancy, test instrument failure, boundary breach, or unsafe condition related to electrical hazards. Investigations shall evaluate hazard identification adequacy, deenergization and isolation practices, absence-of-voltage verification quality, energized work justification, PPE adequacy, tool and instrument suitability, worker qualification and training, supervision and planning, equipment condition and maintenance, and need for engineering, procedural, or training improvements. EHS and operational leadership shall periodically review the Electrical Safety Program for effectiveness and update requirements as needed.
emergencyResponse: Do NOT touch an injured person who may still be in contact with an energized source — deenergize the source first if safe and possible
Activate the site emergency response system immediately for electrical shock, electrocution, arc flash burns, or arc blast injuries
Keep all non-essential personnel away from the electrical incident area
Do not re-enter the area until the electrical hazard has been controlled and it is confirmed safe
Provide first aid or CPR only if trained and after the electrical hazard is controlled — use AED if indicated and available
Treat all electrical shock events as potentially serious — seek medical evaluation even when outward injury appears minimal due to internal injury potential
For arc flash burns, remove the person from the hazard zone, cool burns with clean water if safe, cover with sterile dressings, and do not remove adhered clothing
Preserve the incident scene for investigation where feasible and safe to do so
Notify supervision, EHS, and site management immediately and document all incident details
Do not re-energize equipment or restore power until the incident has been investigated and the equipment has been inspected and cleared

Test instrument inspection or verification records documenting instrument condition, calibration status, and known-source verification results
Incident, near miss, and investigation records for electrical shock, arc flash, arc blast, unexpected energization, and unsafe conditions
Corrective action records including root cause, assigned actions, completion tracking, and effectiveness verification
Contractor coordination records including qualification verification, scope of work, and electrical safety briefing documentation
Audit findings and program review records including recommendations, follow-up actions, and closure dates
Electrical system documentation updates including labeling changes, system modifications, and decommissioning records

APPENDIX A — ELECTRICAL WORK PRE-PLANNING CHECKLIST:
☐ Equipment and task identified
☐ Voltage and hazard level understood
☐ Qualified personnel assigned where required
☐ Deenergization feasibility evaluated
☐ Required isolation points identified
☐ LOTO requirements addressed
☐ Absence-of-voltage verification method known
☐ Arc flash and shock protection requirements identified
☐ Required PPE and tools available and suitable
☐ Boundaries and access controls defined
☐ Emergency response actions understood

APPENDIX B — ELECTRICALLY SAFE WORK CONDITION CHECKLIST:
☐ Load interrupted where required
☐ Disconnecting means opened
☐ Locks and tags applied
☐ Stored electrical energy addressed (capacitors, batteries, UPS, springs)
☐ Test instrument verified functional on known live source before use
☐ Absence of voltage tested phase-to-phase and phase-to-ground as applicable
☐ Test instrument reverified on known live source after use
☐ Controls returned to safe state after testing
☐ Equipment confirmed to be in an electrically safe work condition

APPENDIX C — ENERGIZED ELECTRICAL WORK CHECKLIST:
☐ Work justified — deenergization infeasible or creates greater hazard
☐ Energized work permit or authorization completed and approved
☐ Task scope clearly defined
☐ Shock and arc flash hazards assessed
☐ Boundaries established and access controlled
☐ Required PPE worn correctly and in good condition
☐ Tools and test equipment suitable, rated, and inspected
☐ Access to the area controlled — barricades, signs, attendants as needed
☐ Emergency response capability understood
☐ Only essential qualified personnel involved
☐ Work stops immediately if conditions change or become unclear

APPENDIX D — TEMPORARY WIRING AND PORTABLE ELECTRICAL EQUIPMENT CHECKLIST:
☐ Cord, plug, and insulation condition acceptable — no cuts, cracks, or exposed conductors
☐ Grounding features intact where required
☐ GFCI protection provided where required
☐ Equipment rated for environment and task
☐ Cords routed to prevent damage, trip hazards, and exposure to moisture or heat
☐ No improvised repairs (tape, wire nuts on cords) present
☐ Connections protected from water, pinch points, and heat sources
☐ Temporary wiring appropriate for short-term use only — not permanent installation

APPENDIX E — CONDITIONS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE STOP WORK:
• Equipment identity or voltage is uncertain
• Required qualification is not present for the task
• Deenergization and isolation cannot be confirmed
• Absence of voltage cannot be verified
• Required boundaries or access control cannot be maintained
• Required PPE or suitable tools are unavailable or damaged
• Equipment condition is abnormal, damaged, or unsafe
• Unexpected voltage, backfeed, or stored energy is detected
• An arc flash, shock, near miss, or unsafe condition occurs and remains uncorrected
• A supervisor, EHS, or other responsible person stops the work
• Conditions change such that safe electrical work cannot be assured
