title: Hand and Power Tools Safety Program
summary: A comprehensive Hand and Power Tools Safety Program covering tool hazard identification, selection, pre-use inspection, hand tool safety, portable electric tools, battery-powered tools, pneumatic and hydraulic tools, grinders and abrasive wheels, cutting tools and saws, impact and powder-actuated tools, workpiece control and line-of-fire management, guards and safety devices, PPE, storage and maintenance, contractor coordination, and program review.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for selecting, inspecting, using, maintaining, and controlling hazards associated with hand tools, portable power tools, and related accessories used in workplace operations at [Company Name].

The purpose is to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, lacerations, puncture wounds, amputations, eye injuries, struck-by injuries, caught-in injuries, burns, shock, noise-related injury, flying object hazards, ergonomic strain, equipment damage, and operational disruption resulting from improper tool selection, damaged or modified tools, misuse, inadequate guarding, poor maintenance, improper accessories, unsafe body positioning, and failure to control line-of-fire and energy hazards during tool use.

This program requires systematic hazard identification, correct tool selection, pre-use inspection, guarding, safe work practices, workpiece control, PPE, training, supervision, and periodic program review.

This procedure supports compliance with applicable OSHA standards, manufacturer requirements, and company policies.

Document No.: [Document No.] | Effective Date: [Effective Date] | Revision: [Revision]
Owner: [Owner] | Approved By: [Approved By]
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, laboratories, production areas, maintenance activities, fabrication areas, assembly areas, machine shops, test areas, field locations, construction support areas, warehouses, utilities, and non-routine operations where hand tools or portable power tools are used at [Site Name].

It applies to:
• Employees who use, handle, inspect, maintain, issue, or supervise work involving hand or power tools
• Supervisors and managers responsible for work involving hand and power tools
• EHS personnel
• Engineers, technicians, electricians, mechanics, operators, assemblers, maintenance personnel, inspectors, and support personnel
• Contractors performing work involving hand or power tools onsite

It applies to tools and equipment including, but not limited to:
• Wrenches, screwdrivers, pliers, hammers, chisels, punches, saws, knives and cutting tools, files, clamps, and torque tools
• Portable electric drills, impact drivers, grinders, sanders, circular saws, reciprocating saws, jig saws, nail guns and fastening tools, heat guns, portable band saws, and rotary tools
• Pneumatic tools and hydraulic hand tools
• Powder-actuated tools where applicable
• Battery-powered tools
• Extension cords and tool accessories
• Abrasive wheels, drill bits, cutting blades, sockets, sanding discs, and other consumables or attachments

It applies to activities including, but not limited to:
• Cutting, drilling, grinding, sanding, fastening, chipping, striking, prying, torquing, finishing, deburring, and surface preparation
• Maintenance and repair, fabrication and assembly, construction support
• Troubleshooting and field work, temporary and non-routine work involving tools

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with the Company Electrical Safety Procedure, Hazardous Energy / LOTO Procedure, Machine Guarding and Tooling Procedure, Welding/Cutting/Brazing Procedure, PPE Program, Respiratory Protection Procedure, Pressurized Systems Procedure, Permit-to-Work Procedure, Contractor Safety Management Procedure, and applicable site operational controls where required.

definitions.definition: A component attached to or used with a tool to perform a specific function, such as a drill bit, grinding wheel, sanding disc, blade, socket, or cutting attachment.
A person who has been trained, evaluated, and permitted to use a specific tool or tool type, particularly for higher-risk tools requiring special authorization.
A portable tool powered by a rechargeable or disposable battery pack, requiring specific controls for battery handling, charging, storage, and thermal management.

A tool exhibiting cracks, bends, missing parts, worn components, loose handles, frayed cords, damaged housings, malfunctioning guards or switches, or any other condition that may create a hazard during use.
A barrier, shield, cover, or device attached to or enclosing a tool's hazardous moving parts to prevent contact with the operator's body during normal use.
A tool powered manually by the user, such as a wrench, hammer, screwdriver, pliers, chisel, file, or manual saw.

The path along which a hazard, force, projectile, tool slip, or sudden movement could travel and potentially contact a person. Includes the path of cutting blades, grinding wheels, fastener discharge, kickback, and tool reaction force.
A hand-held or hand-supported tool powered by electricity, battery, compressed air, hydraulic fluid, or powder charge.
A tool that uses an explosive charge or powder load to drive fasteners into hard substrates such as concrete or steel, requiring specific training, authorization, and controls.
A person with the training, skills, and authorization to perform maintenance, repair, or modification on tools, including electrical, mechanical, and pneumatic components.
An accessory that meets the manufacturer's specifications for speed, size, type, application, and material compatibility for the tool on which it is used.
An adjustable support mounted on a bench grinder or similar tool to support the workpiece at the correct angle and distance from the abrasive wheel.
A mechanism that prevents unintended activation of a power tool, requiring deliberate operator action to engage the tool.
Any use of a hand or power tool that deviates from its intended purpose, ignores manufacturer instructions, bypasses safety features, or exposes the user or others to uncontrolled hazards.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Own and maintain the Hand and Power Tools Safety Program. Develop and update tool safety procedures and training requirements. Conduct or support program audits, incident investigations, and corrective actions. Provide technical guidance on tool hazards, PPE, guards, and safe work practices. Monitor regulatory changes and update the program as needed. Track training records and authorization requirements for higher-risk tools.

Ensure employees are trained and authorized before using tools. Verify pre-use inspections are performed and damaged tools are removed from service. Enforce guarding, PPE, and safe work practice requirements. Plan work to reduce line-of-fire exposure and ergonomic strain. Ensure proper tool selection, accessory compatibility, and workpiece control. Investigate and report tool-related incidents and near misses. Allocate time and resources for proper tool maintenance and replacement.

Select the correct tool for each task. Inspect tools and accessories before use. Use guards, safety devices, and required PPE. Secure workpieces and maintain safe body positioning. Do not use damaged, modified, or improvised tools. Report tool defects, unsafe conditions, incidents, and near misses immediately. Follow manufacturer instructions and site procedures. Exercise stop-work authority when tool hazards cannot be controlled.

Issue tools in safe, serviceable condition. Remove damaged or expired tools from circulation. Maintain tool inventory and issuance records where required. Ensure replacement accessories are correctly rated and compatible. Coordinate with maintenance for tool repair and calibration. Manage battery charging locations and charger condition where applicable — [Tool Room / Equipment Control Process].

Perform tool repair, maintenance, and calibration within the scope of their qualifications. Use only approved replacement parts and accessories. Verify tool safety and function before returning to service. Document repairs and maintenance actions where required. Remove irreparable tools from service and ensure proper disposal or tagging.

Evaluate new tool purchases and modifications for safety and suitability. Specify tool and accessory requirements for new processes and equipment. Provide guidance on guarding, workholding, jigs, and fixture design. Support incident investigation with technical analysis of tool failures.

Comply with this procedure and site-specific tool safety requirements. Provide evidence of training and authorization for higher-risk tools. Use only safe, serviceable tools and approved accessories onsite. Follow site PPE, guarding, and work practice requirements. Report incidents, near misses, and tool defects immediately. Do not use unsafe, damaged, or unapproved tools onsite.

training: Correct tool selection based on task, material, environment, and required force or energy
Pre-use inspection requirements for hand tools, electric tools, battery tools, pneumatic tools, and accessories
Guarding and safety device requirements — guards, trigger safeties, deadman switches, side handles, and blade guards
Accessory compatibility and ratings — matching wheels, blades, bits, and attachments to tool speed, type, and intended use
Workpiece securing and line-of-fire control — clamps, vises, jigs, body positioning, and anticipating tool reaction
Safe body positioning — keeping hands, body parts, and personnel out of cutting paths, slip paths, and kickback zones
Electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic, and battery hazards as applicable to the tools being used
Required PPE for tool use — eye, face, hearing, hand, respiratory, and foot protection based on hazard assessment
Higher-risk tool restrictions — powder-actuated tools, grinders, saws, and other tools requiring specific authorization — [Higher-Risk Tool Authorization Requirements]
Storage, transport, and maintenance expectations — proper tool care, battery management, cord protection, and hose management
Incident and near-miss reporting requirements and escalation procedures
Stop-work authority and obligation when tool hazards cannot be controlled
ppe: Safety glasses or goggles for all tool use creating flying particle, fragment, chip, or dust hazards
Face shield for grinding, cutting, chipping, or other operations producing significant projectile or splash hazards
Cut-resistant gloves where appropriate for handling sharp materials, blades, or cutting tools — evaluate entanglement risk for rotating tools
Work gloves appropriate for impact, abrasion, vibration, or thermal protection where needed
Hearing protection for power tool use generating noise above action levels or where area noise monitoring requires it
Respiratory protection where dust, fumes, particulates, or chemical exposure from tool operations requires it
Protective footwear appropriate for the work environment and dropped-tool or heavy-material hazards
Arc-rated or task-specific clothing where electrical interfaces, hot work, or spark-generating tool use requires it
Knee pads, elbow pads, or anti-vibration gloves where ergonomic assessment or task conditions indicate benefit
hazards: Lacerations and puncture wounds from contact with cutting edges, blades, sharp tools, chisels, knives, and deburring tools
Amputations and crushing injuries from contact with rotating or reciprocating power tool components, grinding wheels, saw blades, and drill bits
Struck-by injuries from flying particles, fragments, broken wheel or blade pieces, ejected fasteners, and ricocheting materials
Caught-in and entanglement injuries from loose clothing, gloves, hair, or jewelry contacting rotating tools, drill chucks, or grinding wheels
Eye injuries from metal chips, wood particles, grinding dust, sparks, and flying debris generated during tool operations
Burns from sparks, hot surfaces, friction heat, and overheated tools or workpieces
Electrical shock or electrocution from damaged cords, missing grounds, improper use in wet conditions, or contact with energized systems during tool use
Noise-induced hearing damage from prolonged or repeated exposure to power tool noise above permissible levels
Ergonomic injuries from repetitive motion, excessive force, awkward posture, and vibration exposure during tool use
Kickback and sudden tool reaction from binding, pinching, or improper contact between the tool and workpiece
Fire and ignition hazards from sparks contacting combustible materials, flammable atmospheres, or dust accumulations during grinding, cutting, or drilling
Stored energy hazards from pneumatic pressure, hydraulic pressure, spring-loaded mechanisms, and battery energy in tools
Dropped-tool hazards when working at height, on platforms, or in areas with personnel below
Unintended tool activation from trigger lock engagement, tool movement during transport, or failure of safety mechanisms
procedure: 7.1 TOOL HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND TASK ASSESSMENT — Before tool use begins, the task, tool, accessory, workpiece, and environment shall be evaluated. Assessment shall include: type of tool and energy source, tool condition, accessory compatibility, material being worked, potential for kickback/binding/breakage/sudden movement, flying particles or fragments, sparks/heat/ignition generation, noise and vibration, line-of-fire exposure, need to secure the workpiece, ergonomic strain or awkward posture, electrical exposure, pressure or stored energy for pneumatic/hydraulic tools, environmental conditions (moisture, poor lighting, confined areas, elevated work), and interaction with nearby personnel or simultaneous operations. If the task cannot be performed safely with the selected tool and controls, the work shall not proceed until safer methods or equipment are established.

7.2 HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS FOR TOOL SAFETY — The following control hierarchy shall be applied: (1) Eliminate the need for hazardous tool use through redesign or alternate method; (2) Substitute a safer tool, accessory, or process; (3) Use engineering controls such as guards, clamps, jigs, workholding, spark shields, vacuum attachments, or remote positioning; (4) Use administrative controls such as procedures, training, restricted access, task sequencing, and supervision as supplemental controls; (5) Use PPE as a supporting measure, not as a substitute for safe tool selection and control. Work should be arranged to reduce line-of-fire exposure and direct contact with tool hazards whenever feasible.

7.3 TOOL SELECTION — Only tools appropriate to the task, material, environment, and required force or energy shall be used. Considerations include: intended design and application, required torque/speed/force/cutting capability, workpiece material, work location and access, availability of guards and safety features, ergonomic suitability, electrical classification or grounding needs, noise/dust/spark generation, need for precision or controlled force, and accessory compatibility. Improvised use of tools for unintended functions is prohibited — such as using a screwdriver as a chisel, a wrench as a hammer, pliers in place of the proper gripping tool, or impact tools with non-rated accessories.

7.4 PRE-USE INSPECTION — Tools and accessories shall be inspected before use, at shift start, or as required by site rules. Inspection shall include: general condition and cleanliness, cracks/bends/missing parts/wear/damage, handle condition and secure attachment, trigger and switch function, guard presence and condition, power cord/plug/insulation condition, grounding features where required, battery condition and secure fit, hose/coupling/fitting condition for pneumatic or hydraulic tools, accessory condition/mounting/rating, labels/speed ratings/warnings, and excessive vibration/looseness/unusual noise during safe test operation. Damaged or suspect tools shall be removed from service immediately, tagged, and reported.

7.5 HAND TOOL SAFETY — Hand tools shall be used in a controlled manner to reduce slip, struck-by, puncture, and ergonomic hazards. Requirements include: using the correct hand tool for the task, keeping cutting edges sharp and protected where appropriate, maintaining secure grip and stable footing, positioning the body out of the path of tool slip or sudden release, using non-sparking tools where required by hazard assessment, not using cheater bars/excessive force/improvised extensions unless specifically engineered or approved, not carrying sharp tools loosely in pockets where unsafe, and storing tools to prevent dropped-object and cutting hazards. Struck tools such as chisels and punches shall be free from mushroomed heads or unsafe deformation.

7.6 PORTABLE ELECTRIC TOOL SAFETY — Electric portable tools shall be used in accordance with electrical safety requirements and manufacturer instructions. Requirements include: inspecting cords/plugs/housings/switches before use, using grounding or double-insulated tools as intended, using GFCI protection where required, protecting cords from pinch points/sharp edges/heat/water/traffic, not carrying tools by the cord, not yanking cords to disconnect, disconnecting or isolating power before changing accessories/clearing jams/performing adjustments unless the tool design safely allows otherwise, and keeping hands clear of rotating or cutting parts. Electric tools shall not be used in wet or conductive conditions unless specifically rated and controlled for that environment.

7.7 BATTERY-POWERED TOOL SAFETY — Battery-powered tools shall be used and managed to reduce activation, electrical, thermal, and damage hazards. Requirements include: using only manufacturer-approved batteries and chargers unless an approved equivalent is specifically authorized, inspecting batteries for cracking/swelling/leakage/damage, removing batteries before adjustment/transport/storage where appropriate, preventing unintended trigger activation during transport, protecting batteries from excessive heat/moisture/impact/short-circuit conditions, charging batteries in suitable locations per manufacturer instructions — [Battery Charging Location Requirements], and prohibiting use of damaged batteries or chargers. Battery packs with signs of overheating, deformation, or unusual odor shall be removed from service immediately.

7.8 PNEUMATIC AND HYDRAULIC TOOL SAFETY — Pneumatic and hydraulic tools shall be used in a manner that controls hose whip, pressure release, unintended activation, and stored energy hazards. Requirements include: inspecting hoses/fittings/couplings/tool bodies before use, securing hose connections properly, using whip checks or other retention controls where required, confirming pressure ratings are appropriate, depressurizing the system before disconnecting hoses or servicing tools, routing hoses to avoid damage/trips/line-of-fire hazards, not pointing compressed air or pressurized tool components at personnel, and not exceeding recommended operating pressure. Compressed air shall not be used for personal cleaning or unsafe blow-off practices.

7.9 ABRASIVE WHEELS, GRINDERS, AND SANDING TOOLS — Grinding and abrasive tool use shall control wheel failure, kickback, sparks, flying particles, and improper accessory selection. Requirements include: verifying wheel/disc/accessory rating for tool speed and intended use, inspecting wheels and discs for cracks/chips/wear/damage before use, ensuring guards are installed and positioned correctly, using the correct flange/nut/mounting method, conducting ring testing where required before mounting certain wheels, allowing grinders to reach operating speed before applying to the workpiece, not using side pressure on wheels not designed for side loading, positioning the body out of the line of fire of wheel failure, controlling sparks and ignition hazards, and using face and eye protection. Grinders shall not be operated without required guards except where a manufacturer-designed and specifically approved configuration allows it.

7.10 CUTTING TOOLS, SAWS, AND BLADES — Cutting tools and saws shall be used in a manner that controls cutting path, kickback, blade contact, and workpiece movement. Requirements include: using the correct blade for the tool and material, inspecting blades for damage/dullness/missing teeth/improper mounting, ensuring guards and retracting blade devices function correctly, securing the workpiece where needed, keeping hands and body parts out of the cutting path, maintaining firm control and stable positioning, avoiding overreaching or awkward cutting positions, not bypassing retracting guards or safety features, and allowing blades to stop before setting tools down. Knives and manual cutting tools shall be used with controlled cutting direction away from the body whenever practical.

7.11 IMPACT TOOLS, FASTENING TOOLS, AND POWDER-ACTUATED TOOLS — Impact tools and fastening tools shall be controlled for ricochet, unintended discharge, flying fasteners, and reaction force hazards. Requirements include: using the correct fastener and load for the tool and substrate, inspecting the tool and safety mechanism before use, keeping the tool pointed in a safe direction, not disabling contact safeties or trigger protections, controlling access to the work area, using appropriate PPE including eye and hearing protection, and following manufacturer instructions and site authorization requirements. Powder-actuated tools shall only be used by trained and authorized personnel under site controls specific to that tool type — [Higher-Risk Tool Authorization Requirements].

7.12 WORKPIECE CONTROL, LINE OF FIRE, AND BODY POSITIONING — The workpiece shall be stabilized or restrained as needed to prevent movement, binding, slip, or ejection. Requirements include: use of clamps, vises, fixtures, or jigs, keeping hands clear of pinch points and cutting paths, maintaining body position outside the line of fire, anticipating tool reaction force or kickback, not holding small workpieces by hand where the tool could contact the hand, and not placing body parts behind the workpiece or in the likely slip path of the tool. Unsafe body positioning is a major contributor to tool injuries and shall be actively addressed during task planning and supervision.

7.13 GUARDS, SAFETY DEVICES, AND TOOL MODIFICATIONS — Guards and safety devices shall remain installed and functional unless removed under an approved maintenance or repair process with equivalent protection. Requirements include: not removing wheel guards, blade guards, trigger safeties, side handles, deadman switches, or other protective devices for convenience, using side handles where required for tool control, not taping triggers in the on position unless specifically designed and approved, not modifying housings/switches/plugs/grounding systems/accessory retention devices, and not using makeshift guards or improvised repairs. Modified tools shall be removed from service unless approved by engineering and the manufacturer's requirements or equivalent safety basis are met.

7.14 PPE FOR HAND AND POWER TOOL USE — PPE selection shall be based on the hazards created by the tool, accessory, material, and work environment. Required PPE may include: safety glasses, face shields, cut-resistant gloves where appropriate, work gloves where appropriate, hearing protection, respiratory protection where dust/fumes/particulates require it, protective footwear, and arc-rated or task-specific clothing where electrical or hot work interfaces exist. Glove selection shall consider whether gloves increase entanglement or snag hazards for rotating tools. PPE does not replace safe tool selection, guarding, or workpiece control.

7.15 STORAGE, TRANSPORT, AND MAINTENANCE — Tools shall be stored, transported, and maintained in a way that prevents damage and unintended hazard. Requirements include: storing tools in designated areas, protecting cutting edges and accessories from damage, removing batteries or disconnecting power where appropriate during transport, securing tools against falling or shifting, keeping chargers and battery storage areas orderly and suitable, performing maintenance only by qualified personnel as required, using approved replacement parts and accessories, and returning tools to service only after confirming safe condition. Dirty, damaged, or poorly stored tools shall not be issued or used until corrected.

7.16 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION — Coordination is required when contractors use hand or power tools onsite. Requirements include: type of tools being used, higher-risk tools or special authorization requirements, PPE expectations, electrical and pneumatic interfaces, housekeeping and spark/dust generation, area controls and access restrictions, permit requirements where applicable, and reporting of incidents, tool failures, or unsafe conditions. Contractors shall not use unsafe, damaged, or unapproved tools onsite.

7.17 INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW — Immediate reporting is required for any tool-related injury, near miss, accessory failure, guard removal issue, kickback event, dropped tool event, shock, spark-related incident, or unsafe tool condition. Investigations shall evaluate tool selection, accessory compatibility, guarding and safety device use, workpiece control, body positioning and line-of-fire exposure, training adequacy, supervision and planning, tool condition and maintenance, environmental conditions, and need for engineering/procedural/training improvements. EHS and operational leadership shall periodically review the Hand and Power Tools Safety Program for effectiveness and update requirements as needed.
emergencyResponse: Stop work immediately if a tool-related injury, near miss, or unsafe condition occurs
Remove the injured person from the hazard zone if safe to do so — do not attempt to clear jammed tools while energized or pressurized
Deenergize, depressurize, or otherwise isolate the tool before attempting any corrective action on the tool itself
Apply first aid within training — control bleeding, stabilize injuries, and avoid disturbing embedded objects
For amputations, locate the severed part if possible, wrap in clean moist dressing, place in a sealed bag on ice, and transport with the patient
Activate site emergency response for severe lacerations, amputations, crush injuries, electrical shock, or eye injuries
Keep non-essential personnel clear of the area and preserve the scene for investigation where feasible
Notify supervision and EHS immediately — document all details including tool, accessory, task, and conditions
Do not resume work with the involved tool or in the area until the incident has been investigated and corrective actions are in place
For electrical shock from a power tool, follow the Electrical Safety emergency response procedure — do not touch the person if they may still be in contact with the energized source
documentation: Training and authorization records for employees using hand and power tools, including higher-risk tool authorizations — [Higher-Risk Tool Authorization Requirements]
Tool inspection records where required by site policy, particularly for grinders, powder-actuated tools, and pneumatic tools
Repair and maintenance records documenting tool service, part replacement, and return-to-service verification
Incident, near miss, and investigation records for tool-related injuries, failures, and unsafe conditions
Corrective action records including root cause, assigned actions, completion tracking, and effectiveness verification
Contractor coordination records documenting tool safety briefings, authorization checks, and reported issues
Audit findings and program review records including recommendations, follow-up actions, and closure dates
Tool issuance or inventory records where applicable — [Tool Room / Equipment Control Process]

APPENDIX A — TOOL USE PRE-PLANNING CHECKLIST:
☐ Correct tool selected for the task
☐ Workpiece and material understood
☐ Hazards and line-of-fire exposures identified
☐ Required accessories correct and rated
☐ Guards and safety devices present
☐ Required PPE available
☐ Workpiece securing method defined
☐ Power source or hose setup appropriate
☐ Nearby personnel and simultaneous operations addressed
☐ Emergency and stop-work triggers understood

APPENDIX B — PRE-USE INSPECTION CHECKLIST:
☐ Tool body in acceptable condition
☐ Handle secure and intact
☐ Trigger or switch functions properly
☐ Guard present and functional
☐ Cord, plug, battery, hose, or fitting in good condition
☐ Accessory correct, secure, and undamaged
☐ Labels or ratings legible where relevant
☐ No missing parts, cracks, excessive wear, or unusual looseness present
☐ Tool appears safe to operate

APPENDIX C — GRINDER / SAW / ACCESSORY CHECKLIST:
☐ Accessory rated for tool speed and type
☐ Wheel, disc, or blade undamaged
☐ Guard installed and adjusted properly
☐ Mounting hardware correct and secure
☐ Workpiece restrained if needed
☐ Body positioned out of the line of fire
☐ Sparks, dust, and fragment hazards controlled
☐ Required face, eye, hearing, and respiratory protection addressed

APPENDIX D — BATTERY / ELECTRIC / PNEUMATIC TOOL CHECKLIST:
☐ Battery, charger, cord, or hose in good condition
☐ Grounding or double-insulation requirements met
☐ GFCI protection provided where required
☐ Hose connections secure
☐ Pressure rating appropriate
☐ Tool disconnected or deenergized before accessory changes where required
☐ Routing protects cords and hoses from damage
☐ No signs of overheating, leakage, swelling, or electrical damage present

APPENDIX E — CONDITIONS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE STOP WORK:
• Wrong tool or accessory being used
• Tool, guard, cord, hose, battery, or accessory damaged or questionable
• Workpiece cannot be safely secured or controlled
• Required PPE unavailable or inadequate
• Guard or safety device missing or defeated
• Tool behaves abnormally, vibrates excessively, overheats, or emits unusual noise
• Sparks, dust, movement, or reaction force create uncontrolled hazard
• A near miss, failure, or unsafe condition occurs and remains uncorrected
• A supervisor, EHS, or other responsible person stops the work
• Conditions change such that safe tool use cannot be assured

title: Welding, Cutting, Brazing, and Hot Work Permit Program
summary: A comprehensive Welding, Cutting, Brazing, and Hot Work Permit Program covering hot work hazard identification, permit requirements, area preparation, fire prevention, combustible and flammable hazard control, fire watch, post-work monitoring, gas cylinder safety, ventilation and health exposure controls, PPE, confined space hot work, elevated hot work, process equipment hot work, contractor coordination, equipment inspection, incident reporting, and program review.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, evaluating, authorizing, and safely performing welding, cutting, brazing, soldering, grinding, heating, and other hot work activities that can introduce ignition sources, fire hazards, explosion hazards, health exposure hazards, and operational risk at [Company Name].

The purpose is to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, fire, explosion, burns, eye injuries, respiratory injury, oxygen/fuel gas incidents, toxic fume exposure, equipment damage, property damage, environmental release, and operational disruption resulting from uncontrolled sparks, slag, hot surfaces, open flames, heat-producing equipment, combustible materials, flammable atmospheres, inadequate atmospheric evaluation, poor ventilation, improper cylinder handling, inadequate fire watch, ineffective area preparation, and failure to control hot work permit conditions.

This procedure supports compliance with applicable OSHA standards, NFPA 51B, industry codes, manufacturer requirements, and [Company Name] policies. It applies to all hot work activities performed on company-controlled sites and shall be used by all personnel who perform, authorize, supervise, inspect, support, or work near hot work operations.

COMPLIANCE DISCLAIMER: This SOP template is provided as operational guidance and does not replace applicable federal, state, or local regulatory requirements, consensus standards, or specific internal company procedures. [Company Name] is responsible for ensuring compliance with all applicable regulations and adapting this template to site-specific conditions.
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, laboratories, production areas, fabrication areas, maintenance activities, warehouses, utilities, yards, test areas, field locations, construction support areas, and non-routine operations where welding, cutting, brazing, soldering, or other hot work activities may occur at [Site Name].

It applies to:
• Employees who perform, supervise, authorize, inspect, support, or work near hot work activities
• Welders, cutters, brazers, fitters, fabricators, mechanics, technicians, and maintenance personnel
• Supervisors and managers responsible for hot work planning and execution
• Permit issuers and area owners
• EHS personnel
• Fire watch personnel
• Contractors and subcontractors performing hot work onsite

It applies to hot work activities including, but not limited to:
• Arc welding (MIG, TIG, stick, flux-cored)
• Oxy-fuel cutting
• Plasma cutting
• Brazing and soldering
• Torch heating and thermal gouging
• Grinding where sparks or ignition hazards are created
• Heating with open flame or heat-producing equipment
• Other spark-producing or flame-producing work requiring hot work controls

It applies to hot work performed in, on, or around:
• Buildings and occupied work areas
• Production and fabrication spaces
• Maintenance shops and utility areas
• Storage areas
• Tanks and vessels
• Piping systems
• Elevated platforms and rooftops
• Confined spaces
• Outdoor work areas and temporary work areas
• Areas containing combustible, flammable, explosive, or contamination hazards

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with other required programs such as Permit-to-Work, Confined Space Entry, Hazardous Energy / LOTO, Electrical Safety, Respiratory Protection, Hazard Communication, Pressurized Systems, Health Exposure to Hazardous Materials, Contractor Safety Management, Non-Routine Activities, and Emergency Response requirements where applicable.

definitions.definition: Any area adjacent to, above, below, or on the opposite side of a barrier from the hot work location where sparks, heat, slag, or hot metal could travel and create an ignition hazard.

A specifically designated and approved location designed, maintained, and managed for routine hot work where combustible and flammable hazards have been permanently controlled and fire protection is in place.

A metal-joining process in which a filler metal with a melting point above 840°F (450°C) is distributed between closely fitted surfaces of the joint by capillary action, producing heat and potential ignition hazards.

A material capable of igniting and burning when exposed to sufficient heat, sparks, open flame, or radiant energy. Includes wood, paper, cardboard, cloth, insulation, rubber, plastics, oil residues, dust, and similar materials.

Hot work performed inside a confined space or permit-required confined space, requiring coordination of hot work permit controls with confined space entry permit controls, atmospheric monitoring, ventilation, and rescue planning.
A trained and equipped person assigned to monitor for fire during and after hot work, maintain readiness to respond to incipient fires, and ensure that the work area remains safe from ignition.

Any activity that produces sparks, open flame, extreme heat, or sufficient thermal energy to ignite flammable or combustible materials. Includes welding, cutting, brazing, soldering, grinding, torch heating, thermal gouging, and similar operations.

A documented authorization that identifies the scope, location, hazards, controls, fire prevention measures, and responsibilities for a specific hot work task performed outside a designated authorized hot work area.

The process of displacing or diluting a flammable or oxygen-enriched atmosphere in a vessel, tank, piping system, or enclosed space with an inert gas to prevent combustion or explosion during hot work.

A work location that has been inspected, prepared, and verified as acceptable for hot work based on the removal or protection of combustibles, control of flammable atmospheres, availability of fire protection, and completion of required permit conditions.
A metal-joining process using a filler metal with a melting point below 840°F (450°C), producing heat and potential ignition hazards at a lower intensity than brazing or welding.
Any activity that generates sparks capable of traveling and igniting combustible or flammable materials, including grinding, cutting, drilling into ferrous metals, and chipping.

A metal-joining process that uses heat, pressure, or both to fuse materials together, typically involving electric arc, gas flame, or resistance heating that produces sparks, molten metal, slag, UV radiation, fumes, and heat.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Develop and maintain this program. Establish hot work permit requirements, fire prevention standards, and fire watch expectations. Conduct or support hazard assessments for hot work activities. Verify training, competency, and authorization programs. Provide technical guidance on atmospheric testing, ventilation, fume exposure, cylinder safety, and PPE selection. Support incident investigation and corrective action. Audit hot work permits, fire watch performance, area preparation compliance, and contractor coordination. Conduct periodic program reviews and updates.

Ensure personnel under their direction understand and comply with this procedure. Verify that hot work hazards have been assessed, permits obtained, area preparation completed, and controls established before work begins. Assign qualified operators and fire watch personnel. Stop work when conditions are unsafe or permit conditions are not met. Ensure equipment is inspected and maintained. Report incidents, near misses, and permit deviations. Support training and program improvements.

Inspect the work area before issuing or approving a hot work permit. Verify that combustibles are controlled, fire protection is available, ventilation is adequate, and required isolations are complete. Confirm qualified personnel and fire watch are assigned. Sign or authorize the permit only when conditions are acceptable. Revoke or suspend permits when conditions change. Verify post-work fire monitoring is performed.

Perform hot work only within the scope, location, and conditions authorized by the permit or standing procedure. Inspect equipment before use. Verify area preparation has been completed. Use required PPE. Follow safe work practices for the specific hot work process. Report equipment failures, unsafe conditions, permit deviations, and incidents immediately. Stop work if conditions change or become unsafe.

Remain attentive and present in the work area during hot work and for the required post-work monitoring duration. Monitor the immediate work area and exposed adjacent areas for fire, smoldering, or ignition. Maintain suitable extinguishing equipment immediately available. Know how to activate emergency response. Stop work and alert personnel if fire or unsafe conditions are identified. Do not leave the fire watch post or accept conflicting duties during active monitoring.

Support area preparation, combustible control, isolation, ventilation setup, fire protection readiness, and atmospheric testing where required. Ensure fire suppression systems, detectors, and alarms are managed appropriately during hot work. Support equipment maintenance and cylinder management. Assist with post-work restoration of fire protection and detection systems.

Comply with this procedure and all applicable site requirements for hot work. Obtain required permits and approvals before starting hot work. Use inspected and safe equipment. Provide qualified operators and fire watch as required. Follow cylinder safety, ventilation, and PPE requirements. Report incidents, near misses, and unsafe conditions to the host employer. Participate in coordination meetings and pre-job briefings.

training: Recognition of hot work hazards — fire, explosion, burns, fumes, toxic exposure, and oxygen/fuel gas risks
Hot work permit requirements and authorization limits
Combustible and flammable material identification and control
Area preparation and spark containment measures
Fire watch duties, responsibilities, and post-work monitoring requirements
Gas cylinder handling, storage, transport, and safe use
Ventilation requirements and fume hazard controls
PPE requirements — welding helmets, flame-resistant clothing, gloves, respiratory protection
Confined space and non-routine hot work interfaces
Elevated hot work and adjacent area control
Hot work on containers, vessels, piping, and process equipment
Equipment inspection and setup requirements
Emergency response and fire reporting procedures
Incident and near-miss reporting requirements
Stop-work authority and obligation
ppe: Welding helmet with appropriate lens shade for the specific process and arc type
Safety glasses with side shields worn under the helmet or during adjacent tasks
Face shield where applicable for grinding, cutting, or support tasks
Flame-resistant clothing — long sleeves, high collar, no synthetic or melt-prone materials
Welding gloves appropriate for the hot work process
Leather aprons, jackets, sleeves, or leggings as appropriate for the task and exposure
Hearing protection where noise levels require it
Respiratory protection where fume, gas, or particulate exposure requires it — managed under the Respiratory Protection Program
Protective footwear — leather work boots, no open-toe or synthetic uppers where spark exposure exists
Head protection where overhead hazards or site requirements apply

NOTE: Synthetic clothing, nylon, polyester, or other melt-prone fabrics shall not be worn where spark or heat exposure creates additional burn hazard. PPE does not replace area preparation, fire prevention, or permit controls.
hazards: Fire from sparks, slag, hot metal, open flame, or radiant heat contacting combustible materials
Explosion from flammable vapors, gases, dusts, or residues ignited by hot work
Burns from contact with hot surfaces, molten metal, slag, sparks, or UV/IR radiation
Eye injuries from arc flash, intense light, flying particles, and UV radiation
Respiratory injury from welding fumes, metal oxides, hexavalent chromium, zinc, lead, manganese, cadmium, and coating decomposition products
Toxic fume and gas exposure — carbon monoxide, ozone, nitrogen oxides, phosgene from chlorinated solvents near heat
Oxygen/fuel gas hazards — cylinder rupture, flashback, regulator failure, hose damage, improper connections
Oxygen-enriched or oxygen-deficient atmospheres in enclosed or confined areas
Electrical shock from welding equipment, damaged leads, wet conditions, or improper grounding
Noise from grinding, cutting, and arc processes
Ergonomic strain from sustained awkward positions during welding and cutting tasks
Hidden fire — ignition in concealed spaces, wall cavities, insulation, pipe chases, or adjacent areas hours after hot work completion
Falling sparks and slag from elevated hot work creating fire and burn hazards below
Secondary hazards — falls from elevation during hot work, struck-by from dropped tools or materials, and heat stress
procedure:
**1. PURPOSE**

This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, evaluating, authorizing, and safely performing welding, cutting, brazing, soldering, grinding, heating, and other hot work activities that can introduce ignition sources, fire hazards, explosion hazards, health exposure hazards, and operational risk at [Company Name].

The purpose is to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, fire, explosion, burns, eye injuries, respiratory injury, oxygen/fuel gas incidents, toxic fume exposure, equipment damage, property damage, environmental release, and operational disruption resulting from uncontrolled sparks, slag, hot surfaces, open flames, heat-producing equipment, combustible materials, flammable atmospheres, inadequate atmospheric evaluation, poor ventilation, improper cylinder handling, inadequate fire watch, ineffective area preparation, and failure to control hot work permit conditions.

COMPLIANCE DISCLAIMER: This SOP template is provided as operational guidance and does not replace applicable federal, state, or local regulatory requirements, consensus standards, or specific internal company procedures.

**2. SCOPE**

This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, laboratories, production areas, fabrication areas, maintenance activities, warehouses, utilities, yards, test areas, field locations, construction support areas, and non-routine operations where welding, cutting, brazing, soldering, or other hot work activities may occur at [Site Name].

Personnel covered include welders, cutters, brazers, fitters, fabricators, mechanics, technicians, maintenance personnel, supervisors, managers, permit issuers, area owners, EHS personnel, fire watch personnel, and contractors performing hot work onsite.

Hot work activities covered include arc welding (MIG, TIG, stick, flux-cored), oxy-fuel cutting, plasma cutting, brazing, soldering, torch heating, thermal gouging, grinding where sparks or ignition hazards are created, heating with open flame or heat-producing equipment, and other spark-producing or flame-producing work requiring hot work controls.

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with Permit-to-Work, Confined Space Entry, Hazardous Energy / LOTO, Electrical Safety, Respiratory Protection, Hazard Communication, Pressurized Systems, Health Exposure to Hazardous Materials, Contractor Safety Management, Non-Routine Activities, and Emergency Response programs where applicable.

**3. REFERENCES**

• OSHA 29 CFR 1910 Subpart Q — Welding, Cutting, and Brazing
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.252 — General Requirements
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.253 — Oxygen-Fuel Gas Welding and Cutting
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.254 — Arc Welding and Cutting
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.255 — Resistance Welding
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.146 — Permit-Required Confined Spaces where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134 — Respiratory Protection where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1200 — Hazard Communication
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147 — Lockout/Tagout where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.157 — Portable Fire Extinguishers where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.132 and 1910.133 — PPE and Eye/Face Protection where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart J — Welding and Cutting (Construction) where applicable
• NFPA 51B — Standard for Fire Prevention During Welding, Cutting, and Other Hot Work
• Manufacturer instructions for welding machines, torches, regulators, hoses, cylinders, PPE, local exhaust systems, and related equipment
• [Company Name] Permit to Work Procedure
• [Company Name] Confined Space Entry Procedure
• [Company Name] Hazardous Energy / LOTO Procedure
• [Company Name] Respiratory Protection Program
• [Company Name] Incident Reporting and Investigation Procedure
• [Company Name] Emergency Response Plan
• Site-specific fire prevention, gas cylinder, and hot work controls where applicable
• [Applicable State / Local Requirements]
• [Related Company Procedures]

**4. DEFINITIONS**

• Affected Area — Any area adjacent to, above, below, or on the opposite side of a barrier from the hot work location where sparks, heat, slag, or hot metal could travel and create an ignition hazard.
• Authorized Hot Work Area — A specifically designated and approved location designed, maintained, and managed for routine hot work where combustible and flammable hazards have been permanently controlled.
• Brazing — A metal-joining process using filler metal above 840°F distributed by capillary action.
• Combustible Material — A material capable of igniting when exposed to sufficient heat, sparks, flame, or radiant energy.
• Confined Space Hot Work — Hot work performed inside a confined space requiring coordination of hot work and confined space controls.
• Fire Watch — A trained and equipped person assigned to monitor for fire during and after hot work.
• Hot Work — Any activity producing sparks, open flame, extreme heat, or sufficient thermal energy to ignite flammable or combustible materials.
• Hot Work Permit — Documented authorization identifying scope, hazards, controls, and responsibilities for hot work outside a designated area.
• Inerting — Displacing a flammable atmosphere with inert gas to prevent combustion during hot work.
• Safe Hot Work Area — A location inspected, prepared, and verified as acceptable for hot work.
• Soldering — Metal joining using filler below 840°F.
• Spark-Producing Work — Any activity generating sparks capable of igniting combustible or flammable materials.
• Welding — A metal-joining process using heat, pressure, or both to fuse materials.

**5. POLICY**

[Company Name] will manage welding, cutting, brazing, and other hot work through planning, permit control, fire prevention, atmospheric and exposure control, equipment inspection, trained personnel, fire watch, supervision, and periodic review.

No hot work may be performed unless:
• The work scope is clearly defined
• Hazards have been identified and assessed
• The work area has been inspected and prepared
• Combustibles, flammables, and other ignition hazards have been controlled
• Required permits and approvals are in place
• Appropriate equipment, PPE, and ventilation are available
• Fire watch and post-work monitoring requirements are defined and implemented
• Conditions remain consistent with the approved permit and work plan

The company will prioritize avoiding hot work where feasible by using alternate methods such as cold cutting, mechanical joining, offsite fabrication, pre-fabrication, or work relocation to a designated hot work area.

Employees shall not perform hot work based solely on schedule pressure, convenience, or informal verbal approval when permit review or additional controls are required.

No employee shall begin or continue hot work when permit conditions, fire protection controls, atmospheric conditions, or work area conditions are not acceptable.

**6. RESPONSIBILITIES**

EHS / Program Administrator: Develop and maintain this program. Establish hot work permit requirements, fire prevention standards, and fire watch expectations. Conduct or support hazard assessments. Verify training, competency, and authorization programs. Provide technical guidance on atmospheric testing, ventilation, fume exposure, cylinder safety, and PPE selection. Support incident investigation and corrective action. Audit hot work permits and contractor coordination. Conduct periodic program reviews.

Managers and Supervisors: Ensure compliance with this procedure. Verify hazard assessments, permits, area preparation, and controls before work begins. Assign qualified operators and fire watch. Stop work when conditions are unsafe. Report incidents and near misses.

Permit Issuers / Area Owners: Inspect work areas before issuing permits. Verify combustible control, fire protection, ventilation, and isolations. Confirm qualified personnel and fire watch are assigned. Revoke permits when conditions change. Verify post-work monitoring.

Hot Work Operators: Perform work only within authorized scope and conditions. Inspect equipment before use. Verify area preparation. Use required PPE. Follow safe work practices. Report equipment failures and unsafe conditions immediately.

Fire Watch Personnel: Remain attentive and present during hot work and post-work monitoring. Monitor for fire and ignition. Maintain extinguishing equipment. Know emergency response activation. Stop work if conditions are unsafe.

Maintenance / Facilities / Operations Support: Support area preparation, isolation, ventilation, fire protection, and atmospheric testing. Manage fire suppression systems during hot work. Support equipment and cylinder management.

Contractors: Comply with all site requirements. Obtain required permits. Use inspected equipment. Provide qualified operators and fire watch. Follow cylinder, ventilation, and PPE requirements. Report incidents and near misses.

**7.1 HOT WORK HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND ASSESSMENT**

All hot work tasks, locations, adjacent areas, and environmental conditions shall be evaluated before hot work begins to identify hazards and determine the controls required.

Hazard assessments shall consider:
• Type of hot work activity and ignition sources created
• Combustible and flammable materials in the area — loose materials, stored goods, coatings, insulation, residues
• Hidden combustibles behind walls, under flooring, above ceilings, in adjacent spaces
• Openings, penetrations, cracks, drains, ducting, and pathways for spark and slag travel
• Presence of flammable liquids, vapors, gases, dusts, or residues
• Process equipment contents and contamination history
• Atmospheric hazards in enclosed or confined areas
• Ventilation needs — natural, mechanical, local exhaust
• Fire protection availability — extinguishers, hose stations, suppression systems
• Need for fire watch — during work and post-work
• Potential impact on nearby personnel, equipment, utilities, and operations
• Elevated work or falling spark hazards
• Need for permit-to-work, confined space permit, line breaking control, or LOTO
• Need for isolation of detectors, suppression systems, or process alarms under controlled conditions

If hazards cannot be adequately controlled, the hot work shall not proceed until additional review, planning, or alternate methods are established.

**7.2 HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS FOR HOT WORK**

Hot work hazards shall be controlled using the following preferred order:

1. Eliminate the need for hot work by using alternate methods such as cold cutting, mechanical fastening, adhesive bonding, offsite fabrication, or pre-fabrication
2. Relocate the work to a designated safe hot work area when feasible
3. Remove, isolate, drain, purge, clean, or protect combustible and flammable hazards in the work area
4. Use engineering controls such as fire-resistant barriers, welding screens, welding blankets, fire-resistant covers, spark containment, and ventilation systems
5. Use administrative controls such as hot work permits, area restrictions, fire watch, scheduling, work sequencing, and pre-job reviews as supplemental controls
6. Use PPE as a supporting measure, not as a substitute for area preparation and fire prevention

Whenever feasible, hot work shall be avoided in hazardous or congested areas by changing the method or relocating the work.

**7.3 HOT WORK PERMIT REQUIREMENTS**

A hot work permit shall be required for hot work performed outside a designated authorized hot work area unless site policy defines a controlled exemption for specific low-risk operations.

The hot work permit shall include:
• Work scope, location, and type of hot work
• Date and time limits — start, expected completion, and permit expiration
• Area preparation requirements and combustible/flammable hazard controls
• Gas testing or atmospheric verification requirements where applicable
• Required isolations and shutdowns
• Fire watch assignment and duration
• Required extinguishing equipment
• Ventilation requirements
• PPE requirements
• Post-work fire monitoring duration — minimum [Post-Work Fire Watch Duration]
• Required signatures or approvals — [Hot Work Permit Approval Authority]

The permit shall be posted or otherwise maintained at the work location where practical and shall be valid only for the approved scope, location, and time period. Any change in scope, location, or conditions requires permit review or reissuance.

**7.4 DESIGNATED HOT WORK AREAS**

Routine hot work may be performed in designated hot work areas only when the area has been specifically designed, approved, and maintained for such work at [Designated Hot Work Area Locations].

Designated hot work areas shall:
• Be free of unnecessary combustible and flammable materials
• Have suitable ventilation — general, local exhaust, or both as required
• Have appropriate fire protection equipment available
• Have stable work surfaces and safe access
• Provide shielding from adjacent personnel and operations — welding screens, curtains, or barriers
• Be maintained in a condition appropriate for continued hot work

If conditions in a designated area change such that hazards increase, permit controls or additional review may still be required until conditions are restored.

**7.5 WORK AREA PREPARATION**

The work area shall be prepared before hot work begins to control ignition hazards.

Area preparation shall include:
• Removing combustible materials from the area within a minimum 35-foot radius or as required by site conditions
• Relocating flammable materials and gas cylinders not needed for the work
• Sweeping or cleaning combustible dust and residues where required
• Covering combustible materials with approved fire-resistant blankets or shields when removal is not feasible
• Protecting openings, floor penetrations, drain inlets, ductwork, and wall openings where sparks or hot slag may travel
• Wetting down combustibles only when appropriate and not introducing other hazards such as electrical contact or slip hazards
• Protecting cables, hoses, and sensitive equipment from sparks and heat
• Establishing welding screens or barricades to protect nearby personnel

Area preparation shall consider adjacent spaces, lower levels, overhead spaces, and opposite sides of walls or partitions. The area shall be reinspected if conditions change during the work.

**7.6 COMBUSTIBLE, FLAMMABLE, AND ATMOSPHERIC HAZARD CONTROLS**

Hot work shall not be performed in locations where flammable vapors, combustible dust, explosive residues, or other ignition-sensitive atmospheres may be present unless the hazards have been eliminated or controlled.

Controls shall include as applicable:
• Isolation of process equipment
• Draining and cleaning of vessels, piping, and tanks
• Purging and inerting where appropriate
• Atmospheric testing before and during work where required — [Atmospheric Testing Requirements]
• Ventilation to prevent hazardous accumulation
• Shutdown or suspension of nearby flammable operations
• Removal of combustible waste or residues
• Gas monitoring in enclosed or permit-controlled spaces

Hot work shall not proceed if atmospheric test results, contamination conditions, or process status indicate unsafe ignition risk.

**7.7 VENTILATION AND HEALTH EXPOSURE CONTROLS**

Controls shall be implemented for fumes, gases, particulates, and oxygen displacement hazards from welding, cutting, brazing, and other hot work.

Controls shall include as applicable:
• Local exhaust ventilation positioned at the source of fume generation
• General dilution ventilation to maintain acceptable air quality
• Fume extraction systems — portable or fixed
• Isolation from adjacent personnel where fumes may migrate
• Respiratory protection where required by hazard assessment — managed under the Respiratory Protection Program
• Selection of safer consumables or methods where feasible — low-fume electrodes, water tables
• Review of coatings, residues, metals, and surface contaminants that may generate toxic byproducts when heated

Special attention shall be given to:
• Stainless steel welding — hexavalent chromium exposure
• Galvanized materials — zinc oxide fume exposure
• Coated metals — decomposition of paint, primer, or coating materials
• Painted surfaces — lead or other toxic metals
• Confined spaces and enclosed areas — concentrated fume accumulation

Where airborne hazards cannot be adequately controlled by ventilation, the Respiratory Protection Program shall apply.

**7.8 EQUIPMENT INSPECTION AND SETUP**

Hot work equipment shall be inspected and set up before use.

Inspection and setup shall include:
• Welding machine condition — power source, case, cooling, controls
• Power cord and electrode lead condition — insulation, connections, damage
• Grounding or return lead condition — proper connection and secure attachment
• Torch body, valves, tips, and fittings — condition and function
• Regulators and gauges — condition, calibration status where applicable, proper connection
• Hose condition, routing, and secure connections — no damage, kinks, leaks, or improper repairs
• Flashback arrestors and check valves installed where required
• Cylinder condition and securement — upright, stable, valve protection
• Accessory and consumable condition — electrodes, tips, nozzles, shields
• Fire extinguisher availability — charged, accessible, suitable for the environment
• Adequate separation of hoses and leads from traffic, sharp edges, heat, and pinch points

Defective or questionable hot work equipment shall be removed from service immediately and not returned until repaired and verified safe.

**7.9 GAS CYLINDER SAFETY**

Fuel gas and oxygen cylinders shall be handled, stored, transported, and used safely.

Cylinder safety requirements include:
• Securing cylinders upright where required — chains, straps, carts, or cylinder stands
• Keeping protective valve caps in place when cylinders are not in use or not connected
• Separating oxygen and fuel gas cylinders in storage by a minimum 20-foot distance or a 5-foot fire-resistant barrier as required
• Keeping cylinders away from heat, sparks, slag, impact hazards, and electrical circuits
• Not using oil or grease on oxygen equipment — including regulators, valves, fittings, and hoses
• Opening cylinder valves slowly and using appropriate tools or controls
• Not using damaged regulators, hoses, or fittings
• Moving cylinders with approved carts or methods — not dragging, rolling on valves, or lifting by the cap
• Closing valves and relieving pressure when work is complete or equipment is unattended as required
• Returning empty cylinders to designated storage with valves closed and caps in place

Cylinders shall not be stored or used in a manner that creates a tipping, impact, or fire hazard.

**7.10 ELECTRICAL SAFETY INTERFACE FOR WELDING EQUIPMENT**

Electrical hazards associated with welding equipment shall be controlled in accordance with the Electrical Safety Program.

Controls shall include:
• Inspecting welding machine electrical components before use
• Protecting cords and leads from damage, moisture, and traffic
• Ensuring grounding and connection methods are appropriate for the installation
• Not using damaged insulation or exposed conductors
• Keeping electrode holders and terminals isolated when not in use
• Disconnecting power before maintenance or repair where required
• Using GFCI protection where applicable and required
• Avoiding standing in water or conductive conditions without proper control measures
• Not wrapping electrode leads or power cables around the body

Both welding current and utility electrical hazards shall be considered during work planning and equipment setup.

**7.11 FIRE WATCH REQUIREMENTS**

A fire watch shall be assigned whenever required by permit, area conditions, or task hazard assessment.

Fire watch shall be required when:
• Combustibles cannot be fully removed from the hot work area
• Sparks or heat may travel to adjacent areas
• Openings or penetrations may allow hidden ignition
• Work is performed at elevation or near multiple levels
• Work is performed near sensitive materials, equipment, or operations
• Site policy or permit conditions require it

Fire watch requirements:
• Remain in the area during hot work — attentive and focused on monitoring
• Monitor the immediate work area and exposed adjacent areas
• Have suitable extinguishing equipment readily available — minimum one 20-lb ABC fire extinguisher or equivalent
• Be trained in reporting, emergency action, and incidental fire response within their training level
• Continue post-work monitoring for the required duration — minimum [Post-Work Fire Watch Duration]

Fire watch personnel shall not be assigned additional duties that interfere with active monitoring during hot work or post-work fire watch periods.

**7.12 POST-WORK FIRE MONITORING**

The work area shall be monitored after hot work is completed for hidden ignition, smoldering materials, or delayed fire development.

Post-work monitoring duration shall be based on permit requirements, work conditions, and site policy, with a minimum of [Post-Work Fire Watch Duration] unless extended by permit or risk assessment.

Post-work checks shall include:
• Hidden spaces — wall cavities, pipe chases, insulation, ceiling spaces
• Adjacent rooms or levels — above, below, and beside the work location
• Penetrations and wall openings — conduit, piping, ductwork pathways
• Combustible residues — dust, debris, oil, coatings
• Heated metal transfer to nearby materials — thermal conduction ignition
• Insulation or debris ignition potential

The area shall not be released from fire watch or permit closure until post-work checks are satisfactorily completed and the area is confirmed safe.

**7.13 PPE FOR HOT WORK**

PPE shall be selected based on the task, process, materials, and exposure hazards.

Required PPE shall include as applicable:
• Welding helmet with appropriate lens shade for the specific process and arc type
• Safety glasses with side shields worn under the helmet or during adjacent tasks
• Face shield where applicable for grinding, cutting, or support tasks
• Flame-resistant clothing — long sleeves, high collar, no cuffs, no synthetic or melt-prone materials
• Welding gloves appropriate for the hot work process
• Leather aprons, jackets, sleeves, or leggings as appropriate for the task and exposure level
• Hearing protection where noise levels require it
• Respiratory protection where fume, gas, or particulate exposure requires it
• Protective footwear — leather work boots, no open-toe or synthetic uppers
• Head protection where overhead hazards or site requirements apply

PPE shall be maintained in usable condition and compatible with the hot work process and surrounding hazards. Synthetic or melt-prone clothing shall not be worn where spark or heat exposure creates additional burn hazard.

**7.14 CONFINED SPACE AND ENCLOSED AREA HOT WORK**

Hot work in confined spaces or enclosed areas shall have elevated fire, atmospheric, ventilation, and rescue hazards addressed.

Confined space hot work shall be coordinated with the Confined Space Entry Procedure and shall include:
• Entry permit — integrated with or cross-referenced to the hot work permit
• Atmospheric testing — pre-entry, continuous monitoring during work, and post-work as required
• Ventilation — continuous forced-air ventilation to control fumes, heat, and atmospheric hazards
• Isolation of lines, energy, and process contents
• Rescue planning — enhanced for combined hot work and confined space hazards
• Fire watch or attendant coordination — both functions may need to be covered
• Respiratory protection as required by hazard assessment
• Removal of all combustibles from the space
• Gas cylinder restrictions — cylinders and fuel gas systems shall remain outside the space

Hot work in confined spaces shall not proceed unless both hot work and confined space hazards are addressed and controlled.

**7.15 ELEVATED HOT WORK AND ADJACENT AREA CONTROL**

Hot work above floor level or near elevated edges shall be controlled for falling sparks, slag, hot metal, and dropped tool hazards.

Controls shall include:
• Barricading lower levels to prevent personnel entry into the drop zone
• Removing combustibles from areas below the hot work
• Using welding blankets, screens, and spark containment devices
• Assigning additional fire watch or area monitoring at lower levels where needed
• Protecting personnel working below or in adjacent spaces
• Controlling tool and material storage at elevation to prevent dropped objects
• Coordinating with fall protection requirements for the work platform

Elevated hot work shall not proceed when lower-level or adjacent area exposure cannot be adequately controlled.

**7.16 HOT WORK ON CONTAINERS, PIPING, AND PROCESS EQUIPMENT**

Hot work on tanks, vessels, drums, piping, or process equipment that has contained hazardous materials, flammables, combustibles, or residues shall be treated as high-risk work.

Controls shall include:
• Isolation and lockout/tagout of all energy and process sources
• Draining, cleaning, and decontaminating the equipment
• Venting or purging with inert gas where required
• Atmospheric testing to confirm safe conditions before and during work
• Residue verification — confirming removal of coatings, sludge, and hazardous residues
• Engineering review where required by the complexity or contamination history
• Non-routine work approval where applicable
• Confined space controls where the equipment configuration creates an entry hazard

No hot work shall be performed on containers or systems with unknown contents or uncontrolled residues. When in doubt, the work shall not proceed until the contents and conditions are verified safe.

**7.17 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION**

Coordination shall be required when contractors perform hot work onsite.

Coordination shall include:
• Scope of hot work — process, location, equipment involved
• Permit requirements — company hot work permit or acceptable contractor equivalent
• Area preparation and combustible control responsibilities
• Fire watch assignment — company, contractor, or shared responsibilities
• Cylinder and equipment safety requirements
• Ventilation and health exposure controls
• Restricted access and barricade requirements
• Emergency response expectations and communication
• Incident reporting requirements — immediate notification to the host employer

Contractors shall not begin hot work without required permit review, area preparation, and authorization. Contractor hot work shall be subject to the same controls and permit standards as company hot work.

**7.18 INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW**

All hot work-related fires, near misses, flashbacks, burns, equipment failures, permit deviations, atmospheric concerns, fume exposure issues, and unsafe conditions shall be reported immediately.

Investigations shall evaluate:
• Hazard identification adequacy
• Permit quality and compliance
• Area preparation effectiveness
• Combustible and flammable hazard control
• Fire watch performance
• Equipment inspection and setup
• Ventilation and exposure controls
• Training adequacy
• Supervision and planning
• Need for engineering, procedural, or training improvements

EHS and operational leadership shall periodically review the Welding, Cutting, Brazing, and Hot Work Permit Program for effectiveness and update requirements as needed based on incidents, audits, equipment changes, and lessons learned.
emergencyResponse: FIRE RESPONSE: If fire is detected during or after hot work, activate fire alarm or alert personnel, attempt to extinguish only incipient fires within training and capability, evacuate if fire is beyond incipient stage or if conditions deteriorate, close cylinder valves if safe to do so, contact emergency services, and notify supervision and EHS immediately.
BURN INJURIES: Remove the person from the heat source. Cool the burn with clean water where appropriate. Do not remove adhered clothing. Seek medical evaluation. Report the incident immediately.

FLASHBACK OR CYLINDER INCIDENT: Shut off fuel gas at the cylinder valve if safe. Do not attempt to repair equipment under pressure. Clear the area. Notify supervision and EHS. Do not re-light or reuse equipment until inspected and cleared by qualified personnel.

FUME OR GAS EXPOSURE: Move the affected person to fresh air immediately. Provide first aid within training. Seek medical evaluation. Ventilate the area. Do not re-enter until conditions are verified safe.

ELECTRICAL SHOCK FROM WELDING EQUIPMENT: Do not touch the person if they may still be in contact with the electrical source. Disconnect or de-energize the equipment if safe. Activate emergency response. Provide first aid including CPR only after the electrical hazard is controlled.

POST-INCIDENT ACTIONS: Secure the area. Preserve the scene where appropriate for investigation. Restrict access. Notify supervision, EHS, and emergency services as required. Complete incident reporting. Do not resume hot work until investigation and corrective actions are addressed.

☐ Required isolations and shutdowns completed
☐ Ventilation and exposure controls defined
☐ Required PPE available and suitable
☐ Fire watch requirement determined and assigned
☐ Post-work monitoring duration established
☐ Emergency and stop-work triggers understood

APPENDIX B — HOT WORK PERMIT AREA INSPECTION CHECKLIST

☐ Floors, walls, openings, drains, and penetrations evaluated and protected

☐ Confined space or enclosed area controls addressed where applicable

☐ Area acceptable for the approved work scope

APPENDIX C — FIRE WATCH CHECKLIST

☐ Fire extinguisher or suppression means immediately available
☐ Work area and adjacent exposure areas monitored continuously
☐ Sparks, slag, and hot metal travel observed and tracked
☐ Hidden or lower-level fire risks considered
☐ Work stops if ignition or unsafe conditions identified
☐ Post-work monitoring continues for the required duration
☐ Area not released until the fire watch obligation is complete and conditions are safe

APPENDIX D — HOT WORK EQUIPMENT AND CYLINDER CHECKLIST

☐ Welding machine, torch, and leads in good condition
☐ Regulators, gauges, hoses, and fittings undamaged and properly connected
☐ Flashback arrestors and check valves installed where required
☐ Cylinders secured upright and properly positioned
☐ Valve caps in place where required
☐ No oil or grease contamination present on oxygen equipment
☐ Electrical hazards related to the equipment controlled
☐ Required PPE and welding screens available
☐ Equipment appears safe to use

APPENDIX E — CONDITIONS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE STOP WORK

☐ Required permit is missing, expired, or invalid
☐ Combustibles or flammables cannot be adequately controlled
☐ Unsafe atmospheric conditions identified or suspected
☐ Fire watch absent or ineffective where required
☐ Required extinguishing equipment not available
☐ Ventilation inadequate for the exposure hazard
☐ Equipment, hoses, regulators, or cylinders damaged or unsafe
☐ Scope or location changes without permit review or approval
☐ A fire, flashback, near miss, or unsafe condition occurs and remains uncorrected
☐ A supervisor, permit issuer, EHS, or other responsible person stops the work
☐ Conditions change such that safe hot work cannot be assured

COMPLIANCE AND DEVIATIONS

Failure to comply with this procedure may result in disciplinary action, suspension of work, removal from the work area, contractor consequences where applicable, and increased risk of serious injury, fatality, fire, or explosion.

Deviations from this procedure require EHS and operational management review unless immediate action is required to protect life or health.

No deviation may remove required permit controls, fire prevention measures, atmospheric safeguards, fire watch requirements, PPE, or other critical safeguards without equivalent or greater protection.
