title: Compressed Gas Cylinders Safety Program
summary: A comprehensive Compressed Gas Cylinders Safety Program covering gas hazard identification, cylinder receipt and inspection, storage and segregation, securing, transport, regulator and fitting compatibility, connection and setup, valve operation, leak prevention and response, oxygen/fuel/oxidizer/flammable/toxic/asphyxiant controls, manifold systems, empty cylinder management, contractor coordination, incident reporting, and program review.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, receiving, storing, handling, transporting, using, connecting, disconnecting, and managing compressed gas cylinders and associated gas systems in workplace operations at [Company Name].

The purpose is to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, asphyxiation, toxic exposure, fire, explosion, pressure release, hose whip, struck-by incidents, cylinder falls, regulator failure, equipment damage, property damage, and operational disruption resulting from improper cylinder storage, incompatible gas mixing, inadequate segregation, poor valve protection, improper transport, defective regulators or fittings, uncontrolled release of gas, and unsafe work practices around pressurized gas systems.

This procedure supports compliance with applicable OSHA standards, CGA guidance, NFPA codes, manufacturer requirements, and [Company Name] policies. It applies to all compressed gas cylinder operations performed on company-controlled sites and shall be used by all personnel who handle, transport, connect, disconnect, store, inspect, or use compressed gas cylinders.

COMPLIANCE DISCLAIMER: This SOP template is provided as operational guidance and does not replace applicable federal, state, or local regulatory requirements, consensus standards, or specific internal company procedures. [Company Name] is responsible for ensuring compliance with all applicable regulations and adapting this template to site-specific conditions.
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, laboratories, production areas, fabrication areas, maintenance activities, test areas, warehouses, utilities, field locations, assembly areas, construction support areas, and non-routine operations where compressed gas cylinders are received, stored, moved, connected, used, or managed at [Site Name].

It applies to:
• Employees who handle, transport, connect, disconnect, store, inspect, or use compressed gas cylinders
• Supervisors and managers responsible for areas where compressed gas cylinders are present
• EHS personnel
• Engineers, technicians, mechanics, operators, welders, maintenance personnel, inspectors, laboratory personnel, and support personnel
• Contractors performing work involving compressed gas cylinders onsite

It applies to compressed gas cylinders and associated equipment including, but not limited to:
• Inert gas cylinders, oxidizer cylinders, flammable gas cylinders, toxic gas cylinders, corrosive gas cylinders
• Cryogenic gas cylinders where cylinder handling interfaces exist
• Calibration gas cylinders, specialty gas cylinders, lecture bottles where applicable
• Cylinder carts, regulators, manifolds, pigtails, hoses and tubing
• Flashback arrestors, check valves, valve caps, cylinder stands and racks, gas cabinets where applicable

It applies to gases including, but not limited to:
• Nitrogen, argon, helium, oxygen, hydrogen, acetylene, carbon dioxide, compressed air
• Methane, propane, specialty gas mixtures, calibration gases
• Toxic or corrosive gases where applicable
• Other compressed gases used in operations, laboratories, utilities, welding, test activities, or maintenance work

It applies to activities including, but not limited to:
• Receipt and inspection, storage, cylinder change-out, transport and movement
• Setup and connection, pressure regulation, gas distribution and manifold use
• Welding and cutting support, laboratory use, process gas supply
• Purging and venting, leak checking, return of empty cylinders
• Temporary and non-routine gas cylinder use

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with other required programs such as Welding/Cutting/Brazing and Hot Work Permit, Asphyxiants, Pressurized Systems, Hazard Communication, Respiratory Protection, Health Exposure to Hazardous Materials, Contractor Safety Management, Permit-to-Work, Emergency Response, and applicable facility operations controls where required.

definitions.definition: A gas that can displace oxygen in the breathing zone or impair the body's ability to transport or use oxygen, creating risk of oxygen deficiency, incapacitation, or death.

A gas or mixture of gases having an absolute pressure exceeding 40 psi at 70°F, or an absolute pressure exceeding 104 psi at 130°F, or a liquid having a vapor pressure exceeding 40 psi absolute at 100°F, as determined by ASTM D323.
A removable protective device placed over the cylinder valve when the cylinder is not in use or connected, designed to protect the valve from impact damage during storage and transport.
The valve assembly attached to the cylinder that controls the release of gas and provides the connection point for regulators and other downstream equipment.
A cylinder that has been depleted to the point where it no longer delivers useful gas at the required pressure, but which may still contain residual pressure and product.
A safety device installed in oxygen-fuel gas systems designed to prevent the propagation of a flame or detonation wave back through the gas supply system toward the cylinder.
A small-volume, high-pressure gas cylinder typically used in laboratory, calibration, or analytical applications, often containing specialty, toxic, or corrosive gases.
A system of piping, valves, and connections designed to connect multiple cylinders to a common supply header for higher-volume or continuous gas delivery.
A gas that supports or accelerates combustion of other materials by providing oxygen or an oxidizing environment, increasing fire and explosion risk when combined with fuel or combustible materials.
A pressure-reducing device connected to a cylinder valve that reduces high-pressure cylinder gas to a controlled, usable downstream pressure.
A cylinder that has been restrained against tipping, falling, or uncontrolled movement by means of chains, straps, racks, stands, brackets, cabinets, or other approved restraint methods.
A gas that can cause adverse health effects, injury, illness, or death through inhalation, skin contact, or absorption at relatively low concentrations.
The orientation and location in which a cylinder is positioned for active gas delivery, typically upright and secured, with the regulator connected and downstream equipment ready.
A fixed or removable device designed to protect the cylinder valve from mechanical damage, including valve protection caps, collars, guards, and recessed valve designs.
The uncontrolled, violent movement of a hose, pigtail, or flexible connection caused by sudden pressure release, disconnection, or failure, capable of striking and injuring personnel.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Develop and maintain this program. Establish gas classification, storage, segregation, and labeling requirements. Conduct or support hazard assessments for compressed gas operations. Verify training, competency, and authorization programs. Provide technical guidance on gas hazards, ventilation, monitoring, PPE, and emergency response. Support incident investigation and corrective action. Audit cylinder storage, handling, and contractor compliance. Conduct periodic program reviews and updates.

Ensure personnel under their direction understand and comply with this procedure. Verify that gas hazards are assessed, cylinders are properly stored and secured, compatible equipment is used, and controls are in place before work begins. Stop work when conditions are unsafe. Ensure only trained personnel handle or use cylinders. Report incidents, leaks, near misses, and equipment failures. Support training and program improvements.

Follow all requirements of this procedure. Inspect cylinders and equipment before use. Use only compatible regulators, fittings, and accessories. Secure cylinders during storage, transport, and use. Transport cylinders using approved methods. Report leaks, damaged cylinders, missing labels, equipment failures, and unsafe conditions immediately. Do not use unlabeled, damaged, leaking, or incompatible cylinders. Do not tamper with valves, safety devices, or labels.

Inspect incoming cylinders for damage, labeling, and condition. Store and segregate cylinders according to this procedure and site requirements. Maintain orderly storage areas. Track cylinder inventory where required. Ensure valve caps are in place on stored cylinders. Segregate empty from full cylinders. Coordinate cylinder returns to suppliers.

Maintain gas distribution systems, manifolds, regulators, and related infrastructure. Support leak detection, ventilation, and gas monitoring systems. Perform or coordinate equipment repairs using approved parts and methods. Ensure gas system modifications follow change management requirements. Support hazard assessments and incident investigations.

Design and specify gas systems, storage areas, manifolds, and ventilation in compliance with codes and this procedure. Evaluate gas hazards during system design, modification, and change management. Specify compatible materials, regulators, fittings, and safety devices. Support hazard assessments and engineering reviews for higher-risk gas operations.

Comply with this procedure and all applicable site requirements for compressed gas cylinders. Do not bring cylinders onsite without complying with site storage, labeling, and approval requirements. Use only compatible, inspected equipment. Report leaks, incidents, near misses, and unsafe conditions to the host employer. Participate in coordination meetings and pre-job briefings.

training: Gas hazard recognition — pressure, asphyxiation, flammability, oxidation, toxicity, corrosivity, and cryogenic hazards
Cylinder identification, labeling, and color coding where applicable
Storage and segregation requirements — incompatible gas separation, quantity limits, ventilation
Securing requirements — approved methods, prohibition on unsupported freestanding storage
Transport requirements — approved carts, valve cap protection, safe handling practices
Regulator, hose, and fitting compatibility — gas-specific equipment, prohibition on improvised fittings
Cylinder connection, valve operation, and pressure control procedures
Leak prevention, detection, and response actions
Oxygen, flammable gas, oxidizer, and asphyxiant-specific precautions
Manifold and distribution system controls where applicable
Empty cylinder and return procedures
Emergency response expectations — leak, fire, cylinder fall, pressure release
Incident and near-miss reporting requirements
Stop-work authority and obligation
ppe: Safety glasses or chemical splash goggles based on the gas hazard profile
Face shield where splash, spray, or pressure release hazards exist
Work gloves suitable for cylinder handling — leather or cut-resistant where appropriate
Cryogenic gloves where contact with cryogenic liquids or extremely cold surfaces is possible
Steel-toe safety footwear — required in cylinder handling and storage areas
Hard hat in designated areas or where overhead hazards exist
Respiratory protection where required by gas hazard assessment — managed under the Respiratory Protection Program
Chemical-resistant or gas-specific PPE where toxic or corrosive gases are handled
Hearing protection where high-noise gas discharge or venting operations occur
NOTE: PPE does not replace proper cylinder storage, securement, compatibility, ventilation, or leak prevention controls.
hazards: High-pressure gas release — sudden, violent discharge capable of propelling cylinders, damaging equipment, and injuring personnel
Cylinder falls and tip-over — struck-by injuries from unsecured or improperly handled cylinders
Valve damage — uncontrolled gas release if the valve is sheared or broken during a fall or impact
Asphyxiation — oxygen displacement in enclosed or poorly ventilated areas from inert gas release
Fire and explosion — flammable gas ignition, oxygen-enriched atmosphere combustion, acetylene decomposition
Toxic exposure — inhalation of toxic or corrosive gases causing acute illness, chemical burns, or death
Hose whip — violent hose movement from sudden disconnection or hose failure under pressure
Regulator failure — overpressure, gauge failure, or diaphragm rupture
Incompatible gas mixing — dangerous reactions from cross-connection or improper manifold use
Oil/grease contamination of oxygen systems — spontaneous ignition and fire
Cryogenic hazards — cold burns, rapid gas expansion, and pressure buildup from liquid-to-gas conversion
Improper transport — cylinder damage, valve breakage, and loss of control during movement
Backfeed and cross-contamination in manifolded systems
Secondary hazards — ergonomic strain from cylinder handling, trip hazards from hoses, and ignition from nearby hot work
procedure:
**1. PURPOSE**

This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, receiving, storing, handling, transporting, using, connecting, disconnecting, and managing compressed gas cylinders and associated gas systems in workplace operations at [Company Name].

The purpose is to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, asphyxiation, toxic exposure, fire, explosion, pressure release, hose whip, struck-by incidents, cylinder falls, regulator failure, equipment damage, property damage, and operational disruption resulting from improper cylinder storage, incompatible gas mixing, inadequate segregation, poor valve protection, improper transport, defective regulators or fittings, uncontrolled release of gas, and unsafe work practices around pressurized gas systems.

COMPLIANCE DISCLAIMER: This SOP template is provided as operational guidance and does not replace applicable federal, state, or local regulatory requirements, consensus standards, or specific internal company procedures.

**2. SCOPE**

This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, laboratories, production areas, fabrication areas, maintenance activities, test areas, warehouses, utilities, field locations, assembly areas, construction support areas, and non-routine operations where compressed gas cylinders are received, stored, moved, connected, used, or managed at [Site Name].

Personnel covered include employees who handle, transport, connect, disconnect, store, inspect, or use compressed gas cylinders, supervisors and managers, EHS personnel, engineers, technicians, mechanics, operators, welders, maintenance personnel, laboratory personnel, and contractors.

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with Welding/Cutting/Brazing and Hot Work Permit, Asphyxiants, Pressurized Systems, Hazard Communication, Respiratory Protection, Health Exposure to Hazardous Materials, Contractor Safety Management, Permit-to-Work, and Emergency Response programs where applicable.

**3. REFERENCES**

• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.101 — Compressed Gases (General Requirements)
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.253 — Oxygen-Fuel Gas Welding and Cutting where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1200 — Hazard Communication
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134 — Respiratory Protection where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1450 — Occupational Exposure to Hazardous Chemicals in Laboratories where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.146 — Permit-Required Confined Spaces where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910 Subpart H — Hazardous Materials where applicable
• CGA guidance documents where applicable
• NFPA codes and fire code requirements applicable to compressed gas storage and use
• Manufacturer instructions for cylinders, regulators, manifolds, hoses, carts, gas cabinets, and related equipment
• Safety Data Sheets for gases used onsite
• [Company Name] Hazard Communication Program
• [Company Name] Asphyxiants Safety Procedure
• [Company Name] Pressurized Systems Safety Procedure
• [Company Name] Respiratory Protection Program
• [Company Name] Welding/Cutting/Brazing and Hot Work Permit Procedure
• [Company Name] Incident Reporting and Investigation Procedure
• [Company Name] Emergency Response Plan
• Site-specific gas storage, gas cabinet, and laboratory requirements where applicable
• [Applicable State / Local Requirements]
• [Related Company Procedures]

**4. DEFINITIONS**

• Asphyxiant — A gas that can displace oxygen or impair the body's ability to use oxygen, creating risk of incapacitation or death.
• Compressed Gas — A gas having absolute pressure exceeding 40 psi at 70°F or exceeding 104 psi at 130°F.
• Cylinder Cap / Valve Protection Cap — A removable device placed over the valve to protect it from impact during storage and transport.
• Cylinder Valve — The valve assembly controlling gas release and providing the regulator connection point.
• Empty Cylinder — A cylinder depleted of useful gas but potentially containing residual pressure.
• Flashback Arrestor — A safety device preventing flame propagation back through the gas supply system.
• Lecture Bottle — A small high-pressure cylinder for laboratory, calibration, or analytical use.
• Manifold — A system connecting multiple cylinders to a common supply header.
• Oxidizer — A gas that supports combustion by providing oxygen or an oxidizing environment.
• Regulator — A pressure-reducing device that delivers controlled downstream pressure.
• Secured Cylinder — A cylinder restrained against tipping or falling by approved means.
• Toxic Gas — A gas causing adverse health effects at relatively low concentrations.
• Use Position — The orientation and location for active gas delivery, typically upright and secured.
• Valve Protection Device — A device protecting the cylinder valve from mechanical damage.
• Whip Hazard — Uncontrolled violent movement of a hose or connection caused by sudden pressure release or failure.

**5. POLICY**

[Company Name] will identify and control hazards associated with compressed gas cylinders through proper gas classification, storage and segregation, securing, transport, equipment inspection, connection control, leak prevention, training, supervision, and periodic review.

No compressed gas cylinder shall be stored, moved, connected, or used unless:
• The gas hazard is known and communicated
• The cylinder is in acceptable condition
• The cylinder is properly labeled
• The cylinder is secured where required
• Compatible regulators, fittings, and accessories are used
• Storage, segregation, and ventilation requirements are met
• Personnel are trained and authorized as appropriate

The company will prioritize prevention of cylinder falls, valve damage, incompatibility, overpressure, uncontrolled release, oxygen displacement, and ignition hazards before relying solely on PPE or emergency response.

Employees shall not use unlabeled, damaged, leaking, unsecured, or incompatible cylinders or accessories.

Employees shall not tamper with cylinder valves, safety devices, pressure relief devices, labels, or protective caps, or use adapters, fittings, or repair methods not approved for the service.

**6. RESPONSIBILITIES**

EHS / Program Administrator: Develop and maintain this program. Establish gas classification, storage, segregation, and labeling requirements. Conduct or support hazard assessments. Verify training and authorization programs. Provide technical guidance on gas hazards, ventilation, monitoring, PPE, and emergency response. Support incident investigation. Audit cylinder storage and contractor compliance. Conduct periodic program reviews.

Managers and Supervisors: Ensure compliance with this procedure. Verify hazard assessments, proper storage, compatible equipment, and controls before work begins. Stop work when unsafe. Report incidents and near misses.

Employees / Cylinder Users: Follow all requirements. Inspect cylinders and equipment before use. Use only compatible equipment. Secure cylinders during storage, transport, and use. Report leaks, damage, and unsafe conditions immediately. Do not tamper with valves or safety devices.

Receiving / Tool Room / Storage Custodians: Inspect incoming cylinders. Store and segregate per requirements. Maintain orderly storage areas. Track inventory where required. Coordinate returns.

Maintenance / Facilities / Technical Support: Maintain gas systems, manifolds, and infrastructure. Support leak detection and ventilation systems. Perform repairs using approved parts.

Engineering / System Owners: Design and specify gas systems in compliance with codes. Evaluate hazards during design and modification. Specify compatible materials and safety devices.

Contractors: Comply with all site requirements. Do not bring cylinders onsite without approval. Use inspected, compatible equipment. Report incidents and unsafe conditions.

**7.1 COMPRESSED GAS HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND ASSESSMENT**

All compressed gas operations shall be evaluated to identify hazards and determine required controls before cylinders are stored, moved, or used.

Hazard assessments shall consider:
• Gas identity and classification — inert, oxidizer, flammable, toxic, corrosive, cryogenic
• Pressure and cylinder size
• Flammability, oxidizing properties, toxicity, corrosivity
• Asphyxiation hazard — oxygen displacement potential in enclosed areas
• Cryogenic or temperature-related effects where applicable
• Ventilation and room volume
• Storage and segregation needs — [Approved Cylinder Storage Locations]
• Ignition source presence
• Required PPE
• Need for gas detection, gas cabinets, or special ventilation
• Cylinder stability and transport path
• Regulator and material compatibility
• Use in enclosed or confined areas
• Potential for manifold or system backfeed
• Interaction with nearby operations or incompatible materials

If hazards cannot be adequately understood or controlled, the cylinder shall not be placed into service until additional review and controls are established.

**7.2 HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS FOR COMPRESSED GAS CYLINDER SAFETY**

Compressed gas cylinder hazards shall be controlled using the following preferred order:

1. Eliminate unnecessary cylinder use or reduce the quantity stored or used
2. Substitute a lower-risk gas or supply method where feasible — piped supply, lower-pressure alternatives
3. Use engineering controls such as secure storage racks, gas cabinets, leak detection, ventilation, flashback protection, and manifold design controls
4. Use administrative controls such as segregation, labeling, training, restricted access, and storage limits as supplemental controls
5. Use PPE as a supporting measure, not as a substitute for proper storage, securement, compatibility, and system control

Whenever feasible, gas systems shall be arranged to reduce the chance of cylinder damage, leak, tipping, ignition, or atmospheric hazard.

**7.3 CYLINDER RECEIPT AND INITIAL INSPECTION**

All cylinders shall be inspected upon receipt or initial acceptance into the work area.

Inspection shall include:
• Product label and gas identity — legible and matching the intended gas
• Legibility of markings — cylinder type, test date, owner identification
• Valve protection cap or collar condition — present, undamaged, properly seated
• Evidence of leaks, damage, corrosion, gouging, fire exposure, or deformation
• Physical stability and base/ring condition
• Valve condition — no visible damage, contamination, or obstruction
• Presence of incompatible or improvised fittings
• Cylinder status — full, partial, empty, damaged, or return

Unlabeled, leaking, damaged, or otherwise suspect cylinders shall be isolated, not used, and reported to supervision for controlled disposition.

**7.4 STORAGE AND SEGREGATION**

Cylinders shall be stored in designated areas suitable for the gas hazards present at [Approved Cylinder Storage Locations].

Storage requirements include:
• Upright storage unless specifically designed and approved otherwise
• Secured to prevent tipping or falling — chains, straps, racks, stands, or approved enclosures
• Valve protection caps in place when cylinders are not in use or connected
• Separation of incompatible gases — oxidizers from flammables by minimum [Gas Segregation Distances / Barrier Requirements] or approved fire-resistant barrier
• Protection from excessive heat, ignition sources, direct flame, electrical circuits, corrosive exposure, impact, and vehicle traffic
• Orderly storage for inspection and access
• Segregation of empty cylinders from full cylinders where required by site practice
• Hazard signage at storage locations
• Quantity limits where required by fire code, permit, or site policy

Cylinders shall not be stored in exits, egress routes, stairways, uncontrolled confined areas, or other locations where they create additional hazard.

**7.5 SECURING CYLINDERS**

Cylinders shall be secured at all times during storage and use unless actively moved by approved means.

Securing requirements:
• Approved methods — chain, strap, rack, stand, bracket, or approved cabinet/enclosure
• Attached to a stable structure or approved support
• Restraints positioned at a suitable height and configuration for the cylinder size
• Prohibition on unsupported freestanding storage
• Secure staging on cylinder carts during transport or temporary use where appropriate

Unsecured cylinders shall not be left unattended in a standing position.

**7.6 CYLINDER TRANSPORT AND MOVEMENT**

Cylinders shall be moved using approved methods that protect the cylinder body and valve.

Transport requirements:
• Use approved cylinder carts, cradles, or handling devices
• Keep valve protection caps in place during transport unless permanently protected and actively connected in a controlled move
• Move cylinders upright unless approved equipment and cylinder design allow otherwise
• Do not drag, roll horizontally, drop, or slide cylinders in an uncontrolled manner
• Do not lift cylinders by the cap, valve, regulator, or protective collar unless specifically designed for that method
• Secure cylinders during transport — strap, chain, or cradle restraint
• Control travel paths to avoid impact, stairs, sharp edges, or unstable surfaces

Manual handling shall be minimized for large or heavy cylinders. Use mechanical aids where available.

**7.7 REGULATOR, HOSE, AND FITTING COMPATIBILITY**

Only regulators, hoses, fittings, and accessories specifically designed and approved for the gas service, pressure, and cylinder connection shall be used.

Compatibility requirements:
• Verify regulator compatibility with the gas type and cylinder connection — CGA connection standards
• Prohibit adapters or improvised fittings unless specifically approved for the service — [Specialty Gas Approval Requirements]
• Inspect regulators, gauges, hoses, and connectors before each use
• Use flashback arrestors and check valves where required for oxygen-fuel systems
• Protect hoses and regulators from impact, heat, sparks, traffic, and contamination
• Do not interchange regulators between incompatible gases
• Remove damaged or leaking regulators, hoses, or fittings from service immediately

Improper regulator or fitting substitution is prohibited.

**7.8 CYLINDER CONNECTION AND SETUP**

The user shall verify the cylinder, regulator, and application are correct before connection.

Connection and setup requirements:
• Confirm gas identity and intended service
• Secure the cylinder before connection
• Remove the cap only when the cylinder is secured and ready for controlled use
• Inspect the valve outlet for obvious contamination or damage
• Install the correct regulator and tighten connections properly using correct tools where applicable
• Position hoses and lines to avoid damage, kinks, and line-of-fire exposure
• Verify downstream equipment suitability for the gas and pressure
• Control ignition sources for flammable gases
• Ensure oxygen systems are free of oil, grease, and incompatible contamination

Connections shall be leak checked as required before normal use.

**7.9 CYLINDER VALVE OPERATION AND PRESSURE CONTROL**

Cylinder valves shall be opened, adjusted, and closed in a controlled manner.

Valve operation requirements:
• Open valves slowly
• Use proper tools only when required and approved — do not use excessive force
• Stand out of the line of fire of the regulator outlet and pressure gauges when opening the valve
• Keep the valve key or operating means available where appropriate
• Do not leave cylinders open and unattended when site rules or hazard conditions require closure
• Close the cylinder valve when work is complete, the cylinder is empty, or equipment is unattended as required
• Relieve pressure from the regulator and downstream system when work is complete where appropriate

Cylinders shall not be used without proper pressure control devices when the service requires regulation.

**7.10 LEAK PREVENTION AND LEAK RESPONSE**

Compressed gas systems shall be checked and maintained to prevent leaks.

Leak prevention measures:
• Inspect valves, regulators, hoses, fittings, and manifolds before use
• Use compatible seals and connections
• Leak check after setup where required — approved leak detection solution or instrument
• Avoid mechanical strain on connections
• Protect equipment from vibration, heat, and damage
• Maintain adequate ventilation in cylinder use areas

If a leak is suspected or identified:
• Stop work if safe to do so
• Close the valve if safe and accessible
• Isolate the area as needed based on the gas hazard
• Remove ignition sources for flammable gas leaks
• Notify supervision and EHS immediately
• Escalate emergency response when the release exceeds onsite capability or creates life safety risk — [Emergency Contact / Escalation Process]

Employees shall not attempt to repair cylinder valves or pressure relief devices.

**7.11 OXYGEN, FUEL GAS, OXIDIZER, AND FLAMMABLE GAS CONTROLS**

Additional controls are required due to ignition, fire, and explosion hazards associated with oxidizers and flammable gases.

Controls shall include:
• Keep oxidizers away from oil, grease, fuel, combustibles, and flammable gases as required
• Keep flammable gas cylinders away from ignition sources and hot work exposure unless actively used in a controlled system
• Use approved flashback protection for oxygen-fuel torch systems where required
• Do not use oxygen for ventilation, cooling, cleaning, or as a substitute for compressed air
• Protect fuel gas cylinders and hoses from hot slag, sparks, and high heat
• Maintain proper storage segregation between oxidizers and flammables — [Gas Segregation Distances / Barrier Requirements]

Unsafe mixing, cross-connection, or misuse of oxidizers and flammable gases is prohibited.

**7.12 ASPHYXIANT, TOXIC, AND CORROSIVE GAS CONTROLS**

Additional controls are required for gases that create oxygen deficiency, toxic inhalation hazard, or corrosive exposure.

Controls shall include as applicable:
• Enhanced ventilation — mechanical or forced-air in enclosed areas
• Gas detection or monitoring — fixed or portable instruments
• Restricted storage quantities based on hazard and ventilation
• Controlled access areas — signage, barriers, restricted entry
• Gas cabinets or exhausted enclosures for toxic or corrosive gases
• Respiratory protection where specifically required and authorized
• Special change-out procedures for toxic or corrosive gas cylinders
• Emergency planning and escalation controls
• Increased training for affected personnel

Use of asphyxiants, toxic gases, or corrosive gases in enclosed or poorly ventilated areas shall receive additional review before work begins.

**7.13 MANIFOLDS, DISTRIBUTION SYSTEMS, AND MULTIPLE CYLINDER USE**

Additional controls are required for manifolded or distributed gas systems due to higher volume, backfeed, misconnection, and isolation complexity.

Manifold controls shall include:
• Clearly identifying gas service and direction of flow
• Verifying manifold and pigtail compatibility with the gas
• Securing all connected cylinders appropriately
• Preventing cross-connection between incompatible gases
• Using check valves, flashback arrestors, or isolation controls where required
• Inspecting manifold assemblies and supports regularly
• Clearly defining isolation points and shutdown methods
• Coordinating cylinder change-out under controlled procedures

Higher-risk manifold systems may require engineering review, SOP support, or specialized controls.

**7.14 EMPTY CYLINDERS, RETURN, AND OUT-OF-SERVICE CYLINDERS**

Empty cylinders shall be treated as if they still contain residual pressure unless verified otherwise by approved means.

Empty cylinder requirements:
• Close the valve when the cylinder is empty or removed from service
• Leave residual pressure as required by supplier or gas service practice
• Replace valve protection caps when the cylinder is disconnected and moved to storage or return
• Mark or segregate empties as required by site practice — label "EMPTY" or "MT"
• Do not abandon cylinders in work areas
• Return supplier-owned cylinders promptly through approved channels
• Isolate damaged or questionable cylinders for controlled disposition

Cylinders shall not be discarded, altered, or repurposed without authorized process and regulatory review.

**7.15 HOUSEKEEPING AND AREA CONTROL**

Cylinder storage and use areas shall be maintained in a condition that supports safe access and hazard control.

Housekeeping requirements:
• Maintain clear aisles and access to cylinders and shutoff points
• Prevent accumulation of combustible waste, debris, and trip hazards
• Keep cylinders away from incompatible materials and uncontrolled traffic
• Protect hoses and regulators from mechanical damage
• Use signage, barriers, or markings to identify gas hazards or restricted areas
• Maintain visibility of labels and valve access

Poor housekeeping that obscures leaks, damages cylinders, or impairs emergency access is prohibited.

**7.16 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION**

Coordination shall be required when contractors bring, store, or use compressed gas cylinders onsite.

Coordination shall include:
• Gas types and quantities
• Storage location and segregation requirements
• Securement requirements
• Regulator and hose compatibility
• Hot work interfaces where applicable
• Ventilation and toxic/asphyxiant controls
• Emergency response expectations
• Leak reporting and stop-work requirements

Contractors shall not bring cylinders onsite without complying with site storage, labeling, and approval requirements.

**7.17 INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW**

All compressed gas cylinder incidents shall be reported immediately, including:
• Cylinder falls, tip-over, or valve damage
• Leaks — suspected or confirmed
• Regulator failure, hose whip, or fitting failure
• Fire involving gas equipment
• Near misses and unsafe conditions
• Improper storage, misconnection, or incompatible use

Investigations shall evaluate:
• Gas identification and communication
• Storage and segregation adequacy
• Securement and transport practices
• Regulator and connection compatibility
• Leak prevention measures
• Training adequacy
• Supervision and planning
• Equipment condition and maintenance
• Environmental and ventilation conditions
• Need for engineering, procedural, or training improvements

EHS and operational leadership shall periodically review the Compressed Gas Cylinders Safety Program for effectiveness and update requirements as needed based on incidents, audits, and lessons learned.
emergencyResponse: LEAKING CYLINDER — NON-FLAMMABLE / NON-TOXIC: If safe, close the valve. Ventilate the area. Evacuate if the leak cannot be stopped and the gas is an asphyxiant in an enclosed area. Notify supervision and EHS. Do not re-enter until conditions are verified safe.

LEAKING CYLINDER — FLAMMABLE GAS: Remove ignition sources immediately. If safe, close the valve. Evacuate the area. Do not attempt to repair. Notify supervision, EHS, and emergency services. Restrict access. Ventilate when safe to do so.

LEAKING CYLINDER — TOXIC OR CORROSIVE GAS: Evacuate immediately. Do not attempt to close the valve unless properly trained and equipped. Activate emergency response. Restrict access. Notify EHS, supervision, and emergency services. Use respiratory protection only if authorized, trained, and properly equipped.

CYLINDER FALL OR VALVE DAMAGE: Clear the area. Do not attempt to handle a damaged cylinder with a broken or sheared valve. Contact EHS and supervision. If gas is releasing, treat as a leak response based on the gas type.

FIRE INVOLVING CYLINDERS: Activate fire alarm and evacuate. If incipient and safe, attempt suppression. Do not attempt to move burning or heat-exposed cylinders. Notify emergency services. Cool adjacent cylinders with water if safe and trained to do so. Maintain safe distance from heat-exposed cylinders due to explosion risk.

POST-INCIDENT ACTIONS: Secure the area. Preserve the scene where appropriate. Restrict access. Notify supervision, EHS, and emergency services. Complete incident reporting. Do not resume operations until investigation and corrective actions are addressed.

APPENDIX A — CYLINDER USE PRE-PLANNING CHECKLIST

☐ Gas identity and hazard known
☐ Correct cylinder selected for the application
☐ Storage and use location appropriate — [Approved Cylinder Storage Locations]
☐ Cylinder secured or will be secured before use
☐ Regulator and fittings compatible with the gas and service
☐ Ventilation and area controls adequate
☐ Ignition source controls in place where required
☐ Required PPE available
☐ Emergency and stop-work triggers understood
☐ Nearby personnel and simultaneous operations addressed

APPENDIX B — CYLINDER RECEIPT AND INSPECTION CHECKLIST

☐ Label present and legible — gas identity confirmed
☐ Valve cap or protection device present where required
☐ Cylinder body shows no obvious damage, severe corrosion, or burn marks
☐ Valve and outlet appear intact — no contamination or obstruction
☐ No leak apparent — no hissing, odor, or visible discharge
☐ Status known — full, partial, empty, damaged, or return
☐ Cylinder appears safe to store or place into service

APPENDIX C — STORAGE AND SEGREGATION CHECKLIST

☐ Cylinders upright and secured
☐ Full and empty cylinders segregated where required
☐ Incompatible gases separated appropriately — [Gas Segregation Distances / Barrier Requirements]
☐ Storage area orderly and ventilated as required
☐ Ignition sources and combustibles controlled
☐ Valve caps installed on stored, disconnected cylinders where required
☐ Cylinders protected from traffic, impact, heat, and weather
☐ Labels remain visible and legible
☐ No leaking or damaged cylinders present

APPENDIX D — REGULATOR AND CONNECTION CHECKLIST

☐ Correct regulator selected for the gas and service
☐ Hose and fittings in good condition — no damage, cracks, or contamination
☐ Flashback arrestors and check valves installed where required
☐ Connections tight and correct — proper CGA fitting
☐ Valve opened slowly — stood out of line of fire
☐ Leak check completed where required
☐ Hose routing avoids damage, heat, sharp edges, and trip hazards
☐ Cylinder and regulator setup appears safe for use

APPENDIX E — CONDITIONS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE STOP WORK

☐ Gas identity is uncertain or the cylinder is unlabeled
☐ A cylinder is leaking, damaged, or unstable
☐ Required regulator or fitting compatibility cannot be confirmed
☐ A cylinder is unsecured in a condition creating fall or impact hazard
☐ Ventilation or required gas controls are inadequate
☐ A flammable, toxic, or asphyxiant release is suspected or identified and conditions remain uncontrolled
☐ A fire, regulator failure, hose whip event, or unsafe condition occurs and remains uncorrected
☐ A supervisor, EHS, or other responsible person stops the work
☐ Conditions change such that safe cylinder storage or use cannot be assured

COMPLIANCE AND DEVIATIONS

Failure to comply with this procedure may result in disciplinary action, suspension of work, removal from the work area, contractor consequences where applicable, and increased risk of serious injury, fatality, fire, or explosion.

Deviations from this procedure require EHS and operational management review unless immediate action is required to protect life or health.

No deviation may remove required labeling, storage segregation, securement, compatibility controls, leak prevention measures, or other critical safeguards without equivalent or greater protection.
