title: Cryogenic Safety Program
summary: A comprehensive Cryogenic Safety Program covering cryogenic hazard identification, storage and location controls, securing dewars, transfer operations, pressure control and venting, oxygen deficiency and enrichment controls, PPE, materials compatibility, hose and valve safety, manual handling, laboratory and process use, spill and release response, maintenance and non-routine work, contractor coordination, incident reporting, and program review.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, evaluating, controlling, and safely managing hazards associated with cryogenic liquids, cryogenic gases, cryogenic systems, and related equipment in workplace operations at [Company Name].

The purpose is to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, cold burns, frostbite, tissue damage, eye injury, asphyxiation, oxygen-enriched atmosphere hazards, pressure-related incidents, vessel rupture, hose failure, embrittlement-related equipment failure, slips and falls, fire or combustion hazards associated with oxidizing cryogens, equipment damage, property damage, and operational disruption resulting from improper handling, storage, transfer, venting, pressure buildup, incompatible materials, inadequate PPE, inadequate ventilation, and unsafe work practices involving cryogenic materials.

This procedure supports compliance with applicable OSHA standards, CGA guidance, NFPA codes, manufacturer requirements, and [Company Name] policies. It applies to all cryogenic operations performed on company-controlled sites and shall be used by all personnel who handle, transfer, inspect, operate, maintain, or supervise work involving cryogenic materials or systems.

COMPLIANCE DISCLAIMER: This SOP template is provided as operational guidance and does not replace applicable federal, state, or local regulatory requirements, consensus standards, or specific internal company procedures. [Company Name] is responsible for ensuring compliance with all applicable regulations and adapting this template to site-specific conditions.
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, laboratories, production areas, fabrication areas, maintenance activities, test areas, warehouses, utilities, field locations, assembly areas, construction support areas, and non-routine operations where cryogenic liquids, cryogenic gases, or cryogenic systems are stored, transferred, used, transported, or managed at [Site Name].

It applies to:
• Employees who handle, transfer, inspect, operate, maintain, or supervise work involving cryogenic materials or systems
• Supervisors and managers responsible for areas where cryogenic hazards are present
• EHS personnel
• Engineers, technicians, mechanics, operators, laboratory personnel, maintenance personnel, inspectors, and support personnel
• Contractors performing work involving cryogenic liquids, cryogenic systems, or cryogenic equipment onsite

It applies to cryogenic materials and systems including, but not limited to:
• Liquid nitrogen, liquid oxygen, liquid argon, liquid helium, liquid hydrogen where applicable
• Other cryogenic fluids used onsite
• Dewars, cryogenic storage vessels, phase separators, vacuum-jacketed piping
• Transfer hoses, cryogenic valves and fittings, vented and pressure-building systems
• Portable cryogenic containers, cryogenic freezers, cold traps, cryogenic process equipment
• Gas vent systems, oxygen monitoring systems where applicable, pressure relief devices and vent stacks

It applies to activities including, but not limited to:
• Receipt and acceptance, storage, transfer and fill operations, manual dispensing
• Hose connection and disconnection, purging and venting
• System startup and shutdown, equipment inspection and maintenance
• Dewar movement, laboratory or process use
• Temporary and non-routine cryogenic operations
• Work around cryogenic lines, tanks, and discharge points

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with other required programs such as Asphyxiants, Pressurized Systems, Compressed Gas Cylinders, Hazard Communication, Respiratory Protection, Health Exposure to Hazardous Materials, Electrical Safety, Permit-to-Work, Contractor Safety Management, Non-Routine Activities, Emergency Response, and applicable facility operations controls where required.

definitions.definition: A condition in which cryogenic gas displaces oxygen in the breathing zone or accumulates in enclosed or low-lying areas, reducing oxygen concentration below safe levels and creating risk of incapacitation or death.
The continuous vaporization of cryogenic liquid due to heat input from the environment, producing gas that must be vented or managed to prevent overpressure.
Tissue damage caused by contact with cryogenic liquids, cold vapor, cold surfaces, or materials cooled to cryogenic temperatures, ranging from superficial skin injury to deep tissue destruction.
A liquefied gas maintained at extremely low temperatures, typically below minus 150 degrees Celsius (minus 238 degrees Fahrenheit), which can rapidly expand into large volumes of gas upon warming.
An integrated assembly of vessels, piping, valves, fittings, controls, and support equipment designed for the storage, transfer, distribution, or use of cryogenic materials.
A vacuum-insulated container designed to store and transport cryogenic liquids while minimizing heat transfer and boil-off.

The loss of ductility and increased fracture susceptibility of materials exposed to cryogenic temperatures, potentially leading to catastrophic failure of components not designed for cryogenic service.
An atmosphere containing less than 19.5 percent oxygen by volume, which can result from displacement of air by cryogenic gas boil-off.

An atmosphere containing greater than 23.5 percent oxygen by volume, which significantly increases fire and combustion risk and can result from liquid oxygen use or condensation of oxygen from air on cryogenic surfaces.
A component or circuit on a cryogenic vessel that vaporizes a portion of the stored liquid to increase vessel pressure and support liquid delivery or maintain operating pressure.
A valve, burst disc, or other device designed to automatically relieve excess pressure from a cryogenic vessel or trapped section to prevent overpressure failure.
The controlled movement of cryogenic liquid from one vessel to another using pressure, gravity, or pumping, including fill, dispense, and decant activities.
Piping, tubing, or other pathway that routes boil-off gas or relief discharge from a cryogenic vessel to a safe discharge point away from personnel, ignition sources, and occupied areas.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Develop and maintain this program. Establish cryogenic material classification, storage, transfer, and atmospheric control requirements. Conduct or support hazard assessments for cryogenic operations. Verify training, competency, and authorization programs. Provide technical guidance on cryogenic hazards, ventilation, oxygen monitoring, PPE, and emergency response. Support incident investigation and corrective action. Audit cryogenic storage, handling, and contractor compliance. Conduct periodic program reviews and updates.

Ensure personnel under their direction understand and comply with this procedure. Verify that cryogenic hazards are assessed, equipment is suitable, ventilation and atmospheric controls are in place, and required PPE is available before work begins. Stop work when conditions are unsafe. Ensure only trained personnel handle cryogenic materials. Report incidents, releases, near misses, and equipment failures. Support training and program improvements.

Follow all requirements of this procedure. Inspect cryogenic equipment before use. Use only compatible and approved equipment. Wear required PPE. Report leaks, damage, oxygen alarms, releases, equipment failures, and unsafe conditions immediately. Do not use damaged, leaking, improperly vented, or incompatible equipment. Do not block or defeat pressure relief or venting systems.

Inspect incoming cryogenic containers for damage, labeling, and condition. Store containers in approved locations with adequate ventilation. Maintain orderly storage areas. Track inventory where required. Coordinate container returns or exchanges. Report damaged or suspect containers.

Maintain cryogenic systems, vessels, piping, valves, and associated infrastructure. Support ventilation, oxygen monitoring, and leak detection systems. Perform or coordinate equipment repairs using approved parts and methods suitable for cryogenic service. Ensure system modifications follow change management requirements. Support hazard assessments and incident investigations.

Design and specify cryogenic systems, storage areas, ventilation, oxygen monitoring, and venting in compliance with codes and this procedure. Evaluate cryogenic hazards during system design, modification, and change management. Specify compatible materials, pressure relief, and safety devices. Support hazard assessments and engineering reviews for higher-risk cryogenic operations.

Comply with this procedure and all applicable site requirements for cryogenic materials. Do not introduce cryogenic systems onsite without site coordination and approval. Use only compatible, inspected equipment. Report leaks, releases, incidents, near misses, and unsafe conditions to the host employer. Participate in coordination meetings and pre-job briefings.

training: Cryogenic hazard recognition — cold burns, frostbite, tissue damage, eye injury
Asphyxiation hazard — oxygen displacement in enclosed and low-lying areas
Oxygen enrichment hazard — increased fire and combustion risk from oxidizing cryogens
Pressure buildup and venting hazards — trapped cryogen expansion, relief device function
Proper storage, securing, and location controls for dewars and cryogenic vessels
Transfer operation procedures — controlled fill, slow cooldown, splash prevention
PPE requirements — cryogenic gloves, face shield, protective clothing, footwear
Equipment inspection and materials compatibility — embrittlement prevention
Oxygen monitoring systems and alarm response where applicable
Leak, release, and spill response actions
Housekeeping — ice, frost, condensation, and slip hazard management
Emergency response expectations — release, injury, oxygen alarm
Incident and near-miss reporting requirements
Stop-work authority and obligation
ppe: Safety glasses — required as minimum eye protection during cryogenic work
Face shield — required during transfer, dispensing, or tasks with splash potential
Cryogenic gloves — loose-fitting gloves suitable for splash protection that can be quickly removed if liquid enters the glove
Long sleeves — required to prevent skin exposure to cold vapor and splash
Lab coat, cryogenic apron, or protective outerwear as appropriate for the task and exposure level
Long pants without cuffs where appropriate — prevents pooling of spilled cryogenic liquid
Closed-toe footwear — leather or similar material, no open-toe or mesh shoes
Respiratory protection — only where specifically required by hazard assessment and authorized under the Respiratory Protection Program

NOTE: Cryogenic PPE does not replace proper venting, ventilation, atmospheric monitoring, or equipment design controls. Gloves shall allow rapid removal if liquid enters the glove. PPE selection shall consider splash, cold contact, oxidizer exposure, and confined area work hazards.
hazards: Cold burns and frostbite — contact with cryogenic liquids, cold vapor, or cold surfaces causes rapid tissue freezing and destruction
Asphyxiation — cryogenic gas displaces oxygen in enclosed or poorly ventilated areas, causing rapid incapacitation and death without warning
Oxygen enrichment — liquid oxygen or condensation of oxygen from air on cold surfaces creates fire and combustion risk
Pressure buildup — trapped cryogenic liquid expands rapidly into gas, potentially causing vessel rupture, hose failure, or component failure
Vessel and equipment failure — overpressure, embrittlement, or mechanical damage can cause catastrophic release
Hose failure and spray release — sudden disconnection, hose rupture, or fitting failure creating cold spray and whip hazards
Embrittlement — exposure of incompatible materials to cryogenic temperatures causes loss of ductility and brittle fracture
Slips, trips, and falls — ice formation, frost, and condensation from cryogenic operations create slip hazards
Fire and combustion from oxidizing cryogens — liquid oxygen contact with organic materials, oil, grease, or combustibles can cause spontaneous ignition
Visibility hazards — vapor clouds from cryogenic releases can obscure vision and mask hazards
Manual handling injuries — heavy dewars, awkward containers, and cold surfaces create ergonomic and contact hazards
Secondary hazards — interaction with electrical systems, nearby hot work, process equipment, and simultaneous operations
procedure:
**1. PURPOSE**

This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, evaluating, controlling, and safely managing hazards associated with cryogenic liquids, cryogenic gases, cryogenic systems, and related equipment in workplace operations at [Company Name].

The purpose is to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, cold burns, frostbite, asphyxiation, oxygen-enriched atmosphere hazards, pressure-related incidents, vessel rupture, hose failure, embrittlement-related equipment failure, slips and falls, fire or combustion hazards, equipment damage, property damage, and operational disruption.

COMPLIANCE DISCLAIMER: This SOP template is provided as operational guidance and does not replace applicable federal, state, or local regulatory requirements, consensus standards, or specific internal company procedures.

**2. SCOPE**

This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, laboratories, production areas, fabrication areas, maintenance activities, test areas, warehouses, utilities, field locations, assembly areas, construction support areas, and non-routine operations where cryogenic liquids, cryogenic gases, or cryogenic systems are stored, transferred, used, transported, or managed at [Site Name].

Personnel covered include employees who handle, transfer, inspect, operate, maintain, or supervise cryogenic work, supervisors, managers, EHS personnel, engineers, technicians, operators, laboratory personnel, maintenance personnel, and contractors.

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with Asphyxiants, Pressurized Systems, Compressed Gas Cylinders, Hazard Communication, Respiratory Protection, Health Exposure to Hazardous Materials, Electrical Safety, Permit-to-Work, Contractor Safety Management, Non-Routine Activities, and Emergency Response programs where applicable.

**3. REFERENCES**

• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.101 — Compressed Gases where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910 Subpart H — Hazardous Materials where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1200 — Hazard Communication
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134 — Respiratory Protection where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1450 — Hazardous Chemicals in Laboratories where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.146 — Permit-Required Confined Spaces where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147 — Control of Hazardous Energy where applicable
• OSHA 29 CFR 1910.132 and 1910.133 — PPE and Eye/Face Protection where applicable
• CGA guidance documents for cryogenic systems and oxygen-deficiency controls
• NFPA codes applicable to cryogenic fluids, oxidizers, and compressed gases
• Manufacturer instructions for dewars, vessels, transfer hoses, valves, fittings, PPE, monitoring systems, and associated equipment
• Safety Data Sheets for cryogenic materials used onsite
• [Company Name] Asphyxiants Safety Procedure
• [Company Name] Pressurized Systems Safety Procedure
• [Company Name] Compressed Gas Cylinders Procedure
• [Company Name] Hazard Communication Program
• [Company Name] Respiratory Protection Program
• [Company Name] Incident Reporting and Investigation Procedure
• [Company Name] Emergency Response Plan
• Site-specific cryogenic system requirements, oxygen monitoring plans, and operating instructions
• [Applicable State / Local Requirements]
• [Related Company Procedures]

**4. DEFINITIONS**

• Asphyxiation Hazard — A condition in which cryogenic gas displaces oxygen, reducing concentration below safe levels.
• Boil-Off — Continuous vaporization of cryogenic liquid due to heat input, producing gas requiring venting or management.
• Cold Burn / Frostbite — Tissue damage from contact with cryogenic liquids, cold vapor, or cold surfaces.
• Cryogenic Liquid — A liquefied gas below minus 150°C that can rapidly expand into large gas volumes.
• Cryogenic System — An integrated assembly of vessels, piping, valves, and controls for cryogenic materials.
• Dewar — A vacuum-insulated container for storing and transporting cryogenic liquids.
• Embrittlement — Loss of ductility and increased fracture susceptibility at cryogenic temperatures.
• Oxygen-Deficient Atmosphere — Less than 19.5% oxygen by volume.
• Oxygen-Enriched Atmosphere — Greater than 23.5% oxygen by volume, increasing fire risk.
• Pressure-Building Device — A component that vaporizes stored liquid to maintain vessel pressure.
• Pressure Relief Device — A valve or burst disc that automatically relieves excess pressure.
• Transfer Operation — Controlled movement of cryogenic liquid between vessels.
• Vent Line / Vent System — Pathway routing boil-off gas or relief discharge to a safe location.

**5. POLICY**

[Company Name] will identify and control hazards associated with cryogenic liquids and cryogenic systems through proper material classification, equipment design, storage, transfer controls, venting, pressure relief, PPE, ventilation, monitoring, training, supervision, and periodic review.

No cryogenic material shall be stored, moved, transferred, connected, disconnected, or used unless:
• The cryogenic hazard is known and communicated
• The equipment is appropriate for cryogenic service
• Pressure control and venting provisions are in place
• Ventilation and atmospheric hazard controls are adequate
• Required PPE is available and suitable
• Personnel are trained and authorized as appropriate

The company will prioritize prevention of cold contact, oxygen deficiency, overpressure, vent discharge exposure, oxygen enrichment, and equipment failure before relying solely on PPE or emergency response.

Employees shall not use damaged, leaking, improperly vented, improperly labeled, or incompatible cryogenic equipment.

Employees shall not plug, cap, block, or otherwise defeat pressure relief or venting systems on cryogenic equipment.

**6. RESPONSIBILITIES**

EHS / Program Administrator: Develop and maintain this program. Establish cryogenic material classification, storage, transfer, and atmospheric control requirements. Conduct or support hazard assessments. Verify training and authorization programs. Provide technical guidance on cryogenic hazards, ventilation, oxygen monitoring, PPE, and emergency response. Support incident investigation. Audit cryogenic operations and contractor compliance. Conduct periodic program reviews.

Managers and Supervisors: Ensure compliance with this procedure. Verify hazard assessments, equipment suitability, ventilation, atmospheric controls, and PPE before work begins. Stop work when unsafe. Report incidents and near misses.

Employees / Cryogenic Material Users: Follow all requirements. Inspect equipment before use. Wear required PPE. Report leaks, damage, oxygen alarms, releases, and unsafe conditions immediately. Do not defeat pressure relief or venting systems.

Receiving / Storage / Equipment Custodians: Inspect incoming containers. Store in approved locations with adequate ventilation. Maintain orderly storage. Track inventory where required. Report damaged containers.

Maintenance / Facilities / Technical Support: Maintain cryogenic systems and infrastructure. Support ventilation and oxygen monitoring. Perform repairs using approved cryogenic-rated parts.

Engineering / System Owners: Design and specify cryogenic systems in compliance with codes. Evaluate hazards during design and modification. Specify compatible materials and safety devices.

Contractors: Comply with all site requirements. Do not introduce cryogenic systems without approval. Use inspected, compatible equipment. Report incidents and unsafe conditions.

**7.1 CRYOGENIC HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND ASSESSMENT**

All cryogenic operations shall be evaluated to identify hazards and determine required controls before materials are stored, transferred, moved, or used.

Hazard assessments shall consider:
• Cryogenic material identity and classification
• Temperature and expansion characteristics — expansion ratios (e.g., liquid nitrogen expands approximately 694:1)
• Boil-off rate and gas generation potential
• Asphyxiation hazard — oxygen displacement in enclosed areas
• Oxygen enrichment potential for oxidizing cryogens such as liquid oxygen
• Flammability where applicable — liquid hydrogen
• Pressure and relief requirements
• Venting location and discharge path — [Vent Stack / Vent Discharge Requirements]
• Transfer method and equipment compatibility
• Potential for cold burns or contact injury
• Material embrittlement risk for non-rated components
• Room size, ventilation capacity, and air exchange rate
• Need for oxygen monitoring or gas detection — [Oxygen Monitoring Requirements]
• Presence of enclosed or low-lying areas where gas may accumulate
• Need for barricades or restricted access
• Potential for ice formation, condensation, and slip hazards
• Interaction with nearby operations or incompatible materials

If hazards cannot be adequately understood or controlled, the cryogenic material or system shall not be placed into service until additional review and controls are established.

**7.2 HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS FOR CRYOGENIC SAFETY**

Cryogenic hazards shall be controlled using the following preferred order:

1. Eliminate unnecessary cryogenic use or reduce the quantity handled or stored
2. Substitute lower-risk supply or process methods where feasible
3. Use engineering controls such as insulated vessels, proper venting, pressure relief, oxygen monitoring, ventilation, barricades, and compatible materials
4. Use administrative controls such as restricted access, procedures, training, signage, and operational limits as supplemental controls
5. Use PPE as a supporting measure, not as a substitute for proper equipment design, venting, and atmospheric control

Whenever feasible, cryogenic systems shall be arranged to reduce cold contact, atmospheric displacement, oxygen enrichment, and pressure-related hazards.

**7.3 RECEIPT AND INITIAL INSPECTION**

All cryogenic containers shall be inspected upon receipt or initial acceptance into the work area.

Inspection shall include:
• Product label and cryogen identity — legible and matching the intended material
• Vessel condition — no dents, impact damage, excessive corrosion, or deformation
• Evidence of leaks, damage, heavy frosting in abnormal locations
• Pressure relief device presence and condition
• Vent path condition — unobstructed and properly routed
• Valve condition and operability
• Connection fittings — compatible and undamaged
• Stability and support condition — base ring, wheels, handles as applicable
• Operational status and intended service

Cryogenic containers that are leaking, damaged, unlabeled, or otherwise suspect shall not be used and shall be isolated and reported for evaluation.

**7.4 STORAGE AND LOCATION CONTROLS**

Cryogenic materials shall be stored in approved locations suitable for the hazard profile at [Approved Cryogenic Storage Locations].

Storage requirements include:
• Upright storage unless specifically designed and approved otherwise
• Protection from tipping, impact, excessive heat, and vehicle traffic
• Adequate ventilation — natural or mechanical to prevent gas accumulation
• Vented discharge directed away from occupied walkways, confined areas, air intakes, and ignition sources
• Clear access to shutoff controls, exits, and emergency equipment
• Hazard signage where needed — cryogenic hazard, asphyxiation risk, oxidizer warning as applicable
• Segregation of incompatible materials where appropriate
• Prohibition on storage in uncontrolled confined spaces, exits, stairways, or unventilated enclosed areas

Storage areas shall be evaluated for oxygen deficiency and oxygen enrichment risk based on the cryogen type and quantity present.

**7.5 SECURING DEWARS AND CRYOGENIC CONTAINERS**

Cryogenic vessels and dewars shall be stabilized and secured as appropriate to prevent tipping, movement, or uncontrolled discharge.

Securing requirements:
• Approved stands, restraints, holders, or other support arrangements as needed
• Portable dewars and containers placed on stable, level surfaces
• Containers not left in unstable positions or unsecured in traffic areas
• Mobile units secured during transport or staging
• Wheeled units controlled from rolling or shifting when parked — wheel locks, chocks, or positioning against stable structures

Unstable cryogenic containers shall not be left unattended.

**7.6 TRANSFER OPERATIONS**

Cryogenic transfer operations shall be performed using approved equipment and controlled methods — [Cryogenic Transfer Authorization Requirements].

Pre-transfer planning shall include:
• Source and receiving vessel identification
• Pressure and venting conditions verified
• Hose and fitting compatibility confirmed
• Transfer rate appropriate for the operation
• Potential splash or spray exposure identified
• Vent discharge direction confirmed safe
• Need for slow cooldown or controlled fill evaluated
• Area ventilation and oxygen hazard addressed
• Receiving container suitability confirmed
• Nearby personnel and line-of-fire exposure addressed

During transfer:
• Inspect hoses, valves, and fittings before use
• Use only approved and compatible transfer connections
• Secure equipment and route hoses to minimize damage and trip hazards
• Transfer slowly and deliberately — allow cooldown of receiving vessel
• Avoid overfilling — observe fill level indicators or weight limits
• Keep hands, face, and body away from potential splash and vent discharge points
• Monitor continuously as required by site policy or hazard assessment

Unattended transfer operations are prohibited where site rules or the hazard assessment require active attendance.

**7.7 PRESSURE CONTROL, VENTING, AND RELIEF DEVICES**

Cryogenic liquids can rapidly expand into gas and create dangerous pressure if trapped. Pressure control is critical.

Requirements:
• Each cryogenic vessel or trapped section shall have appropriate pressure relief protection
• Vent paths shall be kept open and unobstructed at all times
• Capping, plugging, or isolating relief devices or normal vents is prohibited unless the system is specifically designed and controlled for that condition
• Vent outlets shall be protected from blockage by ice, debris, or damage
• Pressure-building systems shall be confirmed functioning as intended where used
• Pressure control components shall be suitable for cryogenic service and temperatures

Cryogenic systems shall never be treated as closed systems unless specifically engineered for that purpose with required relief protection in place.

**7.8 OXYGEN DEFICIENCY AND OXYGEN ENRICHMENT CONTROLS**

Atmospheric hazards from gas displacement and oxygen enrichment shall be controlled.

Controls shall include as applicable:
• Area ventilation — natural or mechanical, sufficient for the cryogen volume and boil-off rate
• Oxygen monitoring systems — [Oxygen Monitoring Requirements]
• Restricted access to areas with significant oxygen displacement or enrichment risk
• Alarm response procedures — audible and visual alarms, evacuation triggers
• Quantity limits based on room volume and ventilation capacity
• Controlled vent routing — [Vent Stack / Vent Discharge Requirements]
• Work planning for enclosed or low-lying areas where gas may settle
• Emergency evacuation provisions

Oxygen monitors shall be maintained, calibrated, and responded to in accordance with site requirements where installed.

Employees shall not enter or remain in an area when oxygen deficiency or oxygen enrichment alarms are active unless authorized emergency procedures specifically allow and required protections are in place.

**7.9 PPE FOR CRYOGENIC WORK**

PPE shall be selected based on the cryogenic material, transfer method, exposure potential, and task.

Required PPE shall include as applicable:
• Safety glasses — minimum eye protection
• Face shield — required during transfer, dispensing, or tasks with splash potential
• Cryogenic gloves — loose-fitting, suitable for splash protection, quickly removable if liquid enters
• Long sleeves — prevents skin exposure to cold vapor and splash
• Lab coat, cryogenic apron, or protective outerwear as appropriate
• Long pants without cuffs — prevents pooling of spilled cryogenic liquid
• Closed-toe footwear — leather or similar, no open-toe or mesh
• Respiratory protection — only where specifically required by hazard assessment

PPE selection shall consider splash exposure, cold contact hazard, ease of quick removal if contaminated, compatibility with the task, and additional hazards such as oxidizer exposure or confined area work.

Gloves shall not create entrapment risk — they must allow rapid removal if liquid enters the glove.

**7.10 MATERIALS COMPATIBILITY AND EQUIPMENT INTEGRITY**

Only materials and equipment suitable for cryogenic service shall be used.

Compatibility requirements:
• Use valves, hoses, seals, fittings, and vessels designed for cryogenic temperatures
• Avoid materials prone to brittle failure at low temperature — carbon steel, certain plastics, rubber compounds not rated for cryogenic service
• Prevent contact of cryogenic liquid with incompatible materials or surfaces not designed for service
• Inspect for cracking, abnormal frosting patterns, seal degradation, and damage
• Remove compromised equipment from service immediately

Improvised or non-rated equipment shall not be used for cryogenic service.

**7.11 HOSE, VALVE, AND CONNECTION SAFETY**

Cryogenic hoses, valves, and fittings shall be controlled to prevent leaks, spray release, and hose failure.

Requirements:
• Inspect hoses and fittings before each use
• Confirm compatibility with the cryogen and secure connection
• Protect hoses from kinks, impact, pinch points, and traffic
• Keep personnel out of likely spray or release paths
• Operate valves slowly and deliberately — thermal shock can damage components
• Disconnect only after depressurization and when safe to do so
• Manage cold surfaces and ice buildup that may interfere with safe operation

Employees shall not force frozen valves or fittings in a manner that may damage equipment or create uncontrolled release.

**7.12 MANUAL HANDLING, MOVEMENT, AND TRANSPORT**

Cryogenic containers and dewars shall be moved using approved methods that prevent tipping, impact, and uncontrolled discharge.

Transport requirements:
• Use approved carts, dollies, or handling equipment where required
• Keep portable units upright unless specifically designed otherwise
• Control travel paths to avoid stairs, unstable surfaces, impact, and congestion
• Protect personnel from vent or splash exposure during movement
• Avoid manual lifting of large or awkward containers when mechanical assistance is appropriate
• Secure containers during transport — strap, cradle, or cart restraint

Movement of cryogenic containers shall be planned to prevent collision, sudden release, and operator strain.

**7.13 LABORATORY AND PROCESS USE CONTROLS**

Cryogenic materials used in laboratories, process systems, or test operations shall be managed under controls appropriate to the activity.

Controls shall include:
• Defined operating procedures for the specific application
• Quantity limits based on ventilation and room volume
• Approved work surfaces and supports — compatible with cryogenic temperatures
• Ventilation and oxygen monitoring as required
• Restricted access during fills, venting, or high-volume operations
• Process-specific compatibility checks — materials, seals, joints
• Controlled disposal or evaporation practices — in well-ventilated areas only
• Special review for enclosed systems, cold traps, or process vessels

Open use of cryogenic liquids in poorly ventilated or confined areas shall receive additional review before work begins.

**7.14 SPILL, RELEASE, AND ICE HAZARD CONTROLS**

Cryogenic releases shall receive immediate attention for cold contact, visibility, atmospheric, and slip hazards.

Response actions:
• Stop work immediately
• Keep personnel away from the release area — cold vapor clouds can obscure hazards
• Avoid direct contact with the liquid, vapor cloud, or cold surfaces
• Isolate the area as needed based on the cryogen and release volume
• Notify supervision and EHS immediately
• Allow the material to dissipate only under controlled and safe conditions consistent with the emergency plan
• Escalate emergency response when the release exceeds onsite capability or creates life safety risk

Ice, frost, and condensed moisture from cryogenic operations shall be managed to reduce slip, trip, and equipment interference hazards.

**7.15 MAINTENANCE, CHANGE-OUT, AND NON-ROUTINE WORK**

Maintenance or non-routine work involving cryogenic systems shall be planned and controlled before work begins.

Controls shall include:
• System isolation and depressurization
• Warm-up or temperature stabilization where needed — verify safe contact temperature
• Lockout/tagout where applicable
• Verification of safe condition before work proceeds
• Area ventilation and monitoring during work
• Non-routine work review or permit where required
• Additional engineering review for higher-risk tasks

Employees shall not perform maintenance on cryogenic systems while pressure, cold exposure, or atmospheric hazards remain uncontrolled.

**7.16 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION**

Coordination shall be required when contractors store, move, transfer, or use cryogenic materials onsite.

Coordination shall include:
• Cryogen type and quantity
• Storage location and ventilation requirements
• Transfer method and authorization requirements
• Pressure relief and vent routing
• PPE requirements
• Oxygen monitoring and atmospheric controls
• Emergency response expectations
• Leak or release reporting requirements
• Non-routine work approvals where applicable

Contractors shall not introduce cryogenic systems onsite without complying with site coordination and approval requirements.

**7.17 INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW**

All cryogenic incidents shall be reported immediately, including:
• Cryogenic splash exposure or cold burn
• Equipment failure — vessel, hose, valve, fitting, or relief device
• Vent blockage or relief activation
• Oxygen alarm activation
• Uncontrolled release or spill
• Near misses and unsafe conditions

Investigations shall evaluate:
• Material hazard identification
• Equipment suitability and condition
• Transfer planning and execution
• Pressure control and venting
• Ventilation and oxygen hazard controls
• PPE adequacy
• Training adequacy
• Supervision and planning
• Environmental conditions
• Need for engineering, procedural, or training improvements

EHS and operational leadership shall periodically review the Cryogenic Safety Program for effectiveness and update requirements as needed based on incidents, audits, and lessons learned.
emergencyResponse: CRYOGENIC SPLASH OR COLD BURN: Remove the person from the cold source. Remove contaminated clothing carefully — do not peel adhered material. Flush the affected area with large amounts of lukewarm water. Do not use hot water. Do not rub the area. Seek medical evaluation. Report the incident immediately.

LARGE RELEASE — INERT CRYOGEN: Evacuate the area immediately. Do not enter vapor clouds. Ventilate if safe and possible. Restrict access. Monitor oxygen levels before reentry. Notify supervision, EHS, and emergency services as required.

LARGE RELEASE — LIQUID OXYGEN: Evacuate the area immediately. Eliminate all ignition sources. Do not allow contact with oil, grease, or combustible materials. Restrict access. Ventilate. Notify supervision, EHS, and emergency services. Do not reenter until oxygen levels are verified normal.

PRESSURE RELIEF ACTIVATION OR ABNORMAL VENTING: Move away from the discharge path. Do not attempt to cap or plug the relief device. Restrict access to the area. Notify supervision and EHS. Investigate the cause before resuming normal operations.

EQUIPMENT FAILURE — VESSEL, HOSE, OR VALVE: Clear the area. Do not attempt repairs on pressurized or cold equipment. Isolate if safe. Notify supervision and EHS. Do not return equipment to service until inspected and cleared.
OXYGEN ALARM: Evacuate the monitored area immediately. Do not reenter until the alarm is cleared and oxygen levels are verified safe. Notify supervision and EHS. Investigate the cause.

POST-INCIDENT ACTIONS: Secure the area. Preserve the scene where appropriate. Restrict access. Notify supervision, EHS, and emergency services. Complete incident reporting. Do not resume operations until investigation and corrective actions are addressed.
documentation: Training and authorization records where required
Cryogenic equipment inspection records where required
Maintenance, repair, and calibration records where applicable
Oxygen monitoring and alarm response records where applicable
Transfer operation records or logs where required by site policy
Incident, near miss, and investigation records
Corrective action tracking and closure records
Contractor coordination and qualification records
Audit findings and program review records
Cryogenic system operating or monitoring records where applicable

APPENDIX A — CRYOGENIC USE PRE-PLANNING CHECKLIST

☐ Cryogen identity and hazard known
☐ Correct equipment selected — rated for cryogenic service
☐ Storage and use location appropriate — [Approved Cryogenic Storage Locations]
☐ Ventilation and area controls adequate
☐ Pressure relief and venting provisions present and unobstructed
☐ Transfer method defined and equipment compatible
☐ Required PPE available — cryogenic gloves, face shield, protective clothing
☐ Oxygen monitoring requirements addressed — [Oxygen Monitoring Requirements]
☐ Emergency and stop-work triggers understood
☐ Nearby personnel and simultaneous operations addressed

APPENDIX B — CRYOGENIC EQUIPMENT INSPECTION CHECKLIST

☐ Vessel or dewar labeled and in acceptable condition — no dents, damage, or deformation
☐ Valves and fittings appear intact and operable
☐ Vent path unobstructed — no ice blockage, debris, or damage
☐ Pressure relief devices present and appear intact
☐ Hoses and connections suitable, undamaged, and rated for cryogenic service
☐ No abnormal leaks, frosting patterns, or damage present
☐ Base ring, wheels, handles, and support structures stable
☐ Equipment appears safe to place into service

APPENDIX C — CRYOGENIC TRANSFER CHECKLIST

☐ Source and receiving container correctly identified
☐ Equipment compatible with the cryogen — materials, fittings, seals
☐ Hoses and fittings properly connected and inspected
☐ Venting path understood and unobstructed
☐ Transfer controlled and attended as required
☐ Personnel positioned out of splash and vent discharge paths
☐ Required PPE worn correctly — cryogenic gloves, face shield, protective clothing
☐ Area ventilation and oxygen hazard controls adequate
☐ Slow cooldown performed for receiving vessel as needed
☐ Transfer stops immediately if abnormal conditions occur

APPENDIX D — OXYGEN HAZARD AND VENTING CHECKLIST

☐ Area ventilation functioning as intended
☐ Oxygen monitoring active where required — [Oxygen Monitoring Requirements]
☐ Vent discharge does not create hazard to personnel or occupied areas
☐ Enclosed or low-lying area risks addressed
☐ Alarm and evacuation expectations understood
☐ No vent line blockage, icing, or damage present
☐ Required signage and access controls in place where applicable
☐ Oxygen enrichment hazards addressed for oxidizing cryogens

APPENDIX E — CONDITIONS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE STOP WORK

☐ Cryogen identity is uncertain
☐ A vessel, dewar, hose, valve, or fitting is leaking, damaged, or incompatible
☐ Pressure relief or venting cannot be confirmed
☐ Ventilation or atmospheric controls are inadequate
☐ An oxygen alarm or atmospheric concern is identified and remains uncontrolled
☐ A release, cold exposure, or unsafe condition occurs and remains uncorrected
☐ Abnormal icing, pressure buildup, or equipment behavior is observed
☐ A supervisor, EHS, or other responsible person stops the work
☐ Conditions change such that safe cryogenic storage, transfer, or use cannot be assured

COMPLIANCE AND DEVIATIONS

Failure to comply with this procedure may result in disciplinary action, suspension of work, removal from the work area, contractor consequences where applicable, and increased risk of serious injury, fatality, asphyxiation, or explosion.

Deviations from this procedure require EHS and operational management review unless immediate action is required to protect life or health.

No deviation may remove required venting, pressure relief, atmospheric controls, equipment compatibility, PPE requirements, or other critical safeguards without equivalent or greater protection.

title: Excavation and Trenching Safety Program
summary: A comprehensive Excavation and Trenching Safety Program covering cave-in prevention, underground utility locate, competent person oversight, protective systems, soil classification, access/egress, spoil management, water accumulation, atmospheric hazards, mobile equipment controls, contractor coordination, incident reporting, and program review for OSHA 1926 Subpart P compliance.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, evaluating, planning, authorizing, and safely performing excavation and trenching work in order to control collapse hazards, struck-by hazards, underground utility hazards, atmospheric hazards, water intrusion, mobile equipment hazards, and access/egress hazards at [Company Name] operations.

This program is designed to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, engulfment, trench collapse, asphyxiation, utility strike, electrocution, fire, explosion, flooding, slips, trips, falls, struck-by incidents, equipment damage, property damage, and operational disruption resulting from inadequate planning, failure to identify underground installations, improper spoil placement, inadequate protective systems, poor inspection practices, unstable soil conditions, uncontrolled water accumulation, unsafe access, and failure to stop work when excavation conditions change.

[Company Name] is committed to ensuring that all excavation and trenching work is planned, controlled, and supervised to the standard required to protect every person who may be exposed to excavation hazards.

COMPLIANCE DISCLAIMER: This SOP template is provided as operational guidance and does not replace applicable federal, state, or local regulatory requirements, consensus standards, or specific internal company procedures. [Company Name] is responsible for ensuring compliance with all applicable regulations and adapting this template to site-specific conditions.
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, field locations, construction support activities, utilities work, maintenance activities, site development work, repair projects, and non-routine operations where excavation or trenching work may occur on company-controlled property or under company operational control at [Site Name].

It applies to:
• Employees involved in planning, approving, supervising, inspecting, or performing excavation or trenching work
• Supervisors and managers responsible for excavation work or work near excavations
• EHS personnel
• Competent persons assigned to excavation and trenching activities
• Engineers, project managers, utilities personnel, maintenance personnel, operators, inspectors, and support personnel
• Contractors and subcontractors performing excavation or trenching work onsite

Covered activities include:
• Trenching, excavation for utilities, digging for foundations/pads/footings
• Site grading interfaces involving exposed excavations
• Repair of underground lines, drains, conduits, piping
• Test pits, bell holes, potholing, vacuum excavation where associated hazards remain
• Hand digging near utilities, removal of buried equipment or structures
• Dewatering and drainage work associated with excavations
• Temporary excavation and open hole work
• Entry into excavations and trenches where employee exposure exists

Covered hazards include cave-in and collapse, underground utilities, surface encumbrances, spoil pile hazards, water accumulation, hazardous atmospheres, adjacent structure stability, mobile equipment interaction, falling loads and objects, access/egress limitations, vibration, soil instability, weather-related deterioration, and confined or restricted work areas.

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with Non-Routine Activities, Contractor Safety Management, Permit-to-Work, Confined Space Entry, Hazardous Energy / LOTO, Pressurized Systems, Electrical Safety, Mobile Equipment / PIT Safety, Suspended Loads and Lifting Operations, Emergency Response, and site-specific utility locate and dig permit requirements where applicable.

definitions.definition: A method of protecting employees from cave-ins by excavating the sides of an excavation to form one or a series of horizontal levels or steps, usually with vertical or near-vertical surfaces between levels.

One who is capable of identifying existing and predictable hazards in the surroundings or working conditions which are unsanitary, hazardous, or dangerous to employees, and who has authorization to take prompt corrective measures to eliminate them.

The separation of a mass of soil or rock material from the side of an excavation, or the loss of soil from under a trench shield or support system, and its sudden movement into the excavation, either by falling or sliding, in sufficient quantity so that it could entrap, bury, or otherwise injure and immobilize a person.
Any man-made cut, cavity, trench, or depression in an earth surface, formed by earth removal.

A method of protecting employees from cave-ins, from material that could fall or roll from an excavation face or into an excavation, or from the collapse of adjacent structures. Includes support systems, sloping and benching systems, shield systems, and other approved systems.
A structure that is able to withstand the forces imposed on it by a cave-in and thereby protect employees within the structure. Shields can be permanent or portable.
A structure such as a metal hydraulic, mechanical, or timber shoring system that supports the sides of an excavation and is designed to prevent cave-ins.
A method of protecting employees from cave-ins by cutting back the trench wall at an angle inclined away from the excavation.
Soil and other material removed from an excavation, typically placed adjacent to the excavation during work.

Any object, structure, utility, equipment, or feature located on or near the surface that may create a hazard during excavation work, require removal, or require support to prevent collapse into the excavation.

A narrow excavation (in relation to its length) made below the surface of the ground. In general, the depth is greater than the width, but the width of a trench (measured at the bottom) is not greater than 15 feet.
Any utility line, cable, conduit, pipe, duct, sewer, fiber optic, fuel, gas, water, steam, communication, or other buried system that may be encountered during excavation work.
Water from any source, including surface runoff, rain, groundwater, tidal action, or dewatering of adjacent areas that enters or affects an excavation.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Develop, implement, maintain, and periodically review the Excavation and Trenching Safety Program. Provide technical guidance on protective systems, soil classification, and hazard control. Coordinate training for competent persons, supervisors, and affected employees. Assist with utility locate coordination and dig permit programs. Review excavation incidents, near misses, and corrective actions. Monitor regulatory changes. Conduct periodic audits of excavation work practices.

Ensure excavation work is planned, approved, and controlled in accordance with this program. Assign competent persons for excavation oversight. Verify utility locate, permits, and protective system requirements are met before work begins. Monitor field conditions and ensure stop-work authority is exercised when required. Ensure personnel are trained. Coordinate with contractors. Investigate and report incidents and near misses.

Inspect excavations, adjacent areas, and protective systems before employee exposure begins and as conditions change. Classify soil and ground conditions or ensure classification is completed. Direct installation, maintenance, and removal of protective systems. Evaluate water accumulation, atmospheric conditions, spoil placement, access/egress, and equipment proximity. Order immediate corrective action or evacuation if hazardous conditions develop. Authorize or suspend trench entry based on field conditions.

Follow all excavation safety requirements, procedures, and competent person direction. Use only designated access and egress points. Remain within protective system coverage when exposure to cave-in exists. Report unsafe conditions, soil movement, water accumulation, or utility exposure immediately. Exercise stop-work authority when conditions change. Use required PPE.

Operate equipment near excavations in accordance with site controls and spotter direction. Maintain safe distances from excavation edges. Ensure loads are not swung over occupied excavations unless specifically controlled. Report equipment malfunctions, ground instability, or unsafe conditions immediately. Coordinate with ground workers and competent person.

Provide engineering support for protective system design, soil analysis, and excavation planning where required. Review higher-risk excavations. Support competent person with engineering evaluations. Assist with utility coordination and as-built information.

Comply with all [Company Name] excavation and trenching requirements. Assign competent persons for contractor excavation work. Provide evidence of training and competency as required. Coordinate on utility locate, dig permits, protective systems, and emergency response. Report incidents and near misses immediately. Stop work when conditions create uncontrolled risk.

training: Recognition of excavation and trenching hazards — cave-in, collapse, engulfment, utility strike
Utility locate and underground installation awareness
Protective system basics — sloping, benching, shoring, shielding/trench boxes
Soil classification and changing condition awareness
Access and egress requirements — ladders, ramps, travel distance
Spoil placement and edge loading hazards
Water accumulation and atmospheric hazard awareness
Competent person inspection expectations and authority
Barricade and area control requirements
Mobile equipment interaction and falling load hazards near excavations
Emergency response expectations — cave-in rescue, utility strike response
Incident and near-miss reporting requirements
Stop-work authority and obligation
ppe: Hard hat — required in and around excavation work areas
High-visibility vest or clothing — required where mobile equipment or vehicle traffic is present
Safety glasses with side shields — required as minimum eye protection
Steel-toe or safety-toe boots — required for all excavation work
Gloves — appropriate for the material handling and soil conditions
Hearing protection — where noise from equipment exceeds action levels
Respiratory protection — where atmospheric hazards require it per hazard assessment
Fall protection — where work near excavation edges creates fall exposure not controlled by other means
NOTE: PPE does not replace protective systems, utility controls, competent person oversight, or proper excavation planning.
hazards: Cave-in and collapse — the primary cause of excavation fatalities; soil can weigh over 100 pounds per cubic foot and crush or suffocate workers
Underground utility strike — contact with buried electrical, gas, water, sewer, fiber optic, or communication lines causing electrocution, fire, explosion, or flooding
Engulfment — partial or complete burial of a person by soil creating crushing force, asphyxiation, or immobilization
Hazardous atmospheres — oxygen deficiency, toxic gases, or flammable atmospheres in deep, enclosed, or contaminated excavations
Water accumulation — flooding, erosion, soil saturation, and instability from groundwater, rain, or adjacent dewatering
Falls — into open excavations from edges, unstable soil, or inadequate barricades
Struck-by — from falling soil, rock, tools, materials, or equipment near excavation edges
Mobile equipment — excavators, backhoes, dump trucks, and other equipment operating near occupied trenches
Adjacent structure collapse — undermining foundations, sidewalks, utilities, or nearby structures
Spoil pile hazards — material sliding back into the excavation or overloading excavation edges
Vibration — from traffic, equipment, or nearby operations destabilizing excavation walls
Weather deterioration — rain, freeze/thaw cycles, wind, and temperature changes affecting soil stability
procedure:
**1. PURPOSE**

This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, evaluating, planning, authorizing, and safely performing excavation and trenching work at [Company Name] to control collapse, struck-by, underground utility, atmospheric, water intrusion, mobile equipment, and access/egress hazards.

COMPLIANCE DISCLAIMER: This SOP template is provided as operational guidance and does not replace applicable regulatory requirements.

**2. SCOPE**

Applies to all [Company Name] facilities, field locations, construction support activities, and non-routine operations where excavation or trenching work occurs. Covers employees, competent persons, supervisors, engineers, operators, and contractors involved in excavation work.

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with Non-Routine Activities, Contractor Safety Management, Permit-to-Work, Confined Space Entry, LOTO, Electrical Safety, Mobile Equipment, Suspended Loads, and Emergency Response programs where applicable.

**3. REFERENCES**

• OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart P — Excavations
• OSHA 29 CFR 1926.650 — Scope, Application, and Definitions
• OSHA 29 CFR 1926.651 — Specific Excavation Requirements
• OSHA 29 CFR 1926.652 — Requirements for Protective Systems
• OSHA 29 CFR 1926.21 — Safety Training and Education
• OSHA 29 CFR 1926.651(b) — Underground Installations
• OSHA 29 CFR 1926.651(c) — Access and Egress
• OSHA 29 CFR 1926.651(h) — Water Accumulation Hazards
• OSHA 29 CFR 1926.651(j) — Loose Rock or Soil Protection
• Applicable utility locate / one-call requirements
• Manufacturer instructions for trench boxes, shields, shoring, ladders, pumps, and excavation equipment
• [Company Name] Permit to Work, Non-Routine Activities, Confined Space, Contractor Safety, Incident Reporting, and Emergency Response procedures
• [Applicable State / Local Requirements]
• [Related Company Procedures]

**4. DEFINITIONS**

• Benching — Excavating sides into horizontal steps to prevent cave-in.
• Competent Person — One capable of identifying hazards and authorized to take corrective action.
• Cave-In — Sudden movement of soil into the excavation in sufficient quantity to entrap or injure.
• Excavation — Any man-made cut, cavity, trench, or depression formed by earth removal.
• Protective System — Sloping, benching, shoring, shielding, or engineered system protecting employees.
• Shielding — A structure withstanding cave-in forces to protect workers within.
• Shoring — Support system preventing cave-in of excavation sides.
• Sloping — Cutting the trench wall at an angle away from the excavation.
• Spoil — Material removed from an excavation.
• Surface Encumbrance — Surface objects or structures creating excavation hazards.
• Trench — A narrow excavation where depth exceeds width (bottom width ≤ 15 ft).
• Underground Installation — Any buried utility, conduit, pipe, or system.
• Water Accumulation — Water entering an excavation from any source.

**5. POLICY**

[Company Name] will manage excavation and trenching hazards through planning, utility coordination, competent person oversight, soil assessment, protective systems, access control, inspection, training, and supervision.

No excavation or trenching work shall begin unless the work scope is clearly defined, underground installations have been identified, hazards have been assessed, a competent person has been assigned, protective systems are in place, permits have been obtained, and conditions remain consistent with the plan.

Employees shall not enter an excavation where required protective systems, inspections, utility controls, atmospheric precautions, or access/egress provisions are not in place.

Work shall not continue when conditions, weather, water, vibration, adjacent loading, or other factors create uncontrolled risk.

**6. RESPONSIBILITIES**

EHS / Program Administrator: Develop and maintain this program. Provide technical guidance. Coordinate training. Assist with utility locate and dig permits. Review incidents. Monitor regulations. Conduct audits.

Managers and Supervisors: Ensure excavation work is planned and controlled. Assign competent persons. Verify utility locate, permits, and protective systems. Monitor conditions. Coordinate with contractors. Investigate incidents.

Competent Person: Inspect excavations before exposure and as conditions change. Classify soil. Direct protective system installation. Evaluate water, atmosphere, spoil, access/egress. Order corrective action or evacuation. Authorize or suspend entry.

Employees / Excavation Workers: Follow procedures and competent person direction. Use designated access/egress. Remain within protective systems. Report unsafe conditions. Exercise stop-work authority.

Equipment Operators: Maintain safe distances from edges. Coordinate with ground workers. Report instability or malfunctions.

Engineering / Project Personnel: Support protective system design and soil analysis. Review higher-risk excavations. Assist with utility coordination.

Contractors: Comply with all requirements. Assign competent persons. Coordinate on utility locate, dig permits, and emergency response. Report incidents.

**7.1 EXCAVATION HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND ASSESSMENT**

Before excavation or trenching work begins, the task, location, surrounding conditions, and affected systems shall be evaluated.

The assessment shall consider:
• Excavation depth, width, and length
• Soil condition and stability
• Groundwater, seepage, and runoff potential
• Weather exposure and forecast
• Underground installations — [Utility Locate Process]
• Surface encumbrances — poles, structures, sidewalks, equipment
• Adjacent structures, roads, equipment, and foundations
• Spoil placement requirements
• Mobile equipment proximity and traffic
• Vibration from traffic or nearby operations
• Access and egress requirements
• Falling object hazards — loose rock, soil, tools, materials
• Need for protective systems — type and configuration
• Need for atmospheric testing or ventilation
• Need for dewatering
• Potential for confined or restricted conditions
• Simultaneous operations nearby
• Emergency response access

If excavation hazards cannot be adequately understood or controlled, the work shall not proceed until additional review and controls are established.

**7.2 HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS FOR EXCAVATION AND TRENCHING**

[Company Name] shall apply the following control hierarchy:

1. Eliminate or reduce excavation exposure through alternate methods, rerouting, or design changes where feasible
2. Minimize excavation dimensions and worker exposure duration where feasible
3. Use engineering controls such as sloping, benching, shoring, shielding, barricades, dewatering, and utility isolation
4. Use administrative controls such as permits, inspections, access restriction, spotters, and sequencing as supplemental controls
5. Use PPE as a supporting measure, not as a substitute for protective systems and proper excavation control

Whenever feasible, employee exposure to open excavation hazards shall be reduced by design, planning, and protective systems.

**7.3 PRE-PLANNING, PERMIT, AND APPROVAL REQUIREMENTS**

Excavation work shall be planned before digging begins — [Dig Permit Requirements].

Planning shall include:
• Scope of work and exact location
• Required depth and dimensions
• Underground utility identification — [Utility Locate Process]
• Required permits or dig authorizations
• Protective system needs
• Competent person assignment — [Competent Person Assignment Requirements]
• Equipment and operator needs
• Soil and water concerns
• Access and egress provisions
• Traffic or area control
• Spoil placement plan
• Emergency response expectations
• Contractor coordination
• Restoration and closeout requirements

Higher-risk excavations may require dig permit, JSA, task risk assessment, engineering review, utility owner coordination, confined space review, EHS review, and supervisor or area owner approval.

No excavation work shall begin until required planning and approvals are complete.

**7.4 UNDERGROUND UTILITY LOCATE AND EXPOSURE CONTROL**

Underground installations shall be identified before excavation begins.

Requirements:
• Contact required utility locate / one-call services where applicable
• Review site utility drawings, maps, and records where available
• Coordinate with utility owners or internal facility personnel as needed
• Mark identified utilities where possible
• Use non-destructive means where required to verify location — potholing, vacuum excavation, ground-penetrating radar
• Protect exposed underground installations during work
• Support, isolate, or deenergize utilities where necessary and authorized

When exact location is uncertain, excavation methods shall be adjusted including hand digging, potholing, vacuum excavation, or other controlled exposure methods.

Employees shall not assume buried utilities are absent simply because no visible indication exists. Work shall stop immediately if unknown utilities or unexpected buried objects are encountered.

**7.5 SURFACE ENCUMBRANCES AND ADJACENT STRUCTURES**

Hazards from surface encumbrances and adjacent structures shall be addressed before excavation begins.

Requirements:
• Remove or secure surface obstructions
• Support or protect poles, structures, sidewalks, foundations, and equipment affected by excavation
• Evaluate surcharge loads near excavation edges
• Restrict vehicle and equipment approach distances
• Use additional protective systems where adjacent loads increase risk

Excavations shall not undermine structures or supports unless the condition has been evaluated and controlled through engineering review or competent person assessment.

**7.6 SOIL CLASSIFICATION AND GROUND CONDITION ASSESSMENT**

Soil and ground conditions shall be assessed by a competent person or other qualified method.

Assessment shall consider:
• Soil type and cohesiveness
• Evidence of previously disturbed soil
• Layering, fissures, and cracks
• Water intrusion and saturation
• Vibration effects
• Weather effects — freeze/thaw, rain saturation, drying
• Adjacent loads and surcharge
• Signs of sloughing or movement
• Stability of excavation faces

Soil conditions may change during the work and protective system decisions shall be updated accordingly. When soil classification is uncertain or conditions are degraded, the more conservative protective approach shall be used.

**7.7 PROTECTIVE SYSTEMS**

Employees shall be protected from cave-ins by an adequate protective system unless the excavation is made entirely in stable rock or another specific condition allowed under applicable rules — [Protective System Approval Requirements].

Protective systems include:
• Sloping — cutting excavation walls at safe angles
• Benching — creating horizontal steps in excavation walls
• Shoring — hydraulic, mechanical, or timber support systems
• Shielding — trench boxes, trench shields, and other engineered structures
• Other approved engineered systems

Selection shall consider excavation depth and dimensions, soil classification, water conditions, adjacent loads, vibration, nearby structures, utility conflicts, work activity, and worker positioning.

Protective systems shall be installed, used, and maintained in accordance with applicable requirements and manufacturer or engineering guidance.

Employees shall remain within the protection of the system when exposure to cave-in exists.

**7.8 MEANS OF ACCESS AND EGRESS**

Safe means of access and egress shall be provided where employees enter trenches or excavations as required.

Requirements:
• Ladders, ramps, stairs, or other safe means of egress where required by depth and conditions
• Placement so travel distance is controlled as required by applicable standards
• Ladders extending to an acceptable height above the landing surface where required
• Access kept clear and stable at all times
• Employees shall use only designated access and egress points

Prohibited practices:
• Jumping into excavations
• Climbing on protective systems improperly
• Using spoil piles or equipment as a means of entry or exit

**7.9 SPOIL PILES, MATERIALS, AND EQUIPMENT NEAR EXCAVATION EDGES**

Spoil, tools, materials, and equipment shall be kept back from excavation edges as required to reduce collapse and falling object hazards.

Requirements:
• Spoil piles placed at least the required minimum distance from the excavation edge unless an equivalent protective method is used
• Loose rock, soil, tools, and materials controlled to prevent fall-in hazard
• Equipment kept back to avoid surcharge loading or edge failure
• Heavy equipment movement near edges controlled by planning and spotter use
• Protective systems not overloaded by materials or equipment
• Spoil placement shall consider drainage and runoff so water is not directed into the excavation

**7.10 WATER ACCUMULATION, DRAINAGE, AND DEWATERING**

Excavation work shall account for water accumulation, seepage, runoff, and saturation hazards.

Controls include:
• Surface water diversion
• Pumping and dewatering systems
• Drainage control
• Soil stabilization
• Inspection following rain or seepage events
• Work suspension during unsafe water conditions

Employees shall not work in excavations where water accumulation creates uncontrolled risk unless appropriate precautions and protective measures are in place under competent person oversight.

Water conditions shall be reevaluated after rainfall, storms, or other changes.

**7.11 HAZARDOUS ATMOSPHERES AND CONFINED CONDITIONS**

Excavations shall be evaluated for hazardous atmospheres, especially when:
• Oxygen deficiency may occur
• Toxic gases or vapors may be present
• Flammable atmospheres may be present
• The excavation is near utilities, process systems, sewers, fuel sources, waste areas, or contaminated ground
• The excavation is deep, enclosed, or poorly ventilated

Atmospheric testing, ventilation, respiratory protection review, and confined space evaluation shall be performed where required by the hazard assessment or applicable rules.

Employees shall not enter excavations with known or suspected hazardous atmospheres unless required controls are in place.

**7.12 BARRICADES, AREA PROTECTION, AND PUBLIC/WORKER EXPOSURE CONTROL**

Excavations shall be protected to prevent unauthorized entry, falls, equipment intrusion, or adjacent work exposure.

Controls include:
• Barricades and hard barriers
• Warning tape and signage
• Covers where appropriate and designed for the load
• Flagging and spotters
• Lighting for low-visibility conditions
• Traffic control measures

Area protection shall consider employees, contractors, vehicles, mobile equipment, and pedestrians who may be exposed to the excavation hazard.

**7.13 MOBILE EQUIPMENT AND FALLING LOAD HAZARDS**

Work around excavation edges and trench entry points shall be controlled for interaction with mobile equipment and suspended or moving loads.

Controls include:
• Keeping unnecessary equipment away from trench edges
• Using spotters where visibility is limited
• Preventing employees from working under suspended loads
• Controlling dump or swing hazards from excavators
• Using stop blocks or warning systems where equipment must approach edges
• Coordinating operator and ground worker communication

Employees shall not work in positions where equipment movement, bucket travel, or material discharge creates struck-by risk.

**7.14 INSPECTIONS BY COMPETENT PERSON**

A competent person shall inspect excavations, adjacent areas, and protective systems as required before employee exposure begins and as conditions change.

Inspection shall cover:
• Evidence of potential cave-in, soil movement, or cracking
• Water accumulation
• Protective system condition
• Spoil placement and equipment proximity
• Utility exposure condition
• Access and egress availability
• Barricades and area controls
• Atmospheric concerns where applicable
• Effects of rain, wind, vibration, or nearby operations

Inspections shall be performed after rainstorms, freeze/thaw changes, water intrusion, vibration changes, nearby heavy equipment activity, or protective system movement or damage.

Employees shall not enter or remain in excavations that the competent person determines unsafe.

**7.15 NIGHT WORK, WEATHER, AND CHANGING CONDITIONS**

Excavation work in low light, severe weather, or deteriorating ground conditions shall receive additional review and controls.

Additional controls include:
• Expanded lighting
• Expanded barricades
• Reduced work scope
• Increased inspection frequency
• Suspension of work
• Additional spotters
• Water and mud management
• Weather-specific communication and stop-work thresholds

Excavation work shall stop when visibility, ground stability, weather, or environmental conditions create uncontrolled risk.

**7.16 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION**

Coordination is required when contractors perform excavation or trenching work onsite.

Coordination shall include:
• Scope and location of excavation
• Utility locate responsibilities
• Permit and approval requirements
• Competent person assignment
• Protective system use
• Traffic and area control
• Emergency response expectations
• Soil, water, and atmospheric concerns
• Reporting and stop-work requirements

Contractors shall not begin excavation or allow employee entry into trenches without required coordination, permits, and controls.

**7.17 INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW**

Immediate reporting is required for any cave-in, soil movement, utility strike, water intrusion event, protective system failure, equipment intrusion, near miss, or unsafe condition related to excavation or trenching.

Investigations shall evaluate:
• Planning adequacy
• Utility locate and verification process
• Soil and condition assessment
• Protective system selection and use
• Competent person inspection quality
• Spoil placement and edge loading
• Access/egress adequacy
• Area control effectiveness
• Training adequacy
• Supervision and contractor coordination
• Need for engineering, procedural, or training improvements

EHS and operational leadership shall periodically review the Excavation and Trenching Safety Program for effectiveness and update requirements as needed based on incidents, audits, and lessons learned.

**APPENDIX A — EXCAVATION PRE-PLANNING CHECKLIST**

☐ Scope and exact location identified
☐ Dig permit or approval obtained where required
☐ Underground utility locate process completed
☐ Competent person assigned
☐ Soil and ground condition concerns identified
☐ Protective system needs determined

☐ Access and egress provisions defined
☐ Barricades and area controls addressed
☐ Water, weather, and emergency response concerns reviewed

☐ Simultaneous operations reviewed
☐ Restoration and closeout requirements identified

Reviewed By: _____________________ Date: ___________

**APPENDIX B — COMPETENT PERSON INSPECTION CHECKLIST**

☐ Excavation walls and faces show no unsafe movement or cracking
☐ Protective system installed and in acceptable condition
☐ Spoil piles and equipment kept back appropriately
☐ Access and egress safe and available
☐ Water accumulation controlled
☐ Utility exposures protected
☐ Barricades and warning controls maintained

☐ Weather, vibration, or adjacent activity has not created new hazards

☐ Soil conditions remain consistent with protective system selection
☐ Edge loading from vehicles or equipment is controlled

Inspection Frequency: Before employee exposure and after any condition change
Inspector: _____________________ Date/Time: ___________
Conditions Acceptable: ☐ Yes ☐ No — Corrective actions: ___________

**APPENDIX C — UTILITY LOCATE AND EXPOSURE CHECKLIST**

☐ Utility owners or locate services contacted as required
☐ Site drawings or records reviewed where available
☐ Utility markings present or verification methods defined
☐ Hand digging, potholing, or other controlled methods planned where needed
☐ Exposed utilities protected and supported as required
☐ Work stops if unknown utilities or unexpected buried objects are encountered
☐ Coordination with internal facility personnel completed
☐ Utility type and approximate location communicated to excavation crew

Locate Ticket / Reference No.: ___________
Verified By: _____________________ Date: ___________

**APPENDIX D — TRENCH ENTRY CHECKLIST**

☐ Protective system in place where required
☐ Competent person has inspected the trench
☐ Access and egress available
☐ Spoil and materials properly positioned
☐ Equipment and suspended load hazards controlled
☐ Water accumulation is not creating uncontrolled risk
☐ Atmospheric concerns addressed where required
☐ Barricades and adjacent area controls in place
☐ Employees understand stop-work triggers
☐ Communication with equipment operators established
☐ Emergency response access confirmed

Competent Person Approval: _____________________ Date/Time: ___________

**APPENDIX E — CONDITIONS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE STOP WORK**

Work shall stop immediately and employees shall exit the excavation when any of the following conditions exist:

• Underground utility conditions are uncertain or uncontrolled
• Protective systems are missing, damaged, displaced, or inadequate
• Soil movement, cracking, sloughing, or cave-in indicators are observed
• Water accumulation or seepage creates uncontrolled risk
• Required access and egress are not available
• Hazardous atmospheric conditions are suspected or identified and remain uncontrolled
• Spoil, materials, or equipment create unsafe edge loading or falling hazard
• A utility strike, near miss, collapse event, or unsafe condition occurs and remains uncorrected
• A competent person, supervisor, EHS, or other responsible person stops the work
• Conditions change such that safe excavation or trench entry cannot be assured

Response Protocol:
1. Stop all work immediately
2. Evacuate all personnel from the excavation
3. Secure the area to prevent re-entry
4. Notify the competent person, supervisor, and EHS immediately
5. Do not resume work until the condition is corrected and the competent person authorizes re-entry

All personnel have the right and responsibility to stop work without fear of reprisal.

emergencyResponse: Cave-in or collapse — evacuate all personnel immediately, call emergency services, do not attempt entry rescue without trained rescue personnel and equipment, secure the area, account for all workers, notify supervision and EHS
Utility strike — evacuate the immediate area, do not touch damaged utilities, shut down equipment if safe, call emergency services and utility owner, secure the area, account for all personnel
Water intrusion or flooding — evacuate the excavation immediately, shut down pumps only if safe, secure the area from re-entry, notify the competent person and supervision
Hazardous atmosphere — evacuate the excavation, move to fresh air, call emergency services if exposure occurred, do not re-enter without atmospheric testing and ventilation
Protective system failure — evacuate the excavation immediately, secure the area, notify the competent person and supervision, do not re-enter until the system is repaired or replaced and inspected
Injury or medical emergency — call for medical assistance, provide first aid if trained, do not move injured persons trapped in soil unless rescue-trained, notify supervision and EHS
Near miss or unsafe condition — stop work, evacuate exposed personnel, notify competent person and supervision, do not resume until condition is corrected
