title: Heat and Cold Stress Prevention Program
summary: A comprehensive Heat and Cold Stress Prevention Program covering environmental monitoring, acclimatization, hydration, work/rest cycles, recovery areas, PPE considerations, buddy systems, symptom recognition, emergency response, contractor coordination, and program review aligned with OSHA guidance, NIOSH criteria, and ACGIH TLVs.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, evaluating, controlling, and safely managing employee exposure to environmental heat stress and cold stress in workplace operations at [Company Name].

This program is designed to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, heat rash, heat cramps, heat exhaustion, heat stroke, dehydration, cold stress, frostnip, frostbite, hypothermia, reduced dexterity, reduced judgment, slips and falls, vehicle incidents, equipment damage, property damage, and operational disruption resulting from high or low environmental temperatures, humidity, radiant heat, wind, inadequate acclimatization, inappropriate clothing, poor hydration, prolonged exposure, strenuous work, inadequate warming or cooling opportunities, failure to recognize symptoms, and ineffective work/rest controls.

[Company Name] is committed to ensuring that all work performed in heat- or cold-stress environments is planned, monitored, and supervised to the standard required to protect every person who may be exposed to environmental temperature hazards.

COMPLIANCE DISCLAIMER: This SOP template is provided as operational guidance and does not replace applicable federal, state, or local regulatory requirements, consensus standards, or specific internal company procedures. [Company Name] is responsible for ensuring compliance with all applicable regulations and adapting this template to site-specific conditions.
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, field locations, production areas, fabrication areas, maintenance activities, warehouses, utilities, construction support activities, laboratories, outdoor operations, indoor hot work areas, refrigerated or cold work areas, and non-routine operations where employees or contractors may be exposed to hazardous heat or cold conditions at [Site Name].

It applies to:
• Employees who work in environments where heat or cold stress may occur
• Supervisors and managers responsible for work performed in temperature-stress environments
• EHS personnel
• Engineers, technicians, mechanics, operators, maintenance personnel, inspectors, utility personnel, field personnel, and support personnel
• Contractors and subcontractors performing work in heat- or cold-stress environments onsite

Covered work environments include:
• Outdoor work areas and hot/cold weather operations
• Roofs and elevated exterior work areas
• Test and launch support areas
• Confined or poorly ventilated areas
• Hot process areas, mechanical rooms, boiler or utility areas
• Areas with radiant heat exposure
• Kitchens, curing, drying, or thermal processing areas where applicable
• Refrigerated areas, freezer areas, cryogenic-adjacent environments
• Wind-exposed work areas and remote field work locations

Covered activities include construction support, maintenance and repair, outdoor inspections, material handling, excavation and field work, working at heights in exposed environments, non-routine tasks during extreme temperatures, high-exertion work, tasks requiring heavy or layered PPE, vehicle/equipment/utility support work, and emergency response support.

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with PPE, Respiratory Protection, Confined Space Entry, Non-Routine Activities, Contractor Safety Management, Emergency Response, Working at Heights, Electrical Safety, and applicable site operational controls where required.

definitions.definition: The gradual physiological adaptation of the body to work in hot or cold environments over a period of days, during which the body improves its ability to regulate temperature, manage heat loss or retention, and sustain work performance under environmental stress.

The condition that occurs when the body is unable to maintain adequate core temperature due to cold environmental conditions, wind exposure, wet clothing, or inadequate protection, potentially leading to frostnip, frostbite, hypothermia, or impaired physical and cognitive function.
A measure combining air temperature and relative humidity to estimate how hot it actually feels to the human body, used as a factor in determining heat stress risk and required controls.
Painful muscle spasms, usually in the abdomen, arms, or legs, caused by loss of electrolytes through heavy sweating during strenuous work in hot conditions.

A heat-related illness caused by excessive loss of water and salt through sweating, characterized by heavy sweating, weakness, nausea, dizziness, headache, cool and clammy skin, and elevated pulse. Heat exhaustion can progress to heat stroke if not treated.

A life-threatening medical emergency in which the body's temperature regulation fails, core body temperature rises to dangerous levels, and the person may experience confusion, altered consciousness, seizures, hot and dry skin, and organ failure. Heat stroke requires immediate emergency medical response.

The combination of environmental heat, metabolic heat from physical work, and clothing or PPE effects that creates a thermal load on the body, potentially exceeding the body's ability to cool itself and maintain safe core temperature.
Heat energy emitted from hot surfaces, equipment, processes, or the sun that transfers to the body without direct contact, increasing the thermal load on exposed workers.

A designated location where employees can rest and recover from heat or cold exposure, equipped with appropriate cooling, warming, hydration, or shelter resources to support safe return to normal body temperature.
A measure of the combined cooling effect of air temperature and wind speed on exposed skin, used to evaluate the risk of cold stress, frostnip, and frostbite.

A planned schedule alternating periods of work and rest to manage cumulative heat or cold stress exposure, reduce the risk of temperature-related illness, and allow the body to recover between work periods.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Develop, implement, maintain, and periodically review the Heat and Cold Stress Prevention Program. Provide technical guidance on environmental monitoring, trigger levels, acclimatization, and work/rest schedules. Coordinate training for supervisors, employees, and contractors. Review environmental exposure incidents and near misses. Monitor regulatory and consensus standard changes. Conduct periodic audits of temperature-stress work practices. Maintain program records.

Ensure heat and cold stress hazards are assessed and controlled before work begins. Monitor environmental conditions throughout the shift and adjust controls as conditions change. Enforce hydration, work/rest, acclimatization, and recovery requirements. Ensure employees are trained and aware of symptoms and emergency actions. Assign buddy systems or monitoring where required. Stop work when conditions exceed safe limits. Investigate and report temperature-stress incidents and near misses.

Follow all heat and cold stress prevention requirements. Maintain hydration and use recovery areas as directed. Wear required clothing and PPE appropriate for conditions. Report symptoms in themselves or coworkers immediately. Participate in buddy monitoring. Use stop-work authority when conditions are unsafe. Participate in required training.

rolesAndResponsibilities.role: EHS / Occupational Health / Medical Support Personnel
rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Provide medical guidance on acclimatization, fitness for duty in temperature-stress environments, and return-to-work following heat or cold illness. Support emergency medical response planning. Advise on environmental monitoring methods and trigger levels. Assist with medical evaluation of affected employees.

Provide engineering controls such as shade structures, ventilation, radiant shielding, cooling stations, heated shelters, wind barriers, and insulated enclosures. Support environmental monitoring equipment. Maintain recovery area infrastructure. Evaluate indoor process heat and artificial cold environments.

Comply with [Company Name] heat and cold stress prevention requirements when working onsite. Ensure contractor employees are trained, acclimatized, hydrated, and monitored. Provide required clothing and PPE. Coordinate with site supervision on environmental conditions, work/rest expectations, and emergency response. Report temperature-stress incidents and symptoms immediately. Stop work when conditions are unsafe.

training: Recognition of heat stress and cold stress hazards and contributing factors
Early and severe symptom recognition for heat cramps, heat exhaustion, heat stroke, frostnip, frostbite, and hypothermia
Hydration requirements and recovery expectations
Acclimatization principles and expectations
Clothing and PPE considerations for temperature-stress environments
Work/rest planning and schedule compliance
Buddy system and employee monitoring responsibilities
Environmental monitoring and trigger level awareness
Emergency response expectations and first aid actions
Incident and near-miss reporting procedures
Stop-work authority and obligation
ppe: Lightweight, breathable clothing where task-compatible for heat stress environments
Cooling garments or accessories (cooling vests, neck wraps) where appropriate
Sun-protective clothing, hats, neck protection, and shade aids
Layered clothing systems with moisture management for cold stress environments
Wind-resistant outerwear for cold and wind exposure
Insulated gloves appropriate for the task and temperature
Head, face, and neck protection for cold environments
Insulated footwear for cold work areas
Safety glasses or goggles as required by the primary task hazard
FR clothing where required — with work/rest adjustment for added heat burden
Respiratory protection where required — with work/rest adjustment for added thermal load
hazards: Heat rash, heat cramps, heat exhaustion, and heat stroke from environmental and metabolic heat exposure
Dehydration from inadequate fluid intake during hot conditions or heavy exertion
Frostnip, frostbite, and hypothermia from cold exposure, wind, and wet conditions
Reduced dexterity, grip strength, and fine motor control from cold hands and extremities
Impaired judgment, confusion, and reduced alertness from heat stroke or hypothermia
Slips, trips, and falls from ice, condensation, reduced coordination, or fatigue
Vehicle and equipment incidents from impaired alertness or reduced reaction time
Increased injury risk from heavy or restrictive PPE in hot environments
Respiratory stress from respirator use combined with heat exposure
Delayed symptom recognition in remote, isolated, or lone-worker conditions
Radiant heat exposure from hot process equipment, surfaces, or direct sunlight
Wind chill and exposed skin injury during outdoor cold weather operations
Fatigue and reduced work capacity from prolonged temperature-stress exposure
Compounding hazards from confined spaces, elevated work, or high-exertion tasks during temperature extremes
procedure:
**7.1 HEAT AND COLD STRESS HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND ASSESSMENT**

Before work begins in environments where heat or cold stress may occur, the task, environment, clothing requirements, and workforce condition shall be evaluated.

Assessment factors include:
• Air temperature, relative humidity, heat index, wind chill, wind speed
• Radiant heat, sun exposure or shade availability
• Indoor versus outdoor conditions
• Duration of exposure and workload or physical exertion level
• Type of clothing or PPE required and its thermal burden
• Availability of recovery areas, hydration, and warming or cooling resources
• Acclimatization status of workers
• Presence of medical or personal risk factors as appropriately managed
• Remote or isolated work conditions
• Emergency response access
• Recent and forecasted weather trends
• Work area ventilation or shelter condition

If heat or cold stress hazards cannot be adequately controlled, the work shall not proceed until additional controls are implemented or conditions improve.

**7.2 HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS FOR HEAT AND COLD STRESS**

The preferred order of control is:
1. Eliminate exposure by changing task timing, location, or method where feasible
2. Reduce exposure through work scheduling, reduced exertion, mechanization, or rotation
3. Use engineering controls such as shade, shelters, cooling stations, ventilation, heating, wind barriers, insulated enclosures, or radiant shielding
4. Use administrative controls such as work/rest cycles, hydration plans, acclimatization, buddy systems, and enhanced supervision as supplemental controls
5. Use PPE and clothing as a supporting measure, not as a substitute for proper exposure control

Whenever feasible, work shall be scheduled or modified to reduce the most severe temperature-stress exposure periods.

[Company Name] will ensure that engineering and administrative controls are evaluated and implemented before relying on individual tolerance or protective clothing alone.

**7.3 ENVIRONMENTAL MONITORING AND TRIGGER CONDITIONS**

Environmental conditions shall be monitored or otherwise evaluated when heat or cold exposure may create risk.

Monitoring methods include:
• Local weather data and forecasts
• Site-specific heat index values
• Wind chill estimates
• Wet bulb globe temperature (WBGT) or equivalent heat stress evaluation where applicable
• Observations of sun exposure, humidity, wind, and radiant heat
• Real-time field conditions reported by supervisors and workers
• Trend forecasts for worsening temperatures during the shift

Site-specific trigger levels — [Heat Alert Trigger Levels] and [Cold Weather Trigger Levels] — shall define thresholds for:
• Additional hydration requirements
• Increased break frequency and duration
• Reduced work duration or modified tasks
• Acclimatization precautions for new or returning workers
• Elevated supervision and monitoring
• Work stoppage or postponement
• Mandatory warming or cooling breaks
• Restricted use of heavy PPE or high-exertion tasks

Supervisors and EHS shall adjust controls when environmental conditions worsen during the shift. Monitoring shall continue throughout the work period, not only at the start of the day.

**7.4 ACCLIMATIZATION**

Employees exposed to hot conditions shall be given time to acclimatize, especially when:
• They are new to hot work environments
• Returning from absence of one week or more
• Temperatures rise suddenly or unexpectedly
• Work intensity increases significantly
• Heavy or restrictive PPE is required

Acclimatization shall involve gradual increase in exposure and workload over a period appropriate to the task and conditions. NIOSH guidance recommends up to 7–14 days for full acclimatization for new workers, and at least 3–4 days for experienced workers returning from absence.

Employees exposed to cold conditions may also require gradual adjustment to extended low-temperature work and use of bulky or layered protective clothing.

Unacclimatized employees shall receive closer supervision, more conservative work/rest controls, and additional check-ins during the acclimatization period.

Acclimatization progress shall not be assumed — supervisors shall observe workers for signs of intolerance throughout the adjustment period.

**7.5 WORK PLANNING AND SCHEDULING**

Heat and cold stress hazards shall be considered during task planning.

Planning actions include:
• Scheduling strenuous work during cooler or safer periods of the day
• Delaying non-essential work during extreme conditions
• Rotating employees to reduce individual exposure duration
• Increasing crew size to reduce individual workload
• Using mechanical aids to reduce physical exertion
• Limiting duration of continuous exposure
• Reducing direct sun or wind exposure through positioning and timing
• Coordinating transportation and access to recovery areas
• Adjusting production expectations during adverse environmental conditions

High-risk or non-routine work performed during extreme environmental conditions may require additional review and approval by supervision and EHS before proceeding.

[Work/Rest Schedule Guidance] shall be applied based on environmental conditions, workload intensity, and clothing or PPE factors.

**7.6 HYDRATION, ELECTROLYTES, AND NUTRITION**

Employees working in heat stress environments shall have access to adequate potable drinking water.

Hydration requirements include:
• Water available in sufficient quantity and accessible location — [Hydration Requirements]
• Encouragement of frequent hydration throughout the shift, not only when thirsty
• Replacement of fluids before, during, and after exposure periods
• Consideration of electrolyte replacement when work duration, sweating rate, and conditions warrant
• Avoidance of reliance on thirst alone as the only indicator of hydration need
• Supervisor attention to employees who are not drinking adequately during hot conditions

Employees working in cold environments shall also maintain hydration and nutrition, as cold exposure and layered clothing can contribute to dehydration and fatigue that may not be immediately obvious.

Alcohol or substance use that impairs temperature regulation, awareness, or safe work judgment is prohibited consistent with company policy.

Water sources shall be clean, replenished regularly, and located within reasonable access of the work area.

**7.7 RECOVERY AREAS, SHADE, SHELTER, AND WARMING/COOLING STATIONS**

Recovery areas shall be provided as appropriate to environmental conditions and work — [Recovery Area Requirements].

For heat stress recovery:
• Shade structures or shaded areas
• Air-conditioned spaces where available
• Fans or air movement where appropriate
• Cooling stations with water, ice, or cooling accessories
• Vehicles with climate control where appropriate and safe to use
• Cool-down rest opportunities during and between work periods

For cold stress recovery:
• Heated shelters or warm-up areas
• Wind-protected locations
• Dry clothing storage or change-out locations
• Warm beverages where appropriate and safe

Recovery areas shall be accessible and sufficient for the crew size and work duration involved. Recovery breaks shall not be treated as optional when trigger conditions are met.

Employees shall be encouraged to use recovery areas proactively, not only after symptoms develop.

**7.8 CLOTHING AND PPE CONSIDERATIONS**

Clothing and PPE shall be selected with consideration for temperature stress and task hazards.

For heat stress:
• Lightweight, breathable garments where task-compatible
• Cooling garments or accessories (cooling vests, neck wraps) where appropriate
• Sun-protective clothing, hats, neck protection, and shade aids where appropriate
• Evaluation of the added heat burden created by flame-resistant clothing, chemical PPE, or respiratory protection
• Adjustment of work/rest schedules when heavy PPE increases thermal load

For cold stress:
• Layered clothing systems allowing moisture management and adjustable insulation
• Wind-resistant outerwear
• Insulated gloves appropriate for the task
• Head, face, and neck protection
• Insulated footwear
• Dry change-out clothing where needed

Clothing shall remain compatible with the primary task hazards and shall not create additional entanglement, visibility, ignition, electrical, or other safety risks.

PPE selection shall balance thermal protection with operational safety — neither temperature comfort nor task PPE requirements shall be sacrificed without proper evaluation.

**7.9 BUDDY SYSTEM, MONITORING, AND SUPERVISOR OVERSIGHT**

Employees working in heat or cold stress environments shall be monitored for symptoms and changing condition, especially during elevated-risk periods.

Monitoring methods include:
• Buddy system or team monitoring — workers observe each other for symptoms
• Direct supervisor observation and regular check-ins
• Increased check-in frequency during extreme conditions or high-exertion tasks
• Work pacing oversight to prevent overexertion
• Closer monitoring of new, unacclimatized, or returning employees
• Remote worker communication checks at defined intervals

Supervisors shall not rely solely on self-reporting when conditions are extreme or symptoms may impair judgment. Heat stroke and hypothermia can impair the ability to recognize and report one's own condition.

Buddy system expectations and symptom recognition shall be communicated to all workers before work begins in elevated-risk conditions.

**7.10 HEAT STRESS RECOGNITION AND IMMEDIATE RESPONSE**

Early recognition of heat stress signs is critical. Signs and symptoms include:
• Excessive sweating or cessation of sweating
• Fatigue, weakness, and reduced work capacity
• Headache, dizziness, and lightheadedness
• Nausea and vomiting
• Muscle cramps
• Confusion, irritability, and altered behavior
• Unsteady movement and loss of coordination
• Rapid pulse and shallow breathing
• Hot, red, dry skin (late-stage heat stroke indicator)

If heat stress symptoms are suspected:
1. Stop work immediately
2. Move the affected person to a cooler or shaded area
3. Loosen or remove unnecessary PPE or heavy clothing as appropriate
4. Provide cool drinking water if the person is conscious and able to drink safely
5. Use active cooling measures — cold water, ice packs to neck/armpits/groin, fan/mist cooling
6. Notify supervision immediately
7. Escalate emergency medical response when symptoms are severe, worsening, or consistent with heat stroke

**Heat stroke is a medical emergency requiring immediate emergency response. Do not delay calling for emergency help.**

The affected person shall not be left alone until evaluated and cleared by medical personnel.

**7.11 COLD STRESS RECOGNITION AND IMMEDIATE RESPONSE**

Recognition of cold stress signs is essential. Signs and symptoms include:
• Shivering (early warning — absence of shivering in severe cold is a danger sign)
• Numbness, tingling, and loss of sensation in extremities
• Reduced dexterity and grip strength
• Confusion, slurred speech, and impaired judgment
• Clumsiness and loss of coordination
• Pale, waxy, or blue-gray skin
• Fatigue and drowsiness
• Stiff muscles and slow movement

If cold stress symptoms are suspected:
1. Stop work immediately
2. Move the affected person to a warm or protected area
3. Remove wet clothing where appropriate and safe
4. Provide gradual warming using blankets, warm beverages (if conscious), warm packs to core areas
5. Notify supervision immediately
6. Escalate emergency medical response when symptoms are severe, worsening, or consistent with hypothermia or frostbite

Rapid rewarming methods shall only be used when appropriate and consistent with emergency response expectations. Improper rewarming of frostbitten tissue can cause additional damage.

The affected person shall not be left alone and shall not return to cold exposure until medically evaluated.

**7.12 HIGH-RISK TASKS AND SPECIAL CONDITIONS**

Additional controls shall be implemented for work involving:
• Heavy exertion, direct sunlight, or hot process equipment
• Reflective surfaces increasing radiant heat
• High humidity reducing evaporative cooling
• Full-body or restrictive PPE (chemical suits, FR clothing, full-face respirators)
• Wind exposure during cold conditions
• Wet clothing or precipitation
• Elevated work or remote/isolated work
• Confined or enclosed environments with limited airflow
• Night work during cold conditions
• Lone work with delayed emergency response access
• Multiple simultaneous stressors (heat + PPE + exertion)

High-risk temperature-stress tasks shall receive enhanced planning and supervision. Work/rest schedules shall be adjusted more conservatively for high-risk combinations.

Supervisors shall evaluate whether the combined risk factors warrant postponing, modifying, or adding controls to the planned work.

**7.13 INDOOR HEAT SOURCES AND ARTIFICIAL COLD ENVIRONMENTS**

Indoor work areas shall be evaluated for heat and cold stress due to process conditions or artificial environments.

Indoor heat sources include:
• Mechanical or boiler rooms
• Equipment rooms with high heat output
• Hot fabrication spaces (welding, forging, casting)
• Radiant heat process areas
• Ovens, furnaces, curing chambers, and drying areas
• Steam or hot water system areas

Artificial cold environments include:
• Refrigerated warehouses and cold storage facilities
• Freezers and blast freezers
• Controlled environmental chambers
• Cryogenic-adjacent work areas

Indoor work shall be evaluated for airflow, radiant heat, humidity, and exposure duration with the same rigor as outdoor work. Indoor environments can create heat or cold stress conditions that are less visible but equally hazardous.

Ventilation, cooling, heating, and recovery area requirements apply to indoor temperature-stress environments.

**7.14 EMERGENCY RESPONSE AND MEDICAL EVALUATION**

Emergency response expectations for heat and cold stress shall be defined before work begins in elevated-risk conditions.

Emergency planning shall include:
• How to summon assistance — phone numbers, radio channels, alarm systems
• Site-specific emergency contacts and medical resources
• Location of recovery areas and first aid supplies
• Transportation arrangements to medical facilities where relevant
• Access arrangements for emergency responders, especially at remote or field locations
• Elevated attention for remote work, elevated work, and confined space work during temperature extremes

Employees showing signs of serious heat illness or serious cold-related illness shall receive prompt medical evaluation.

Employees shall not return to work after a significant heat or cold stress event until evaluated and cleared consistent with site medical and supervisory requirements.

All emergency responses shall be documented and reviewed as part of the incident reporting process.

**7.15 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION**

Coordination is required when contractors perform work in heat- or cold-stress environments onsite.

Coordination shall address:
• Environmental conditions and forecasted changes
• Work/rest expectations and schedule adjustments
• Hydration access or warming/cooling area access
• Clothing and PPE expectations
• Elevated-risk tasks and additional controls
• Emergency response expectations and contacts
• Reporting and stop-work requirements
• Supervisor communication and employee monitoring expectations

Contractors shall not continue work under uncontrolled heat or cold stress conditions.

[Company Name] shall communicate site-specific trigger levels, recovery area locations, and emergency procedures to contractor supervision before work begins.

**7.16 INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW**

Immediate reporting is required for any heat-related illness, cold-related illness, dehydration event, near miss, environmental exposure concern, symptom report, or unsafe condition related to heat or cold stress.

Investigations shall evaluate:
• Environmental conditions at the time of the event
• Planning adequacy — were conditions assessed and controls defined?
• Work/rest schedule effectiveness
• Hydration and recovery access
• Acclimatization status and history
• Clothing and PPE considerations
• Supervision and monitoring adequacy
• Communication and emergency response effectiveness
• Training adequacy
• Need for engineering, procedural, or training improvements

EHS and operational leadership shall periodically review the Heat and Cold Stress Prevention Program for effectiveness and update requirements as needed based on incidents, weather trends, audits, regulatory changes, and lessons learned.

Program reviews shall include evaluation of trigger levels, work/rest schedules, recovery area adequacy, monitoring practices, and training effectiveness.
emergencyResponse: Heat stroke is a medical emergency — call emergency services immediately, do not delay
Move affected person to cooler or warmer area immediately based on condition
Remove unnecessary PPE or wet clothing as appropriate
Provide cool water for heat stress or gradual warming for cold stress if person is conscious
Use active cooling measures for heat emergencies — ice packs to neck, armpits, groin
Do not use rapid rewarming methods for frostbite without medical guidance
Do not leave affected person alone — monitor continuously until medical help arrives
Notify supervision and EHS immediately for any temperature-stress medical event
Document conditions, symptoms, timeline, and actions taken
Employee shall not return to work until medically evaluated and cleared

title: Noise Exposure and Hearing Conservation Program
summary: Enterprise-grade program for identifying, evaluating, controlling, and managing employee exposure to hazardous occupational noise. Covers exposure assessment, engineering and administrative controls, hearing protection selection and use, audiometric testing, posted noise areas, impulse noise, communication and alarm considerations, equipment condition, contractor coordination, and incident reporting. Includes five operational appendices with pre-planning, HPD use, noise area inspection, threshold shift response, and stop-work checklists.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, evaluating, controlling, and safely managing employee exposure to hazardous occupational noise and for implementing an effective hearing conservation program where required at [Company Name].

The purpose is to prevent injury, serious injury, permanent hearing loss, temporary threshold shift, tinnitus, reduced situational awareness, communication failures, alarm recognition failures, operational errors, equipment incidents, property damage, and operational disruption resulting from excessive noise exposure, inadequate exposure assessment, lack of engineering or administrative controls, improper selection or use of hearing protection, ineffective training, poor maintenance of noisy equipment, and failure to recognize changing noise hazards.

Occupational noise-induced hearing loss is permanent and irreversible. This program requires systematic noise hazard identification, exposure monitoring, source controls, hearing protection, audiometric program support, training, supervision, and periodic program review to protect all personnel.

This procedure supports compliance with OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95, OSHA 29 CFR 1926.52, applicable NIOSH and ACGIH guidance, and [Company Name] policies.
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, laboratories, production areas, fabrication areas, maintenance activities, test areas, warehouses, utilities, field locations, assembly areas, construction support areas, and non-routine operations where employees or contractors may be exposed to hazardous occupational noise.

It applies to:
• All employees who work in areas with hazardous or potentially hazardous noise exposure
• Supervisors and managers responsible for work performed in noisy environments
• EHS personnel
• Engineers, technicians, mechanics, operators, welders, fabricators, maintenance personnel, inspectors, utilities personnel, laboratory personnel, and support personnel
• Contractors and subcontractors performing work in noisy environments onsite or under company operational control

Work environments and activities include but are not limited to:
• Fabrication and machine shop operations
• Grinding and cutting operations
• Welding and metalworking support areas
• Engine, pump, compressor, and generator areas
• Test stands and test support areas
• Utility plants and mechanical rooms
• Pneumatic tool use
• Heavy equipment and mobile equipment operation
• Construction support activities
• Outdoor field work near high-noise equipment
• Temporary and non-routine activities involving elevated noise
• Impact, impulse, intermittent, and continuous noise environments

Noise sources include but are not limited to:
• Powered hand tools, compressors, blowers, fans, ventilation systems, pumps, turbines, engines, generators, impact tools, metal fabrication equipment, pressurized gas release, test operations, mobile equipment, alarm systems, temporary construction equipment, and other equipment or operations capable of producing hazardous sound levels

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with PPE, Hand and Power Tools, Welding/Cutting/Brazing and Hot Work Permit, Pressurized Systems, Mobile Equipment / PIT Safety, Non-Routine Activities, Contractor Safety Management, Emergency Response, and applicable site operational controls.

definitions.definition: An 8-hour time-weighted average (TWA) of 85 decibels measured on the A-weighted scale (dBA), which triggers hearing conservation program requirements including audiometric testing, hearing protection availability, and training.
A record of hearing ability measured at specific frequencies, used to establish baseline hearing levels and detect changes over time as part of a hearing conservation program.
Steady-state sound that remains at a relatively constant level over a period of time, as opposed to intermittent or impulse noise.
A unit of measurement expressing the intensity of sound. The A-weighted scale (dBA) is used for occupational noise exposure measurement because it approximates human hearing response.
A personal monitoring instrument worn by an employee to measure cumulative noise exposure over a work shift, expressed as a percentage of the allowable dose or as a TWA.
A structured program required when employee noise exposures equal or exceed an 8-hour TWA of 85 dBA, including monitoring, audiometric testing, hearing protection, training, and recordkeeping.
A device worn to reduce the level of sound entering the ear, including earplugs, earmuffs, and banded hearing protectors.
Short-duration, high-intensity sound events such as hammering, metal striking, powder-actuated tool use, or pressure release, which can cause immediate hearing damage even with brief exposure.
A systematic evaluation of workplace noise levels using area measurements, personal dosimetry, or task-based measurements to determine employee exposure and the need for controls.

A laboratory-derived rating in decibels assigned to hearing protection devices indicating their noise-reducing capability under ideal conditions. Field attenuation is typically lower than the rated NRR.
A change in hearing threshold of an average of 10 dB or more at 2000, 3000, and 4000 Hz in either ear relative to the baseline audiogram, indicating potentially significant hearing change.
A temporary reduction in hearing sensitivity following noise exposure that typically recovers after a period of quiet. Repeated temporary shifts may lead to permanent hearing loss.
The average noise exposure level over an 8-hour work shift, calculated from personal dosimetry or representative area measurements, used to determine compliance and program inclusion.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Administer and maintain the Noise Exposure and Hearing Conservation Program. Coordinate noise exposure monitoring and evaluation. Maintain monitoring records and program documentation. Coordinate audiometric testing arrangements where applicable. Review hearing protection selection. Conduct or coordinate program audits and annual reviews. Support engineering and purchasing in evaluating quieter equipment alternatives. Manage training program content and scheduling. Track threshold shift notifications and corrective actions. Coordinate with occupational health and industrial hygiene support as needed.

Ensure employees under their direction comply with hearing conservation requirements. Enforce hearing protection use in posted or designated areas. Monitor employee compliance with work practices and HPD use. Report equipment producing abnormal or increased noise. Ensure new and transferred employees receive required training before noise exposure. Support noise monitoring access and scheduling. Ensure hearing protection supplies are maintained and available. Address communication and alarm audibility concerns in noisy work areas. Investigate and report noise-related incidents, symptoms, and near misses.

Wear required hearing protection properly and consistently in designated areas and during designated tasks. Attend required hearing conservation training. Participate in audiometric testing as required by the program. Report hearing symptoms, tinnitus, communication difficulties, or concerns to supervision. Report abnormally loud equipment or changing noise conditions. Inspect and maintain hearing protection in usable condition. Do not remove hearing protection in posted noise areas. Follow posted area requirements and task-specific noise controls. Exercise stop-work authority when noise conditions are unsafe.

rolesAndResponsibilities.role: EHS / Industrial Hygiene / Occupational Health Support
rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Conduct or coordinate noise exposure monitoring using appropriate methods. Evaluate monitoring results and recommend exposure controls. Review engineering and administrative control effectiveness. Support hearing protection selection and fit guidance. Coordinate audiometric testing and manage threshold shift follow-up. Advise on combined exposure conditions (noise plus heat, respirators, ototoxic agents where applicable). Maintain monitoring and program records. Support incident investigation and corrective action development. Provide technical support for equipment evaluations and noise reduction projects.

Evaluate noise during equipment design, purchase, installation, and modification. Implement engineering noise controls such as enclosures, barriers, silencers, isolation, and damping. Maintain equipment in a condition that minimizes excessive noise. Repair mufflers, housings, bearings, mounts, and other noise-related components promptly. Report and evaluate equipment producing abnormal or escalating noise. Support noise monitoring access and facility modifications for noise reduction. Consider noise in preventive maintenance, replacement, and capital project planning.

Comply with [Company Name] hearing conservation requirements while working onsite. Provide appropriate hearing protection for their personnel in designated noise areas. Coordinate with [Company Name] supervision before introducing equipment that may create new noise hazards. Report noise-related incidents, symptoms, and unsafe conditions. Follow posted noise area requirements, signage, and restricted access controls. Participate in site coordination for temporary high-noise activities.

training: Recognition of occupational noise hazards and common noise sources in the workplace
Effects of noise on hearing — temporary threshold shift, permanent hearing loss, tinnitus, and cumulative damage
Purpose and requirements of the hearing conservation program including action level, monitoring, and audiometric testing
Proper selection, fitting, insertion, use, care, storage, and replacement of hearing protection devices
Limitations of hearing protection — NRR versus field attenuation, proper fit importance, and dual protection requirements
Noise area identification — posted zones, signage, temporary controls, and restricted access expectations
Communication and alarm awareness — hand signals, radios, visual alarms, and planning for noisy environments
Audiometric testing purpose, employee responsibilities, and threshold shift follow-up expectations where applicable
Equipment condition reporting — recognizing abnormal noise as a potential maintenance and safety concern
Combined exposure considerations — noise with heat stress, respirator use, heavy PPE, or ototoxic agents
Incident, symptom, and near-miss reporting requirements
Stop-work authority and obligation when noise conditions create uncontrolled risk
ppe: Disposable earplugs — foam or pre-molded, selected for the noise level, comfort, and task compatibility
Reusable earplugs — flanged or custom-molded, maintained in clean and usable condition
Earmuffs — over-the-ear protection selected for noise level, compatibility with hard hats and other PPE, and comfort
Banded hearing protectors — semi-insert protectors for intermittent noise exposure where convenient donning/removal is needed
Dual protection (earplugs plus earmuffs) — required where noise exposure exceeds levels adequately controlled by a single device
Communication headsets — approved hearing protection with integrated communication where verbal coordination is critical
Eye protection — safety glasses or face shields as required by the concurrent task
Hard hat — where overhead or impact hazards coexist with noise exposure
Additional PPE as required by the concurrent task hazard assessment
hazards: Permanent noise-induced hearing loss from chronic overexposure to hazardous sound levels
Temporary threshold shift from acute noise exposure, potentially leading to permanent damage with repeated events
Tinnitus — persistent ringing or buzzing sensation from noise-damaged hearing
Reduced situational awareness from noise masking environmental cues, equipment sounds, or approaching hazards
Communication failure — inability to hear verbal instructions, radio calls, or team coordination in noisy environments
Alarm recognition failure — inability to hear fire alarms, evacuation signals, equipment warnings, or vehicle backup alarms
Impulse and impact noise damage from hammering, metal striking, powder-actuated tools, pressure release, or test operations
Combined exposure risk — noise plus heat stress, respirator use, heavy PPE, or ototoxic chemical exposure increasing susceptibility
Equipment-related noise hazards from abnormal vibration, bearing failure, loose components, or degraded mufflers
Distraction and startle from unexpected high-noise events leading to dropped tools, missteps, or loss of focus near hazards
Contractor-introduced noise hazards from temporary equipment, construction activities, or unfamiliar operations
procedure:
7.1 NOISE HAZARD IDENTIFICATION AND ASSESSMENT

Before work begins or when new equipment, processes, or conditions are introduced, evaluate the noise hazard profile including: type of equipment or process, expected sound level, continuous/intermittent/impulse noise characteristics, duration and frequency of exposure, number of exposed employees, distance from the source, work area configuration and reflective surfaces, use of multiple noisy tools or simultaneous operations, need for communication/alarm recognition/verbal coordination, use of PPE that may affect hearing protection choice, maintenance condition of equipment, and similar historical monitoring data where available.

If hazardous noise exposure cannot be adequately understood or controlled, the work shall not proceed without appropriate interim protective measures such as hearing protection, restricted access, or reduced exposure duration.

7.2 HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS FOR NOISE EXPOSURE

Noise hazards shall be controlled using the following preferred order:

1. ELIMINATE the noisy task or source where feasible
2. SUBSTITUTE quieter equipment, tooling, or methods
3. ENGINEERING CONTROLS — enclosures, barriers, damping, isolation mounts, mufflers, silencers, acoustic treatment, remote operation, or equipment redesign
4. ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS — exposure time limits, rotation, scheduling noisy work when fewer people are present, restricted access, posted zones, and signage as supplemental controls
5. HEARING PROTECTION — as a supporting measure, not as a substitute for feasible source control

Whenever feasible, noise shall be reduced at the source or along the transmission path before relying solely on hearing protection devices.

7.3 NOISE EXPOSURE MONITORING AND EVALUATION

Noise exposure shall be monitored or otherwise evaluated when:
• New equipment or processes are introduced
• Existing operations change in a manner that may affect noise levels
• Employees report excessive noise or hearing symptoms
• Equipment condition suggests rising noise levels
• Regulatory or program requirements apply
• Historical monitoring is no longer representative of current conditions

Methods may include personal dosimetry, area sound level measurements, task-based measurements, impulse or peak noise evaluation, and observation-based screening followed by formal assessment where needed.

Results shall be reviewed by qualified personnel and used to determine:
• Whether employees must be included in the hearing conservation program
• Whether engineering or administrative controls are needed
• Whether hearing protection is required or must be upgraded
• Whether signage and area controls are needed

Affected employees shall be informed of monitoring results.

7.4 HEARING CONSERVATION PROGRAM INCLUSION

Employees shall be included in the hearing conservation program when their noise exposure equals or exceeds an 8-hour time-weighted average (TWA) of 85 dBA, or when other site policy criteria require inclusion.

The 85 dBA TWA is the action level that triggers hearing conservation program requirements.

Program inclusion requires:
• Baseline and periodic audiometric testing
• Hearing protection availability and required use
• Employee training on noise hazards and controls
• Exposure reassessment as conditions change
• Follow-up actions when threshold shifts or symptoms are identified

Inclusion decisions shall be based on monitoring results, exposure patterns, and applicable program criteria. Employees shall remain in the program as long as their exposure profile warrants continued protection.

7.5 ENGINEERING AND ADMINISTRATIVE NOISE CONTROLS

Feasible engineering and administrative controls shall be implemented to reduce noise exposure.

Engineering controls include:
• Equipment enclosures and acoustic curtains or barriers
• Silencers or mufflers on exhaust or discharge points
• Vibration isolation mounts and damping materials
• Process redesign or remote operation
• Equipment relocation to reduce exposure to occupied areas
• Lower-noise tooling and components
• Preventive maintenance to reduce abnormal noise from worn bearings, loose components, or degraded mufflers

Administrative controls include:
• Limiting time in high-noise areas
• Rotating personnel to reduce individual exposure duration
• Scheduling noisy work when fewer people are present
• Restricting access to high-noise zones
• Establishing and enforcing posted hearing protection areas
• Staggering simultaneous noisy tasks where feasible

Administrative controls shall not be used as the sole long-term substitute for feasible engineering controls where engineering solutions are practical.

7.6 HEARING PROTECTION SELECTION

Hearing protection devices shall be selected based on the nature and level of exposure, compatibility with the task, and user needs.

Selection factors include:
• Noise exposure level and type (continuous vs. impulse)
• Need for verbal communication and alarm recognition
• Other PPE being worn (hard hat, safety glasses, face shield, respirator)
• Environmental conditions (heat, humidity, dust)
• Comfort, fit, and user acceptance
• Hygiene and replacement needs
• NRR and realistic field attenuation

Hearing protection options include:
• Disposable foam earplugs
• Reusable flanged or custom-molded earplugs
• Banded hearing protectors for intermittent use
• Earmuffs — standard or hard-hat-mounted
• Dual protection (earplugs + earmuffs) for exposures exceeding single-device capability
• Communication headsets with integrated hearing protection where verbal coordination is critical

Hearing protection shall not create additional hazards by interfering with required hard hats, eye protection, face shields, communication devices, or task performance. Multiple types and sizes shall be made available to ensure adequate fit and comfort.

7.7 HEARING PROTECTION USE AND CARE

Employees required to wear hearing protection shall use it properly and consistently in designated areas or during designated tasks.

Requirements include:
• Inserting or positioning HPDs correctly before entering the noise hazard area
• Keeping hearing protection in place for the full duration of exposure
• Replacing damaged, dirty, or worn-out hearing protection as needed
• Storing reusable hearing protection in a clean manner
• Maintaining earmuff cushions, bands, and seals in usable condition
• Not modifying hearing protection devices in a manner that reduces effectiveness

**CRITICAL:** Even brief removal of hearing protection in high-noise areas significantly reduces the effective protection. An employee who removes earplugs for even a few minutes per hour in a 95 dBA environment may receive more noise exposure during those unprotected minutes than during the entire protected period.

Supervisors shall address improper wear, removal in hazard areas, or repeated noncompliance promptly.

7.8 POSTED NOISE AREAS AND SIGNAGE

Areas or tasks requiring hearing protection shall be clearly identified as appropriate to site conditions.

Controls include:
• Posted signage at entrances or access points to hearing protection required areas
• Floor markings or boundary indicators where appropriate
• Equipment labels on high-noise machines
• Task-specific instructions for intermittent noisy operations
• Temporary signage for non-routine noisy operations or construction activities
• Restricted access to extremely high-noise areas where feasible

Posted or controlled areas shall be updated when monitoring results, equipment changes, or layout modifications affect the noise hazard. Signage shall be maintained in legible and visible condition.

7.9 AUDIOMETRIC TESTING AND THRESHOLD SHIFT RESPONSE

Audiometrictesting shall be provided where required by the hearing conservation program and applicable standards.

Employees whose exposures equal or exceed an 8-hour TWA of 85 dBA shall be made available audiometric testing.

Baseline audiograms shall be established within 6 months of an employee's first exposure at or above the action level, unless mobile testing services are used, in which case the baseline may be obtained within 1 year. When the baseline is obtained more than 6 months after first exposure, employees shall use hearing protectors during the interim period.

Ongoing requirements include:
• Annual audiometric testing for employees who remain exposed at or above the action level
• Review of test results by qualified personnel
• Notification and follow-up when threshold shifts are identified
• Evaluation of adequacy of hearing protection and exposure controls
• Retraining or refitting where needed

When a standard threshold shift (STS) or other significant hearing change is identified, evaluate:
• Ongoing exposure conditions and monitoring data
• Hearing protection selection, fit, and consistent use
• Training adequacy
• Need for additional engineering or administrative controls
• Need for occupational health follow-up or referral

7.10 IMPULSE, IMPACT, AND SHORT-DURATION HIGH NOISE TASKS

Tasks that create brief but highly hazardous noise exposure shall receive specific review.

Examples include:
• Impact tools — chipping hammers, pneumatic nailers, metal forming
• Pressure release events — gas venting, relief valve activation
• Hammering or metal striking operations
• Test operations — engine testing, pressure testing, functional checks
• Equipment startup events with high transient noise
• Powder-actuated tool use
• Explosive or pyrotechnic operations where separately controlled

Evaluation shall address:
• Hearing protection requirements — type and NRR adequate for peak exposure
• Need for dual hearing protection
• Restricted access during high-noise events
• Communication and alarm recognition impacts
• Task duration control
• Non-routine work planning and additional authorization where applicable

7.11 COMMUNICATION, ALARM AUDIBILITY, AND SITUATIONAL AWARENESS

Controls shall be implemented where noise and hearing protection affect communication and the ability to hear alarms, instructions, or warnings.

Controls include:
• Pre-task communication planning — establish signals, assignments, and contingencies before entering noisy areas
• Hand signals — standardized and understood by all team members
• Radios or communication headsets approved for the environment
• Spotters for mobile equipment or crane operations in noisy areas
• Visual alarm systems — strobe lights, rotating beacons, or other visual indicators where audible alarms may be masked
• Restricted access during high-noise work to limit the number of personnel at risk
• Work sequencing to reduce conflicting communication demands

**CRITICAL:** Noise controls and hearing protection shall be managed so they do not create new uncontrolled hazards from communication failure. If workers cannot hear critical alarms or instructions while wearing required hearing protection, additional controls (visual alarms, buddy systems, or communication devices) must be implemented.

7.12 EQUIPMENT CONDITION AND MAINTENANCE

Unusual or rising equipment noise shall be treated as a potential maintenance or safety problem.

Requirements include:
• Reporting equipment that becomes unusually loud
• Inspecting and maintaining mufflers, guards, housings, bearings, mounts, and other noise-related components
• Repairing leaks, looseness, imbalance, and wear contributing to excessive noise
• Considering noise during equipment purchasing, replacement, and modification decisions
• Including noise evaluation in preventive maintenance programs
• Documenting equipment noise concerns and resolutions

Noisy equipment shall not remain in service without evaluation when the sound change suggests abnormal condition or increased hazard.

7.13 COMBINED EXPOSURE AND TASK INTERFACE CONSIDERATIONS

Combined exposure conditions shall be addressed when noise hazards interact with other workplace factors.

Consider noise in combination with:
• Heat stress — hearing protection combined with other PPE increases heat burden
• Respirator use — respirators may interfere with earmuff seal and affect attenuation
• Heavy or layered PPE — bulky equipment may displace earmuffs or impair fit
• Remote or isolated work — reduced ability to summon help or hear warnings
• Mobile equipment interaction — reduced ability to hear approaching equipment
• Confined spaces — amplified noise in enclosed geometry
• Hot work — welding hoods may conflict with earmuff positioning
• Communication-critical tasks — tasks requiring continuous verbal coordination
• High-consequence operations — where communication failure could cause serious injury

Work planning shall address both hearing protection requirements and the added operational risks created by noise.

7.14 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION

Coordination is required when contractors perform work in noisy environments onsite.

Coordination shall include:
• Notification of noise area requirements and posted zones
• Hearing protection expectations — type and availability
• Temporary high-noise activities — advance coordination with site supervision
• Restricted access and signage requirements
• Communication and alarm concerns
• Reporting and stop-work requirements
• Whether contractor activities introduce new noise hazards affecting other personnel onsite

Contractors shall not allow personnel to work in hazardous noise areas without appropriate hearing protection and site coordination.

7.15 INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW

Immediate reporting is required for any hearing-related symptom, tinnitus complaint, sudden noise incident, threshold shift notification, near miss, alarm audibility concern, or unsafe noise condition.

Investigations shall evaluate:
• Exposure level and duration
• Monitoring adequacy and data currency
• Engineering and administrative controls in place
• Hearing protection selection, fit, and consistent use
• Equipment condition and maintenance history
• Communication and alarm effectiveness
• Training adequacy
• Supervision and planning
• Need for engineering, procedural, or training improvements

EHS and operational leadership shall periodically review the Noise Exposure and Hearing Conservation Program for effectiveness and update requirements as needed based on monitoring results, audiometric data, incidents, audits, and lessons learned.

---

APPENDIX A — Noise Hazard Pre-Planning Checklist

☐ Noise sources identified for the task or area
☐ Exposure duration and task demands understood
☐ Existing monitoring data available or assessment needs addressed
☐ Required hearing protection available and appropriate for the exposure
☐ Area signage or temporary controls in place as needed
☐ Communication and alarm needs considered
☐ Contractor coordination addressed where applicable
☐ Stop-work triggers understood
☐ Equipment condition reviewed for abnormal noise

APPENDIX B — Hearing Protection Use Checklist

☐ Correct hearing protection selected for the noise level and task
☐ Hearing protection in usable condition — no damage, degradation, or contamination
☐ Earplugs inserted properly or earmuffs seated correctly over both ears
☐ Hearing protection compatible with other required PPE (hard hat, safety glasses, respirator)
☐ Dual protection used where required by exposure level
☐ Spare hearing protection available where needed
☐ Employee understands not to remove protection during exposure

APPENDIX C — Noise Area Inspection Checklist

☐ Posted hearing protection signs present and legible where required
☐ Employees wearing hearing protection properly in designated areas
☐ Noisy equipment operating as expected without abnormal sound change
☐ Access to high-noise areas controlled as needed
☐ Communication and alarm concerns being managed
☐ Temporary high-noise tasks identified and controlled
☐ Hearing protection supplies available and maintained

APPENDIX D — Hearing Symptom / Threshold Shift Response Checklist

☐ Exposure conditions reviewed
☐ Employee notified and directed appropriately under program requirements
☐ Hearing protection selection and use reevaluated
☐ Monitoring data reviewed or updated if needed
☐ Supervisory and EHS follow-up completed
☐ Corrective actions identified and tracked
☐ Issue documented and investigated as appropriate

APPENDIX E — Conditions Requiring Immediate Stop Work

• Hazardous noise exposure is suspected or identified and required hearing protection is unavailable
• Posted or required hearing protection is not being used in a controlled area
• Equipment produces abnormal, escalating, or uncontrolled noise suggesting unsafe condition
• Communication or alarm recognition is compromised in a manner that creates immediate risk
• A sudden high-noise event, hearing injury, or unsafe condition occurs and remains uncontrolled
• A supervisor, EHS, or other responsible person stops the work
• Conditions change such that safe work under the noise exposure conditions cannot be assured
emergencyResponse: If an employee reports sudden hearing loss, ear pain, tinnitus, or dizziness following a noise event, stop work immediately and remove the employee from the noise exposure area
Notify supervision and EHS immediately
Do not allow the affected employee to return to noise exposure until evaluated and cleared consistent with site medical and supervisory requirements
Secure the area and restrict access if the noise source is uncontrolled or escalating
Document the event including noise source, duration, hearing protection worn, and symptoms reported
Escalate emergency medical response when symptoms are severe or when the noise event caused blast or overpressure exposure
Preserve the scene for investigation — do not restart equipment or resume work until the cause is understood and corrective actions are in place
documentation: Training records — initial, annual, and refresher hearing conservation training
Noise exposure monitoring records — personal dosimetry, area measurements, and task-based evaluations
Area and task evaluation records — noise surveys, posted area designations, and equipment assessments
Hearing conservation program inclusion records — list of employees enrolled based on exposure
Audiometric testing records — managed under applicable confidentiality and retention requirements
Incident, symptom report, near miss, and investigation records
Corrective action records — engineering controls, procedural changes, and HPD upgrades
Contractor coordination records
Audit findings and program review records
