title: Hazardous Waste Management and Universal Waste Management Program
summary: Enterprise-grade program for identifying, characterizing, accumulating, labeling, storing, inspecting, transporting, and arranging proper disposal or recycling of hazardous waste and universal waste. Covers waste determination, generator category awareness, satellite and central accumulation, container compatibility, labeling and marking, universal waste categories (batteries, lamps, aerosol cans, mercury-containing equipment, pesticides), spill prevention, off-site shipment coordination, contractor coordination, and incident reporting. Includes five operational appendices with pre-planning, container inspection, universal waste inspection, shipment coordination, and stop-work checklists.

sections.purpose:
This procedure establishes the minimum requirements for identifying, characterizing, accumulating, labeling, storing, inspecting, transporting, and arranging proper disposal or recycling of hazardous waste and universal waste generated by [Company Name] operations.

The purpose is to prevent injury, serious injury, fatality, fire, explosion, toxic exposure, incompatible waste reactions, spills, environmental releases, regulatory noncompliance, property damage, and operational disruption resulting from improper waste identification, poor container management, incompatible storage, inadequate labeling, uncontrolled accumulation, failure to evaluate waste streams, and improper off-site shipment practices.

Hazardous waste mismanagement can result in immediate safety hazards, long-term environmental contamination, significant regulatory liability, and serious harm to employees, the community, and the environment. This program requires systematic waste determination, compliant accumulation, proper container and labeling management, compatible storage, inspection, training, emergency preparedness, waste minimization, and proper off-site shipment to protect all personnel and the environment.

This procedure supports compliance with the Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA), 40 CFR Parts 260–273, applicable state hazardous waste and universal waste regulations, DOT hazardous materials transportation requirements, and [Company Name] policies.
sections.scope:
This procedure applies to all [Company Name] facilities, operations, departments, laboratories, production areas, fabrication areas, maintenance activities, test areas, warehouses, utilities, field locations, assembly areas, construction support areas, and non-routine operations where hazardous waste or universal waste is generated, accumulated, handled, stored, shipped, or otherwise managed.

It applies to:
• Employees who generate, handle, inspect, label, move, consolidate, or arrange shipment of hazardous waste or universal waste
• Supervisors and managers responsible for operations that generate regulated waste
• EHS personnel
• Engineers, technicians, mechanics, operators, maintenance personnel, laboratory personnel, inspectors, facilities personnel, and support personnel
• Contractors generating or handling regulated waste onsite or under company operational control

Regulated waste streams include but are not limited to:
• Ignitable, corrosive, reactive, or toxic hazardous waste
• Listed hazardous wastes where applicable
• Waste solvents, paints, coatings, adhesives, and contaminated residues
• Hazardous waste aerosols where managed outside universal waste rules
• Contaminated absorbents and cleanup materials where applicable
• Waste acids, caustics, and process chemicals
• Hazardous laboratory wastes and maintenance wastes
• Waste batteries, lamps, aerosol cans, mercury-containing equipment, and pesticides managed as universal waste

This SOP shall be used in conjunction with Hazard Communication, SPCC, SWPPP, Health Exposure to Hazardous Materials, Contractor Safety Management, Non-Routine Activities, Emergency Response, and site-specific environmental compliance procedures.

definitions.definition: The date on which hazardous waste first begins to accumulate in a container at a satellite accumulation area (when moved to a central accumulation area) or at a central accumulation area, triggering applicable time limits for storage before shipment.

A designated area where hazardous waste is consolidated and stored prior to off-site shipment, subject to generator category-specific requirements for time limits, inspection, containment, labeling, aisle space, and emergency preparedness.

A solid waste that exhibits one or more hazardous characteristics (ignitability, corrosivity, reactivity, toxicity) or is specifically listed as hazardous under applicable federal or state regulations.

The process of evaluating whether a waste is hazardous by applying applicable regulatory criteria including listing, characteristics testing, process knowledge, SDS review, and analytical data where needed.

A location at or near the point of generation where up to the applicable quantity of hazardous waste may be accumulated under the control of the operator generating the waste, without triggering full central accumulation area requirements.

A category of hazardous waste subject to streamlined management standards under 40 CFR Part 273 or equivalent state rules, including batteries, lamps, aerosol cans, mercury-containing equipment, and pesticides, plus any state-added categories.

definitions.term: Small Quantity Handler of Universal Waste
definitions.definition: A handler who accumulates less than 5,000 kilograms of universal waste at any time, subject to reduced requirements for training, labeling, and accumulation time tracking.

definitions.term: Large Quantity Handler of Universal Waste
definitions.definition: A handler who accumulates 5,000 kilograms or more of universal waste at any time, subject to additional requirements for notification, tracking, and employee training.
Any portable device in which a material is stored, transported, treated, disposed of, or otherwise handled, including drums, pails, buckets, bags, boxes, and other approved receptacles.

The reduction, to the extent feasible, of hazardous waste that is generated or subsequently treated, stored, or disposed of, through source reduction, recycling, improved processes, and material substitution.

rolesAndResponsibilities.responsibility: Administer and maintain the Hazardous Waste and Universal Waste Management Program. Coordinate hazardous waste determinations and waste stream evaluations. Maintain generator category awareness and regulatory compliance documentation. Oversee accumulation area compliance, inspection programs, and corrective actions. Coordinate off-site shipment, transporter, and destination facility approvals. Manage training program content and scheduling. Track regulatory changes at federal, state, and local levels. Conduct or coordinate program audits and periodic reviews. Support incident investigation and corrective action development. Serve as the primary point of contact for regulatory inquiries and inspections.

Ensure employees under their direction comply with waste management requirements. Ensure waste containers are available, compatible, labeled, and properly managed in their areas. Report new or changed waste streams to EHS for determination. Monitor accumulation areas within their responsibility. Ensure employees receive required training before handling regulated waste. Investigate and report waste-related incidents, spills, and near misses. Support waste minimization efforts. Coordinate with EHS on contractor waste generation and handling.

Identify waste streams generated by their work and follow established procedures. Place waste only in designated, compatible, and properly labeled containers. Keep containers closed except when adding or removing waste where required. Report spills, leaks, container damage, unlabeled containers, or unknown waste to supervision immediately. Do not mix incompatible wastes. Do not place hazardous waste in ordinary trash or recycling. Attend required training. Exercise stop-work authority when waste conditions are unsafe.

Conduct required inspections of satellite and central accumulation areas at the specified frequency. Verify container condition, closure, labeling, dating, segregation, and secondary containment. Maintain housekeeping and aisle space. Ensure spill response materials and emergency equipment are available and functional. Report deficiencies and initiate corrective actions. Coordinate container movement and pickup scheduling with EHS and shipping personnel.

Coordinate off-site hazardous waste and universal waste shipments through approved transporters and destination facilities. Prepare or verify manifests, shipping papers, and waste profile documentation. Verify container closure, labeling, marking, and packaging before shipment. Maintain shipment records and manifest tracking. Support DOT compliance for regulated waste shipments. Coordinate with EHS on new waste streams, destination facility approvals, and regulatory requirements.

Consider waste generation during process design, modification, and material selection. Support waste minimization through source reduction, material substitution, and process improvement. Notify EHS when process changes may create new or altered waste streams. Support hazardous waste determination with process knowledge, chemical information, and analytical data where needed. Incorporate waste management requirements into project planning and change management.

Comply with [Company Name] waste management requirements while working onsite. Coordinate waste generation, container use, storage, and disposal with [Company Name] supervision and EHS. Do not leave waste onsite, move waste into company areas, or ship waste from site without approved coordination. Report spills, releases, and waste-related incidents immediately. Attend required site orientation and waste management training. Follow posted area requirements and waste segregation controls.

training: Hazardous waste identification basics — characteristics, listings, and the escalation process for waste determination
Universal waste category awareness — batteries, lamps, aerosol cans, mercury-containing equipment, pesticides, and state-expanded categories
Container selection, compatibility, closure, and condition requirements
Labeling and marking requirements for hazardous waste and universal waste — including accumulation start dates
Satellite accumulation area rules — location, quantity limits, operator control, and movement triggers
Central accumulation area rules — time limits, inspection, segregation, containment, and emergency preparedness
Incompatible waste segregation and prohibited practices — no mixing, no drain disposal, no unauthorized treatment
Spill prevention and response — containment, cleanup, reporting, and escalation
Emergency procedures — fire, release, incompatible reaction, and medical response
Off-site shipment coordination basics — manifesting, transporter, and documentation awareness
Waste minimization and source reduction awareness
Incident and near-miss reporting requirements
Stop-work authority and obligation when waste conditions create uncontrolled risk
ppe: Chemical-resistant gloves appropriate to the waste being handled
Safety glasses or chemical splash goggles based on splash or vapor risk
Face shield where splash exposure to corrosive, toxic, or reactive waste is possible
Chemical-resistant apron or protective clothing where skin contact risk exists
Steel-toe safety footwear
Respiratory protection where vapor, dust, or atmospheric hazards are present — managed under the Respiratory Protection Program
Spill response PPE — as specified in the spill response plan for the waste type
Additional PPE as required by the hazard assessment and Safety Data Sheet for the waste stream
hazards: Chemical exposure from contact with hazardous waste — skin absorption, inhalation of vapors or dusts, eye contact, or ingestion
Fire and explosion from ignitable waste, incompatible mixing, or uncontrolled vapor accumulation
Incompatible waste reactions — exothermic reactions, toxic gas generation, fire, or violent decomposition from mixing incompatible materials
Environmental release — spills, leaks, or container failures causing soil, water, or air contamination
Corrosive burns from waste acids, caustics, or reactive chemicals
Toxic exposure from listed or characteristic hazardous wastes during handling, transfer, or cleanup
Mercury exposure from broken lamps, thermometers, switches, or mercury-containing equipment managed as universal waste
Battery hazards — acid or alkaline electrolyte leaks, short circuits, thermal events from damaged lithium batteries
Aerosol can hazards — pressurized contents, flammable propellants, and puncture/explosion risk during improper handling
Regulatory noncompliance — fines, enforcement actions, and operational shutdown from improper waste management
Property damage from spills, fires, or container failures in accumulation areas
Contamination of clean waste streams through careless segregation or mislabeling
procedure:
7.1 HAZARDOUS WASTE DETERMINATION

A hazardous waste determination shall be performed for each waste stream at the point of generation and whenever the process, material inputs, or waste characteristics change.

The determination shall evaluate:
• Whether the material is a solid waste under applicable definitions
• Whether any exclusion or exemption applies
• Whether the waste is listed under 40 CFR 261 Subpart D or equivalent state listings
• Whether the waste exhibits a hazardous characteristic — ignitability, corrosivity, reactivity, or toxicity
• Applicable process knowledge — raw materials, process chemistry, SDSs, and historical waste data
• Analytical data where process knowledge is insufficient or uncertain
• State-specific requirements that may expand the federal hazardous waste definition

Hazardous waste determinations shall be documented and retained as required by applicable regulations and [Company Name] policy.

Employees who are uncertain whether a waste is hazardous shall not guess — they shall stop, contain the material safely, and escalate to EHS or the designated waste determination authority.

[Hazardous Waste Determination Process — Insert site-specific workflow here]

7.2 GENERATOR CATEGORY AWARENESS AND SITE APPLICABILITY

The site shall determine and maintain awareness of its applicable generator category under RCRA and state regulations.

[Generator Category — Insert applicable category: Very Small Quantity Generator (VSQG), Small Quantity Generator (SQG), or Large Quantity Generator (LQG)]

Generator category affects:
• Accumulation time limits (e.g., 270 days for SQG, 90 days for LQG)
• Quantity limits on site at any time
• Training depth and frequency requirements
• Preparedness and prevention obligations
• Contingency planning and emergency coordinator requirements
• Manifest, identification number, and biennial reporting requirements
• Inspection frequency and documentation

This SOP is written as a robust framework applicable to all generator categories. Each site shall tailor accumulation limits, training depth, contingency planning, and reporting to match its actual generator status and state-specific requirements.

7.3 WASTE MINIMIZATION AND SOURCE REDUCTION

Waste generation shall be minimized where feasible through:
• Process changes that reduce hazardous byproducts
• Material substitution — using less hazardous inputs where technically equivalent
• Better inventory control — reducing expired or excess chemical purchases
• Improved housekeeping — preventing contamination of clean materials
• Segregation of clean versus contaminated material to avoid unnecessary hazardous waste volume
• Reuse and recycling where appropriate and approved
• Improved maintenance practices — reducing waste from leaks, spills, and equipment failures

Waste minimization shall be considered whenever new processes, chemicals, or recurring waste streams are reviewed. Engineering and process owners shall collaborate with EHS to identify reduction opportunities during design, modification, and procurement activities.

Waste minimization progress shall be documented where required by generator category or company policy.

7.4 CONTAINER SELECTION AND COMPATIBILITY

Hazardous waste and universal waste shall be accumulated in containers that are:
• Compatible with the waste — chemically and physically suitable for the contents
• In good condition — structurally sound, free of leaks, corrosion, dents, or damage that could compromise integrity
• Appropriately sized for the waste volume and accumulation point

Requirements include:
• Using suitable containers for the chemical and physical hazards present (e.g., poly drums for corrosives, steel drums for solvents where compatible)
• Keeping containers closed except when adding or removing waste where required by regulation
• Prohibiting the use of rusted, bulging, cracked, or compromised containers
• Keeping incompatible wastes in separate compatible containers
• Using secondary containment where required by regulation or where prudent based on waste characteristics and location

Damaged or leaking containers shall be overpacked, transferred to a sound container, or otherwise managed promptly and safely. Leaking containers shall not be left unattended.

7.5 LABELING AND MARKING REQUIREMENTS

Hazardous waste containers shall be labeled in accordance with applicable generator requirements and site procedures.

Required label elements include:
• The words "Hazardous Waste" clearly visible
• Hazard indication — applicable hazard(s) of the waste
• Accumulation start date — required when the container is moved to a central accumulation area or when otherwise required by generator rules
• Waste description or waste stream identification
• Area or point-of-generation identifier where applicable

Universal waste shall be marked with the appropriate category designation:
• "Universal Waste—Battery(ies)"
• "Universal Waste—Lamp(s)"
• "Universal Waste—Aerosol Can(s)"
• "Universal Waste—Mercury-Containing Equipment"
• "Universal Waste—Pesticide(s)"
• [Additional state-specific universal waste category markings as applicable]

Labels shall be legible, durable, and visible. Faded, damaged, or missing labels shall be replaced immediately.

[State-Specific Marking or Dating Rules — Insert applicable requirements here]

7.6 SATELLITE ACCUMULATION AREAS (SAAs)

Satellite accumulation areas shall be managed at or near the point of generation under applicable generator requirements.

SAA requirements include:
• Location at or near the point where waste is initially generated
• Under the control of the operator generating the waste
• Compatible and properly labeled containers
• Containers kept closed except when adding or removing waste where required
• Good housekeeping and spill control in the immediate area
• Accumulation up to the applicable quantity limit (typically 55 gallons of hazardous waste or 1 quart of acutely hazardous waste per waste stream)

When the quantity limit is reached or exceeded, the container shall be dated and moved to a central accumulation area within the applicable timeframe (typically 3 days).

[Satellite Accumulation Area Requirements — Insert site-specific rules, quantity triggers, and movement procedures here]

7.7 CENTRAL ACCUMULATION AREAS (CAAs)

Central accumulation areas shall be designated and managed to meet applicable hazardous waste generator requirements.

CAA requirements include:
• Clearly defined area boundaries — marked, posted, or otherwise identified
• Compatible waste segregation — acids separated from bases, oxidizers from ignitables, etc.
• Proper labels and accumulation start dates on all containers
• Required aisle space for inspection access and emergency response
• Spill response materials and emergency equipment readily available
• Inspection at the required frequency (weekly for LQG, or as required by generator category)
• Secondary containment where required by regulation or waste characteristics
• Protection from weather, traffic, and physical damage where appropriate
• Posted emergency information where required — emergency coordinator, fire department, hospital, spill response

Only trained or authorized personnel shall manage waste in central accumulation areas.

Accumulation time limits shall be tracked and shipment coordinated before the applicable deadline.

[Central Accumulation Area Requirements — Insert site-specific area designations, inspection frequency, and time limits here]

7.8 INSPECTION REQUIREMENTS

Hazardous waste and universal waste accumulation areas shall be inspected at frequencies required by the applicable site program and legal requirements.

Inspection elements include:
• Container condition — no leaks, corrosion, swelling, or structural damage
• Container closure — lids and bungs secure where required
• Labeling and dating — present, legible, and accurate
• Evidence of leaks, spills, or residue on or around containers
• Compatibility and segregation — incompatible wastes properly separated
• Housekeeping — area clean, organized, and free of debris
• Secondary containment condition — intact, free of accumulated liquids except captured waste
• Access to emergency equipment — fire extinguishers, spill kits, eyewash, communication devices
• Universal waste packaging — intact, no breakage, no visible release
• Signs of overaccumulation or improper storage

Deficiencies shall be corrected promptly and tracked to closure. Inspection records shall be maintained as required.

[Waste Inspection Frequency — Insert site-specific schedule here]

7.9 INCOMPATIBLE WASTE SEGREGATION AND PROHIBITED PRACTICES

Hazardous waste and universal waste shall be managed to prevent incompatible reactions and unsafe conditions.

Requirements include:
• Segregation of acids, bases, oxidizers, reactives, ignitables, toxics, and other incompatible materials as appropriate
• Physical separation — distance, containment barriers, or separate storage areas
• Prohibition on mixing hazardous waste streams unless specifically evaluated and approved by EHS
• Prohibition on placing hazardous waste in ordinary trash, recycling, or sanitary waste streams
• Prohibition on evaporating, diluting, or drain-disposing waste unless explicitly authorized under applicable regulations
• Prohibition on compacting, crushing, puncturing, draining, or dismantling regulated waste items except under approved procedures allowed by applicable regulation (e.g., approved aerosol can puncturing under universal waste rules)

**CRITICAL:** Residuals from approved universal waste handling activities (e.g., liquid from punctured aerosol cans, broken lamp debris, leaked battery acid) may themselves be hazardous waste and must be evaluated and managed accordingly.

7.10 UNIVERSAL WASTE MANAGEMENT

Universal waste shall be managed under applicable federal (40 CFR Part 273) and state rules.

[Universal Waste Categories Used Onsite — Insert applicable categories]

General universal waste requirements include:
• Proper marking or labeling with the applicable universal waste category designation
• Protection from breakage, release, and weather where applicable
• Management of damaged items and residues under correct procedures
• Employee training appropriate to handler category (small quantity or large quantity)
• Shipment to approved handlers or destination facilities
• Accumulation time control — universal waste may not be accumulated for more than one year from the date generated or received unless for the purpose of accumulating a sufficient quantity for shipment

[State-Specific Universal Waste Rules — Insert state-adopted categories, expanded requirements, or additional controls here]

7.11 SPECIFIC UNIVERSAL WASTE CONTROLS

**BATTERIES**
• Store in compatible containers or on pallets with terminal protection where applicable
• Prevent short circuits by taping terminals, bagging individually, or using terminal caps
• Segregate damaged, leaking, or swollen batteries immediately — overpack and label
• Do not place batteries in ordinary trash
• Lithium batteries require additional precautions — isolation, fire-rated containers, or enhanced packaging where required by site policy

**LAMPS (Fluorescent, HID, LED where applicable)**
• Store in original packaging, fiber boxes, or approved lamp containers
• Protect from breakage during handling, storage, and transport
• Broken lamps shall be cleaned up using appropriate methods (mercury-safe cleanup for mercury-containing lamps) and the debris managed as hazardous waste
• Do not crush or intentionally break lamps unless using an approved drum-top crusher under applicable regulatory authorization

**AEROSOL CANS**
• Store upright in compatible containers
• Protect from puncture, excessive heat, and ignition sources
• Do not puncture, drain, or dismantle aerosol cans except under approved procedures with proper equipment as allowed by applicable regulation
• Manage residual liquids from approved puncturing operations as hazardous waste if they exhibit hazardous characteristics

**MERCURY-CONTAINING EQUIPMENT**
• Handle carefully to prevent breakage and mercury release
• Store in sealed containers with cushioning to prevent damage
• Clean up mercury spills using approved mercury spill kits — do not vacuum mercury with standard equipment
• Manage mercury-contaminated debris as hazardous waste

**PESTICIDES**
• Manage recalled or unused pesticides as universal waste where applicable
• Store in original containers with labels intact where possible
• Follow manufacturer and label instructions for safe handling
• Coordinate disposal through approved pesticide waste handlers

7.12 SPILL PREVENTION, RESPONSE, AND EMERGENCY PREPAREDNESS

Waste handling and accumulation areas shall be managed to prevent spills and releases.

Preventive controls include:
• Compatible containers in good condition
• Secondary containment where needed or required
• Spill kits and response materials staged at accumulation areas
• Good housekeeping and organized storage
• Segregated and incompatible waste separation
• Employee training on spill prevention and response

Emergency preparedness includes:
• Emergency contacts and response instructions posted or available at accumulation areas
• Fire extinguishers, eyewash, and communication devices accessible
• Evacuation routes identified
• Medical response escalation defined

Immediate reporting and controlled response is required for spills, releases, container failures, incompatible reactions, or unknown waste events. Employees shall not attempt cleanup of large, unknown, or reactive spills — evacuate the area and summon trained response personnel.

[Emergency Contact / Contingency Information — Insert site-specific contacts here]

7.13 OFF-SITE SHIPMENT AND DISPOSAL / RECYCLING COORDINATION

Hazardous waste and universal waste shall be shipped only through approved processes.

Requirements include:
• Use of approved transporters where required by regulation
• Shipment to approved destination facilities or recyclers
• Completion of hazardous waste manifests (EPA Form 8700-22) for hazardous waste shipments
• Shipping papers and documentation for universal waste shipments
• Container packaging, marking, closure, and labeling verification before pickup
• Record retention — manifests, shipping papers, and exception reports
• Coordination with site shipping and environmental personnel
• Compliance with DOT hazardous materials transportation requirements where applicable
• Exception report procedures when manifests are not returned within the required timeframe

[Approved Transporters / Destination Facilities — Insert approved vendors and coordination process here]

7.14 TRAINING REQUIREMENTS BY ROLE

Training shall be provided appropriate to employee duties and the site's generator category.

All waste generators shall receive:
• Waste identification basics and escalation process
• Container compatibility, closure, and condition requirements
• Labeling and dating requirements
• Satellite versus central accumulation rules
• Spill prevention and response
• Emergency procedures
• Stop-work and reporting expectations

Accumulation area custodians shall additionally receive:
• Detailed inspection requirements and documentation
• Incompatible waste segregation
• Secondary containment management
• Emergency equipment awareness

Shipping and environmental support personnel shall additionally receive:
• Manifest preparation and processing
• DOT marking, labeling, and packaging requirements
• Transporter and facility coordination
• Record retention and exception reporting

Large quantity generators shall provide annual refresher training as required by 40 CFR 262.17(a)(7) or equivalent state requirements.

Training records shall be maintained and available for inspection.

7.15 RECORDKEEPING

Records shall be maintained in accordance with applicable law and [Company Name] policy.

Required records include:
• Hazardous waste determinations and supporting documentation
• Waste stream profiles and characterization data
• Inspection logs — satellite and central accumulation area inspections
• Training records — initial, annual, and refresher training
• Hazardous waste manifests and shipping records — retained for at least 3 years or as required
• Land disposal restriction (LDR) notices and certifications where required
• Universal waste shipment records
• Incident, spill, and near-miss reports
• Corrective action records
• Generator category support documentation
• Biennial reports where required by generator category
• State-specific reporting and notification records
• Exception reports for unreturned manifests

7.16 CONTRACTOR COORDINATION

Contractor waste generation and handling shall be coordinated under the Contractor Safety Management Program and this SOP.

Coordination shall include:
• Waste ownership and generator responsibility — clearly defined before work begins
• Container and labeling requirements
• Approved storage locations
• Spill response expectations
• Shipment authority — contractors shall not ship waste from site without approved coordination
• Unknown waste escalation — contractors shall notify [Company Name] immediately if unknown waste is encountered
• Training and supervision expectations

Contractors shall not:
• Leave waste onsite without coordination
• Move waste into company accumulation areas without authorization
• Ship waste from site without approved manifesting and coordination
• Mix contractor-generated waste with company waste streams without EHS approval
• Dispose of waste through unapproved methods

7.17 INCIDENT REPORTING AND PROGRAM REVIEW

Immediate reporting is required for any spill, release, incompatible waste event, mislabeling, unknown waste discovery, container failure, shipment error, inspection deficiency, regulatory concern, or unsafe condition related to hazardous waste or universal waste.

Investigations shall evaluate:
• Waste identification and characterization adequacy
• Container and labeling controls
• Compatibility and segregation practices
• Storage conditions and accumulation controls
• Inspection quality and frequency
• Training adequacy
• Shipping and documentation controls
• Supervision and contractor coordination
• Need for engineering, procedural, or training improvements

EHS and operational leadership shall periodically review the Hazardous Waste Management and Universal Waste Management Program for effectiveness and update requirements as needed based on incidents, audits, regulatory changes, and lessons learned.

---

APPENDIX A — Waste Pre-Planning Checklist

☐ Waste stream identified or escalation path defined
☐ Hazardous waste determination responsibility assigned
☐ Correct container available and compatible with the waste
☐ Labeling requirements understood — hazardous waste or universal waste marking
☐ Storage area identified — SAA or CAA as appropriate
☐ Compatibility and segregation needs addressed
☐ Spill response materials available
☐ Inspection expectations known
☐ Shipment or pickup process defined where needed
☐ Contractor coordination addressed where applicable

APPENDIX B — Hazardous Waste Container Inspection Checklist

☐ Container in good condition — no leaks, corrosion, dents, or structural damage
☐ Container closed where required — lid and bung secure
☐ Label present and legible — "Hazardous Waste" clearly visible
☐ Hazard indication present on the label
☐ Accumulation start date present where required
☐ No leaks, drips, or residue visible on or around the container
☐ Incompatible wastes properly segregated
☐ Secondary containment functional and free of accumulated liquids (except captured waste)
☐ Aisle space and access maintained
☐ Emergency equipment accessible — fire extinguisher, spill kit, eyewash

APPENDIX C — Universal Waste Inspection Checklist

☐ Universal waste in the correct category and properly identified
☐ Containers or packaging structurally sound
☐ Labels and markings present and legible — correct category designation
☐ Waste protected from breakage, release, or weather damage
☐ Damaged items managed under correct procedure — overpacked, cleaned up, residues evaluated
☐ Accumulation time control maintained — not exceeding one year
☐ Shipment planning current and coordinated
☐ No unapproved dismantling, puncturing, crushing, or draining occurring

APPENDIX D — Shipment / Disposal Coordination Checklist

☐ Waste profile or determination current and on file
☐ Container closure and condition acceptable for transport
☐ Marking and labeling complete — hazardous waste labels, DOT markings where required
☐ Required shipping paperwork prepared — manifest, LDR notice, shipping papers
☐ Approved transporter and receiving facility confirmed
☐ Pickup authorization complete
☐ Records retention steps assigned
☐ Emergency contact information available for the shipment

APPENDIX E — Conditions Requiring Immediate Stop Work

• Waste identity is uncertain and uncontrolled
• A container is leaking, damaged, unlabeled, or incompatible with its contents
• Hazardous and nonhazardous wastes are being mixed improperly
• Incompatible wastes are stored together without adequate segregation
• Universal waste is being mishandled or damaged in a way that creates release risk
• Required labels, dates, or storage controls are missing
• A spill, reaction, release, or unsafe condition occurs and remains uncontrolled
• A supervisor, EHS, or other responsible person stops the work
• Conditions change such that safe waste management cannot be assured
emergencyResponse: If a spill, release, container failure, or incompatible reaction occurs, stop work immediately and evacuate the immediate area if needed
Notify supervision and EHS immediately — provide waste identity, quantity, and location if known
Do not attempt cleanup of large, unknown, or reactive spills — summon trained response personnel
For small, known spills within employee training and capability, use appropriate spill kits and PPE
Contain the release if safe to do so — use absorbents, berms, or overpack containers as appropriate
Keep personnel away from the affected area until the situation is evaluated and controlled
Escalate to emergency response when the release exceeds onsite capability, threatens drains or waterways, creates fire or explosion risk, or causes injury or exposure
Document the event including waste type, quantity, location, cause, response actions, and personnel involved
documentation: Hazardous waste determination records and supporting documentation
Waste stream profiles and characterization data
Training records — initial, annual, and refresher
Inspection records — SAA and CAA inspections with deficiency tracking
Hazardous waste manifests and shipping records
Universal waste shipment records
Land disposal restriction (LDR) notices where required
Incident, spill, near miss, and investigation records
Corrective action records
Contractor coordination records
Audit findings and program review records
State-specific reports and notifications where required
